claims.claim: Anaplastic oligodendroglioma, IDH-mutant and 1p/19q-codeleted is not recommended.
Oligodendroglioma, IDH-mutant and 1p/19q-codeleted, is a diffusely infiltrating glioma.
Oligodendroglioma, IDH-mutant and 1p/19q-codeleted, has IDH1 or IDH2 mutation.
Oligodendroglioma, IDH-mutant and 1p/19q-codeleted, has codeletion of chromosome arms 1p and 19q.
Oligodendroglioma, IDH-mutant and 1p/19q-codeleted, is classified as CNS WHO grade 2 or 3.
9450/3 Oligodendroglioma, IDH-mutant and 1p/19q-codeleted, grade 2
9451/3 Oligodendroglioma, IDH-mutant and 1p/19q-codeleted, grade 3
2A00.0Y Other specified gliomas of brain
XH7K31 Oligodendroglioma, IDH-mutant and 1p/19q-codeleted
Seizures are the presenting symptom in approximately two thirds of patients with IDH-mutant and 1p/19q-codeleted oligodendroglioma.

Additional common initial symptoms of IDH-mutant and 1p/19q-codeleted oligodendroglioma include headache, other signs of increased intracranial pressure, focal neurological deficits, and cognitive changes.
The signs and symptoms of IDH-mutant and 1p/19q-codeleted oligodendroglioma are nonspecific and depend on the tumour’s location and speed of growth.

With advanced imaging becoming more widely available for symptom screening, incidental diagnosis of IDH-mutant and 1p/19q-codeleted oligodendroglioma is more frequently reported, accounting for 10% of cases in one study.

Clinical factors associated with more favourable outcomes in IDH-mutant and 1p/19q-codeleted oligodendroglioma include younger patient age at diagnosis, frontal lobe location, presentation with seizures, high postoperative Karnofsky score, and macroscopically complete surgical removal.
Many clinical factors, including younger patient age, are confirmed in studies on molecularly defined oligodendroglioma, but limited follow-up remains an issue.

Among 5542 histologically defined oligodendrogliomas registered in the Central Brain Tumor Registry of the United States (CBTRUS) database, 59% were located in the frontal lobe, 14% in the temporal lobe, 10% in the parietal lobe, and 1% in the occipital lobe.
Among 470 genetically defined CNS WHO grade 3 oligodendrogliomas of the French national POLA network, 62% were frontal tumours, 16% were temporal, 15% were parietal, and 6% were occipital.
Other studies have shown a clear predilection for the frontal lobes in IDH-mutant and 1p/19q-codeleted oligodendrogliomas.
Less common locations for oligodendrogliomas include the posterior fossa, basal ganglia, and brainstem.
Exceptional cases of IDH-mutant and 1p/19q-codeleted oligodendroglioma show widespread intracerebral dissemination corresponding to a gliomatosis cerebri pattern.
Leptomeningeal spread is occasionally seen in patients with IDH-mutant and 1p/19q-codeleted oligodendroglioma, in particular at recurrence.
Primary leptomeningeal manifestation of IDH-mutant and 1p/19q-codeleted oligodendroglioma has been reported.
Rare cases of intramedullary spinal oligodendroglioma are on record, but data on genotype are usually lacking.
Rarely, patients may present with multifocal tumours.
Individual cases of morphologically defined oligodendrogliomas (not genetically characterized) that developed from ovarian teratomas have been reported.
Oligodendrogliomas typically show a dense network of branching capillaries resembling chicken wire.
In some cases, the capillary stroma tends to subdivide the tumour into lobules.
In CNS WHO grade 3 tumours, focal or dispersed pathological microvascular proliferation is frequent.
Oligodendrogliomas have a tendency to develop intratumoural haemorrhages.
Oligodendroglioma, IDH-mutant and 1p/19q-codeleted, CNS WHO grade 2; oligodendroglioma, IDH-mutant and 1p/19q-codeleted, CNS WHO grade 3.

Rare cases of diffuse gliomas with classic oligodendroglioma histology in which molecular testing for combined IDH mutation and 1p/19q codeletion failed can be histologically classified as oligodendroglioma NOS and designated as CNS WHO grade 2 or 3 depending on the presence or absence of histological features of anaplasia.
Immunohistochemical demonstration of IDH mutation and retained nuclear positivity for ATRX may support the oligodendroglioma diagnosis.
Unless successfully tested for 1p/19q codeletion, such tumours cannot be classified as oligodendroglioma, IDH-mutant and 1p/19q-codeleted.

Immunohistochemical positivity for oligodendroglioma-associated markers such as a-internexin and NOGO-A, reduced nuclear H3 p.K28me3 (K27me3) immunostaining, and immunohistochemical loss of nuclear CIC or FUBP1 expression are not sufficient to substitute for 1p/19q codeletion testing.

The diagnosis of oligoastrocytoma NOS is reserved for diffuse gliomas that are composed of a conspicuous mixture of two distinct neoplastic cell types morphologically resembling tumour cells with either oligodendroglial or astrocytic features, and in which testing for IDH mutation, nuclear ATRX expression, and 1p/19q codeletion failed or could not be completed.

The diagnosis of oligoastrocytoma is discouraged because molecular analyses have shown that these tumours carry a genetic profile typical of either IDH-mutant astrocytoma or IDH-mutant and 1p/19q-codeleted oligodendroglioma.
Diffuse gliomas with mixed or ambiguous histological features should be evaluated for IDH mutation and loss of nuclear ATRX expression, as well as for 1p/19q codeletion when nuclear ATRX is retained.

Rare cases of dual-genotype oligoastrocytomas have been reported, characterized by two distinct IDH-mutant tumour cell populations: one showing astrocytoma-associated alterations, such as ATRX loss and TP53 mutation, and the other showing oligodendroglioma-associated 1p/19q codeletion.

The WHO Classification of Tumours does not consider dual-genotype oligoastrocytomas to be a distinct type or sub-type of IDH-mutant diffuse glioma, but they may be tentatively classified as dual-genotype oligoastrocytoma NEC.
In cytological preparations, oligodendroglial tumour cells show uniform round nuclei and well-delineated cytoplasm, with only a modest degree of glial fibrillarity.
The perinuclear haloes that are typical of histological preparations are not appreciated in smear specimens.
In CNS WHO grade 3 oligodendrogliomas, intensely eosinophilic cytoplasmic granules are occasionally noted.
Reactive astrocytes harbouring eosinophilic cytoplasm with multipolar processes may also be present.
In some cases, microcalcifications and prominent vasculature can be appreciated.
IDH-mutant and 1p/19q-codeleted oligodendrogliomas characteristically extend into adjacent brain in a diffuse manner.
IDH-mutant and 1p/19q-codeleted oligodendrogliomas occasionally have a gliomatosis cerebri pattern.
In late-stage disease, distant leptomeningeal spread may occur in some patients with oligodendrogliomas.
Rare cases of extracranial metastases of oligodendrogliomas, mostly CNS WHO grade 3, have been reported.
Patients with progressive oligodendrogliomas without treatment options may show slow clinical deterioration despite the presence of large enhancing lesions.
Oligodendrogliomas grow diffusely in the cortex and white matter.
Some oligodendrogliomas feature distinct nodules of higher cellularity against a background of diffuse infiltration.
Occasional oligodendrogliomas show a gliomatosis cerebri-like pattern involving more than two cerebral lobes.
Within the cortex, oligodendroglioma tumour cells often form secondary structures such as perineuronal satellitosis, perivascular aggregates, and subpial accumulations.
Circumscribed leptomeningeal infiltration by oligodendrogliomas may induce a desmoplastic reaction.
Oligodendrogliomas can have a rare spongioblastic growth pattern consisting of parallel rows of tumour cells with somewhat elongated nuclei forming rhythmic palisades.
Occasionally, perivascular pseudorosettes are seen in oligodendrogliomas, although some of these are a result of perivascular neuropil formation within foci of neurocytic differentiation.
The patterns of oligodendrogliomas are generally present only focally.
IDH-mutant and 1p/19q-codeleted oligodendrogliomas usually appear on CT as hypodense or isodense mass lesions that are typically located in the cortex and subcortical white matter.
Calcifications are commonly seen in IDH-mutant and 1p/19q-codeleted oligodendrogliomas, but they are not diagnostic.
Some IDH-mutant and 1p/19q-codeleted oligodendrogliomas show intratumoural haemorrhages and/or areas of cystic degeneration.
MRI typically shows a T1-hypointense and T2-hyperintense mass with indistinct tumour margins in IDH-mutant and 1p/19q-codeleted oligodendrogliomas.
Signal intensities on T1-weighted and T2-weighted MRI are often heterogeneous in IDH-mutant and 1p/19q-codeleted oligodendrogliomas.
Gadolinium contrast enhancement can be detected in <20% of CNS WHO grade 2 oligodendrogliomas.
Gadolinium contrast enhancement is present in >70% of CNS WHO grade 3 oligodendrogliomas, where it is associated with microvascular proliferation and less favourable prognosis.
IDH-mutant and 1p/19q-codeleted oligodendrogliomas showed higher microvascularity (higher rCBV) and higher vascular heterogeneity than IDH-mutant diffuse astrocytomas of corresponding grade.

Magnetic resonance spectroscopy and radiomics can identify differences in certain features between 1p/19q-codeleted and 1p/19q-intact low-grade diffuse gliomas, but these techniques have limited sensitivity and specificity (~80% in validation series) and cannot yet replace molecular diagnostics.

Demonstration of elevated 2-hydroxyglutarate levels by magnetic resonance spectroscopy is a new means of non-invasively detecting IDH-mutant gliomas (including oligodendrogliomas), but it remains technically challenging.
PET imaging may allow the distinction between CNS WHO grade 2 and 3 IDH-mutant gliomas, but reported series tend to be small and unvalidated.
The presence of contrast enhancement on imaging is indicative of worse outcome in IDH-mutant CNS WHO grade 2 and 3 gliomas, including oligodendrogliomas.

An increased growth rate on follow-up MRI has been associated with histological features of anaplasia, including microvascular proliferation and higher mitotic count, with contrast enhancement on neuroimaging, and with shorter progression-free survival (PFS).
The reported incidence rate of histologically diagnosed oligodendrogliomas ranges from 0.10 in the Republic of Korea to 0.50 in France.
In the USA, the incidence rate of histologically diagnosed oligodendrogliomas is 0.23.
For histologically diagnosed CNS WHO grade 3 oligodendrogliomas, the incidence rate is 0.06 in the Republic of Korea.
For histologically diagnosed CNS WHO grade 3 oligodendrogliomas, the incidence rate is 0.11 in the USA.
For histologically diagnosed CNS WHO grade 3 oligodendrogliomas, the incidence rate is 0.39 in France.
0.9% of all brain tumours in the USA are CNS WHO grade 2 oligodendrogliomas.
0.4% of all brain tumours in the USA are CNS WHO grade 3 oligodendrogliomas.
Approximately one third of all oligodendroglial tumours correspond to CNS WHO grade 3.
A decrease in the incidence of oligodendrogliomas from 2000 to 2013 has been reported.
Oligodendrogliomas manifest preferentially in adults, with a median age at diagnosis of 43 years for CNS WHO grade 2 oligodendroglioma.
The median age at diagnosis for CNS WHO grade 3 oligodendroglioma is 50 years.
The median age for patients with IDH-mutant and 1p/19q-codeleted CNS WHO grade 2 oligodendrogliomas is 41 years.
The median age for patients with IDH-mutant and 1p/19q-codeleted CNS WHO grade 3 oligodendrogliomas is 47 years.
Histologically defined CNS WHO grade 3 oligodendroglioma shows a slight male predominance, with an M:F ratio of 1.2:1.
CNS WHO grade 3 oligodendroglioma is more common in White populations than in Black populations, with an incidence ratio of 2.3:1.
Oligodendrogliomas are rare in children.
In one study, 3 (14%) of 22 tumours with the typical morphological characteristics of oligodendroglioma demonstrated IDH1 p.R132H mutation and 1p/19q codeletion.
The 3 patients with IDH-mutant and 1p/19q-codeleted oligodendrogliomas were aged 16-19 years.
The etiology of IDH-mutant and 1p/19q-codeleted oligodendroglioma is unclear.
Most tumours develop sporadically, in the absence of documented familial clustering or a hereditary cancer predisposition syndrome.
Both familial and sporadic gliomas frequently display shared genomic landscapes, and common core pathways might be targeted by both germline and somatic alterations.
Earlier studies identified SNPs in the BICRA (GLTSCR1) and ERCC2 genes as well as the GSTT1 null genotype with increased risk of oligodendroglioma.
Germline mutations of POT1, a shelterin complex gene, have been associated with familial oligodendroglioma.
Cases of familial oligodendroglioma with 1p/19q codeletion have been reported.
Pathological production of 2-hydroxyglutarate, resulting from somatic mutations in IDH1 or IDH2, is found in all oligodendrogliomas and IDH-mutant astrocytomas.
Variants (particularly rs5839764) in or near the D2HGDH gene, which codes for D-2-hydroxyglutarate dehydrogenase, showed genome-wide association with IDH-mutant gliomas.
The same study identified rs111976262, located near the FAM20C gene, as showing genome-wide association with IDH-mutant, TERT promoter-mutant, and 1p/19q-codeleted oligodendrogliomas.

Gliomas have been reported in specific hereditary cancer syndromes including germline BRCA1 mutations, constitutional mismatch repair deficiency syndrome, Lynch syndrome (also known as hereditary non-polyposis colorectal cancer), and hereditary retinoblastoma, yet oligodendrogliomas are uncommon.

Patients with the enchondromatosis syndromes Ollier disease and Maffucci syndrome, which are associated with somatic (or postzygotic) IDH mosaicism, present with multiple benign cartilaginous tumours.

A retrospective cohort study showed that patients with enchondromatosis syndromes may develop gliomas with an anatomical presentation and a grading distribution similar to those of gliomas in non-syndromic patients, but they are typically younger and more often have multicentric lesions.
None of the gliomas in the enchondromatosis cohort harboured 1p/19q codeletion.
The potential role of viral infections in the etiology of IDH mutant and 1p/19q-codeleted oligodendroglioma has been debated.
Several studies have reported the detection of CMV in gliomas including oligodendrogliomas.
Other studies have concluded that CMV is not present in gliomas.
There have been contradictory findings reported for members of the polyomavirus family (BK virus, JC virus, SV40).
Whole-genome and RNA sequencing revealed only a low-percentage association between HPV and/or HBV and low-grade gliomas including oligodendrogliomas.
Previous findings of CMV in gliomas were probably a result of laboratory contamination.

Dysregulation of the immune system, including immunodeficiency due to HIV infection, posttransplant immunosuppression therapy, or demyelinating disease, has been associated with rare cases of oligodendroglioma.
Epidemiological data do not indicate an increased incidence of gliomas in patients with autoimmune disease.
Rat models have shown that nitrosoureas (e.g. ethylnitrosourea and methylnitrosourea) are chemical carcinogens that may induce CNS tumours, including gliomas with an oligodendroglial phenotype.
Cancer studies in humans are not available for nitrosoureas.
Oligodendroglioma typically appears macroscopically as a relatively well-defined, soft, greyish-pink mass located in the cortex and white matter.
Oligodendroglioma shows blurring of the grey matter-white matter boundary.
Local invasion into the overlying leptomeninges may be seen in oligodendroglioma.
Calcification is frequent in oligodendroglioma and may impart a gritty texture.
Densely calcified areas may occur as intratumoural stones in oligodendroglioma.
Zones of cystic degeneration are common in oligodendroglioma.
Intratumoural haemorrhages are common in oligodendroglioma.
Rare cases of oligodendroglioma with extensive mucoid degeneration look gelatinous.
Areas of necrosis may be discernible in CNS WHO grade 3 oligodendroglioma tumours.
The cell (or cells) of origin of IDH-mutant and 1p/19q-codeleted oligodendroglioma remains unknown.

Morphology and single-cell RNA-sequencing analysis of human tumours supports the notion that oligodendrogliomas are composed of a mixture of malignant cell types that recapitulate oligodendroglial and astrocytic lineages, as well as neural precursor-like cells.
Experimental transformation of immortalized human glial cells with IDH1 p.R132H reprogrammes their cellular lineage and favours the emergence of a neural precursor state.

Experiments in transgenic mice indicate that gliomas with oligodendroglial histology may originate from different cell types in the CNS, including neural precursor cells, astrocytes, and oligodendroglial precursor cells.
An oligodendroglioma-like phenotype is commonly found in transgenic brain tumours, despite such tumours showing a variety of targeted cell types and oncogenic events.
Studies have suggested that oligodendrogliomas probably originate from oligodendroglial precursor cells.

Oligodendroglial precursor cells have also been suggested as the cell of origin in other classes of gliomas and may give rise to either oligodendroglial or astrocytic phenotypes in gliomas, depending on the genes driving transformation.
IDH-mutant and 1p/19q-codeleted oligodendrogliomas show concurrent hypermethylation of multiple CpG islands, corresponding to the glioma CpG island methylator phenotype (G-CIMP).

IDH mutation causes increased levels of 2-hydroxyglutarate, which functions as a competitive inhibitor of a-ketoglutarate-dependent dioxygenases, including histone demethylases and the TET family of 5-methylcytosine hydroxylases.
Increased levels of 2-hydroxyglutarate lead to increased histone methylation and G-CIMP.
DNA methylation profiles of IDH-mutant and 1p/19q-codeleted oligodendrogliomas differ from those of IDH-mutant but 1p/19q-intact astrocytomas, and they can be used for diagnostic purposes.

G-CIMP may correlate with epigenetic silencing of multiple genes in oligodendrogliomas, including genes on 1p and 19q, as well as genes on other chromosomes, such as the tumour suppressors CDKN2A, CDKN2B, and RB1.
MGMT promoter methylation is detectable in the majority of oligodendrogliomas.
At the mRNA level, IDH-mutant and 1p/19q-codeleted oligodendrogliomas typically show a proneural glioblastoma-like gene-expression signature.
Mitotic activity is low or absent in CNS WHO grade 2 oligodendrogliomas, but it is usually prominent in CNS WHO grade 3 tumours.

The Ki-67 (MIB1) proliferation index is usually low (< 5%) in CNS WHO grade 2 oligodendrogliomas and elevated in CNS WHO grade 3 oligodendrogliomas, being generally > 10% in the large French national POLA cohort of CNS WHO grade 3 tumours.
A definitive Ki-67 (MIB1) cut-off value has not been established due to marked variability in staining results between institutions and non-uniform counting approaches.
One study reported a Ki-67 index of > 15% as an independent marker of shorter survival in patients with IDH-mutant and 1p/19q-codeleted CNS WHO grade 3 oligodendrogliomas.
A mitotic count of > 5 mitoses/mm² was associated with shorter survival but only on univariate, not multivariate, analysis.
Another study showed that mitotic count was not associated with outcome in patients with IDH-mutant and 1p/19q-codeleted oligodendrogliomas.
Other proliferation markers, such as PCNA, TOP2A, MCM2, and MCM6, also correlate with CNS WHO grade and/or survival but do not provide clear advantages over Ki-67 (MIB1).

A study of 220 patients with IDH-mutant and 1p/19q-codeleted CNS WHO grade 3 oligodendroglioma revealed that labelling index values of > 50% for MCM6 and > 15% for Ki-67 correlated with shorter OS in univariate and multivariate analyses.
The MCM6 and Ki-67 indices also correlated with OS in 30 patients with CNS WHO grade 2 oligodendrogliomas.

High mitotic count (> 2.5 mitoses/mm², equating to > 6 mitoses/10 HPF of 0.55 mm in diameter and 0.24 mm² in area) was associated with an increased growth rate on follow-up MRI and shorter PFS in patients with CNS WHO grade 2 and 3 oligodendrogliomas.
Most oligodendrogliomas demonstrate immunoreactivity with the antibody against IDH1 p.R132H.
IDH-mutant and 1p/19q codeleted oligodendrogliomas retain nuclear expression of ATRX.
IDH-mutant and 1p/19q codeleted oligodendrogliomas typically lack widespread nuclear p53 staining.
Oligodendrogliomas are immunopositive for MAP2, S100, and CD57 (LEU7).
The oligodendrocyte lineage transcription factors OLIG1, OLIG2, and SOX10 are expressed in oligodendrogliomas.
GFAP is detectable in intermingled reactive astrocytes but may also stain neoplastic cells such as minigemistocytes and gliofibrillary oligodendrocytes.

Antigens expressed by normal oligodendrocytes, including myelin basic protein (MBP), myelin proteolipid protein (PLP), myelin-associated glycoprotein (MAG), galactolipids, certain gangliosides, and several enzymes are not diagnostically useful markers for oligodendrogliomas.
Synaptophysin immunoreactivity of residual neuropil between the tumour cells is frequent and should not be mistaken for neuronal or neurocytic differentiation.
Oligodendrogliomas may also contain neoplastic cells that express synaptophysin and/or NeuN and neurofilaments.
Immunostaining for a-internexin protein is frequent in oligodendrogliomas.
In one study, a-internexin protein was found in 88.5% of IDH-mutant and 1p/19q codeleted CNS WHO grade 3 oligodendrogliomas.
A-internexin protein cannot be considered a surrogate marker for 1p/19q codeletion.
NOGO-A positivity is common in oligodendrogliomas but not exclusive.
Reduced nuclear expression of H3 p.K28me3 (K27me3) has been associated with 1p/19q codeletion in IDH-mutant gliomas.
Reduced nuclear expression of H3 p.K28me3 (K27me3) cannot substitute for 1p/19q testing.

Classic oligodendroglioma cells have uniformly round nuclei that are slightly larger than those of normal oligodendrocytes and show an increase in chromatin density or a delicate salt-and-pepper pattern.
A distinct nuclear membrane is often apparent in classic oligodendroglioma cells.
In formalin-fixed, paraffin-embedded tissue, tumour cells often appear as rounded cells with well-defined cell membranes and clear cytoplasm around the central spherical nucleus.
The typical honeycomb or fried-egg appearance of oligodendroglioma cells is artefactual but is a helpful diagnostic feature.

The honeycomb or fried-egg artefact is not seen in smear preparations or frozen sections and may also be absent in rapidly fixed tissue and in formalin-fixed, paraffin-embedded sections made from frozen material.
Reactive astrocytes are scattered throughout oligodendrogliomas and are particularly prominent at the tumour borders.

Oligodendrogliomas may contain tumour cells that look like small gemistocytes with a rounded belly of eccentric cytoplasm that is positive for GFAP, which are termed 'minigemistocytes' or 'microgemistocytes'.
Gliofibrillary oligodendrocytes are typical-looking oligodendroglioma cells with a thin perinuclear rim of positivity for GFAP.
Gliofibrillary oligodendrocytes and minigemistocytes are more commonly seen in CNS WHO grade 3 tumours.
GFAP-negative mucocytes or even signet-ring cells are occasionally present in oligodendrogliomas, with individual cases reported to consist largely of signet-ring cells.
Eosinophilic granular cells occur in some oligodendrogliomas.
Rare cases of oligodendrogliomas with neurocytic or ganglioglioma-like differentiation have been reported.
Occasional CNS WHO grade 3 oligodendrogliomas feature multinucleated giant cells.
Rare cases of oligodendrogliomas contain sarcomatous areas.
The presence of various cellular phenotypes does not preclude an oligodendroglioma diagnosis if the tumour is IDH-mutant and 1p/19q-codeleted.
Tumour cells with fibrillary or gemistocytic astrocytic morphology are compatible with an oligodendroglioma diagnosis when IDH mutation and 1p/19q codeletion are present.

Detection of combined IDH mutation and 1p/19q codeletion indicates an IDH-mutant and 1p/19q-codeleted oligodendroglioma, irrespective of oligodendroglial, oligoastrocytic, astrocytic, or ambiguous features on histology.
Microcalcifications are frequent in oligodendrogliomas and are found within the tumour itself or in the invaded brain.
Calcifications were recorded in 71 (45%) of 157 CNS WHO grade 3 IDH-mutant and 1p/19q-codeleted oligodendrogliomas.
Mineralization along blood vessels in oligodendrogliomas typically takes the form of small, punctate calcifications.
Microcalcifications in the brain, called calcospherites, tend to be larger with an irregular and sometimes laminated appearance.
Microcalcifications are not specific for oligodendroglioma and may not be found histologically even when demonstrated on CT due to incomplete tumour sampling.
Areas characterized by extracellular mucin deposition and/or microcyst formation are frequent in oligodendrogliomas.
Rare oligodendrogliomas are characterized by marked desmoplasia.

Histological features linked to worse prognosis in oligodendrogliomas include necrosis, high mitotic activity, increased cellularity, nuclear atypia, cellular pleomorphism, and microvascular proliferation.

The prognostic significance of histological features such as necrosis, high mitotic activity, increased cellularity, nuclear atypia, cellular pleomorphism, and microvascular proliferation requires reevaluation in patients with molecularly characterized tumours.

In IDH-mutant and 1p/19q-codeleted CNS WHO grade 3 oligodendrogliomas, high mitotic count (>2.5 mitoses/mm², equating to >6 mitoses/10 HPF of 0.55 mm in diameter and 0.24 mm² in area) was linked to shorter PFS and OS in both univariate and multivariate analyses.
The presence of microvascular proliferation and/or necrosis was of prognostic significance in cases lacking CDKN2A homozygous deletion.
The entity-defining alterations in oligodendrogliomas are missense mutations affecting IDH1 codon 132 or IDH2 codon 172 combined with whole-arm deletions of 1p and 19q.
More than 90% of IDH mutations in oligodendrogliomas correspond to the canonical IDH1 p.R132H mutation.
The 1p/19q codeletion has been cytogenetically linked to an unbalanced translocation between chromosomes 1 and 19 that results in loss of the der(1;19)(p10;q10) chromosome.
Incomplete/partial deletions on either chromosome arm are not compatible with the diagnosis of IDH-mutant and 1p/19q-codeleted oligodendroglioma.
The vast majority of IDH-mutant and 1p/19q-codeleted oligodendrogliomas carry TERT promoter hotspot mutations.
IDH-mutant and 1p/19q-codeleted oligodendrogliomas arising in teenagers often lack TERT promoter mutation.
TERT promoter mutation is assumed to be an early (i.e. clonal) event in oligodendroglioma development.
TERT promoter mutations generate de novo ETS transcription factor binding sites, resulting in transcriptional upregulation of TERT expression.
Mutations of CIC, located in chromosome band 19q13.2, are frequent in IDH-mutant and 1p/19q-codeleted oligodendrogliomas.
CIC is a constitutive transcriptional repressor of genes essential in development, cellular growth, and metabolism.
CIC mutations in oligodendrogliomas are hemizygous and include almost equal proportions of nonsense or truncating mutations and recurrent missense mutations.
Approximately 20-30% of IDH-mutant and 1p/19q-codeleted oligodendrogliomas harbour somatic mutations of FUBP1.
FUBP1 is a transcriptional regulator essential for normal stem cell self-renewal.
Approximately 15% of oligodendrogliomas carry mutations in NOTCH1.
NOTCH1 mutation was linked to shorter survival in one study.
Deletions on 9p involving the CDKN2A and/or CDKN2B locus have been associated with CNS WHO grade 3.
Homozygous CDKN2A deletion was indicative of short survival in a prospective cohort study of patients with CNS WHO grade 3 IDH mutant and 1p/19q-codeleted oligodendroglioma.
Oligodendrogliomas are molecularly defined by IDH1 or IDH2 mutations and 1p/19q codeletion.
Diagnosis of oligodendrogliomas requires demonstration of IDH mutation by IDH1 p.R132H immunohistochemistry and/or sequencing of the IDH1 or IDH2 gene.
Detection of a TERT promoter mutation in an IDH-mutant glioma is not sufficient for an oligodendroglioma diagnosis.
IDH-mutant and 1p/19q-codeleted oligodendrogliomas may histologically mimic various other lesions.
Macrophage-rich lesions characteristic of demyelinating diseases or resulting from cerebral infarction are readily distinguished by immunostaining for macrophage markers and lack of IDH mutation.
The relative increase of oligodendrocytes sometimes seen in partial lobectomy specimens performed for intractable seizures also lacks IDH mutation.
IDH-mutant astrocytomas lack 1p/19q codeletion and show frequent nuclear p53 immunostaining and loss of nuclear ATRX.
Loss of nuclear ATRX is sufficient to diagnose an IDH-mutant astrocytoma without additional testing for 1p/19q codeletion.
TERT promoter mutations are common in IDH-mutant and 1p/19q-codeleted oligodendrogliomas.
Rare cases of IDH-mutant and 1p/19q-codeleted oligodendrogliomas have been reported to lack TERT promoter mutation.
Some IDH-mutant but 1p/19q-intact astrocytomas may carry TERT promoter mutations.
Neurocytoma, liponeurocytoma, and dysembryoplastic neuroepithelial tumour can be ruled out by their lack of IDH mutation.

Ependymomas containing clear cells differ from oligodendrogliomas by their perivascular pseudorosettes and dotlike or ring-shaped EMA immunoreactivity, as well as a lack of IDH mutation and frequent ZFTA (C11orf95) fusions.
Clear cell meningioma can be distinguished by EMA and desmoplakin positivity, IDH-wildtype status, and loss of nuclear SMARCE1.
Metastatic clear cell carcinomas differ from oligodendrogliomas by their sharp tumour borders, cytokeratin and EMA positivity, and lack of IDH mutation.
Pilocytic astrocytomas with oligodendroglial features are IDH-wildtype and carry MAPK pathway gene alterations, in particular FGFR1 alterations.
Rare cases of IDH-mutant and 1p/19q-codeleted oligodendrogliomas with KIAA1549::BRAF fusions have been reported.
In children, diffuse gliomas with MYB, MYBL1, FGFR1, or BRAF alterations may have histological features of oligodendroglioma or oligoastrocytoma but are biologically distinct tumours.

The differential diagnosis of diffuse leptomeningeal glioneuronal tumour is facilitated by the clinical presentation and the combination of MAPK pathway gene alteration (in particular KIAA1549::BRAF fusion) and 1p deletion (or 1p/19q codeletion) but absence of IDH mutation.

Malignant small cell astrocytic tumours, including IDH-wildtype glioblastomas and H3 G34-mutant diffuse hemispheric gliomas, must be separated from highly cellular oligodendrogliomas by their IDH-wildtype status and specific alterations, including frequent EGFR amplification and chromosome 10 loss.
Mutations leading to H3 p.G35 (G34) variants are specific alterations in malignant small cell astrocytic tumours.
Oligodendroglioma, IDH-mutant and 1p/19q-codeleted, is a diffusely infiltrating glioma.
Oligodendroglioma, IDH-mutant and 1p/19q-codeleted, is characterized by IDH1 codon 132 or IDH2 codon 172 missense mutation.
Oligodendroglioma, IDH-mutant and 1p/19q-codeleted, is characterized by combined whole-arm deletions of 1p and 19q.
A DNA methylome profile of oligodendroglioma, IDH-mutant and 1p/19q-codeleted, is desirable for diagnosis.
Retained nuclear expression of ATRX is desirable for diagnosing oligodendroglioma, IDH-mutant and 1p/19q-codeleted.
TERT promoter mutation is desirable for diagnosing oligodendroglioma, IDH-mutant and 1p/19q-codeleted.
IDH mutation analysis may not be required when DNA methylome profiling unequivocally assigns the tumour to the methylation class oligodendroglioma, IDH-mutant and 1p/19q-codeleted.
The optimal postoperative treatment of patients with IDH-mutant and 1p/19q-codeleted oligodendrogliomas of CNS WHO grade 2 is a matter of ongoing discussion.
After tumour resection, radiotherapy and chemotherapy are often deferred until tumour progression because therapy-associated neurotoxicity is a major concern.
Patients with symptomatic and progressive tumours, CNS WHO grade 3 tumours, or large residual tumours after surgery usually receive immediate further treatment with radiotherapy and/or chemotherapy.

The European Organisation for Research and Treatment of Cancer (EORTC) 22845 trial showed that adjuvant radiotherapy prolonged progression-free survival (PFS) but not overall survival (OS) in patients with progressive CNS WHO grade 2 gliomas.
Long-term follow-up data from randomized trials showed a major increase in overall survival (OS) after radiotherapy plus PCV chemotherapy in patients with CNS WHO grade 3 oligodendrogliomas.
Adjuvant chemotherapy with temozolomide or PCV may be a feasible therapeutic strategy for patients with progressive CNS WHO grade 2 oligodendroglioma.
Survival data for histologically diagnosed tumours in older studies and population-based registries are confounded by the inclusion of gliomas without IDH mutation and 1p/19q codeletion.
Retrospective molecular stratification of older series confirmed that only subsets (30-80%) of tumours corresponded to IDH-mutant and 1p/19q-codeleted oligodendrogliomas.
IDH-mutant and 1p/19q-codeleted oligodendrogliomas are associated with favourable response to therapy and median survival times of >10 years.

Patients with CNS WHO grade 3 IDH-mutant and 1p/19q-codeleted oligodendroglioma who participated in prospective clinical trials and were treated with a combination of radiotherapy and procarbazine, lomustine, and vincristine (PCV) chemotherapy showed a median survival of >14 years.
Oligodendrogliomas generally recur locally but may show leptomeningeal spread.
Malignant progression at recurrence is common, although it usually takes longer in oligodendroglioma than in IDH-mutant astrocytoma.
Oligodendrogliomas comprise a continuous spectrum of tumours ranging from well-differentiated, slow-growing neoplasms to frankly malignant tumours with rapid growth.
In prior editions of the WHO Classification of CNS tumours, two grades were distinguished: oligodendroglioma, CNS WHO grade 2, and oligodendroglioma, CNS WHO grade 3.
CNS WHO grade retained prognostic significance in patients with IDH-mutant and 1p/19q-codeleted oligodendrogliomas.

Histological features linked to higher grade are high cellularity, marked cytological atypia, brisk mitotic activity, pathological microvascular proliferation, and necrosis with or without palisading.
CNS WHO grade 3 oligodendrogliomas usually show several features such as high cellularity, marked cytological atypia, brisk mitotic activity, pathological microvascular proliferation, and necrosis.

Microvascular proliferation and brisk mitotic activity, defined as > 2.5 mitoses/mm² (equating to > 6 mitoses/10 HPF of 0.55 mm in diameter and 0.24 mm² in area) have been reported as indicators of short survival in a study of histologically defined oligodendrogliomas.

Other studies of 1p/19q-codeleted CNS WHO grade 3 oligodendrogliomas suggested that microvascular proliferation and microvascular proliferation with necrosis are linked to shorter survival than elevated mitotic activity of > 2.5 mitoses/mm² without microvascular proliferation and necrosis.
Data defining a clear cut-off point for a mitotic count that distinguishes CNS WHO grade 2 from CNS WHO grade 3 of IDH-mutant and 1p/19q-codeleted oligodendrogliomas are not available.
Detection of rare mitoses in a resection specimen is not sufficient for diagnosing CNS WHO grade 3 IDH-mutant and 1p/19q-codeleted oligodendroglioma.
In borderline cases, proliferation markers like Ki-67 (MIB1) and attention to clinical and neuroradiological features may provide helpful additional information.

Homozygous deletion involving the CDKN2A and/or CDKN2B locus is found in a small subset (< 10%) of CNS WHO grade 3 oligodendrogliomas but not in CNS WHO grade 2 oligodendrogliomas, and it has been linked to reduced survival, independent of microvascular proliferation with or without necrosis.
CDKN2A homozygous deletion may serve as a molecular marker of CNS WHO grade 3 in IDH-mutant and 1p/19q-codeleted oligodendrogliomas.
Greater extent of resection has been associated with longer overall survival (OS) and PFS in patients with CNS WHO grade 2 oligodendroglioma, but it did not prolong the time to malignant progression.
Studies using volumetric tumour assessment show that extensive resections are associated with improved outcomes.
Leaving some tumour tissue behind appears to have less impact on the survival of patients with oligodendroglioma than on that of patients with IDH-mutant astrocytoma.
Older studies reported CNS WHO grade as an independent predictor of survival for patients with oligodendroglial tumours.

In one study of patients with gliomas with concurrent IDH mutation and TERT promoter mutation, patients with grade 2 tumours had longer survival times than those with grade 3 tumours (median OS: 205.5 months vs 127.3 months, respectively).
A recent multicentre study observed a median OS of 188 months in patients with grade 2 oligodendrogliomas versus 119 months in patients with grade 3 tumours.
A study of 176 patients with IDH-mutant and 1p/19q-codeleted oligodendrogliomas (CNS WHO grades 2 and 3) revealed shorter OS for patients with CNS WHO grade 3 tumours.
A retrospective analysis of 212 patients with IDH-mutant and 1p/19q-codeleted oligodendrogliomas did not detect CNS WHO grade as a significant predictor of OS.
Data from a combined cohort from Japan and The Cancer Genome Atlas (TCGA) suggested that grading had a limited prognostic role.

comprehensiveness.criterion: Complete workflow coverage from presentation to follow-up
comprehensiveness.explanation: Assess the guideline's coverage of the entire clinical workflow, from initial presentation and imaging through pathology, molecular diagnostics, integrated diagnosis, management, and follow-up. This ensures that all stages of patient care are addressed, providing a comprehensive framework for clinicians managing oligodendroglioma, IDH-mutant and 1p/19q-codeleted.

Evaluate the guideline's clarity in defining the disease entity, including boundaries, grading, and accurate ICD-O/ICD-11 coding. This ensures that the guideline provides a clear and precise framework for diagnosis and classification, reducing ambiguity in clinical practice.

Examine how the guideline addresses cases where molecular testing is inconclusive or incomplete, providing criteria for NOS/NEC classifications. This is crucial for guiding clinicians in situations where definitive molecular diagnosis cannot be achieved.

Assess the guideline's coverage of the typical anatomic locations, dissemination patterns, and epidemiological data, including demographic stratification. This information is vital for understanding the disease's behavior and prevalence, aiding in accurate diagnosis and management.

Evaluate the guideline's description of clinical symptoms and imaging characteristics across modalities like CT, MRI, and PET. Comprehensive phenotype coverage aids in accurate diagnosis and differentiation from other CNS tumors.

Review the guideline's discussion of potential etiological factors, including genetic predispositions, germline risks, and model systems. Understanding the underlying mechanisms is essential for developing targeted therapies and preventive strategies.

Assess the guideline's coverage of molecular alterations, including entity-defining mutations, co-alterations, and progression-related events. This ensures that the guideline provides a robust framework for molecular diagnosis, critical for personalized treatment planning.

insight_depth.criterion: Mechanistic depth linking genetics and phenotype
insight_depth.explanation: Evaluate the guideline's integration of genetic and epigenetic insights with phenotypic characteristics, providing a coherent understanding of the disease's pathogenesis. This depth is crucial for developing targeted therapies and improving diagnostic accuracy.

insight_depth.criterion: Integration across imaging, histology, and molecular diagnostics
insight_depth.explanation: Assess the guideline's ability to integrate findings from imaging, histology, IHC, and molecular diagnostics into a cohesive diagnostic reasoning process. This integration is essential for accurate diagnosis and effective treatment planning.

insight_depth.criterion: Transparent diagnostic reasoning and decision points
insight_depth.explanation: Examine the guideline's clarity in outlining diagnostic decision points, branching logic, and common pitfalls. This transparency aids clinicians in navigating complex cases and resolving conflicts between diagnostic modalities.

insight_depth.criterion: Prognostic and predictive stratification depth
insight_depth.explanation: Evaluate the guideline's depth in stratifying patients based on prognostic and predictive factors, with biologically plausible rationale. This stratification is crucial for personalized treatment planning and improving patient outcomes.

insight_depth.criterion: Evidence synthesis maturity
insight_depth.explanation: Assess the guideline's ability to synthesize evidence, handle heterogeneity, acknowledge limitations, and convey strength-of-evidence. Mature synthesis ensures that recommendations are based on robust and reliable data.

insight_depth.criterion: Clinical impact reasoning
insight_depth.explanation: Review the guideline's explanation of how specific biomarkers or histologic features influence diagnosis, grade, management, surveillance, and trial eligibility. Clear clinical impact reasoning aids in translating diagnostic findings into actionable clinical decisions.

accuracy_standards.criterion: Correct and current WHO entity naming and grading
accuracy_standards.explanation: Ensure the guideline uses accurate and up-to-date WHO nomenclature and grading conventions, reflecting any relevant interim updates. This accuracy is crucial for maintaining consistency with global diagnostic standards.

accuracy_standards.criterion: Accurate ICD-O/ICD-11 coding usage
accuracy_standards.explanation: Evaluate the guideline's precision in applying ICD-O/ICD-11 codes and handling synonyms or deprecated terms. Accurate coding is essential for proper classification and billing in clinical practice.

accuracy_standards.criterion: Molecular terminology accuracy
accuracy_standards.explanation: Assess the guideline's correctness in molecular terminology, including variant naming, promoter methylation phrasing, and CNV conventions. Accurate terminology ensures clear communication and reduces diagnostic errors.

accuracy_standards.criterion: Internal consistency across document
accuracy_standards.explanation: Review the guideline for any contradictory thresholds, criteria, or marker interpretations. Internal consistency is vital for providing clear and reliable diagnostic guidance.

accuracy_standards.criterion: Diagnostic accuracy safeguards
accuracy_standards.explanation: Evaluate the guideline's discussion of assay limitations, false positives/negatives, pre-analytic variables, and sampling bias. Safeguards are essential for ensuring diagnostic reliability and minimizing errors.

accuracy_standards.criterion: Consistency with reporting standards
accuracy_standards.explanation: Ensure the guideline aligns with widely used reporting standards and best practices for integrated diagnosis narratives. Consistency with standards facilitates clear communication and enhances diagnostic reliability.

readability_utility.explanation: Assess the guideline's logical organization and progression of arguments and recommendations in a clinically sensible order. Clear flow aids clinicians in understanding and applying the guideline effectively.

Evaluate the guideline's use of concrete quantitative data to support key claims, such as sample sizes, effect sizes, and confidence intervals. Evidence-backed statements enhance credibility and facilitate informed decision-making.

Review the guideline's explicit guidance on diagnosis, reporting, testing strategy, management, and follow-up. Actionable recommendations are crucial for translating diagnostic findings into clinical practice.

Assess the guideline's use of tables, figures, algorithms, and decision trees that materially improve decision-making. High-yield presentation aids clinicians in navigating complex diagnostic and management scenarios.

Ensure the guideline provides clear definitions of terms, thresholds, and required reporting elements for integrated diagnosis. Clarity in definitions and reporting enhances diagnostic accuracy and communication.

Evaluate the guideline's consideration of resource-stratified pathways, turnaround time awareness, and practical constraints. Feasibility ensures the guideline's applicability across various clinical environments.

System.role_and_constraints: You are a medical guideline writer. Based on authoritative literature, write a chapter of an authoritative medical report. You need to follow the chapter format below.

content:
# Malignant peripheral nerve sheath tumour

# Definition

Malignant peripheral nerve sheath tumour (MPNST) is a malignant spindle cell tumour often arising from a peripheral nerve, from a preexisting benign nerve sheath tumour, or in a patient with neurofibromatosis type 1 (NF1), and often showing limited Schwannian differentiation. Molecular hallmarks are the combined genetic inactivation of NF1, CDKN2A and/or CDKN2B, and SUZ12 or EED genes, as well as complex genomic rearrangements.

# ICD-O coding / ICD-11 coding

9540/3 Malignant peripheral nerve sheath tumour

2A02.0Y & XH2XP8 Other specified gliomas of spinal cord, cranial nerves, or other parts of the central nervous system & Malignant peripheral nerve sheath tumour

# Related terminology

Not recommended: malignant schwannoma, neurofibrosarcoma, neurogenic sarcoma.

# Subtype(s)

Epithelioid malignant peripheral nerve sheath tumour; perineurial malignant peripheral nerve sheath tumour

# Localization

The most common locations are extremities, the trunk, and the head and neck area [1, 2].

# Clinical features

MPNST occurs most commonly in patients aged 20-50 years. MPNSTs in children are usually associated with NF1. The mean age of patients with NF1-associated MPNST is about a decade younger than that of patients with sporadic tumours. Patients with MPNSTs often present with enlarging masses that may cause pain or other neuropathic symptoms [3, 4]. PET-CT imaging is sensitive but not specific for the detection of MPNST in patients with NF1 [5].

# Imaging

None

# Spread

None

# Epidemiology

MPNSTs account for approximately $2 - 10\%$ of soft tissue sarcomas [6, 7], with epithelioid MPNST being particularly rare $(\sim 5\%$ of all cases). The estimated overall incidence is 1.46 cases per 1 million person-years; there is slightly greater risk in Black people and lower risk in Asian and Latino/a people than in White people; the M:F ratio is roughly 1.2:1 [8, 36].

About $50\%$ of all MPNSTs are associated with NF1. In this setting, they most commonly arise from deep-seated plexiform neurofibromas or large intraneural neurofibromas [3]. The lifetime risk for MPNST in patients with NF1 is $10\%$ [9, 10]. About $10\%$ of all MPNSTs are associated with previous irradiation [11]. Epithelioid MPNST is not associated with NF1, but it has been associated with malignant transformation of schwannoma and has histological and molecular features in common with epithelioid schwannoma [12, 13].

# Etiology

None

# Pathogenesis

The pathogenesis of MPNST is complex and incompletely understood. Most available data are for NF1-associated tumours. In that setting, MPNSTs commonly develop from plexiform neurofibromas or localized intraneural neurofibromas, in which a subpopulation of Schwann cells already carries a biallelic inactivation of NF1 [14]. An intermediate lesion is the atypical neurofibromatous neoplasm of uncertain biological potential (ANNUBP), which frequently harbours homozygous CDKN2A and/or CDKN2B deletions [15, 16]. MPNSTs are molecularly characterized by two additional hallmarks: inactivation of SUZ12 or EED (core components of PRC2) [17, 18, 19] as well as complex genomic rearrangements including numerous chromosomal deletions and oncogene amplifications [20, 21]. In addition, some MPNSTs harbour mutations in TP53 [22, 23, 24].

PRC2 mediates the deposition of H3 p.K28me3 $( \mathsf { K } 2 7 \mathsf { m e } 3 )$, an important repressive MAPK that plays a critical role in cellular differentiation and cellular identity [25, 26]. Inactivation of PRC2 leads to a complete global loss of H3 p.K28me3 (K27me3) in tumour cells, which can be demonstrated by immunohistochemistry. About $80 \%$ of conventional high-grade MPNSTs show loss of H3 p.K28me3 (K27me3) [27]. Conventional MPNSTs with loss of H3 p.K28me3 (K27me3) and those with retained H3 p.K28me3 (K27me3) form two distinct DNA methylation classes [21]. Those with loss of H3 p.K28me3 (K27me3) frequently display losses of 1p, 9p, 10q, 11, 17p, and segments of 17q including NF1 and SUZ12. The most frequent gains involve 7p, 8q, and larger parts of 17q. Amplified oncogenes include EGFR, PDGFRA, and MET. Tumours with retained H3 p.K28me3 (K27me3) are predominately paraspinal and show more frequent losses of 3q and gains of 5p [21].

The pathogenesis of epithelioid MPNST is distinct from that of conventional MPNST. Epithelioid MPNSTs are not associated with NF1 and do not harbour most of the genetic alterations present in conventional MPNST; they retain H3 p.K28me3 (K27me3) and are driven by genetic inactivation of SMARCB1 in the vast majority of cases [19, 28, 13]. Recurrent chromosomal alterations in epithelioid MPNST include loss of 22q, deletion of 9p including CDKN2A and/or CDKN2B, and gain of 2q [13].

The pathogenesis of perineurial MPNST is currently unknown.

# Macroscopic appearance

Most MPNSTs are $> 50 \ \mathsf { mm }$ in diameter at diagnosis. They have a tan-white, fleshy cut surface, often with areas of haemorrhage and necrosis. They may occur with fusiform enlargement of a peripheral nerve and may include a precursor neurofibroma.

# Histopathology

Conventional high-grade MPNST appears as a hypercellular spindle cell tumour, with tumour cells arranged in interlacing fascicles. The nuclei are wavy or buckled and considerably larger and more atypical than in neurofibroma. There is pale eosinophilic and often indistinct cytoplasm. The cell density often alternates between highly cellular and less cellular areas, creating a marbled appearance. The cellularity around vessels may be increased, and tumour cell herniation into blood vessels is a common feature. Mitotic activity is usually brisk [15]. Well demarcated areas of necrosis are frequently present. About $15\%$ of MPNSTs show evidence of divergent differentiation. There may be mesenchymal osseous, cartilaginous, or rhabdomyosarcomatous (skeletal muscle) heterologous elements, or epithelial elements such as mucinous glands or islands of squamous differentiation. Conventional MPNST with prominent rhabdomyosarcomatous differentiation has been termed “malignant triton tumour”. Additionally, MPNST may show a broader morphological range and occasionally resemble various mesenchymal tumours, including synovial sarcoma, solitary fibrous tumour, and undifferentiated pleomorphic sarcoma. MPNST may diffusely infiltrate peripheral nerve tissue as well as precursor neurofibromas. Therefore, it is not uncommon to find areas with different degrees of malignant progression in the same tumour mass (e.g. neurofibroma, ANNUBP, low-grade MPNST, high-grade MPNST), especially in NF1-associated tumours.

To improve reproducibility and association with clinical and molecular parameters, a consensus nomenclature for the spectrum of NF1-associated nerve sheath tumours has been proposed. The extent of mitotic activity and the presence of necrosis is critical both for the distinction of ANNUBP from MPNST and for the grading of MPNST (low-grade or high-grade) [15].

ANNUBP, atypical neurofibromatous neoplasm of uncertain biological potential; MPNST, malignant peripheral nerve sheath tumour. $\mathsf { a } \mathsf { 1 \ mm } ^ { 2 } \sim 5$ HPF of $0 . 51 ~ \mathsf { mm }$ in diameter and $0 . 20 ~ \mathsf { mm } ^ { 2 }$ in area.

Immunohistochemically, the majority of MPNSTs are negative for Schwann cell markers like S100, SOX10, and GFAP. Importantly, if positivity is present, the staining is typically not diffuse but patchy or restricted to a subpopulation of cells, sometimes attributable to entrapped neural remnants. Only a minority of MPNSTs retain a Schwann cell phenotype. Surrogate molecular immunostains are often more helpful than lineage markers. As many as $90\%$ of conventional MPNSTs show loss of neurofibromin [29, 30, 21], although a commercial antibody is not currently available. Complete loss of H3 p.K28me3 (K27me3) is detectable in $50 \mathrm { - } 80\%$ of all MPNSTs, with the highest frequencies reported in high-grade and radiation-induced MPNSTs [27, 31]. A small subset of MPNSTs demonstrate ATRX loss and an alternative lengthening-of-telomeres phenotype [32, 33].

The tumour cells of epithelioid MPNST are predominantly epithelioid with abundant eosinophilic cytoplasm and nuclei with visible nucleoli. They often show a lobulated growth pattern and a fibrotic or myxoid matrix. Immunohistochemically, they are consistently strongly and diffusely positive for S100 and SOX10 but negative for melanocytic markers. Epithelioid MPNSTs retain H3 p.K28me3 (K27me3), but most show a loss of SMARCB1 expression [13].

Very rare malignant tumours with perineurial features have been described and termed “perineurial MPNST”. They are composed of spindle cells arranged in intersecting fascicles or whorls and exhibit frank histological signs of malignancy, such as frequent mitoses and necrosis. Immunohistochemically, tumour cells are S100-negative but express EMA and (variably) CD34 [34]. Perineurial MPNST is not associated with NF1.

# Immunophenotype

None

# Differential diagnosis

None

# Cytology

Not clinically relevant

# Diagnostic molecular pathology

DNA methylation-based classification may unequivocally define a tumour as MPNST, especially one with retained H3 p.K28me3 (K27me3). Other molecular data may provide supportive information for the diagnosis of MPNST, such as mutations in NF1, SUZ12, or EED, or the chromosomal copy-number profile. Chromosomal alterations are particularly helpful in borderline cases to distinguish between ANNUBP (CDKN2A and/or CDKN2B deletion and no or very few other alterations) and MPNST (complex genomic profile).

# Essential and desirable diagnostic criteria

Box 6.06 Diagnostic criteria for conventional malignant peripheral nerve sheath tumour (MPNST)

Essential:
Histopathologically consistent malignant spindle cell tumour in a patient with NF1 OR in a pre-existing neurofibroma OR Malignant spindle cell tumour associated with a peripheral nerve AND no more than focal/patchy S100/SOX10 expression AND no SS18::SSX (SSX1, SSX2, or SSX4) fusion gene present OR Malignant spindle cell tumour associated with a peripheral nerve AND evidence of PRC2 inactivation (molecularly or via loss of H3 p.K28me3 [K27me3] immunostaining) OR Tumour with features of ANNUBP in a patient with NF1, but with a mitotic count of at least 1.5–4.5 mitoses/mm² (3–9 mitoses/10 HPF of 0.51 mm in diameter and 0.20 mm² in area) OR Unresolved lesion with the methylation profile of MPNST

Desirable:
Loss of H3 p.K28me3 (K27me3)
Loss of neurofibromin expression

ANNUBP, atypical neurofibromatous neoplasm of uncertain biological potential; NF1, neurofibromatosis type 1.
aIn the setting of NF1, a diagnosis other than conventional MPNST demands strong molecular evidence (e.g. detection of a fusion gene pathognomonic for another tumour type).

# Grading / Staging

Not clinically relevant

# Prognosis and prediction

In a recent study, the 5-year overall survival rate of MPNST associated with NF1 was 35%, whereas it was 65% for sporadic MPNST. Deep location, positive surgical margins, and high-grade histology were additional factors associated with a poorer outcome [1]. The prognosis of radiation-induced MPNST is even worse, with a reported 5-year overall survival rate of 23.5% [35]. Epithelioid and perineurial MPNSTs seem to be less aggressive than conventional MPNST [28, 34].

# References
1. Le Guellec S, Decouvelaere AV, Filleron T, et al. Malignant peripheral nerve sheath tumor is a challenging diagnosis: a systematic pathology review, immunohistochemistry, and molecular analysis in 160 patients from the French Sarcoma Group database. Am J Surg Pathol. 2016 Jul;40(7):896-908. PMID:27158754
2. Ducatman BS, Scheithauer BW, Piepgras DG, et al. Malignant peripheral nerve sheath tumors. A clinicopathologic study of 120 cases. Cancer. 1986 May 15;57(10):2006-21. PMID:3082508
3. Ferner RE, Gutmann DH. International consensus Statement on malignant peripheral nerve sheath tumors in neurofibromatosis. Cancer Res. 2002 Mar 1;62(5):1573-7. PMID:11894862
4. Stewart DR, Korf BR, Nathanson KL, et al. Care of adults with neurofibromatosis type 1: a clinical practice resource of the American College of Medical Genetics and Genomics (ACMG). Genet Med. 2018 Jul;20(7):671-82. PMID:30006586
5. Brinkman M, Jentjens S, Boone K, et al. Evaluation of the most commonly used (semi)quantitative Parameters of 18F-FDG PET/CT. Nucl Med Commun. 2018 Nov;39(11):961-8. PMID:30106798
6. Brennan MF, Antonescu CR, Moraco N, et al. Lessons learned from the study of 10,000 patients with soft tissue sarcoma. Ann Surg. 2014 Sep;260(3):416-21. PMID:25115417
7. Widemann BC. Current status of sporadic and neurofibromatosis type 1-associated malignant peripheral nerve sheath tumors. Curr Oncol Rep. 2009 Jul;11(4):322-8. PMID:19508838
8. Bates JE, Peterson CR, Dhakal S, et al. Malignant peripheral nerve sheath tumors (MPNST): a SEER analysis of incidence across the age spectrum and therapeutic interventions in the pediatric population. Pediatr Blood Cancer. 2014 Nov;61(11):1955-60. PMID:25130403
9. Evans DG, Baser ME, McGaughran J, et al. Malignant peripheral nerve sheath tumours in neurofibromatosis 1. J Med Genet. 2002 May;39(5):311-5. PMID:12011145

claims.claim: The terms malignant schwannoma, neurofibrosarcoma, and neurogenic sarcoma are not recommended.
The ICD-O code for Malignant peripheral nerve sheath tumour is 9540/3.
The ICD-11 code for Malignant peripheral nerve sheath tumour is 2A02.0Y.
The ICD-11 code XH2XP8 refers to Other specified gliomas of spinal cord, cranial nerves, or other parts of the central nervous system and Malignant peripheral nerve sheath tumour.
Malignant peripheral nerve sheath tumour (MPNST) is a malignant spindle cell tumour.
MPNST often arises from a peripheral nerve.
MPNST can arise from a preexisting benign nerve sheath tumour.
MPNST can occur in a patient with neurofibromatosis type 1 (NF1).
MPNST often shows limited Schwannian differentiation.
Molecular hallmarks of MPNST include the combined genetic inactivation of NF1, CDKN2A and/or CDKN2B, and SUZ12 or EED genes.
MPNST is characterized by complex genomic rearrangements.
Epithelioid malignant peripheral nerve sheath tumour is a subtype of malignant peripheral nerve sheath tumour.
Perineurial malignant peripheral nerve sheath tumour is a subtype of malignant peripheral nerve sheath tumour.
The most common locations are extremities.
The trunk is a common location.
The head and neck area are common locations.
MPNST occurs most commonly in patients aged 20-50 years.
MPNSTs in children are usually associated with NF1.
The mean age of patients with NF1-associated MPNST is about a decade younger than that of patients with sporadic tumours.
Patients with MPNSTs often present with enlarging masses that may cause pain or other neuropathic symptoms.
PET-CT imaging is sensitive but not specific for the detection of MPNST in patients with NF1.
Most MPNSTs are > 50 mm in diameter at diagnosis.
MPNSTs have a tan-white, fleshy cut surface, often with areas of haemorrhage and necrosis.
MPNSTs may occur with fusiform enlargement of a peripheral nerve.
MPNSTs may include a precursor neurofibroma.
MPNSTs account for approximately 2-10% of soft tissue sarcomas.
Epithelioid MPNST is particularly rare, accounting for approximately 5% of all MPNST cases.
The estimated overall incidence of MPNST is 1.46 cases per 1 million person-years.
There is slightly greater risk of MPNST in Black people and lower risk in Asian and Latino/a people than in White people.
The male-to-female ratio for MPNST is roughly 1.2:1.
About 50% of all MPNSTs are associated with NF1.
MPNSTs associated with NF1 most commonly arise from deep-seated plexiform neurofibromas or large intraneural neurofibromas.
The lifetime risk for MPNST in patients with NF1 is 10%.
About 10% of all MPNSTs are associated with previous irradiation.
Epithelioid MPNST is not associated with NF1.
Epithelioid MPNST has been associated with malignant transformation of schwannoma.
Epithelioid MPNST has histological and molecular features in common with epithelioid schwannoma.
The pathogenesis of MPNST is complex and incompletely understood.
Most available data on MPNST are for NF1-associated tumours.
MPNSTs commonly develop from plexiform neurofibromas or localized intraneural neurofibromas in NF1-associated tumours.
A subpopulation of Schwann cells in NF1-associated tumours carries a biallelic inactivation of NF1.

An intermediate lesion in NF1-associated tumours is the atypical neurofibromatous neoplasm of uncertain biological potential (ANNUBP), which frequently harbours homozygous CDKN2A and/or CDKN2B deletions.
MPNSTs are molecularly characterized by inactivation of SUZ12 or EED (core components of PRC2).
MPNSTs are characterized by complex genomic rearrangements including numerous chromosomal deletions and oncogene amplifications.
Some MPNSTs harbour mutations in TP53.
PRC2 mediates the deposition of H3 p.K28me3 (K27me3), an important repressive MAPK that plays a critical role in cellular differentiation and cellular identity.
Inactivation of PRC2 leads to a complete global loss of H3 p.K28me3 (K27me3) in tumour cells, which can be demonstrated by immunohistochemistry.
About 80% of conventional high-grade MPNSTs show loss of H3 p.K28me3 (K27me3).
Conventional MPNSTs with loss of H3 p.K28me3 (K27me3) and those with retained H3 p.K28me3 (K27me3) form two distinct DNA methylation classes.
Conventional MPNSTs with loss of H3 p.K28me3 (K27me3) frequently display losses of 1p, 9p, 10q, 11, 17p, and segments of 17q including NF1 and SUZ12.
The most frequent gains in conventional MPNSTs with loss of H3 p.K28me3 (K27me3) involve 7p, 8q, and larger parts of 17q.
Amplified oncogenes in conventional MPNSTs with loss of H3 p.K28me3 (K27me3) include EGFR, PDGFRA, and MET.
Tumours with retained H3 p.K28me3 (K27me3) are predominately paraspinal and show more frequent losses of 3q and gains of 5p.
The pathogenesis of epithelioid MPNST is distinct from that of conventional MPNST.
Epithelioid MPNSTs are not associated with NF1 and do not harbour most of the genetic alterations present in conventional MPNST.
Epithelioid MPNSTs retain H3 p.K28me3 (K27me3) and are driven by genetic inactivation of SMARCB1 in the vast majority of cases.
Recurrent chromosomal alterations in epithelioid MPNST include loss of 22q, deletion of 9p including CDKN2A and/or CDKN2B, and gain of 2q.
The pathogenesis of perineurial MPNST is currently unknown.
Conventional high-grade MPNST appears as a hypercellular spindle cell tumour, with tumour cells arranged in interlacing fascicles.
The nuclei in conventional high-grade MPNST are wavy or buckled and considerably larger and more atypical than in neurofibroma.
The cell density in conventional high-grade MPNST often alternates between highly cellular and less cellular areas, creating a marbled appearance.
Tumour cell herniation into blood vessels is a common feature of conventional high-grade MPNST.
Mitotic activity in conventional high-grade MPNST is usually brisk.
Well-demarcated areas of necrosis are frequently present in conventional high-grade MPNST.
About 15% of MPNSTs show evidence of divergent differentiation.
Conventional MPNST with prominent rhabdomyosarcomatous differentiation has been termed 'malignant triton tumour'.
MPNST may diffusely infiltrate peripheral nerve tissue as well as precursor neurofibromas.
It is not uncommon to find areas with different degrees of malignant progression in the same tumour mass, especially in NF1-associated tumours.
The extent of mitotic activity and the presence of necrosis is critical both for the distinction of ANNUBP from MPNST and for the grading of MPNST.
The majority of MPNSTs are negative for Schwann cell markers like S100, SOX10, and GFAP.
As many as 90% of conventional MPNSTs show loss of neurofibromin.
Complete loss of H3 p.K28me3 (K27me3) is detectable in 50-80% of all MPNSTs, with the highest frequencies reported in high-grade and radiation-induced MPNSTs.
A small subset of MPNSTs demonstrate ATRX loss and an alternative lengthening-of-telomeres phenotype.
The tumour cells of epithelioid MPNST are predominantly epithelioid with abundant eosinophilic cytoplasm and nuclei with visible nucleoli.
Epithelioid MPNSTs are consistently strongly and diffusely positive for S100 and SOX10 but negative for melanocytic markers.
Epithelioid MPNSTs retain H3 p.K28me3 (K27me3), but most show a loss of SMARCB1 expression.
Very rare malignant tumours with perineurial features have been described and termed 'perineurial MPNST'.
Perineurial MPNSTs are composed of spindle cells arranged in intersecting fascicles or whorls and exhibit frank histological signs of malignancy, such as frequent mitoses and necrosis.
Perineurial MPNST is not associated with NF1.
DNA methylation-based Classification may unequivocally define a tumour as MPNST, especially one with retained H3 p.K28me3 (K27me3).
Mutations in NF1, SUZ12, or EED may provide supportive information for the diagnosis of MPNST.
The chromosomal copy-number profile may provide supportive information for the diagnosis of MPNST.
Chromosomal alterations are particularly helpful in borderline cases to distinguish between ANNUBP and MPNST.
ANNUBP is characterized by CDKN2A and/or CDKN2B deletion and no or very few other alterations.
MPNST is characterized by a complex genomic profile.

A histopathologically consistent malignant spindle cell tumour in a patient with NF1 or in a pre-existing neurofibroma is an essential diagnostic criterion for conventional malignant peripheral nerve sheath tumour (MPNST).

A malignant spindle cell tumour associated with a peripheral nerve and no more than focal/patchy S100/SOX10 expression and no SS18::SSX (SSX1, SSX2, or SSX4) fusion gene present is an essential diagnostic criterion for conventional MPNST.

A malignant spindle cell tumour associated with a peripheral nerve and evidence of PRC2 inactivation, either molecularly or via loss of H3 p.K28me3 (K27me3) immunostaining, is an essential diagnostic criterion for conventional MPNST.

A tumour with features of atypical neurofibromatous neoplasm of uncertain biological potential (ANNUBP) in a patient with NF1, but with a mitotic count of at least 1.5–4.5 mitoses/mm² (3–9 mitoses/10 HPF of 0.51 mm in diameter and 0.20 mm² in area), is an essential diagnostic criterion for conventional MPNST.
An unresolved lesion with the methylation profile of MPNST is an essential diagnostic criterion for conventional MPNST.
Loss of H3 p.K28me3 (K27me3) is a desirable diagnostic criterion for conventional MPNST.
Loss of neurofibromin expression is a desirable diagnostic criterion for conventional MPNST.
In the setting of NF1, a diagnosis other than conventional MPNST demands strong molecular evidence, such as detection of a fusion gene pathognomonic for another tumour type.
In a recent study, the 5-year overall survival rate of MPNST associated with NF1 was $3 5 \%$.
The 5-year overall survival rate for sporadic MPNST was $6 5 \%$.
Deep location, positive surgical margins, and high-grade histology were additional factors associated with a poorer outcome.
The prognosis of radiation-induced MPNST is even worse, with a reported 5-year overall survival rate of $2 3 . 5 \%$.
Epithelioid and perineurial MPNSTs seem to be less aggressive than conventional MPNST.
