claims.claim: 8070/3 Squamous cell carcinoma NOS
2B70.1 Squamous cell carcinoma of oesophagus
Oesophageal squamous cell carcinoma NOS is a malignant oesophageal epithelial neoplasm.
Oesophageal squamous cell carcinoma NOS displays squamous cell differentiation.
Squamous cell differentiation in oesophageal squamous cell carcinoma NOS is characterized by keratinocyte-type cells with intercellular bridges and/or keratinization.
Verrucous squamous cell carcinoma (8o51/3)
Squamous cell carcinoma, spindle cell (8o74/3)
Basaloid squamous cell carcinoma (8083/3)
Squamous cell carcinoma is located most commonly in the middle third of the oesophagus.
Squamous cell carcinoma is located in the lower third of the oesophagus.
In a large Japanese series, slightly more than half (55%) of squamous cell carcinomas occurred in the middle oesophagus.
In a large Japanese series, 32% of squamous cell carcinomas occurred in the lower oesophagus.
In a large Japanese series, 14% of squamous cell carcinomas occurred in the upper oesophagus.
In a series from Hong Kong SAR, China, about half (49%) of the tumours were located in the middle oesophagus.
In a series from Hong Kong SAR, China, 41% of the tumours were located in the lower oesophagus.
In a series from Hong Kong SAR, China, 10% of the tumours were located in the upper oesophagus.
The most common clinical symptom is dysphagia, which usually indicates advanced disease.
Chest pain, painful swallowing, and weight loss can also be presenting symptoms.
Upper oesophageal tumours may be associated with hoarseness due to recurrent laryngeal nerve involvement.
Early-stage disease is generally asymptomatic and discovered incidentally.
Any persistent ulceration or refractory stricture in the oesophagus should arouse concern for malignancy.
The gold standard for diagnosis is upper GI tract endoscopy and biopsy for histological analysis, with or without brushings for cytological examination.

Image-enhanced endoscopy (e.g. narrow-band imaging) and chromoendoscopy may help identify early-stage lesions by highlighting an abnormal vascular pattern or iodine negativity associated with neoplasia.
Evolving techniques such as confocal endomicroscopy may facilitate better targeting of biopsies.
EUS is useful for evaluating the depth of invasion into the oesophageal wall and may reveal regional lymph node metastasis.
EUS is unreliable in diffusely infiltrating disease.
Endoscopic mucosal resection or endoscopic submucosal dissection is the best procedure for determining depth of invasion in superficial disease.
CT and FDG PET are useful for staging more-advanced disease and response after chemoradiation.
The etiology of oesophageal squamous cell carcinoma is multifactorial.
The etiology of oesophageal squamous cell carcinoma is heavily dependent on the population being studied.

Oesophageal squamous cell carcinoma develops by stepwise progression, with accumulating genetic abnormalities driving progression from histologically normal squamous mucosa to low-grade intraepithelial neoplasia (dysplasia) to high-grade intraepithelial neoplasia and finally to invasive squamous cell carcinoma.
TP53 mutation is a key early driver mutation.

Genetic changes identified at the intraepithelial neoplasia stage include aneuploidy, copy-number alterations, changes related to the amplification of genes such as EGFR, and the silencing of genes such as CDKN2A due to promoter hypermethylation.
The specific mutations required for the invasion beyond the basement membrane that is characteristic of invasive squamous cell carcinoma are still unknown.
Acquisition of invasive and migratory capability via epithelial-mesenchymal transition is important.
ElF5A2 amplification has been shown to be a factor in inducing the invasive and migratory phenotype.

Low socioeconomic status has been found to be associated with increased risk of oesophageal squamous cell carcinoma in multiple studies, even when confounding variables (e.g. tobacco use and alcohol consumption) were controlled for.
Tobacco use (smoking or chewing) remains the most important risk factor for oesophageal squamous cell carcinoma in high-income countries.
Risk of oesophageal squamous cell carcinoma is dependent on both intensity and duration of tobacco use.
Cessation of tobacco use is associated with decreasing risk of oesophageal squamous cell carcinoma after 5 years and a return to the baseline risk level of a lifelong non-smoker after 10-20 years.
Tobacco-specific nitrosamines and polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons are believed to be the main carcinogenic substances in tobacco.
Alcohol consumption has been causally linked to oesophageal squamous cell carcinoma.
Consumption of more than 3 drinks per day is associated with a risk of oesophageal squamous cell carcinoma almost 5 times that in individuals who consume less than 1 drink per day.
Acetaldehyde, a metabolite of ethanol, is a known carcinogen able to cause DNA abnormalities and structural and functional alterations to protein.
ALDH2 and ALDH1A1 mutations, which are found almost exclusively in eastern Asian populations, lead to the accumulation of acetaldehyde following the consumption of alcohol.
Heterozygous and homozygous ALDH2 mutations are associated with risks of oesophageal squamous cell carcinoma 4 times and 13 times that in individuals without ALDH2 mutation, respectively.

Drinking very hot beverages appears to be an important risk factor for oesophageal squamous cell carcinoma in Uruguay, southern Brazil, northern Argentina, the United Republic of Tanzania, and the Islamic Republic of Iran.
Diets high in fruits and vegetables are associated with a reduction of risk of oesophageal squamous cell carcinoma (a 10-25% reduction in patients who smoke).
Pickled vegetables are associated with increased risk of oesophageal squamous cell carcinoma, possibly explained by the generation of carcinogenic mycotoxins and presence of nitrosamine compounds.

There is limited evidence that some micronutrients may affect risk of oesophageal squamous cell carcinoma; the best evidence suggests a protective effect of selenium and riboflavin in high-risk populations.
Increased body mass index is associated with lower risk of oesophageal squamous cell carcinoma, but the mechanism underlying this association is unknown.

Several genetic diseases are associated with increased risk of oesophageal squamous cell carcinoma; the best characterized are tylosis, a keratosis disorder caused by an autosomal dominant mutation in RHBDF2 at 17q25.3, and Fanconi anaemia.
HPV DNA has been identified in oesophageal squamous cell carcinoma cells.
In most cases, HPV appears to be an innocent bystander in oesophageal squamous cell carcinoma; viral integration and transcription is uncommon.
The current consensus is that HPV infection is unlikely to be a substantial risk factor for the development of oesophageal squamous cell carcinoma.
The most important genetic abnormality in oesophageal squamous cell carcinoma appears to be TP53 mutation, which is found in 59-93% of all cases.
Mutations in cell-cycle regulatory genes such as CDKN2A (P16), RB1, NFE2L2, CHEK1, and CHEK2 have been identified in oesophageal squamous cell carcinoma.
Amplifications of cell-cycle regulatory genes CCND1, CDK4, CDK6, and MDM2 have been identified in oesophageal squamous cell carcinoma.
NOTCH1 and NOTCH3 mutations are detected in as many as 28% of oesophageal squamous cell carcinoma cases.
EGFR overexpression, reported in 59.6-76% of oesophageal squamous cell carcinoma cases, is an adverse prognostic factor.
Mutations and/or amplifications in RAS and AKT family oncogenes are seen in at least 75% of oesophageal squamous cell carcinoma cases.
DNA methylation, chromatin modification, RNA editing, and loss of genomic imprinting are relatively common in oesophageal squamous cell carcinoma.
Promoter hypermethylation-related silencing of CDKN2A leads to overexpression of p53 in oesophageal squamous cell carcinoma.
Tylosis, an autosomal dominant disorder caused by a missense mutation in RHBDF2, results in overactivation of the EGFR signalling pathway.
Fanconi anaemia, an autosomal recessive disorder, affects the Fanconi anaemia pathway, which is normally active in DNA repair.
Chromosome aneuploidy, in particular gains in chromosomes 3, 10, 12, and 20, is common in oesophageal squamous cell carcinoma.
Copy-number alterations result from mutations and amplifications of genes in oesophageal squamous cell carcinoma.
SNPs, including TP53 polymorphisms, have been detected in patients with oesophageal squamous cell carcinoma.
A common somatic mutation signature in oesophageal squamous cell carcinoma is G:C—>A:T transitions, which is a typical mutation signature induced by acetaldehyde.
Oesophageal squamous cell carcinoma is the sixth leading cause of cancer-related death worldwide.
There is marked geographical variation in the incidence of oesophageal squamous cell carcinoma among regions and even within individual countries.
The highest incidence rates of oesophageal squamous cell carcinoma (> 50 cases/100,000 person-years) are found in the Asian oesophageal cancer belt, which stretches from eastern to central Asia.

Intermediate incidence rates of oesophageal squamous cell carcinoma (10-50 cases/100,000 person-years) are observed along the Indian coast of Africa, in southern Brazil and Uruguay, and in parts of the Caribbean.
The incidence of oesophageal squamous cell carcinoma has been decreasing in North American and European countries over the past few decades.
The incidence of oesophageal adenocarcinoma has been increasing in North American and European countries.
Even within high-incidence countries, there is marked variation in the incidence of oesophageal squamous cell carcinoma.
The incidence rate of oesophageal squamous cell carcinoma is much lower in the southern provinces of China than in the north-east.
Oesophageal squamous cell carcinoma is more common in males, who account for 69% of cases.
In North American and European countries, males are at least 4 times as likely as females to develop oesophageal squamous cell carcinoma.
In high-incidence countries such as China and the Islamic Republic of Iran, the male predominance in oesophageal squamous cell carcinoma is less pronounced.
The median patient age at diagnosis of oesophageal squamous cell carcinoma is in the seventh decade of life.
Most patients with oesophageal squamous cell carcinoma present in their fifth to eighth decade of life.
Multiple squamous cell carcinomas can occur in the oesophagus and other parts of the aerodigestive tract, such as the oral cavity and oropharynx.

The occurrence of multiple squamous cell carcinomas in the oesophagus and other parts of the aerodigestive tract is related to the common exposure of these regions to risk factors such as tobacco smoke and to the rich lymphatic networks in the oesophagus.
Squamous cell carcinoma often presents at an advanced pathological stage with an ulcerative mass.
The most useful macroscopic classification has been provided by the Japan Esophageal Society.
Squamous cell carcinoma has both vertical and horizontal growth of neoplastic squamous epithelium beyond the basement membrane.
Grading of squamous cell carcinoma is based on the degree of cytological atypia, mitotic activity, and presence of keratinization.
A three-tiered system (grades 1, 2, 3) is commonly applied for grading squamous cell carcinoma.
A two-tiered system (grade 1-2 vs grade 3) may be clinically relevant for grading squamous cell carcinoma.
The pathological distinction between grade 1 and grade 2 squamous cell carcinoma often shows high interobserver variation.
Grade 1 (well-differentiated) squamous cell carcinoma contains enlarged cells with abundant eosinophilic cytoplasm and keratin pearl production.
In Grade 1 squamous cell carcinoma, cytological atypia is minimal and the mitotic rate is low.
In Grade 1 squamous cell carcinoma, the invasive margin is pushing and the cells remain well ordered.
Grade 2 (moderately differentiated) squamous cell carcinoma has evident cytological atypia and the cells are less ordered.
In Grade 2 squamous cell carcinoma, mitotic figures are easily identified.
In Grade 2 squamous cell carcinoma, there is usually surface parakeratosis, but keratin pearl formation is infrequent.
Grade 3 (poorly differentiated) squamous cell carcinoma consists predominantly of basal-like cells forming nests, which may show central necrosis.
In Grade 3 squamous cell carcinoma, the tumour nests consist of sheets or pavement-like arrangements of tumour cells with occasional parakeratotic or keratinizing cells.
Most patients with advanced oesophageal squamous cell carcinoma are treated with combined preoperative chemotherapy and radiotherapy.
Combined preoperative chemotherapy and radiotherapy usually induces progressive changes in both the tumour cells and the peritumoural stroma, with macroscopic tumour regression.
Cellular changes include nuclear enlargement or shrinkage, nuclear vacuolation, apoptosis, and necrosis.
Keratin released from the dying cells may accumulate, undergo dystrophic calcification, and elicit a surrounding giant cell reaction.
A neutrophilic or chronic inflammatory cell response may be seen.
There is fibrosis and sometimes stromal elastosis.
Regional vessels typically show arteriosclerosis.
The extent of tumour regression is an important prognostic factor.
Tumour regression is graded on histological examination by comparing the amount of residual tumour with the amount of therapy-induced fibrosis.
The most widely used method of assessing tumour regression grade (TRG) is the Mandard system.
Another system relies on the estimated percentage reduction in tumour volume, with < 10% residual tumour constituting a good prognostic finding.
Pathological complete response (i.e. complete or nearly complete tumour eradication) is the primary goal of preoperative therapy.
In small biopsy specimens, it may be difficult to differentiate conventional squamous cell carcinoma from other subtypes of squamous cell carcinoma.
In small biopsy specimens, it may be difficult to differentiate conventional squamous cell carcinoma from undifferentiated carcinoma.
In small biopsy specimens, it may be difficult to differentiate conventional squamous cell carcinoma from adenocarcinoma.
At present, no molecular tests are required.
Cytological sampling using directed mucosal brushings is cost-effective for screening in high-risk populations.
Blind sampling using techniques such as the Cytosponge procedure is cost-effective for screening in high-risk populations.
Cytological sampling using directed mucosal brushings facilitates primary diagnosis of early squamous neoplasia.
Blind sampling using techniques such as the Cytosponge procedure facilitates primary diagnosis of early squamous neoplasia.
Histological evidence of vertical and horizontal growth of neoplastic squamous epithelium is essential.
Definite evidence of invasion is essential.
Verrucous squamous cell carcinoma is a subtype often arising in the setting of chronic irritation, oesophagitis, or previous oesophageal injury.
Most cases of verrucous squamous cell carcinoma are identified in the lower third of the oesophagus as a protuberant mass.
Association with HPV51 and HPV11 has been demonstrated in some cases of verrucous squamous cell carcinoma.
The tumour comprises exceedingly differentiated squamous cells with minimal cytological atypia, minimal mitotic activity, and surface papillary projections.
Keratinization occurs abruptly in verrucous squamous cell carcinoma, with no intervening granular cell layer, and cup-like collections of keratin are present at the base of the papillary structures.
The invasive front of verrucous squamous cell carcinoma is characterized by broad bulbous pushing projections.
Verrucous squamous cell carcinoma is slow-growing, and metastases are uncommon.
Spindle cell squamous cell carcinoma typically has a polypoid growth pattern.
Microscopically, spindle cell squamous cell carcinoma has a biphasic pattern of neoplastic squamous epithelium and spindle cells.
The spindle cell component of spindle cell squamous cell carcinoma is a high-grade malignancy, which may show osseous, cartilaginous, or skeletal muscle differentiation.

Although spindle cell squamous cell carcinoma tumours tend to be large, the prognosis is sometimes better than that of conventional squamous cell carcinoma of the same size, primarily because of the intraluminal rather than deeply invasive growth.
Basaloid squamous cell carcinoma shows a marked male predominance and accounts for approximately 5% of all oesophageal cancers.
Unlike similar tumours in the oropharynx, oesophageal basaloid squamous cell carcinoma has no association with HPV infection.
Basaloid squamous cell carcinoma tumours show a solid or nested growth pattern of basaloid cells, sometimes with central comedonecrosis and occasionally with pseudoglandular or cribriform formations.
The tumour morphology of basaloid squamous cell carcinoma has been confused with rare primary adenoid cystic carcinoma.
Exclusion of high-grade neuroendocrine carcinoma (NEC) by immunohistochemistry is often required for basaloid squamous cell carcinoma.
Areas of squamous cell carcinoma in situ or conventional invasive squamous cell carcinoma are relatively common in basaloid squamous cell carcinoma.
Basaloid squamous cell carcinoma is highly aggressive, with a worse prognosis than conventional squamous cell carcinoma although the difference is often not statistically significant.
Oesophageal squamous cell carcinoma should be staged using the eighth editions (2017) of the Union for International Cancer Control (UICC) TNM classification.
Oesophageal squamous cell carcinoma should be staged using the eighth editions (2017) of the American Joint Committee on Cancer (AJCC) cancer staging manual.
Depth of invasion provides a clinically relevant division of oesophageal squamous cell carcinoma into superficial (or early) disease versus advanced (or late) disease.
Superficial disease is invasion restricted to the mucosa and submucosa.
Superficial disease has a low risk of regional lymph node metastasis and is curable by local resection if submucosal invasion is < 200 μm deep.
Advanced disease is invasion beyond the muscularis propria, with a high risk of regional or systemic metastasis.
Superficial disease, which accounts for as many as 35% of all cases in Japan, is associated with a 5-year survival rate of approximately 85%.
The 5-year survival rate with advanced disease is < 10-15%.
The most important prognostic factor is staging, which is used to determine the most appropriate treatment regimen for a given patient.
Despite the frequent overexpression of EGFR (HER1) in squamous cell carcinoma, targeted therapy against this protein has failed to improve survival.
There is a potentially promising role for immune checkpoint inhibitors as novel therapies in advanced disease.

comprehensiveness.criterion: Complete workflow from presentation to follow-up
comprehensiveness.explanation: Assess the guideline's coverage of the entire clinical workflow, from initial presentation and imaging to pathology, molecular diagnostics, integrated diagnosis, management, and follow-up. This ensures comprehensive guidance for clinicians managing oesophageal squamous cell carcinoma NOS.

Evaluate the guideline's clarity in defining oesophageal squamous cell carcinoma NOS, including ICD-O and ICD-11 coding. Accurate coding is essential for proper classification and epidemiological tracking.
Review the guideline's approach to subtypes and NOS/NEC-style uncertainties, ensuring it provides minimum and ideal diagnostic sets for accurate classification and management.

Check the guideline's coverage of anatomical distribution, dissemination patterns, and epidemiological data, including demographic stratification, to inform risk assessment and targeted interventions.
Assess the guideline's explanation of etiology and mechanisms, including germline/syndromic risks and genetic factors, to provide a comprehensive understanding of disease causation.
Evaluate the guideline's coverage of macroscopic and microscopic pathology, immunophenotype, and cytology, ensuring these elements are well-integrated into the diagnostic process.
Review the guideline's coverage of molecular pathology, including entity-defining alterations, co-alterations, and progression-related events, ensuring comprehensive molecular diagnostic guidance.

insight_depth.criterion: Mechanistic depth linking genetics and phenotype
insight_depth.explanation: Evaluate the guideline's depth in linking genetic and epigenetic factors to the clinical phenotype, providing a coherent understanding of disease mechanisms and progression.

insight_depth.criterion: Integration across imaging, histology, and molecular diagnostics
insight_depth.explanation: Assess the guideline's ability to integrate findings from imaging, histology, IHC, and molecular diagnostics into a layered diagnostic reasoning process, enhancing clinical decision-making.

insight_depth.criterion: Transparent diagnostic reasoning and conflict resolution
insight_depth.explanation: Review the guideline's clarity in diagnostic reasoning, including decision points, branching logic, and strategies for resolving conflicts between modalities, ensuring robust diagnostic conclusions.

insight_depth.criterion: Prognostic and predictive stratification depth
insight_depth.explanation: Evaluate the guideline's depth in stratifying prognostic and predictive factors, providing biologically plausible rationale and clear risk grouping for personalized patient management.

insight_depth.criterion: Evidence synthesis maturity
insight_depth.explanation: Assess the guideline's maturity in synthesizing evidence, handling heterogeneity, acknowledging limitations, and conveying strength-of-evidence, ensuring reliable clinical recommendations.

insight_depth.criterion: Clinical impact reasoning for biomarkers and histologic features
insight_depth.explanation: Review the guideline's reasoning on how specific biomarkers or histologic features influence diagnosis, grading, management, surveillance, and trial eligibility, ensuring actionable insights.

insight_depth.criterion: Cell-of-origin hypotheses and diagnostic integration
insight_depth.explanation: Evaluate the guideline's exploration of cell-of-origin hypotheses and their integration into diagnostic reasoning, enhancing understanding of tumor biology and therapeutic targets.

accuracy_standards.criterion: Correct and current WHO entity naming and grading
accuracy_standards.explanation: Ensure the guideline uses accurate and current WHO entity naming and grading conventions, aligning with major interim updates for consistency in classification.

accuracy_standards.criterion: Accurate ICD-O/ICD-11 coding and synonym handling
accuracy_standards.explanation: Evaluate the guideline's use of ICD-O/ICD-11 coding, ensuring precision and clarity in handling synonyms and deprecated terms for accurate disease classification.

accuracy_standards.criterion: Molecular terminology accuracy and assay interpretation
accuracy_standards.explanation: Review the guideline's accuracy in molecular terminology, including variant/fusion naming and CNV conventions, ensuring correct assay interpretation and diagnostic reliability.

accuracy_standards.criterion: Internal consistency across thresholds and criteria
accuracy_standards.explanation: Assess the guideline for internal consistency, ensuring no contradictory thresholds, criteria, or marker interpretations, maintaining coherence throughout the document.

accuracy_standards.criterion: Diagnostic accuracy safeguards
accuracy_standards.explanation: Evaluate the guideline's safeguards for diagnostic accuracy, including assay limitations, false positives/negatives, pre-analytic variables, and therapy effects, ensuring reliable outcomes.

accuracy_standards.criterion: Consistency with reporting standards and best practices
accuracy_standards.explanation: Review the guideline's adherence to widely used reporting standards and best practices for integrated diagnosis narratives, ensuring clarity and precision in clinical documentation.

accuracy_standards.criterion: Terminology precision in histopathology and molecular diagnostics
accuracy_standards.explanation: Ensure the guideline uses precise terminology in histopathology and molecular diagnostics, avoiding ambiguity and enhancing diagnostic clarity.

readability_utility.explanation: Assess the guideline's logical coherence and clinical flow, ensuring arguments and recommendations progress in a sensible order with clear transitions, avoiding circular reasoning.
Evaluate the guideline's use of evidence-backed statements, ensuring key claims are supported with quantitative anchors like sample sizes and effect sizes, enhancing credibility.
Review the guideline's explicit guidance on diagnosis, reporting, testing strategy, management, and follow-up, ensuring practical usability in real-world multidisciplinary care.
Assess the guideline's use of tables, figures, and algorithms that materially improve decision-making, ensuring they are high-yield and not merely decorative.
Evaluate the guideline's clarity in defining terms, thresholds, and required reporting elements for integrated diagnosis, ensuring comprehensive and precise documentation.
Review the guideline's feasibility across different resource settings, ensuring resource-stratified pathways, turnaround time awareness, and practical constraints are addressed.
Ensure the guideline provides practical utility for multidisciplinary care teams, facilitating collaboration and effective patient management across specialties.

System.role_and_constraints: You are a medical guideline writer. Based on authoritative literature, write a chapter of an authoritative medical report. You need to follow the chapter format below.

content:
# Extranodal marginal zone lymphoma of mucosa-associated lymphoid tissue (MALT lymphoma) involving the digestive tract

# Definition

Extranodal marginal zone lymphoma of mucosa-associated lymphoid tissue (MALT lymphoma) involving the digestive tract is an extranodal low-grade B-cell lymphoma arising in mucosal or glandular tissues, recapitulating the cytoarchitectural features of mucosa-associated lymphoid tissue (MALT). It is composed of small lymphoid cells, often including marginal zone cells.

# ICD-O coding / ICD-11 coding

9699/3 Extranodal marginal zone lymphoma of mucosa-associated lymphoid tissue (MALT lymphoma)

2A85.1 Extranodal marginal zone B-cell lymphoma of mucosa-associated lymphoid tissue of stomach
2A85.3 Extranodal marginal zone B-cell lymphoma, primary site excluding stomach or skin

# Related terminology

None

# Subtype(s)

Immunoproliferative small intestinal disease (9764/3)

# Localization

Primary MALT lymphoma may occur at any site in the digestive system, including the GI tract, liver, and pancreatobiliary tract [1, 2]. Immunoproliferative small intestinal disease (IPSID), also called α-heavy chain disease (αHCD), involves the GI tract (mainly the small intestine) and mesenteric lymph nodes.

# Clinical features

Patients with MALT lymphoma present with site-related or nonspecific symptoms, although some can be asymptomatic [1, 2]. Endoscopically, MALT lymphoma of the GI tract appears as superficial spreading and mass-forming lesions [3]. It is often multifocal within the involved organ.

# Imaging

None

# Spread

None

# Epidemiology

MALT lymphoma accounts for $7 - 8 \%$ of all B-cell lymphomas [4], $30 - 60 \%$ of primary gastric lymphomas, and $2 - 28 \%$ of primary intestinal lymphomas [5, 6, 7, 8]. IPSID/aHCD predominantly occurs in young adults, with a geographical restriction to the Middle East, Africa, and eastern Asia. Primary MALT lymphomas of oesophagus [9, 10], liver [11, 12], biliary tract [13, 14], and pancreas [15] are very rare.

Most patients are adults, with a median age in the sixth decade of life. Men and women are about equally affected [4, 16, 2].

# Etiology

The best-studied association involves Helicobacter pylori and gastric MALT lymphoma, with the bacterium present in as many as $90\%$ of cases [17, 18, 19]. The proliferation of lymphoma cells in $H _ { \ast }$ pylori-infected patients depends on the presence of T cells specifically activated by H. pylori antigens [20]. The importance of this stimulation in vivo has been clearly demonstrated by the induction of remission in MALT lymphomas with H. pylori eradication [21]. Recent studies suggest that the incidence of gastric MALT lymphoma is decreasing and that only $32\%$ of cases are now associated with $H,$ pylori, as a result of effective therapy [22, 23]. A similar role has been suggested for Campylobacter infection in IPSID/αHCD [18].

There are variations in the role of chronic inflammation and type of recurrent genetic alterations in the various anatomical sites (see Table 11.02).

# Pathogenesis

Four translocations are specifically associated with MALT lymphomas: t(11;18)(q21;q21), t(1;14)(p22;q32), t(14;18)(q32;q21), and t(3;14)(p14;q32). They result in the production of a chimeric protein (BIRC3-MALT1) or in transcriptional deregulation of BCL10, MALT1, and FOXP1, respectively. Trisomy of chromosome 3 or 18 is frequent in MALT lymphomas but is nonspecific. The frequencies of the translocations or trisomies vary markedly with the primary site of disease. The chromosomal translocations all converge on the activation of the same oncogenic pathway associated with $\cdot \kappa \mathsf { B }$ [24]. The t(11;18) (q21:q21)/BIRC3-MALT1 translocation, which occurs in $6-26\%$ of gastric MALT lymphomas, is significantly associated with H. pylori negativity and nuclear BCL10 expression, and it can identify cases that will not respond to $H,$ pylori eradication [25, 26, 27, 28]. This translocation is also found in $12\mathrm{-}56\%$ of intestinal MALT lymphomas [29], sometimes together with gastric and multifocal intestinal involvement. The translocation is rarely seen in colorectal MALT lymphoma. The t(11;18)(q21;q21)/BIRC3-MALT1 translocation is associated with a low risk of additional genetic damage and hence transformation into diffuse large B-cell lymphoma (DLBCL). The t(14;18)/IGH-MALT1 translocation is often detected in hepatic MALT lymphoma [11]. The translocations t(1;14)/BCL10-MALT1 and t(3;14)/IGH-FOXP1 are infrequently found. The latter is associated with an increased risk of transformation to DLBCL. Extra copies of MALT1 and FOXP1, often suggestive of partial and complete trisomies 18 and 3, are detected in $25\%$ and $17\%$ of gastric cases, respectively. The presence of extra copies of MALT1 is significantly associated with progression or relapse of lymphoma, and is an adverse prognostic factor for event-free survival [30].

# Macroscopic appearance

Thickening of the mucosa may be mild or macroscopically obvious. Mass-forming lesions [3] may occur. MALT lymphoma is often multifocal within the involved organ.

# Histopathology

MALT lymphomas variably recapitulate the cytoarchitectural features of Peyer patches, the prototypical normal MALT. Tumours arising at any anatomical site share similar histopathological characteristics, despite site-specific differences in etiology and molecular cytogenetic abnormalities (see Table 11.02, p. 379).

MALT lymphoma is composed of small B cells that most typically include marginal zone cells (small to intermediate-sized cells with pale cytoplasm and a slightly irregular nucleus, also called centrocyte-like cells), monocytoid cells, and small lymphocytes, as well as scattered immunoblasts and centroblast-like cells [1]. Plasma cell differentiation is occasionally observed in MALT lymphomas of the GI tract, but it is a constant feature of IPSID. Dutcher bodies, immunoglobulin inclusions (crystals or globules), and amyloid can be present.

The lymphoma cells infiltrate around reactive lymphoid follicles in a marginal zone pattern. They also extend to the interfollicular regions. The neoplastic cells can erode, colonize, and eventually overrun the reactive follicles (a process called follicular colonization). Lymphoma cells typically infiltrate the epithelium, forming lymphoepithelial lesions, which are defined as aggregates of $\geq 3$ marginal zone cells with destruction of the glandular epithelium, often together with eosinophilic degeneration of epithelial cells [31]. Lymphoepithelial lesions are usually less prominent in non-gastric sites.

Scattered large cells resembling centroblasts or immunoblasts are usually present, but are in the minority. When solid or sheet-like proliferations of transformed cells are present, the tumour should be diagnosed as DLBCL, with the presence of concurrent MALT lymphoma noted. The term “high-grade MALT lymphoma" should not be used, and the term “MALT lymphoma" should not be applied to a DLBCL even if it has arisen in a MALT site or is associated with lymphoepithelial lesions.

The immunophenotype of lymphoma cells in MALT lymphoma is similar to that of marginal zone B cells: ${ \mathsf { C D } } 2 0 +$ CD79a+, BCL2+, BCL6-, CD5-, CD10-, CD23-, $C D 4 3 + 1 -$ CD110 $+ / -$ (weak), and typically expressing IgM (less often IgA or IgG and rarely IgD). There is no specific marker for MALT lymphoma, with IRTA1 and MNDA1 being possible markers for marginal zone lymphomas [32, 33, 34]. Negative staining for cyclin D1/SOX11 and CD10/BCL6 is useful for distinguishing MALT lymphoma from mantle cell lymphoma and follicular lymphoma, respectively. Coexpression of CD5, CD23, and LEF1 distinguishes chronic lymphocytic leukaemia from MALT lymphoma, although occasional expression of CD5 or CD23 may be seen in MALT lymphoma. Immunostaining with antikeratin antibodies may aid in the identification of lymphoepithelial lesions.

## IPSID/αHCD

IPSID/αHCD is a subtype of gastrointestinal MALT lymphoma with secretion of defective immunoglobulin α-heavy chain [35, 36]. IPSID is a MALT lymphoma with marked plasma cell differentiation [37, 36].

Early stages manifest as a dense lymphoplasmacytic infiltrate confined to the mucosa and/or submucosa. Villous blunting or atrophy of the small intestine may be seen. Advanced stages are characterized by histological transformation to DLBCL, usually also with plasmacytic differentiation. Immunohistochemical studies demonstrate the production of α-heavy chain without light chain. This peculiar feature is due to the deletion of most of the IGHV (VH) and all of the CH1 domains, but with intact C-terminal regions resulting in expression of a truncated heavy chain, which is unable to assemble with light chains.

# Immunophenotype

None

# Differential diagnosis

None

# Cytology

Not clinically relevant

# Diagnostic molecular pathology

Not clinically relevant

# Essential and desirable diagnostic criteria

Essential: involvement of extranodal site; diffuse to perifollicular infiltrate of centrocyte-like cells and small lymphoid cells, often with scattered large lymphoid cells; monocytoid cells and plasma cells may or may not be present; positivity for B-lineage markers; immunohistochemical exclusion of mantle cell lymphoma (CD5, cyclin D1) and follicular lymphoma (CD10, BCL6).

Desirable: presence of lymphoepithelial lesions.

# Grading / Staging

Lymphoid neoplasms are staged according to the Lugano classification, which has been adopted by the eighth edition of the Union for International Cancer Control (UICC) TNM classification [38].

# Prognosis and prediction

MALT lymphomas have an indolent clinical course and are slow to disseminate [39]. H. pylori eradication results in complete remission in 60-100% of gastric MALT lymphoma patients. Early-stage IPSID/aHCD also responds to antibiotics. The t(11;18) BIRC3-MALT1 translocation confers resistance to H. pylori eradication therapy. MALT lymphomas are sensitive to radiotherapy, and local treatment can be followed by prolonged disease-free intervals. Involvement of multiple extranodal sites and even bone marrow involvement do not appear to confer an adverse prognosis [41, 18].

# References

claims.claim: 9699/3 Extranodal marginal zone lymphoma of mucosa-associated lymphoid tissue (MALT lymphoma)
2A85.1 Extranodal marginal zone B-cell lymphoma of mucosa-associated lymphoid tissue of stomach
2A85.3 Extranodal marginal zone B-cell lymphoma, primary site excluding stomach or skin
Extranodal marginal zone lymphoma of mucosa-associated lymphoid tissue (MALT lymphoma) involving the digestive tract is an extranodal low-grade B-cell lymphoma.
MALT lymphoma arises in mucosal or glandular tissues, recapitulating the cytoarchitectural features of mucosa-associated lymphoid tissue (MALT).
MALT lymphoma is composed of small lymphoid cells, often including marginal zone cells.
Primary MALT lymphoma may occur at any site in the digestive system, including the Gl tract, liver, and pancreatobiliary tract.
Immunoproliferative small intestinal disease (IPSID), also called α-heavy chain disease (αHCD), involves the Gl tract (mainly the small intestine) and mesenteric lymph nodes.
Immunoproliferative small intestinal disease is coded as 9764/3.
MALT lymphoma accounts for $7 - 8 \%$ of all B-cell lymphomas.
MALT lymphoma accounts for $30 - 60 \%$ of primary gastric lymphomas.
MALT lymphoma accounts for $2 - 28 \%$ of primary intestinal lymphomas.
IPSID/aHCD predominantly occurs in young adults, with a geographical restriction to the Middle East, Africa, and eastern Asia.
Primary MALT lymphomas of oesophagus are very rare.
Primary MALT lymphomas of liver are very rare.
Primary MALT lymphomas of biliary tract are very rare.
Primary MALT lymphomas of pancreas are very rare.
Most patients with MALT lymphoma are adults, with a median age in the sixth decade of life.
Men and women are about equally affected by MALT lymphoma.
The best-studied association involves Helicobacter pylori and gastric MALT lymphoma, with the bacterium present in as many as 90% of cases.
The proliferation of lymphoma cells in H. pylori-infected patients depends on the presence of T cells specifically activated by H. pylori antigens.
The importance of T cell stimulation in vivo has been clearly demonstrated by the induction of remission in MALT lymphomas with H. pylori eradication.
Recent studies suggest that the incidence of gastric MALT lymphoma is decreasing and that only 32% of cases are now associated with H. pylori, as a result of effective therapy.
A similar role has been suggested for Campylobacter infection in IPSID/αHCD.
Four translocations are specifically associated with MALT lymphomas: t(11;18)(q21;q21), t(1;14)(p22;q32), t(14;18)(q32;q21), and t(3;14)(p14;q32).
These translocations result in the production of a chimeric protein (BIRC3-MALT1) or in transcriptional deregulation of BCL10, MALT1, and FOXP1, respectively.
Trisomy of chromosome 3 or 18 is frequent in MALT lymphomas but is nonspecific.
The frequencies of the translocations or trisomies vary markedly with the primary site of disease.
The chromosomal translocations all converge on the activation of the same oncogenic pathway associated with κB.
The t(11;18)(q21;q21)/BIRC3-MALT1 translocation occurs in 6-26% of gastric MALT lymphomas.
The t(11;18)(q21;q21)/BIRC3-MALT1 translocation is significantly associated with H. pylori negativity and nuclear BCL10 expression.
The t(11;18)(q21;q21)/BIRC3-MALT1 translocation can identify cases that will not respond to H. pylori eradication.
The t(11;18)(q21;q21)/BIRC3-MALT1 translocation is found in 12-56% of intestinal MALT lymphomas.
The t(11;18)(q21;q21)/BIRC3-MALT1 translocation is rarely seen in colorectal MALT lymphoma.
The t(11;18)(q21;q21)/BIRC3-MALT1 translocation is associated with a low risk of additional genetic damage and hence transformation into diffuse large B-cell lymphoma (DLBCL).
The t(14;18)/IGH-MALT1 translocation is often detected in hepatic MALT lymphoma.
The translocations t(1;14)/BCL10-MALT1 and t(3;14)/IGH-FOXP1 are infrequently found.
The t(3;14)/IGH-FOXP1 translocation is associated with an increased risk of transformation to DLBCL.
Extra copies of MALT1 and FOXP1, often suggestive of partial and complete trisomies 18 and 3, are detected in 25% and 17% of gastric cases, respectively.
The presence of extra copies of MALT1 is significantly associated with progression or relapse of lymphoma and is an adverse prognostic factor for event-free survival.
Thickening of the mucosa may be mild or macroscopically obvious.
Mass-forming lesions may occur.
MALT lymphoma is often multifocal within the involved organ.
MALT lymphomas variably recapitulate the cytoarchitectural features of Peyer patches, the prototypical normal MALT.
Tumours arising at any anatomical site share similar histopathological characteristics, despite site-specific differences in etiology and molecular cytogenetic abnormalities.
MALT lymphoma is composed of small B cells that most typically include marginal zone cells, monocytoid cells, and small lymphocytes, as well as scattered immunoblasts and centroblast-like cells.
Plasmacell differentiation is occasionally observed in MALT lymphomas of the GI tract, but it is a constant feature of IPSID.
Dutcher bodies, immunoglobulin inclusions (crystals or globules), and amyloid can be present in MALT lymphomas.
The lymphoma cells infiltrate around reactive lymphoid follicles in a marginal zone pattern.

Lymphoepithelial lesions are usually less prominent in non-gastric sites.
Scattered large cells resembling centroblasts or immunoblasts are usually present, but are in the minority.
When solid or sheet-like proliferations of transformed cells are present, the tumour should be diagnosed as DLBCL, with the presence of concurrent MALT lymphoma noted.

The term 'high-grade MALT lymphoma' should not be used, and the term 'MALT lymphoma' should not be applied to a DLBCL even if it has arisen in a MALT site or is associated with lymphoepithelial lesions.

The immunophenotype of lymphoma cells in MALT lymphoma is similar to that of marginal zone B cells: CD20+, CD79a+, BCL2+, BCL6-, CD5-, CD10-, CD23-, CD43+, CD110+/- (weak), and typically expressing IgM (less often IgA or IgG and rarely IgD).
There is no specific marker for MALT lymphoma, with IRTA1 and MNDA1 being possible markers for marginal zone lymphomas.
Negative staining for cyclin D1/SOX11 and CD10/BCL6 is useful for distinguishing MALT lymphoma from mantle cell lymphoma and follicular lymphoma, respectively.
Coexpression of CD5, CD23, and LEF1 distinguishes chronic lymphocytic leukaemia from MALT lymphoma, although occasional expression of CD5 or CD23 may be seen in MALT lymphoma.
Immunostaining with antikeratin antibodies may aid in the identification of lymphoepithelial lesions.
IPSID/αHCD is a subtype of gastrointestinal MALT lymphoma with secretion of defective immunoglobulin α-heavy chain.
IPSID is a MALT lymphoma with marked plasma cell differentiation.
Early stages of IPSID/αHCD manifest as a dense lymphoplasmacytic infiltrate confined to the mucosa and/or submucosa.
Villous blunting or atrophy of the small intestine may be seen in early stages of IPSID/αHCD.
Advanced stages of IPSID/αHCD are characterized by histological transformation to DLBCL, usually also with plasmacytic differentiation.
Immunohistochemical studies demonstrate the production of α-heavy chain without light chain in IPSID/αHCD.

The production of α-heavy chain without light chain in IPSID/αHCD is due to the deletion of most of the IGHV (VH) and all of the CH1 domains, but with intact C-terminal regions resulting in expression of a truncated heavy chain.
Involvement of extranodal site is essential for diagnosis.
Diffuse to perifollicular infiltrate of centrocyte-like cells and small lymphoid cells is essential for diagnosis.
Scattered large lymphoid cells are often present and essential for diagnosis.
Monocytoid cells and plasma cells may or may not be present.
Positivity for B-lineage markers is essential for diagnosis.
Immunohistochemical exclusion of mantle cell lymphoma (CD5, cyclin D1) is essential for diagnosis.
Immunohistochemical exclusion of follicular lymphoma (CD10, BCL6) is essential for diagnosis.
Presence of lymphoepithelial lesions is desirable for diagnosis.
Lymphoid neoplasms are staged according to the Lugano classification.
The Lugano classification has been adopted by the eighth edition of the Union for International Cancer Control (UICC) TNM classification.
Patients with MALT lymphoma present with site-related or nonspecific symptoms, although some can be asymptomatic.
Endoscopically, MALT lymphoma of the GI tract appears as superficial spreading and mass-forming lesions.
MALT lymphoma is often multifocal within the involved organ.
MALT lymphomas have an indolent clinical course and are slow to disseminate.
H. pylori eradication results in complete remission in 60-100% of gastric MALT lymphoma patients.
Early-stage IPSID/aHCD also responds to antibiotics.
The t(11;18)IBIRC3-MALT1 translocation confers resistance to H. pylori eradication therapy.
MALT lymphomas are sensitive to radiotherapy, and local treatment can be followed by prolonged disease-free intervals.
Involvement of multiple extranodal sites and even bone marrow involvement do not appear to confer an adverse prognosis.

comprehensiveness.criterion: Complete workflow coverage from presentation to follow-up
comprehensiveness.explanation: Assess if the guideline covers the entire clinical workflow for MALT lymphoma, from initial presentation through imaging, pathology, molecular diagnostics, integrated diagnosis, management, and follow-up. This ensures comprehensive guidance for clinicians managing this disease.

Evaluate the guideline's coverage of baseline definitions, entity boundaries, grading, and ICD-O/ICD-11 coding. Accurate coding and clear definitions are crucial for standardized diagnosis and treatment planning.

Check if the guideline addresses subtypes of MALT lymphoma and provides guidance on handling NOS/NEC-style diagnostic uncertainty, including minimum and ideal diagnostic sets. This is important for accurate diagnosis and treatment.

Assess the guideline's coverage of MALT lymphoma's anatomic distribution, dissemination patterns, epidemiology, and demographic stratification. Understanding these aspects aids in accurate diagnosis and targeted treatment.

Evaluate the guideline's coverage of etiology and mechanisms, including associations with infections like Helicobacter pylori and genetic alterations. This information is crucial for understanding disease pathogenesis and guiding treatment.

Check if the guideline thoroughly covers macroscopic and microscopic pathology, immunophenotype, and cytology roles in diagnosis. Comprehensive pathology coverage is essential for accurate diagnosis and differentiation from other lymphomas.

Assess the guideline's coverage of diagnostic molecular pathology, including entity-defining alterations, co-alterations, progression-related events, and CNV patterns. Molecular insights are crucial for precise diagnosis and treatment planning.

insight_depth.criterion: Mechanistic depth linking genetics and phenotype
insight_depth.explanation: Evaluate the guideline's depth in linking genetic and epigenetic alterations to the clinical phenotype of MALT lymphoma. Understanding these mechanisms aids in accurate diagnosis and targeted therapy.

insight_depth.criterion: Integration across imaging, histology, IHC, and molecular
insight_depth.explanation: Assess the guideline's ability to integrate findings from imaging, histology, immunohistochemistry, and molecular diagnostics into a coherent diagnostic reasoning process. This integration is crucial for accurate and comprehensive diagnosis.

insight_depth.criterion: Transparent diagnostic reasoning with decision points
insight_depth.explanation: Check if the guideline provides transparent diagnostic reasoning, including decision points, branching logic, and common pitfalls. Clear reasoning helps clinicians navigate complex cases and avoid diagnostic errors.

insight_depth.criterion: Prognostic and predictive stratification depth
insight_depth.explanation: Evaluate the guideline's depth in prognostic and predictive stratification, with biologically plausible rationale and clear risk grouping. Accurate stratification informs treatment decisions and patient management.

insight_depth.criterion: Evidence synthesis maturity and heterogeneity handling
insight_depth.explanation: Assess the guideline's maturity in evidence synthesis, including how conclusions are drawn, heterogeneity handled, limitations acknowledged, and strength-of-evidence conveyed. Robust evidence synthesis supports reliable clinical decision-making.

insight_depth.criterion: Clinical impact reasoning for biomarkers and histologic features
insight_depth.explanation: Check if the guideline provides reasoning on how specific biomarkers or histologic features impact diagnosis, grade, management, surveillance, and trial eligibility. Understanding clinical impact guides effective patient care.

accuracy_standards.criterion: Correct and current WHO entity naming and grading
accuracy_standards.explanation: Evaluate the guideline's adherence to correct and current WHO entity naming and grading conventions, ensuring alignment with major interim updates. Accurate naming and grading are essential for standardized diagnosis.

accuracy_standards.criterion: Accurate ICD-O/ICD-11 coding usage
accuracy_standards.explanation: Assess the guideline's accuracy in ICD-O/ICD-11 coding usage and handling of synonyms/deprecated terms. Precise coding is crucial for consistent diagnosis and treatment planning.

accuracy_standards.criterion: Molecular terminology accuracy and assay interpretation
accuracy_standards.explanation: Check the guideline's accuracy in molecular terminology, including variant/fusion naming, promoter methylation phrasing, and CNV conventions, along with correct assay interpretation. Accurate molecular terminology supports precise diagnosis.

accuracy_standards.criterion: Internal consistency across thresholds and criteria
accuracy_standards.explanation: Evaluate the guideline for internal consistency, ensuring no contradictory thresholds, criteria, or marker interpretations. Consistency is crucial for reliable diagnosis and treatment recommendations.

accuracy_standards.criterion: Diagnostic accuracy safeguards and assay limitations
accuracy_standards.explanation: Assess the guideline's inclusion of diagnostic accuracy safeguards, such as assay limitations, false positives/negatives, pre-analytic variables, sampling bias, and therapy effects. Safeguards ensure accurate and reliable diagnosis.

accuracy_standards.criterion: Consistency with reporting standards and best practices
accuracy_standards.explanation: Check the guideline's consistency with widely used reporting standards and best practices for integrated diagnosis narratives. Adherence to standards ensures reliable and standardized reporting.

readability_utility.explanation: Evaluate the guideline's logical coherence and clinical flow, ensuring arguments and recommendations progress in a clinically sensible order with clear transitions. Coherent flow aids in understanding and application.

Assess the guideline's use of evidence-backed statements, supported by concrete quantitative anchors like sample sizes, effect sizes, percentages, and confidence intervals. Evidence-backed statements enhance credibility and reliability.

readability_utility.criterion: Actionable guidance for diagnosis and management
readability_utility.explanation: Check if the guideline provides explicit 'what to do' guidance for diagnosis, reporting, testing strategy, management, and follow-up. Actionable guidance supports effective clinical decision-making.

readability_utility.criterion: High-yield presentation with tables and algorithms
readability_utility.explanation: Evaluate the guideline's use of tables, figures, and algorithms that materially improve decision-making, such as diagnostic criteria summaries, differential diagnosis matrices, and decision trees. High-yield presentation aids in understanding and application.

readability_utility.criterion: Clear definitions and required reporting elements
readability_utility.explanation: Assess the guideline's clarity in defining terms, thresholds, and required reporting elements for integrated diagnosis. Clear definitions ensure accurate and standardized reporting.

readability_utility.criterion: Feasibility across settings and resource-stratified pathways
readability_utility.explanation: Check the guideline's feasibility across different clinical settings, including resource-stratified pathways for basic vs advanced diagnostics, turnaround time awareness, and practical constraints. Feasibility ensures applicability in diverse settings.

System.role_and_constraints: You are a medical guideline writer. Based on authoritative literature, write a chapter of an authoritative medical report. You need to follow the chapter format below.

content:
# Gastric undifferentiated carcinoma

# Definition

Undifferentiated carcinoma of the stomach is a primary gastric carcinoma composed of anaplastic cells showing no specific cytological or architectural type of differentiation.

# ICD-O coding / ICD-11 coding

8020/3 Carcinoma, undifferentiated, NOS

2B72 & XH1YY4 Malignant neoplasms of stomach & Carcinoma, undifferentiated, NOS

# Related terminology

Acceptable: anaplastic carcinoma; pleomorphic carcinoma.
Not recommended: giant cell carcinoma.

# Subtype(s)

Large cell carcinoma with rhabdoid phenotype (8014/3); pleomorphic carcinoma (8022/3); sarcomatoid carcinoma (8033/3); carcinoma with osteoclast-like giant cells (8035/3)

# Localization

No specific localization within the stomach is observed, but diffuse involvement of more than one region is not uncommon.

# Clinical features

Most patients present with a large ulcerated and extensively necrotic transmural fungating mass, which is often associated with extensive synchronous regional metastases. Most patients with initially localized disease develop widespread metastases shortly after diagnosis and surgery, and most patients die of their disease within 1 year [1, 2].

# Imaging

None

# Spread

None

# Epidemiology

Limited data are available. Undifferentiated rhabdoid carcinomas have been estimated to account for 0.1–0.3% of all gastric carcinomas and 5.6% of solid adenocarcinomas [3,4].

# Etiology

Unknown

# Pathogenesis

The frequent presence of a variable glandular component or areas of transition to adenocarcinoma in otherwise undifferentiated tumours suggests an origin via dedifferentiation. In cases originating from other gastric cancer types via dedifferentiation, the genotype is essentially dictated by the clone of origin. In some cases, the undifferentiated phenotype is probably driven by various components of the SWI/SNF chromatin-remodeling complex; in particular, loss of SMARCB1 (INI1), SMARCA4, and ARID1A has been reported. In a subset of cases, SWI/SNF loss is superimposed on mismatch repair deficiency [1, 2].

# Macroscopic appearance

The carcinoma is a large, ulcerated, necrotic, and usually transmural fungating mass, often with extensive regional lymph node involvement.

# Histopathology

Anaplastic carcinoma is composed of diffuse sheets of anaplastic, large to medium-sized polygonal cells, with frequent pleomorphic tumour giant cells. A variable rhabdoid cell component is common and may be the predominant pattern; these cells frequently show binucleation or multinucleation. Other histological patterns that may be seen focally or as the dominant pattern include the spindled sarcomatoid pleomorphic pattern, undifferentiated carcinoma with osteoclast-like giant cells, and carcinoma with lymphoepithelioma-like features [5, 6]. Depending on the extent of sampling, a glandular component may be observed and can vary from minimal to prominent. The stroma may be myxoid or mucinous, and it commonly contains mononuclear inflammatory cells. Immunohistochemistry, which is not specific, reveals variable expression of pancytokeratin. EMA staining may be helpful in keratin-poor examples. Vimentin is consistently expressed, frequently with a perinuclear dot-like pattern [1, 2]. Gastric undifferentiated carcinoma should be distinguished from EBV-associated carcinoma with lymphoid stroma, aggressive lymphomas (including anaplastic large cell lymphoma), metastatic melanoma, germ cell neoplasms, PEComa, and other types of poorly differentiated sarcomas with an epithelioid large cell pattern.

# Immunophenotype

None

# Differential diagnosis

None

# Cytology

Gastric undifferentiated carcinoma contains malignant cells without evidence of differentiation.

# Diagnostic molecular pathology

See Gastric adenocarcinoma (p. 85). ERBB2 (HER2) immunohistochemistry is recommended in all gastric carcinomas for which treatment is appropriate, but there are no specific recommendations.

# Essential and desirable diagnostic criteria

Essential: malignant cells without histological evidence of differentiation; equivocal immunohistochemistry.

# Grading / Staging

See Gastric adenocarcinoma (p.85).

# Prognosis and prediction

Because of the rarity of this entity and its highly aggressive nature, no specific prognostic features have been identified. Disease stage at diagnosis might be prognostically relevant.

# References

1. Agaimy A, Rau TT, Hartmann A, et al. SMARCB1 (INH)-negative rhabdoid carcinomas of the gastrointestinal tract: clinicopathologic and molecular study of a highly aggressive variant with literature review. Am J Surg Pathol. 2014 Jul;38(7):910—20. PMID:24503755
2. Agaimy A, Daum O, Märkl B, et al. SWI/SNF complex-deficient undifferentiated/rhabdoid carcinomas of the gastrointestinal tract: a series of 13 cases highlighting mutually exclusive loss of SMARCA4 and SMARCA2 and frequent co-inactivation of SMARCB1 and SMARCA2. Am J Surg Pathol. 2016 Apr;40(4):544–53. PMID:26551623
3. Ueyama T, Nagai E, Yao T, et al. Vimentin-positive gastric carcinomas with rhabdoid features. A clinicopathologic and immunohistochemical study. Am J Surg Pathol. 1993 Aug;17(8):813-9. PMID:7687828
4. Utsunomiya T, Yao T, Masuda K, et al. Vimentin-positive adenocarcinomas of the stomach: co-expression of vimentin and cytokeratin. Histopathology. 1996 Dec;29(6):507-16. PMID:8971557
5. Jing H, Geng M, Meng Q, et al. Sarcomatoid carcinoma of the stomach with osteoclast-like giant cells. Tumori. 2012 May-Jun;98(3):82e-5e. PMID:22825525
6. Willems S, Carneiro F, Geboes K. Gastric carcinoma with osteoclast-like giant cells and lymphoepithelioma-like carcinoma of the stomach: two of a kind? Histopathology. 2005 Sep;47(3):331-3. PMID:16115241

comprehensiveness.explanation: Assess the guideline's coverage from initial presentation through to follow-up, ensuring all clinical, pathological, and molecular steps are included. This is crucial for comprehensive patient management and ensures no critical diagnostic or therapeutic steps are omitted.

comprehensiveness.criterion: Definitional and coding completeness for gastric undifferentiated carcinoma
comprehensiveness.explanation: Evaluate the guideline's clarity in defining the disease, including ICD-O and ICD-11 coding. Accurate coding is essential for proper classification and treatment planning, and ensures alignment with international standards.

Examine how the guideline addresses subtypes and NOS/NEC categories, providing minimum and ideal diagnostic criteria. This is important for accurate diagnosis and treatment, especially in cases with ambiguous histological features.

comprehensiveness.criterion: Anatomic distribution and dissemination pattern coverage
comprehensiveness.explanation: Assess the guideline's description of tumor localization and spread within the stomach. Understanding these patterns is vital for staging and treatment planning, as well as for predicting patient outcomes.

Evaluate the guideline's coverage of macroscopic and microscopic pathology, including immunophenotype. Comprehensive pathology information is crucial for accurate diagnosis and differentiation from other gastric tumors.

Assess the guideline's coverage of molecular alterations, co-alterations, and progression-related events. This is essential for understanding the genetic basis of the disease and for identifying potential therapeutic targets.

Evaluate the guideline's inclusion of prognostic and predictive factors, including stage at diagnosis. This information is critical for patient counseling and for tailoring treatment strategies to individual risk profiles.

insight_depth.explanation: Assess the guideline's explanation of how genetic alterations influence the undifferentiated phenotype. Understanding these mechanisms is crucial for developing targeted therapies and for advancing research in gastric carcinoma.

insight_depth.criterion: Integration across imaging, histology, IHC, and molecular data
insight_depth.explanation: Evaluate how well the guideline integrates data from various diagnostic modalities into a coherent diagnostic framework. This integration is essential for accurate diagnosis and for avoiding misinterpretation of results.

insight_depth.criterion: Transparent diagnostic reasoning and decision points
insight_depth.explanation: Examine the guideline's clarity in outlining diagnostic decision points and potential pitfalls. Clear reasoning helps clinicians navigate complex cases and ensures consistent application of diagnostic criteria.

insight_depth.criterion: Prognostic and predictive stratification depth
insight_depth.explanation: Assess the guideline's depth in stratifying patients based on prognostic and predictive factors. This stratification is important for personalized treatment planning and for identifying patients who may benefit from clinical trials.

insight_depth.criterion: Evidence synthesis maturity and limitations acknowledgment
insight_depth.explanation: Evaluate the guideline's ability to synthesize evidence and acknowledge limitations. Mature evidence synthesis ensures that recommendations are based on the best available data and that uncertainties are transparently communicated.

accuracy_standards.criterion: Correct and current WHO entity naming and grading
accuracy_standards.explanation: Ensure the guideline uses the correct and current WHO terminology and grading conventions. Accurate naming and grading are essential for consistency in diagnosis and treatment across different healthcare settings.

accuracy_standards.criterion: Accurate ICD-O/ICD-11 coding and synonym handling
accuracy_standards.explanation: Assess the guideline's accuracy in ICD coding and handling of synonyms and deprecated terms. Proper coding is crucial for disease classification, epidemiological studies, and healthcare reimbursement.

accuracy_standards.criterion: Molecular terminology accuracy and assay interpretation
accuracy_standards.explanation: Evaluate the guideline's use of precise molecular terminology and correct interpretation of assays. Accurate molecular descriptions are vital for understanding the disease's genetic basis and for guiding targeted therapies.

accuracy_standards.criterion: Internal consistency and diagnostic accuracy safeguards
accuracy_standards.explanation: Ensure the guideline maintains internal consistency and includes safeguards against diagnostic errors. Consistency and accuracy are critical for reliable diagnosis and for minimizing the risk of misdiagnosis.

accuracy_standards.criterion: Consistency with reporting standards and best practices
accuracy_standards.explanation: Assess the guideline's alignment with widely used reporting standards and best practices. Consistency with standards ensures that diagnoses are communicated effectively and that patient care is optimized.

readability_utility.explanation: Evaluate the guideline's logical organization and clinical flow. A coherent structure ensures that clinicians can easily follow the diagnostic and treatment pathways, leading to more efficient patient care.
Assess the guideline's use of evidence-backed statements with quantitative data. Providing concrete data supports the credibility of recommendations and helps clinicians make informed decisions.

readability_utility.criterion: Actionable guidance for diagnosis and management
readability_utility.explanation: Evaluate the guideline's provision of clear, actionable guidance for diagnosis, reporting, and management. Actionable recommendations are essential for translating guidelines into clinical practice.

Assess the guideline's use of tables, figures, and algorithms to enhance decision-making. High-yield presentation tools help clinicians quickly access critical information and make informed decisions.

Evaluate the guideline's feasibility for implementation in various healthcare settings, considering resource availability and practical constraints. Feasibility ensures that guidelines can be applied widely and effectively.

System.role_and_constraints: You are a medical guideline writer. Based on authoritative literature, write a chapter of an authoritative medical report. You need to follow the chapter format below.

content:
# Appendiceal mucinous neoplasm

# Definition

Mucinous neoplasm of the appendix is an appendiceal neoplasm characterized by mucinous epithelial proliferation with extracellular mucin and pushing tumour margins.

# ICD-O coding / ICD-11 coding

8480/1 Low-grade appendiceal mucinous neoplasm  
8480/2 High-grade appendiceal mucinous neoplasm

2E92.4Y & XHoEK3 Other specified benign neoplasm of the large intestine & Mucinous cystic neoplasm with low-grade dysplasia  
2E61.Y & XH81P3 Carcinoma in situ of other specified digestive organs & Mucinous cystic tumour with high-grade dysplasia

# Related terminology

Not recommended: mucinous tumour of uncertain malignant potential; mucinous cystadenoma of the appendix; borderline tumour of the appendix; mucinous cystadenocarcinoma of the appendix.

# Subtype(s)

None

# Localization

Appendiceal mucinous neoplasm is a mucinous neoplasm occurring in the appendix.

# Clinical features

Patients can present with features of appendicitis, with or without appendiceal perforation. The clinical presentations of patients with peritoneal dissemination may include progressive abdominal distention, new onset of an umbilical hernia, or a palpable mass on abdominal or pelvic examination. CT or ultrasound may show a soft tissue mass in the appendix that may appear fluid-filled. Curvilinear calcification of the wall increases specificity but is present in only about half of all cases.

# Imaging

None

# Spread

None

# Epidemiology

Appendiceal mucinous neoplasms tend to occur in adults in their sixth decade of life, but there is a wide age range. They occur in men and women with roughly equal frequency.

# Etiology

Unknown

# Pathogenesis

The vast majority of low-grade appendiceal mucinous neoplasms (LAMNs) have KRAS mutations [6], and the majority have GNAS mutations. However, GNAS mutations are somewhat less common in high-grade mucinous tumours, suggesting that they may not arise from low-grade tumours [1, 2]. GNAS mutations may play a role in the abundant mucin production in these tumours [2]. Mutations typical of colorectal carcinoma, such as APC, TP53, and SMAD4 mutations, are not common in appendiceal mucinous neoplasms, and are more common in high-grade tumours [3, 1, 4]. Other mutations have been described in a minority of cases, including mutations in FAT4, SMAD2, AKT1, MET, JAK3, PIK3CA, STK11, and RB1 [4, 5]. The mutation profile shows an excess of $\complement \top$ transitions, suggesting that deamination of 5-methylcytosine is a mutational mechanism in these tumours [5, 1]. Microsatellite instability, BRAF mutations, and loss of DNA mismatch repair protein expression are not features of LAMN [6, 7].

# Macroscopic appearance

The appendix may appear grossly unremarkable or demonstrate dilatation as a result of abnormal accumulation of mucin. Gross perforation with mucin extrusion may be evident. The wall may be attenuated, or markedly thickened and partially calcified.

# Histopathology

The microscopic appearance of LAMN can take several forms. The classic pattern is replacement of the normal appendiceal mucosa by a filiform villous mucinous epithelial proliferation. The tumour cells tend to have tall cytoplasmic mucin vacuoles that compress the nucleus, creating a hypermucinous but cytologically bland appearance. Other tumours may show an undulating or scalloped appearance, with columnar epithelial cells with nuclear pseudostratification growing on fibrotic submucosal tissue. Some cases are characterized by an attenuated or flattened monolayer of mucinous epithelium. The degree of atypia is mild, resembling low-grade colonic dysplasia at most. The lymphoid tissue of the appendix is usually absent. The wall can have varying degrees of fibrosis, hyalinization, and calcification. The mucin can dissect through the structures of the appendix and extend to the peritoneal surface or cause rupture of the appendix. Intramural glandular epithelium protruding into or through the appendiceal wall and exhibiting a rounded, pushing pattern of invasion can occur. If an infiltrative pattern is observed, this qualifies as adenocarcinoma. Serosal involvement can consist of mucin at the surface, or it can consist of replacement of a portion of the appendix with a hyalinizing reaction with strips of low-grade mucinous epithelial cells associated with extracellular mucin [8, 9, 10].

High-grade appendiceal mucinous neoplasm (HAMN) is rare. It shows histological features similar to those of LAMN, including subepithelial fibrosis, a broad pushing margin, broad front pushing invasion, rupture, and peritoneal dissemination, but the neoplastic epithelium has unequivocal high-grade features [11]. Architecturally, the neoplastic epithelium may show micropapillary features, cribriforming, or piling up of the epithelial cells, but most often a single layer of cells is present. The epithelial cells in HAMNs have enlarged, hyperchromatic, and pleomorphic nuclei. Mitotic figures may be frequent, including atypical mitoses. There may be more single-cell necrosis of the epithelial cells or sloughing of necrotic epithelial cells into the lumen of the appendix.

Fig 4 ll mucin with fibrosis and epithelial cells, consistent with localized pseudomyxoma peritonei.

Ruptured diverticular disease of the appendix often shows mucin extrusion onto the appendix surface and hyperplastic changes of the mucosa that are frequently misinterpreted as LAMN. Appendices with diverticular disease show mucosal alterations but maintained crypts separated by lamina propria, maintained lymphoid tissue, and often a Schwann cell proliferation in the lamina propria [12]. Serrated polyps generally have complex serration and lack the filiform villous pattern or undulating pattern. Serrated polyps typically maintain the mucosal architecture of the appendix and are not associated with mucin dissection through the wall or pseudomyxoma peritonei. Mucinous adenocarcinoma shows at least focal destructive or infiltrative invasion characterized by pools of mucin with floating clusters or strips of epithelium, cribriform glands, or infiltrative glands with desmoplastic stromal response. Dissemination to the peritoneal cavity occurs in both HAMN and mucinous adenocarcinoma, and it should not be the sole factor in distinguishing the two.

# Immunophenotype

None

# Differential diagnosis

None

# Cytology

Not clinically relevant

# Diagnostic molecular pathology

Not clinically relevant

e pei tumour. The presence of signet-ring cells in peritoneal mucin may be prognostically significant even if they do not constitute 50% of the tumour. However, signet-ring cells floating (psediefoatl)

# Essential and desirable diagnostic criteria

LAMN
Essential: a filiform or villous mucinous epithelium with tall cytoplasmic mucin vacuoles and compressed bland nuclei or epithelial undulations/scalloping with columnar cells with nuclear pseudostratification (the mucinous epithelium could also be monolayered and attenuated, with only mild atypia); a broad pushing margin; various degrees of extracellular mucin (with absent lymphoid tissue), fibrosis, hyalinization, and calcification of the appendiceal wall.

HAMN
Essential: Histological features similar to those of LAMN, with the addition of micropapillary features, cribriforming, piling up of epithelial cells with high-grade features (i.e. enlarged, hyperchromatic, and pleomorphic nuclei; numerous atypical mitotic figures; single-cell necrosis; and sloughed necrotic epithelial cells in the lumen of the appendix).

Note: See also Table 5.02 (p. 145).

# Grading / Staging

According to the Union for International Cancer Control (UICC) staging system, LAMN and HAMN are considered in situ (pTis) if confined to the submucosa and muscularis propria. A tumour that extends into subserosa is staged as pT3. Tumours that perforate the serosa and involve the appendiceal serosa are considered pT4a. HAMN is staged using the same staging system as invasive appendiceal adenocarcinoma. Mucin and/or epithelial cells that involve peritoneal surfaces beyond the appendix are staged as pM1a if the mucin is acellular and pM1b if the mucin contains mucinous epithelial cells.

# Prognosis and prediction

The prognosis of LAMN is highly stage-dependent, with tumours limited to the appendix having an excellent prognosis and those with peritoneal dissemination having a variable prognosis [10, 13, 8]. The prognosis in disseminated tumours depends on the grade of the peritoneal mucinous epithelium [14, 15], the extent of disease, and the ability to achieve complete cytoreduction of macroscopically visible tumour within the abdomen [16]. The addition of hyperthermic intraperitoneal chemotherapy (HIPEC) together with complete cytoreduction has increased survival [17].

HAMNs are rare, and there are limited data regarding their natural history when they are confined to the appendix. Currently, the management of patients with HAMNs confined to the appendix and the role of additional surgery are uncertain.

# References

3. Davison JM, Hartman DJ, Singhi AD, et al. Loss of SMAD4 protein expression associated with high tumor grade and poor prognosis in disseminated appendiceal mucinous neoplasms. Am J Surg Pathol. 2014 May;38(5):583–92. PMID:24618609
4. Liu ZH, Lian BF, Dong QZ, et al. Whole-exome mutational and transcriptional landscapes of combined hepatocellular cholangiocarcinoma reveal molecular diversity. Biochim Biophys Acta Mol Basis Dis. 2018 Jun;1864 6 Pt B:2360–8. PMID:29408647
5. Pengelly RJ, Rowaiye B, Pickard K, et al. Analysis of mutation and loss of heterozygosity by whole-exome sequencing yields insights into pseudomyxoma peritonei. J Mol Diagn. 2018 Sep;20(5):635–42. PMID:29936525
6. Zauber P, Berman E, Marotta S, et al. Ki-ras gene mutations are invariably present in low-grade mucinous tumors of the vermiform appendix. Scand J Gastroenterol. 2011 Jul;46(7-8):869–74. PMID:21443421
7. Misdraji J, Burgart LJ, Lauwers GY. Defective mismatch repair in the pathogenesis of low-grade appendiceal mucinous neoplasms and adenocarcinomas. Mod Pathol. 2004 Dec;17(12):1447–54. PMID:15354187
8. Pai RK, Beck AH, Northrup JM, et al. Appendiceal mucinous neoplasms: clinicopathologic study of 116 cases with analysis of factors predicting recurrence. Am J Surg Pathol. 2009 Oct;33(10):1425–39. PMID:19641451
9. Misraji J, Yantis RK, Graeme-Cook F, et al. Appendiceal mucinous neoplasms: a clinicopathologic analysis of 107 cases. Am J Surg Pathol. 2003 Aug;27(8):1089–103. PMID:12838241
10. Carr NJ, McCarthy WF, Sobin LH. Epithelial neoplasms and tumor-like lesions of the appendix: A clinicopathologic study of 184 patients with a multivariate analysis of prognostic factors. Cancer. 1995 Feb 1;75(3):757–68. PMID:7828125
11. Carr NJ, Cecil TD, Mohamed F, et al. A consensus for classification and pathologic reporting of pseudomyxoma peritonei and associated appendiceal neoplasia: the results of the Peritoneal Surface Oncology Group International (PSOGI) modified Delphi process. Am J Surg Pathol. 2016 Jan;40(1):14–26. PMID:26492811
12. Hsu M, Young RH, Misdrij J, Ruptured appendiceal diverticula mimicking low-grade appendiceal mucinous neoplasms. Am J Surg Pathol. 2009 Oct;33(10):1515–21. PMID:19623035
13. Yantiss RK, Shia J, Klimstra DS, et al. Prognostic significance of localized extra-appendiceal mucin deposition in appendiceal mucinous neoplasms. Am J Surg Pathol. 2009 Feb;33(2):248–55. PMID:18852679
14. Ronnett BM, Yan H, Kurman RJ, et al. Patients with pseudomyxoma peritonei associated with disseminated peritoneal adenomucinosis have a significantly more favorable prognosis than patients with peritoneal mucinous carcinomatosis. Cancer. 2001 Jul 1;92(1):85–91. PMID:11443613
15. Bradley RF, Stewart JH 4th, Russell GB, et al. Pseudomyxoma peritonei of appendiceal origin: a clinicopathologic analysis of 101 patients uniformly treated at a single institution with literature review. Am J Surg Pathol. 2006 May;30(5):551–9. PMID:16699309
16. Youssef H, Newman C, Chandrakumaran K, et al. Operative findings, early complications, and long-term survival in 456 patients with pseudomyxoma peritonei syndrome of appendiceal origin. Dis Colon Rectum. 2011 Mar;54(3):293–9. PMID:21304299
17. Sugarbaker PH, Chang D. Results of treatment of 385 patients with peritoneal surface spread of appendiceal malignancy. Ann Surg Oncol. 1999 Dec;6(8):727–31. PMID:10622499
