claims.claim: 8500/3 Ductadenocarcinoma NOS
2C10.0 Adenocarcinoma of pancreas
Pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma (PDAC) is an invasive pancreatic epithelial neoplasm with glandular (ductal) differentiation.
PDAC usually demonstrates luminal and/or intracellular mucin production.
PDAC does not have a substantial component of any other histological type.
Duct cell adenocarcinoma is also known as infiltrating duct carcinoma.
Duct cell adenocarcinoma is also known as tubular adenocarcinoma.
Colloid carcinoma is also known as mucinous non-cystic carcinoma.
Undifferentiated carcinoma, sarcomatoid type is also known as spindle cell carcinoma.
Undifferentiated carcinoma, sarcomatoid type is also known as sarcomatoid carcinoma.
Undifferentiated carcinoma, anaplastic type includes giant cell carcinoma.
Undifferentiated carcinoma, anaplastic type includes anaplastic carcinoma.
Undifferentiated carcinoma, anaplastic type includes pleomorphic large cell carcinoma.
Acceptable: osteoclastic giant cell carcinoma.
claims.section: Undifferentiated carcinoma with osteoclast-like giant cells

claims.claim: Two thirds of ductal adenocarcinomas arise in the head of the pancreas.
The remainder of ductal adenocarcinomas arise in the body or tail of the pancreas.
The vast majority of ductal adenocarcinomas are solitary.
Multifocal disease can occur in ductal adenocarcinomas.
Very rarely, ectopic pancreatic tissue can give rise to pancreatic intraepithelial neoplasia (PanIN) lesions.
Very rarely, ectopic pancreatic tissue can give rise to an invasive carcinoma.
Colloid carcinoma is classified with the code 8480/3.
Poorly cohesive carcinoma is classified with the code 8490/3.
Signet-ring cell carcinoma is classified with the code 8490/3.
Medullary carcinoma NOS is classified with the code 8510/3.
Adenosquamous carcinoma is classified with the code 8560/3.
Hepatoid carcinoma is classified with the code 8576/3.
Large cell carcinoma with rhabdoid phenotype is classified with the code 8014/3.
Carcinoma, undifferentiated, NOS is classified with the code 8020/3.
Undifferentiated carcinoma with osteoclast-like giant cells is classified with the code 8035/3.
Clinical features include decreased appetite and indigestion, changes in bowel habits, fatigue, back pain, unexplained weight loss, and jaundice.
New-onset diabetes (type 3c) may be the first manifestation of pancreatic cancer.
Depression may be a presenting symptom of pancreatic cancer.
Symptoms of advanced pancreatic cancer are related to liver metastasis and/or invasion of adjacent organs or of the peritoneal cavity.
Patients occasionally present with migratory thrombophlebitis.
Patients rarely present with acute pancreatitis.

Multidetector CT with dual-phase or multiphase dynamic contrast using early arterial, pancreatic, and late venous phases is one of the best imaging modalities for the pancreas and the surrounding vasculature.
PDAC usually appears as an irregular solid hypodense mass with abrupt cut-off and upstream dilatation of the pancreatic duct.
The double-duct sign is virtually pathognomonic of carcinoma of the head of the pancreas.
MRI may be more sensitive than CT for the detection and evaluation of liver metastases.
Magnetic resonance cholangiopancreatography provides excellent resolution of the duct system.
EUS allows high-resolution imaging of the pancreas and surrounding lymph nodes and vessels; it also allows tissue sampling, which remains the gold standard for diagnosis.
PET may have diagnostic value, especially in cases with enlarged lymph nodes or of persisting masses after therapy.
The serum markers CA19-9 and CEA are not useful, by themselves, for screening of asymptomatic individuals but can be used to monitor established disease.
The best-known risk factor for pancreatic cancer is tobacco smoking.
The risk of pancreatic cancer in smokers is 2-3 times that in non-smokers.
A dose-response relationship and a favourable effect of quitting smoking on pancreatic cancer risk have been shown in many populations.
The proportion of pancreatic cancer cases attributable to tobacco smoking has been estimated to be 20-30% in men and 10% in women.
Passive (secondhand) smoking has been linked to pancreatic cancer risk.
The use of smokeless tobacco products has been linked to pancreatic cancer risk.
Nutritional and dietary factors related to pancreatic cancer include obesity, low physical activity, high intake of (saturated) fats, and low intake of vegetables and fruits.
A positive association between alcohol consumption and pancreatic cancer has been reported in some studies.
The current evidence is consistent with a possible weak effect of heavy alcohol consumption on pancreatic cancer risk.
Several medical conditions are associated with subsequent risk of pancreatic cancer, most notably diabetes mellitus and chronic pancreatitis.
A history of pancreatitis increases the risk of pancreatic cancer 2-fold to 10-fold.
The risk of pancreatic cancer is particularly high in individuals with hereditary pancreatitis.
An increased risk of pancreatic cancer has been shown in several studies of patients with diabetes mellitus.
The relative risk of pancreatic cancer in patients with diabetes mellitus is likely to fall in the range 1.5-2.
Although studies are conflicting, gastrectomy patients may have an increased risk of pancreatic cancer.

Some features of the descriptive epidemiology of pancreatic cancer, such as a high incidence among Black people in the USA but a low incidence in Africa, and a higher risk among men and urban residents, can be explained by differences in smoking habits, diabetes, and obesity.
Genome-wide association studies have linked variants in the ABO locus to pancreatic cancer susceptibility.
Individuals with type O blood were found to have a lower risk of pancreatic cancer than those with type A or B.
Worldwide, 458,918 new cases of PDAC were estimated in 2018.
The age-adjusted incidence rate of PDAC among both sexes is 6.2 cases per 100,000 person-years in higher-income countries.
The age-adjusted incidence rate of PDAC among both sexes is 1.5 cases per 100,000 person-years in lower-income countries.
The highest rates of PDAC have been recorded among black people in the USA, with about 17 cases per 100,000 person-years among men and 14 cases per 100,000 person-years among women.

The lowest rates of PDAC (< 2 cases per 100,000 person-years among men and 1 case per 100,000 person-years among women) have been recorded in India, northern and central Africa, and south-eastern Asia.
Most patients are diagnosed with PDAC at an age of 55-85 years.
The median age at diagnosis of PDAC in the USA is 70 years.
Globally, the male-to-female ratio for PDAC is 1.1:1.
Because of the very poor survival, mortality rates for PDAC are similar to incidence rates, with a mortality-to-incidence ratio of 0.94.
An apparent increase in incidence and mortality of PDAC has occurred since the 1970s, particularly in high-income countries.
The increase in PDAC incidence and mortality in high-income countries can be partially attributed to diagnostic improvements.
Recent reports indicate a steady increase of PDAC incidence rates in Europe and North America, probably as a result of ageing populations and increasing risk factors.
It has been predicted that by 2030, pancreatic cancer will become the second leading cause of cancer-related death in the USA.
Urban populations have higher rates of PDAC than rural populations.
Migrant studies suggest that first-generation migrants from low-risk to high-risk areas experience, after 15 or 20 years, rates of PDAC that are higher than those of the country of emigration.
Migrant studies suggest an important role of environmental exposures occurring late in life in the incidence of PDAC.
Ductal adenocarcinomas are firm and poorly defined yellowish-white masses.
Ductal adenocarcinomas usually do not have haemorrhagic necrosis but occasionally have microcystic or macrocystic areas.
Most resected carcinomas of the pancreas head (60%) are 2-4 cm (mean: 3.5 cm).
Carcinomas of the body/tail are usually somewhat larger than those in the pancreas head.
Cancers < 2 cm are rare (12%).
Carcinomas in the pancreatic head typically lead to stenosis and proximal dilatation of the common bile duct and/or the main pancreatic duct (double duct sign on imaging).

Stenosis and proximal dilatation of the common bile duct and/or the main pancreatic duct causes fibrosclerotic atrophy of the upstream nonneoplastic pancreatic tissue (obstructive chronic pancreatitis).
Most pancreatic carcinomas infiltrate surrounding structures, including the ampulla of Vater, the duodenal wall, peripancreatic and retroperitoneal tissues, and the superior mesenteric vessels.
Carcinomas in the pancreatic body or tail typically do not involve the common bile duct.
Carcinomas in the pancreatic body or tail may infiltrate the stomach wall, left side of the colon, spleen, and left adrenal gland.
Cigarette smoking is one of the leading causes of pancreatic cancer.
Cigarette smoke contains carcinogens that damage DNA such as 4-(methylnitrosamino)-1-(3-pyridyl)-1-butanone (NNK) and benzo[a]pyrene (BaP).
DNA mutations are known to drive the formation of pancreatic cancer.
Longstanding chronic pancreatitis is a risk factor for pancreatic cancer.
Repeated episodes of inflammation, injury, and repair drive neoplasia.
Pancreatic cancer is essentially a genetic disease, caused by inherited (germline) and somatic mutations.
The main somatic genetic drivers of pancreatic cancer were discovered in the late 1980s and the 1990s.
The exomes of pancreatic cancer were first sequenced in 2008.

Several large publicly funded next-generation sequencing efforts, such as the International Cancer Genome Consortium (ICGC) and The Cancer Genome Atlas (TCGA), have further defined the comprehensive molecular landscapes of pancreatic cancer.
Oncogenic mutations of KRAS (chromosome 12p) are one of the most common recurrent abnormalities in the pancreatic cancer genome.
Loss-of-function mutations and/or deletions of the tumour suppressor genes TP53 (chromosome 17p), SMAD4 (DPC4) (chromosome 18q), and CDKN2A (P16) (chromosome 9p) are common in pancreatic cancer.
Oncogenic point mutations of KRAS on codon 12, 13, or 61 are found in >90% of pancreatic cancers.

The resulting constitutive activation of the RAS signalling pathway has a profound impact on cell-autonomous growth and survival, as well as paracrine effects on the tumour microenvironment that enable cancer cells to escape the immune system.
KRAS mutations are one of the earliest genetic alterations in the multistep progression of pancreatic cancer.
Animal models have validated the seminal role of KRAS mutations in pancreatic cancer initiation and maintenance.
The CDKN2A (P16) tumour suppressor gene is inactivated in 40% of pancreatic carcinomas by homozygous deletion.
The CDKN2A (P16) tumour suppressor gene is inactivated in 40% of pancreatic carcinomas by loss of one allele coupled with an intragenic mutation in the other.
The CDKN2A (P16) tumour suppressor gene is inactivated in an additional 15% of pancreatic carcinomas by hypermethylation of the CDKN2A (P16) promoter.
The TP53 gene is inactivated in 75% of pancreatic carcinomas by loss of one allele coupled with an intragenic mutation in the second allele.
The SMAD4 (DPC4) tumour suppressor gene is inactivated in 55% of pancreatic carcinomas.
The SMAD4 (DPC4) tumour suppressor gene is inactivated in 35% of pancreatic carcinomas by homozygous deletion.
The SMAD4 (DPC4) tumour suppressor gene is inactivated in 20% of pancreatic carcinomas by loss of one allele coupled with an intragenic mutation in the second allele.
A large number of genomic 'hills' are altered at a lower prevalence in pancreatic cancer.

Mutations of genes involved in DNA repair, most commonly BRCA2 mutations, but also other somatic mutations such as PALB2, ATM, CHEK2, and RAD51 mutations, can be found in as many as 15-20% of pancreatic cancers.

Pancreatic cancers with defects in DNA repair represent a unique subset that can be targeted with platinum drugs (e.g., cisplatin) or with a new class of agents known as poly (ADP-ribose) polymerase (PARP) inhibitors.
Defects in epigenetic drivers that regulate chromatin accessibility, and therefore gene expression, are found in almost 40% of pancreatic cancers.
Defects in chromatin regulators cause widespread transcriptional deregulation in pancreatic cancer that promotes tumour growth and metastases.
Identifying therapeutic agents that can target pancreatic cancers with these driver mutations remains an area of active investigation.
Carcinomas of ductal lineage with distinct prognostic or molecular features are discussed under the subheading Histological subtypes.

Morphological patterns of conventional ductal adenocarcinomas do not alter prognosis and do not have distinct biological behaviour or clinical symptomatology to warrant classification as a specific subtype.
The large duct pattern of ductal adenocarcinoma is characterized by neoplastic ducts measuring > 0.5 mm and sometimes having a deceptively bland-looking morphology.

The large duct pattern can mimic the branching of an intraductal papillary mucinous neoplasm (IPMN) until one recognizes that the glands are haphazardly arranged and the neoplastic cells are markedly dysplastic.
Ductal adenocarcinoma with a foamy gland pattern is important to recognize.

The neoplastic cells of foamy gland adenocarcinoma have a lacy-looking microvesicular cytoplasm, basally located hyperchromatic (and often raisinoid) nuclei reminiscent of gastric foveolar epithelium, and a brush border-like apical condensation zone at the luminal side of the neoplastic cells.
Foamy gland adenocarcinoma cells look deceptively bland, particularly on frozen sections or small biopsies.
A clear cell subtype of ductal adenocarcinoma that resembles metastatic renal carcinoma has been described.
Some PDACs exhibit a substantially vacuolated pattern.
A cystic papillary pattern, which may be related to the large duct pattern, has been reported.
The cystic papillary pattern should not be mistaken for IPMN because it has the histological features of and behaves similarly to a poorly differentiated ductal adenocarcinoma.
There is no immunohistochemical marker that unequivocally distinguishes ductal adenocarcinoma from extrapancreatic mucin-producing adenocarcinomas, notably bile duct and gastric carcinomas.
CK7, CK8, CK18, and CK19 are consistently expressed in ductal adenocarcinoma.
Markers that are usually positive in ductal adenocarcinoma are CEA, CA19-9, CA125, B72.3, DUPAN-2, EMA (MUC1), and MUC5AC (but not MUC2).
Some markers also label the apical cell membranes of normal duct cells, particularly in chronic pancreatitis.
Ductal adenocarcinomas are usually negative for vimentin and (with rare exceptions) neuroendocrine markers (synaptophysin, chromogranin A) or acinar markers (trypsin, BCL10).
Nuclear expression of SMAD4 (DPC4) is lost in 55% of the carcinomas.
Nuclear expression of p16 (CDKN2A) is lost in 75% of the carcinomas.
p53 expression is altered in 75-80% of the cancers with either nuclear overexpression or no expression (null pattern).

Markers whose overexpression has been reported in PDACs include EGF and its receptor ERBB2 (c-erbB-2, HER2), TGFα and TGF-β, PDGFA and PDGFB, VEGF and its receptors, metallothionein, CD44v6, claudin-4, claudin-18, ANxA8, disintegrin and metalloproteinase domain-containing protein 9 (ADAM9), K homology domain containing protein overexpressed in cancer (KOC), S100A4, S100A6, and S100P.
Ductal adenocarcinoma must be distinguished from advanced chronic pancreatitis and other primary neoplasms of the pancreas.
The grading of ductal adenocarcinoma is based on combined assessment of the degree of glandular differentiation, mucin production, mitotic activity and nuclear features.
If there is intratumoural heterogeneity, the higher grade is assigned.
If only a minor component (< 50% of the tumour) is of higher grade, the higher grade is assigned.
There is a correlation between grade and survival.
Grade is an independent prognostic variable.

Most ductal adenocarcinomas are well to moderately differentiated and form duct-like glandular structures, which haphazardly infiltrate the pancreatic parenchyma and elicit a strong desmoplastic stromal response.

In well-differentiated carcinomas, the duct-like structures, which may be difficult to recognize as neoplastic glands, occur side by side with glands showing features more diagnostic of adenocarcinoma.
Most characteristic are ruptured glands that are partly lined by cellular stroma, into which mucin is leaking.
Rarely, the histology of the carcinoma is dominated by a large duct pattern.
In moderately differentiated adenocarcinomas, the intratumoural heterogeneity is more conspicuous, with abundant glands forming cribriform, papillary, micropapillary, and/or gyriform patterns.
Common to both well-differentiated and moderately differentiated carcinomas is a desmoplastic stroma that encompasses the neoplastic glands, sometimes in a ductocentric pattern.
The stroma is hypovascular and composed of collagen fibres, which are often interspersed with fibroblasts, myofibroblasts, scattered lymphocytes, and macrophages.
The neoplastic cells are columnar to cuboidal and produce mucins that stain with Alcian blue and the PAS stain.
The cytoplasm of the neoplastic cells is eosinophilic but sometimes foamy or even clear.
The nuclei are round to ovoid with little pleomorphism.
Occasionally there are bigger nuclei, 3-4 times the size of non-neoplastic nuclei.
The nucleoli are often distinct, and the mitotic rate is moderate.
Neoplastic glands can infiltrate the peripancreatic fatty tissue (following interlobular septa) and infiltrate pre-existing structures such as nerves, vessels, and ducts.
Some neoplastic glands may lie individually within fatty tissue (naked ducts) or invade the duodenum and the ampulla up to the mucosa.
Perineural invasion is common and occurs within and particularly outside the pancreas, where nerves are abundant.
Perineural invasion is a highly tumour-specific finding; nonneoplastic glandular inclusion in a nerve is exceedingly rare.
Lymphatic invasion is found in the peripancreatic tissue and is associated with lymph node metastasis.
When adenocarcinoma invades veins, it may replace the vascular endothelium and can even mimic PaniN.
Carcinomas may invade back into non-neoplastic ducts, a process called cancerization of the ducts.
This change is usually accompanied by ductocentric desmoplasia, and it can be indistinguishable from high-grade PanIN.
Perineural invasion, venous invasion, and cancerization of the ducts are all pathways by which the invasive carcinoma can reach far beyond the main neoplastic mass.
The infiltrating neoplastic glands may be intimately associated with non-neoplastic islets or single islet cells.
A gland in an islet is not specific for cancer, and only in exceptional cases do the endocrine cells constitute a truly neoplastic component of the ductal carcinoma.

In cases of severe duct obstruction by the carcinoma, there is marked upstream duct dilatation and almost complete fibrotic atrophy of the upstream parenchyma, often with intense non-neoplastic clustering (aggregation) of the remaining islets.
Poorly differentiated ductal adenocarcinomas are heterogeneous, and they are composed of solid or cribriform cell sheets and individual pleomorphic cells embedded in loosely arranged stroma.
Foci of necrosis and haemorrhage may occur.
The pleomorphic neoplastic cells (occasionally with squamoid or spindle cell differentiation) are not well polarized, produce little or no mucin, and have many mitoses.
The neoplastic tissue destroys the parenchyma and may infiltrate widely into the peripancreatic tissue.
Intraductal tumour extension is seen less often than in better-differentiated carcinomas, whereas perineural, lymphatic, and blood vessel invasion are equally prevalent.
Colloid carcinomas are adenocarcinomas in which ≥ 80% of the neoplastic epithelium is suspended in extracellular mucin pools.
Colloid carcinomas tend to be large and well demarcated.
Most colloid carcinomas arise in association with intestinal-type IPMNs.

The epithelium within the mucin of colloid carcinomas is arranged in strips, either free-floating or attached to the surrounding fibrous stroma, as well as in clusters, in glands, and as individual cells.
Some floating cells in colloid carcinomas may be of the signet-ring type.
Neoplastic cells of colloid carcinoma show intestinal differentiation.
There is strong expression of CDX2 and MuC2 in colloid carcinomas, which are not substantially expressed in conventional ductal adenocarcinoma.
In patients with IPMN, neoplastic cells floating in pools of stromal mucin and perineural invasion help distinguish a colloid carcinoma component from stromal mucin spillage.
Colloid carcinomas have a substantially better prognosis than conventional ductal adenocarcinomas.
Colloid carcinomas have a 5-year survival rate > 55%.
Squamous differentiation is uncommon in pancreatic carcinomas and usually occurs in association with conventional ductal adenocarcinoma.
True squamous cell carcinomas show exclusively squamous differentiation, with no evidence of gland formation or mucin production.
The squamous component should account for ≥ 30% of the neoplasm in order for it to qualify as adenosquamous.
Adenosquamous carcinomas account for only 1-4% of exocrine pancreatic malignancies.
Pure squamous cell carcinoma of the pancreas is extremely rare.
A metastasis from another site (e.g., lung) should be excluded if a neoplasm has purely squamous differentiation.
Sampling often reveals glandular differentiation or histochemical evidence of mucin production in a pancreatic carcinoma with predominantly squamous differentiation.
Most adenosquamous carcinomas are infiltrative, yellowish-white to grey, firm masses.
Central necrosis and cystic degeneration are common in adenosquamous carcinomas.
Some adenosquamous carcinomas are deceptively demarcated, accentuating the differential diagnosis with a metastasis from another site.
The adenocarcinoma component forms glandular structures, often with intracellular or luminal mucin.

Squamous differentiation is characterized by solid clusters or sheets of polygonal cells with distinct cellular borders, prominent intercellular junctions, dense eosinophilic cytoplasm, and varying degrees of keratinization.
The squamous component often expresses p63, p40, and low-molecular-weight cytokeratins.
Almost all cases of adenosquamous carcinomas harbor KRAS mutations at codon 12.
Adenosquamous carcinomas show highly enriched TP53 mutations, along with 3p loss and mutations in the RNA surveillance gene UPF1.
Immunohistochemically, adenosquamous carcinomas show loss of p16 (CDKN2A) protein expression, loss of SMAD4 (DPC4) protein, and strong nuclear p53 immunoreactivity.
The molecular signature of adenosquamous carcinomas is similar to that found in PDACs.
Emerging evidence suggests that adenosquamous carcinomas belong to the basal-like genomic subtype of pancreatic carcinoma.
Adenosquamous carcinomas appear to have a worse prognosis than pure ductal adenocarcinomas even when resected, with a median survival time of about 9 months.
The presence of any squamous component in the neoplasm appears to portend a worse prognosis.
Hepatoid carcinoma of the pancreas is an extremely rare malignant epithelial neoplasm.
Hepatoid carcinoma is defined as a carcinoma in which ≥ 50% of the neoplasm displays histological and immunohistochemical evidence of hepatocellular differentiation.
Hepatoid carcinomas are composed of large polygonal cells with abundant eosinophilic cytoplasm.
An associated component of ductal adenocarcinoma may be present in hepatoid carcinomas.
Some hepatoid carcinomas are probably related to acinar cell carcinomas, which can express hepatocellular markers and produce AFP.
AFP is not specific for hepatocellular differentiation and can also be observed in pancreatoblastomas and acinar, neuroendocrine, and ductal neoplasms without hepatoid morphological features.
The expression of hepatocyte-specific antigen (Hep Par-1) can be seen in nonhepatoid neoplasms including intraductal oncocytic papillary neoplasm.
A canalicular pattern of labelling with antibodies to polyclonal CEA and CD10 can be helpful but is not entirely specific.
FISH for albumin and immunohistochemistry for arginase are more reliable markers of hepatocellular differentiation.
A pancreatic metastasis from an occult hepatocellular carcinoma must be excluded before establishing the diagnosis of a hepatoid carcinoma primary in the pancreas.
Data on the prognosis of hepatoid carcinomas are minimal.
Invasive micropapillary carcinoma is an adenocarcinoma in which ≥ 50% of the neoplasm consists of small solid nests of cells suspended within stromal lacunae.

A micropapillary pattern, as originally described in the breast and later in the urothelium, can also be seen in the pancreas, more commonly as a focal finding, which occurs in < 5% of pancreatobiliary type adenocarcinomas.
In some cases, the micropapillary pattern can be the predominant pattern, warranting a diagnosis of invasive micropapillary carcinoma.
Micropapillary carcinomas are often associated with prominent intra-epithelial neutrophilia.
Micropapillary carcinomas behave more aggressively.
Medullary carcinomas are poorly differentiated carcinomas with limited gland formation, a pushing border, and syncytial growth.
Medullary carcinoma is composed of sheets and nests of poorly differentiated epithelial cells that largely lack gland formation.
The periphery of medullary carcinoma is circumscribed, pushing rather than infiltrating the surrounding fibrous stroma.
Intercellular borders in medullary carcinoma are indistinct, producing a syncytial growth pattern.
Tumour-associated inflammatory infiltrates are typical in medullary carcinoma, both within the stroma and admixed with the neoplastic cells (tumour-infiltrating lymphocytes).
Medullary carcinomas express keratin.
Immunohistochemistry may demonstrate the loss of expression of one or more DNA mismatch repair proteins in medullary carcinomas.
Medullary carcinomas are more common in the ampulla and duodenum and are exceedingly uncommon in the pancreas.
Before a diagnosis of medullary carcinoma can be rendered, origin in the adjacent GI tract must be excluded.
Medullary carcinomas may arise sporadically or in the setting of Lynch syndrome.
Medullary carcinomas are often microsatellite-instable.
Medullary carcinomas are wildtype for the KRAS gene.
Some poorly differentiated carcinomas with EBV infection of the neoplastic cells histologically resemble medullary carcinoma.
Despite poor differentiation, the prognosis for patients with medullary carcinomas may be somewhat better than for those with conventional ductal adenocarcinomas.
Based on the presence of microsatellite instability, medullary carcinomas may be responsive to treatment with immunotherapy.
The extremely rare pancreatic signet-ring cell carcinoma shows infiltration of individual poorly cohesive cells or of cords or sheets.
At least 80% of the neoplasm consists of individually arrayed, poorly cohesive cells, often with intracellular mucin vacuoles peripherally displacing the nuclei.
A variable amount of extracellular mucin is usually present in pancreatic signet-ring cell carcinoma.
A carcinoma with signet-ring cell morphology in the pancreas should be presumed to be metastatic from the stomach or breast in particular before it can be classified as primary.
Undifferentiated carcinoma is a malignant epithelial neoplasm in which a substantial component of the neoplasm does not show a definitive direction of differentiation.
Undifferentiated carcinomas typically have a more diffuse sheet-like growth pattern without overt glandular differentiation.
Unlike ductal adenocarcinomas, undifferentiated carcinomas are poorly cohesive and hypercellular, and they often have only scant stroma.
Most undifferentiated carcinomas express vimentin and usually also keratin but not E-cadherin.
The prognosis for undifferentiated carcinoma is extremely poor, with an average survival time of just 5 months.
Anaplastic undifferentiated carcinomas are composed of pleomorphic mononuclear cells admixed with bizarre-appearing giant cells with eosinophilic cytoplasm.
At least 80% of anaplastic undifferentiated carcinoma consists of solid sheets of cells lacking gland formation and showing markedly pleomorphic nuclei.
Keratin expression is typically present in anaplastic undifferentiated carcinomas.
Sarcomatoid undifferentiated carcinomas have cells with spindle cell morphology, which may contain heterologous elements including bone and cartilage.
At least 80% of sarcomatoid undifferentiated carcinoma displays spindle cell features, with or without heterologous differentiation.
Very rare examples of sarcomatoid undifferentiated carcinomas with rhabdoid cells have been described in the pancreas.
Sarcomatoid undifferentiated carcinomas are characterized by diffuse sheets of rhabdoid cells, often with some degree of non-cohesiveness and a myxoid matrix.
Loss of nuclear expression of SMARC1 (INI1), a core subunit of the SWI/SNF chromatin-remodelling complex, is characteristic of sarcomatoid undifferentiated carcinomas.
Carcinosarcomas have a biphasic pattern, containing both sarcomatoid elements (with or without heterologous elements) and components with obvious epithelial morphology.
Each component of a carcinosarcoma should arbitrarily constitute 30% of the neoplasm to qualify as carcinosarcoma.
Each component of a carcinosarcoma is immunophenotypically similar to its pure counterpart.
Rare neoplasms of uncertain clinical significance have been reported.
Carcinomas of probable ductal phenotype include oncocytic carcinoma.
Carcinomas of probable ductal phenotype include non-mucinous, glycogen-poor cystadenocarcinoma.
Carcinomas of probable ductal phenotype include choriocarcinoma.
Carcinomas of probable ductal phenotype include clear cell carcinoma.
Carcinomas of probable ductal phenotype include ciliated cell adenocarcinoma.
Most of these neoplasms are reported to have a distinctive histological appearance.
The clinical and biological significance of these neoplasms is not well defined.
These neoplasms are not considered separate subtypes at this time.
Some of these neoplasms have been reclassified immunohistochemically as other specific types of pancreatic carcinoma.
Some of these neoplasms are regarded as patterns of growth rather than distinct subtypes.
The mean age of patients with an undifferentiated carcinoma with osteoclast-like giant cells is 62 years, but there is a wide range (32-93 years).
claims.section: Undifferentiated carcinoma with osteoclast-like giant cells

claims.claim: This distinctive neoplasm occurs mostly in the pancreas but rarely also in the bile ducts and other organs.
claims.section: Undifferentiated carcinoma with osteoclast-like giant cells

claims.claim: The neoplasm contains three cell types: non-neoplastic osteoclast-like multinucleated giant cells, a mononuclear histiocytic component, and the neoplastic mononuclear cell component.
claims.section: Undifferentiated carcinoma with osteoclast-like giant cells

claims.claim: The multinucleated cells express histiocytic markers and lack epithelial differentiation.
claims.section: Undifferentiated carcinoma with osteoclast-like giant cells

claims.claim: The multinucleated cells are often found in areas adjacent to haemorrhage or necrosis.
claims.section: Undifferentiated carcinoma with osteoclast-like giant cells

claims.claim: The mononuclear histiocytic component may be inconspicuous by routine histology but is abundantly demonstrated by immunohistochemistry for histiocytic markers.
claims.section: Undifferentiated carcinoma with osteoclast-like giant cells

claims.claim: The neoplastic cells vary from spindle-shaped to epithelioid and can be very large and pleomorphic.
claims.section: Undifferentiated carcinoma with osteoclast-like giant cells

claims.claim: The neoplastic cells are usually noncohesive and may be found within the cytoplasm of the osteoclast-like giant cells.
claims.section: Undifferentiated carcinoma with osteoclast-like giant cells

claims.claim: The neoplastic cells can show keratin positivity, but not inevitably.
claims.section: Undifferentiated carcinoma with osteoclast-like giant cells

claims.claim: The neoplastic cells have a high Ki-67 proliferation index and mutations in KRAS and TP53.
claims.section: Undifferentiated carcinoma with osteoclast-like giant cells

claims.claim: The presence of a conventional ductal adenocarcinoma or adenocarcinoma precursor component provides evidence of the relationship of undifferentiated carcinomas with osteoclast-like giant cells to ductal adenocarcinomas.
claims.section: Undifferentiated carcinoma with osteoclast-like giant cells

claims.claim: The clinical behaviour of this tumour type appears to be unpredictable, but many behave unexpectedly well and in fact a substantial proportion of patients are alive after many years.
claims.section: Undifferentiated carcinoma with osteoclast-like giant cells

claims.claim: Grading this tumour on the basis of the predominance of the neoplastic cell component may have prognostic value.
claims.section: Undifferentiated carcinoma with osteoclast-like giant cells

claims.claim: Malignant glands of varying degrees of differentiation haphazardly infiltrate pancreatic and peripancreatic tissue.
Perineural and vascular invasion is desirable for diagnosis.
Recognition of one of the specific subtypes of pancreatic carcinoma is desirable for diagnosis.
Cytology diagnostic of adenocarcinoma is most often obtained by FNA (typically EUS-guided FNA).
Ultrathin needles, which allow for the acquisition of tissue fragments for formalin-fixed, paraffin-embedded cytohistology, have improved diagnostic yield and accuracy of EUS-guided FNA.
Pancreatobiliary duct brushing techniques have also improved.
Brushing cytology is inferior to EUS-guided FNA for the diagnosis of malignancy.
Ancillary tests of bile duct brushing specimens, such as FISH and next-generation sequencing, may add value to cytology for the detection of malignancy.
Ductal adenocarcinomas are distinguished from other solid neoplasms of the pancreas on the basis of both smear pattern and cytomorphology.
The smear pattern of adenocarcinomas is one of scattered cellular glandular clusters admixed with single cells.

The smear pattern of adenocarcinomas contrasts with the diffuse, uniform solid cellular smear pattern produced by the monotonous tumour cells of parenchyma-rich, stroma-poor neoplasms such as acinar cell carcinomas and neuroendocrine tumours (NETs).
Moderately-poorly differentiated adenocarcinomas are recognized by overt features of malignancy.
Nuclei in moderately-poorly differentiated adenocarcinomas are enlarged and hyperchromatic and display irregular nuclear membranes.
The cytoplasm in moderately-poorly differentiated adenocarcinomas ranges from scant and non-mucinous to abundant and mucinous.

Subtypes such as adenosquamous carcinoma, undifferentiated carcinoma, and undifferentiated carcinoma with osteoclast-type giant cells can be recognized by unique cytological features that recapitulate the histology.
Well-differentiated adenocarcinomas are more challenging to diagnose because the aspirated ductal cells can be difficult to distinguish from atypical but reactive glandular cells in pancreatitis.
Well-differentiated adenocarcinomas can be difficult to distinguish from gastrointestinal contamination from the EUS-guided biopsy procedure.

Cytological criteria for well-differentiated adenocarcinoma include irregular spacing of cells in a cohesive group, anisonucleosis of 4:1 in a single group, parachromatin clearing, irregular nuclear membranes, and finely vacuolated lacy-looking cytoplasm.
The consequential loss of the SMAD4 (DPC4) protein is relatively cancer-specific and thus serves as a diagnostic aid in the histopathological evaluation of pancreatic biopsies.
Next-generation DNA sequencing has definitively established the genomic landscape of pancreatic cancer.
Transcriptomic profiling has defined discrete subtypes of pancreatic cancer characterized by distinct gene signatures, therapeutic responsiveness, and natural history.
Pancreatic cancers can be dichotomized into the basal-like subtype (also known as the squamous or quasimesenchymal subtype) and the classic subtype, based on expression patterns in the epithelium.
Two subtypes (normal and activated) have been suggested based on expression pattern restricted to the stroma.

The basal-like epithelial signature and activated stromal signature each tend to be independently associated with more-aggressive disease, reduced response to cytotoxic therapies, and worse survival outcomes than the classic epithelial or normal stromal subtypes of cancer.
Certain genomic alterations (e.g., chromatin driver mutations) are more frequently associated with the aggressive basal-like cancers.
The basal-like subtype demonstrates a particularly immune-suppressive milieu that favours tumour progression.
Each of the two main epithelial subtypes is associated with distinct transcription factor profiles and epigenetic alterations that underlie the defining gene signatures.
The elucidation of distinct transcription factor profiles and epigenetic alterations provides an opportunity for therapeutic targeting of subtype-specific dependencies in pancreatic cancer.
Ductal adenocarcinomas spread early to the retroperitoneal tissues, the various local peripancreatic and more-distal lymph node groups, and the liver.
Lung, bone, and adrenal metastases are mostly seen in advanced tumour stages.
Cerebral metastases are uncommon in ductal adenocarcinomas.
For accurate staging of resected pancreatic carcinomas, the use of protocols that describe standardized processing and sampling of tumour tissue is recommended.
Involvement of the retroperitoneal resection margin is one of the factors determining survival in ductal adenocarcinomas.
Ductal adenocarcinoma is pathologically staged according to the eighth edition (2017) of the Union for International Cancer Control (UICC) TNM classification.
The T category in the UICC TNM classification is based on tumour size and independent of any extrapancreatic tumour extension.
Node-positive disease in ductal adenocarcinoma has been subdivided into N1 and N2, based on the number of positive lymph nodes.
A slightly modified classification is proposed by the Japan Pancreas Society.
Neoadjuvant chemoradiation or chemotherapy is increasingly used as a treatment option in addition to surgical resection for ductal adenocarcinoma.
Several scoring systems have been reported for evaluation of the extent of residual carcinoma in posttherapy resection specimens.

Correlation of the extent of residual carcinoma with survival reveals that patients with < 5% viable residual tumour have much better disease-free and overall survival than those with > 5% viable residual tumour.
Ductal adenocarcinoma is fatal in almost all cases.
The mean survival time of untreated patients with ductal adenocarcinoma is 3-5 months.
The mean survival time after surgical resection of ductal adenocarcinoma is 10-20 months.
Only 10-20% of patients have surgically resectable carcinomas at the time of diagnosis.
The overall 5-year survival rate for ductal adenocarcinoma is 8%.
The 5-year survival rate for patients treated by surgical resection is 15-25%.
Resectability is the most important determinant of prognosis for ductal adenocarcinoma.
70-90% of surgically resected carcinomas recur within 2 years after surgery.
Recurrence of surgically resected carcinomas most commonly occurs locally in the bed of the pancreas and more distantly in the liver.
The peritoneal cavity or lymph nodes are also common sites of recurrence for surgically resected carcinomas.
Neoadjuvant and adjuvant chemotherapy with combinations that include gemcitabine or fluorouracil prolong survival time only slightly.
Stage is the most important prognosticator for ductal adenocarcinoma.
Survival time is longer in patients with carcinomas confined to the pancreas and < 30 mm in greatest dimension than in patients with carcinomas that extend beyond the gland and are > 30 mm in size.
Size is more important than extension beyond the pancreas in the staging system.
Patients with no evidence of residual tumour after resection (R0) have the most favourable prognosis of those who are treated surgically.
Lymph node metastases substantially worsen prognosis.
Lymph node status is a factor not only determining short-term survival (< 5 years) after surgical resection but also predicting long-term survival (≥ 5 years).
The lymph node ratio (the ratio of the number of nodes harboring a metastasis to the total number of nodes examined) is an important predictor of survival after surgery.
It is suggested that at least 12 lymph nodes must be examined for proper staging of node-negative resections.
Carcinomas of the tail of the pancreas tend to present at a more advanced stage than those of the head.
Histological features are not as strong prognosticators as stage.
Tumour grade, mitotic index, and severity of cellular atypia have been correlated with postoperative survival.
Major vessel involvement, vessel invasion, perineural invasion, and resection margin status are also prognostic indicators.
Other reported prognosticators include CA19-9, obesity, race, site of metastases (with lung only being favourable), and SMAD4 gene status of the cancer.
A post-resection nomogram has been developed to predict survival of a patient with pancreatic cancer, accounting for various variables in addition to stage.

comprehensiveness.criterion: Complete Workflow from Presentation to Follow-up
comprehensiveness.explanation: The guideline should cover the entire clinical workflow for PDAC, from initial presentation and imaging to pathology, molecular diagnostics, integrated diagnosis, management, and follow-up. This ensures that all aspects of patient care are addressed, providing a comprehensive framework for clinicians to follow.

The guideline must provide a clear definition of PDAC, including entity boundaries, grading, and accurate ICD-O/ICD-11 coding. This ensures that the disease is correctly identified and classified, facilitating consistent diagnosis and treatment across different healthcare settings.

The guideline should address the various subtypes of PDAC and provide guidance on handling NOS/NEC-style uncertainties. This includes specifying minimum and ideal diagnostic sets to ensure accurate classification and management of different PDAC variants.

Coverage of the anatomic distribution, dissemination patterns, and epidemiological data, including demographic stratification, is essential. This information helps in understanding the disease's prevalence and risk factors, aiding in targeted screening and prevention strategies.

The guideline should comprehensively cover the clinical and imaging phenotypes of PDAC across modalities such as CT, MRI, and PET. This ensures that clinicians have a detailed understanding of the disease's presentation and can accurately diagnose and monitor it.

The guideline must include detailed information on the etiology and mechanisms of PDAC, including germline and syndromic risks, GWAS/SNP evidence, and model systems. This knowledge is crucial for understanding disease pathogenesis and developing targeted therapies.

Comprehensive coverage of macro/micro pathology, immunophenotype, and cytology is necessary. These elements play a critical role in the diagnosis and classification of PDAC, guiding treatment decisions and prognostication.

insight_depth.criterion: Mechanistic Depth Linking Genetics and Phenotype
insight_depth.explanation: The guideline should provide a deep mechanistic understanding of how genetic and epigenetic alterations in PDAC lead to specific phenotypes. This includes linking cell-of-origin hypotheses with observed clinical and pathological features, aiding in targeted therapy development.

insight_depth.criterion: Integration Across Diagnostic Modalities
insight_depth.explanation: High-quality integration of imaging, histology, IHC, and molecular data into a coherent diagnostic framework is essential. This ensures that all available information is used to make accurate and comprehensive diagnoses, improving patient outcomes.

insight_depth.criterion: Transparent Diagnostic Reasoning
insight_depth.explanation: The guideline should outline clear decision points, branching logic, and common pitfalls in the diagnostic process. This transparency helps clinicians navigate ambiguous cases and resolve conflicts between different diagnostic modalities.

insight_depth.criterion: Prognostic and Predictive Stratification Depth
insight_depth.explanation: The guideline should provide detailed stratification of PDAC cases based on prognostic and predictive factors, with biologically plausible rationales. This stratification aids in personalized treatment planning and risk assessment.

insight_depth.criterion: Evidence Synthesis Maturity
insight_depth.explanation: The guideline should demonstrate mature evidence synthesis, acknowledging heterogeneity, limitations, and strength-of-evidence. This ensures that recommendations are based on robust data and are applicable to diverse clinical scenarios.

insight_depth.criterion: Clinical Impact Reasoning
insight_depth.explanation: The guideline should clearly articulate how specific biomarkers or histologic features impact diagnosis, grading, management, and trial eligibility. This reasoning helps clinicians understand the clinical significance of diagnostic findings.

accuracy_standards.criterion: Correct and Current WHO Entity Naming
accuracy_standards.explanation: The guideline must use correct and current WHO entity names and grading conventions, ensuring alignment with major interim updates. This accuracy is crucial for maintaining consistency in diagnosis and treatment across different healthcare settings.

accuracy_standards.criterion: Accurate ICD-O/ICD-11 Coding Usage
accuracy_standards.explanation: The guideline should use accurate ICD-O/ICD-11 coding and clearly handle synonyms and deprecated terms. This ensures that PDAC cases are correctly classified and reported, facilitating epidemiological studies and healthcare planning.

accuracy_standards.criterion: Molecular Terminology Accuracy
accuracy_standards.explanation: The guideline must use precise molecular terminology, including variant and fusion naming, promoter methylation phrasing, and CNV conventions. Accurate interpretation of molecular assays is essential for reliable diagnosis and treatment planning.

accuracy_standards.criterion: Internal Consistency
accuracy_standards.explanation: The guideline should maintain internal consistency, avoiding contradictory thresholds, criteria, or marker interpretations. This consistency ensures that clinicians receive clear and unambiguous guidance, reducing the risk of diagnostic errors.

accuracy_standards.criterion: Diagnostic Accuracy Safeguards
accuracy_standards.explanation: The guideline should address assay limitations, false positives/negatives, pre-analytic variables, sampling bias, and therapy effects. These safeguards help ensure that diagnostic tests are used appropriately and that results are interpreted correctly.

accuracy_standards.criterion: Consistency with Reporting Standards
accuracy_standards.explanation: The guideline should be consistent with widely used reporting standards and best practices for integrated diagnosis narratives. This consistency facilitates communication between healthcare providers and ensures that diagnostic information is conveyed effectively.

readability_utility.explanation: The guideline should be organized logically, with arguments and recommendations progressing in a clinically sensible order. Clear transitions and the absence of circular reasoning enhance readability and ensure that clinicians can easily follow the guidance.

Key claims in the guideline should be supported by concrete quantitative data, such as sample sizes, effect sizes, and confidence intervals. This evidence-based approach enhances the credibility of the guideline and aids in clinical decision-making.

The guideline should provide explicit 'what to do' guidance for diagnosis, reporting, testing strategy, management, and follow-up. This actionability ensures that clinicians can apply the guideline effectively in real-world practice.

The guideline should use tables, figures, and algorithms to present information in a high-yield manner. These visual aids should materially improve decision-making, rather than serving as decorative elements.

The guideline should clearly define terms, thresholds, and required reporting elements for integrated diagnosis. This clarity ensures that all clinicians have a common understanding of key concepts and can report findings consistently.

The guideline should consider resource-stratified pathways, turnaround time awareness, and practical constraints. This feasibility ensures that the guideline can be applied in diverse healthcare settings, from basic to advanced diagnostics.

System.role_and_constraints: You are a medical guideline writer. Based on authoritative literature, write a chapter of an authoritative medical report. You need to follow the chapter format below.

content:
# KSHV/HHV8-associated multicentric Castleman disease

# Definition

Kaposi sarcoma-associated herpesvirus / human herpesvirus 8 (KSHV/HHV8)-associated multicentric Castleman disease (KSHV/HHV8-MCD) is a lymphoproliferative disorder featuring characteristic KSHV/HHV8-infected plasmablasts, idiopathic multicentric Castleman disease (iMCD)-like morphology, and systemic inflammatory symptoms due to proinflammatory hypercytokinaemia.

# ICD-O coding / ICD-11 coding

None

4B2Y Other specified disorders involving the immune system

# Related terminology

Acceptable: HHV8-positive multicentric Castleman disease.
Not recommended: plasmablastic multicentric Castleman disease.

# Subtype(s)

None

# Localization

KSHV/HHV8-MCD involves lymph nodes (most frequently axillary, abdominal, pelvic, mediastinal, and cervical) and the spleen. Bone marrow involvement has been reported [12].

# Clinical features

Gastrointestinal symptoms, autoimmune haemolytic anaemia, or haemophagocytic lymphohistiocytosis. Most patients, even with treatment, experience recurrent episodes of systemic inflammatory symptoms (flares), which correlate with elevations in KSHV/HHV8 viral load and cytokine levels, particularly human interleukin-6 (huIL-6), IL-10, and KSHV/HHV8-associated vIL-6 [3,11,22,19,16,23,27,14,31,21]. The majority have Kaposi sarcoma, and 10–20% have a concurrent or subsequent diagnosis of lymphoma, which is often KSHV/HHV8-related [3,9,11,19,16,23,27,14,26,40,15].

Patients present with fever and other systemic symptoms, including fatigue, weight loss, and myalgias. They exhibit many laboratory abnormalities, including elevated C-reactive protein, anaemia, and hypoalbuminaemia. Nearly all have diffuse lymphadenopathy, and many have hepatosplenomegaly, effusions, and oedema. Some patients have pulmonary and/or

# Imaging

None

# Spread

None

# Epidemiology

The majority (∼80%) of patients with KSHV/HHV8-MCD are HIV positive. In HIV-positive patients, the M:F ratio is 8:1, the median age is about 40-45 years, the HIV viral load is low or undetectable, and the median $\mathsf { C D 4 + }$ cell count is usually 150-300/mL [3,11,22,16,23,27,26,24,10]. HIV negative patients are older (median age is about 65 years) with a lower M:F ratio (2.4:1) [19,16,26]. Most reported cases are in White people; however, KSHV/HHV8-MCD is likely to be underreported in sub-Saharan Africa [16,23,27]. The incidence of KSHV/HHV8-MCD is increasing in HIV-positive patients and is most recently at 8.3 cases per 10,000 patient years [30].

# Etiology

KSHV/HHV8 is the etiological agent, although immunosuppression (e.g. related to HIV infection or immune senescence) and genetic characteristics are additional contributing factors [16,26,2,25].

# Pathogenesis

The pathogenesis of KSHV/HHV8-MCD is complex and incompletely understood. Many of the manifestations are secondary to hypercytokinaemia and B-cell proliferation. A key role of B cells is demonstrated by the effectiveness of B-cell depleting therapies, such as rituximab [11,10,4]. The KSHV/HHV8-encoded vIL-6 is a key cytokine expressed in the infected cells, promoting cell proliferation, differentiation, and survival, as well as angiogenesis and hIL-6 secretion [14,35,36,37]. Other KSHV/HHV8 gene products, including vFLiP, vIRF3/LANA2, and viral microRNAs, impact KSHV/HHV8-infected cell survival, the host immune response, and cytokine production [4,37,39,38]. Expression of other lytic viral genes, in addition to vIL-6, has been reported in KSHV/HHV8-MCD [13]. Furthermore, infected B cells may represent a source of viraemia, contributing to the clinical manifestations [21]. Cellular proteins (such as XBP1, which activates vIL-6 directly) and suboptimal invariant natural killer T-cell response may also play a role in disease development [25,32].

# Macroscopic appearance

The macroscopic appearance is similar to that of iMCD.

# Histopathology

Lymph nodes and splenic white pulp show histological features similar to those seen in iMCD, with most cases exhibiting plasmacytic or mixed patterns (see Table 4.06, p.305, in Tumourlike lesions with B-cell predominance: Introduction). KSHV/HHV8-MCD, in contrast to iMCD, contains a variable number of plasmablasts, which are medium-sized to large cells with one to two nucleoli and a moderate amount of amphophilic cytoplasm. The plasmablasts are located primarily in the mantle zone but may be seen in intrafollicular and perifollicular areas. Plasmablasts may be scattered individually, or occasionally form small clusters or larger aggregates (plasmablastic aggregates, formerly called microlymphomas). Foci of Kaposi sarcoma are often present [9,19,14,33,5,17].

The plasmablasts are immunopositive for latency-associated nuclear antigen (LANA), a product of the KSHV/HHV8 gene ORF73 [9,19,5,8]. A proportion of plasmablasts are positive for vIL-6; expression of other KSHV/HHV8-related proteins, such as vlRF1, has been documented. The plasmablasts are positive for IRF4 (MUM1) and Blimp1 and express monotypic cytoplasmic IgM lambda. They are dim to negative for CD20 and other B-cell markers, negative for T-cell antigens, and they lack CD138 expression [9,19,5,8,18]. Although there is monotypic lambda light chain expression, the plasmablasts are polyclonal B cells according to molecular studies [8]. In general, the KSHV/HHV8-infected plasmablasts are negative for EBV [9,19,5,8]. Interfollicular KSHV/HHV8-negative polytypic plasma cells are numerous and usually IgM-negative [9].

The differential diagnosis includes iMCD, HIV-associated lymphadenopathy (particularly mixed follicular hyperplasia/involution and follicular involution), MCD associated with POEMS syndrome (polyneuropathy, organomegaly, endocrinopathy, myeloma protein, and skin changes), lymphadenopathy associated with autoimmune disorders, classic Hodgkin lymphoma, and non-Hodgkin lymphomas such as follicular lymphoma with small hypocellular follicles [6,29,20]. Clinical correlation, IG and TR gene rearrangement studies, and immunostaining for immunoglobulin light chains, IgM, IgG, IgG4, CD30, and LANA are helpful in separating these lesions.

# Immunophenotype

None

# Differential diagnosis

None

# Cytology

KSHV/HHV8-MCD cannot be diagnosed by FNA with certainty. FNAs can show changes associated with reactive lymph nodes, and there are reports documenting a heterogeneous lymphoid cell population with branching capillaries often closely associated with groups of germinal centre cells [7].

# Diagnostic molecular pathology

Molecular studies are not necessary for the diagnosis.

# Essential and desirable diagnostic criteria

Essential: lymph node showing the histological features of iMCD with KSHV/HHV8-positive plasmablasts (LANA-positive by immunostaining) in a patient with clinical and laboratory findings as outlined in Table 4.06 (p.305) in Tumour-like lesions with B-cell predominance: Introduction.

# Grading / Staging

Not relevant

# Prognosis and prediction

KSHV/HHV8-MCD is a relapsing and remitting disease punctuated by flares, with systemic symptoms, elevated cytokine levels, elevated KSHV/HHV8 viral load, and lymphadenopathy. Patients with KSHV/HHV8-MCD have an increased risk of developing lymphoma; many have other KSHV/HHV8-associated diseases [3,16,23,15,4]. In the past, overall survival was $\leq 2$ years [14,17]. However, with better treatments, particularly rituximab (with liposomal doxorubicin for those with concurrent Kaposi sarcoma) and, in HIV-positive patients, antiretroviral therapy (ART), survival is much improved. Treatment with rituximab (and ART in HIV-positive patients) is also associated with a decreased risk of lymphoma development [11,10,4,28].

comprehensiveness.explanation: Assess if the guideline covers the entire workflow from presentation to follow-up, including clinical features, imaging, pathology, molecular diagnostics, and management strategies. This ensures comprehensive guidance for clinicians managing KSHV/HHV8-MCD.
Evaluate the guideline's definition of KSHV/HHV8-MCD, including boundaries, grading, and ICD-O/ICD-11 coding. Accurate definitions and coding are crucial for standardized diagnosis and reporting.

Check if the guideline thoroughly describes the anatomic distribution, dissemination patterns, and epidemiological data, including demographic stratification. This information is vital for understanding disease prevalence and risk factors.

Assess the guideline's coverage of macroscopic and microscopic pathology, including immunophenotype details. Comprehensive pathology information is essential for accurate diagnosis and differentiation from similar conditions.

Evaluate the guideline's coverage of molecular pathology, including entity-defining alterations and progression-related events. Molecular insights are crucial for precise diagnosis and understanding disease mechanisms.

Check if the guideline provides structured guidance on differential diagnosis, highlighting similarities and distinguishing tests. This helps clinicians differentiate KSHV/HHV8-MCD from other lymphoproliferative disorders.
Assess the guideline's coverage of prognostic factors and treatment evidence, including recurrence and progression context. This information is crucial for patient management and predicting outcomes.

insight_depth.criterion: Mechanistic depth linking genetics and phenotype
insight_depth.explanation: Evaluate the guideline's depth in linking genetic and epigenetic factors to clinical phenotype. Understanding these mechanisms is crucial for developing targeted therapies and improving diagnostic accuracy.

insight_depth.criterion: Integration across imaging, histology, and molecular diagnostics
insight_depth.explanation: Assess the guideline's ability to integrate findings from imaging, histology, and molecular diagnostics into coherent diagnostic reasoning. This integration is essential for accurate and comprehensive diagnosis.

insight_depth.criterion: Transparent diagnostic reasoning and decision points
insight_depth.explanation: Check if the guideline provides clear diagnostic reasoning, including decision points and common pitfalls. Transparency in reasoning helps clinicians make informed decisions and avoid diagnostic errors.

insight_depth.criterion: Prognostic stratification depth with biological rationale
insight_depth.explanation: Evaluate the guideline's depth in prognostic stratification, supported by biological rationale. Clear risk grouping aids in personalized patient management and treatment planning.

insight_depth.criterion: Evidence synthesis maturity and limitations acknowledgment
insight_depth.explanation: Assess the guideline's maturity in synthesizing evidence, acknowledging limitations, and conveying strength-of-evidence. This ensures that recommendations are based on robust and reliable data.

insight_depth.criterion: Clinical impact reasoning for biomarkers and histologic features
insight_depth.explanation: Evaluate the guideline's reasoning on how specific biomarkers and histologic features impact diagnosis, management, and trial eligibility. This reasoning is crucial for translating diagnostic findings into clinical practice.

accuracy_standards.criterion: Correct WHO entity naming and grading conventions
accuracy_standards.explanation: Assess the guideline's adherence to current WHO entity naming and grading conventions. Accurate naming and grading are essential for standardized diagnosis and communication across healthcare settings.

accuracy_standards.criterion: Accurate ICD-O/ICD-11 coding usage
accuracy_standards.explanation: Evaluate the guideline's use of ICD-O/ICD-11 coding, ensuring clarity in handling synonyms and deprecated terms. Precise coding is crucial for accurate disease classification and reporting.

accuracy_standards.criterion: Molecular terminology accuracy and assay interpretation
accuracy_standards.explanation: Check the guideline's accuracy in molecular terminology and assay interpretation, including variant naming and CNV conventions. Correct interpretation is vital for reliable molecular diagnostics.

accuracy_standards.criterion: Internal consistency across document criteria
accuracy_standards.explanation: Assess the guideline for internal consistency, ensuring no contradictory thresholds or marker interpretations. Consistency is crucial for maintaining diagnostic accuracy and reliability.

accuracy_standards.criterion: Diagnostic accuracy safeguards and assay limitations
accuracy_standards.explanation: Evaluate the guideline's inclusion of diagnostic accuracy safeguards, addressing assay limitations and pre-analytic variables. Safeguards help prevent false positives/negatives and ensure reliable results.

accuracy_standards.criterion: Consistency with reporting standards and best practices
accuracy_standards.explanation: Check the guideline's consistency with widely used reporting standards and best practices for integrated diagnosis narratives. Adherence to standards ensures clarity and reliability in diagnostic reporting.

readability_utility.explanation: Assess the guideline's logical coherence and clinical flow, ensuring arguments and recommendations progress in a sensible order. Clear transitions enhance usability and understanding for clinicians.
Evaluate the guideline's use of evidence-backed statements, supported by quantitative data such as sample sizes and effect sizes. Concrete data enhances credibility and aids clinical decision-making.

readability_utility.criterion: Actionability in diagnosis and management guidance
readability_utility.explanation: Check the guideline's actionability, providing explicit guidance for diagnosis, reporting, testing strategy, management, and follow-up. Clear guidance is essential for practical application in clinical settings.

readability_utility.criterion: High-yield presentation with tables and algorithms
readability_utility.explanation: Assess the guideline's use of tables, figures, and algorithms to improve decision-making. High-yield presentation aids in understanding complex information and enhances practical usability.

readability_utility.criterion: Clear definitions and required reporting elements
readability_utility.explanation: Evaluate the guideline's clarity in defining terms, thresholds, and required reporting elements for integrated diagnosis. Clear definitions ensure accurate communication and reporting.
Check the guideline's feasibility across different settings, considering resource-stratified pathways and practical constraints. Feasibility ensures applicability in diverse healthcare environments.

System.role_and_constraints: You are a medical guideline writer. Based on authoritative literature, write a chapter of an authoritative medical report. You need to follow the chapter format below.

content:
# Primary cutaneous marginal zone lymphoma

# Definition

Primary cutaneous marginal zone lymphoma (PCMZL) is an indolent cutaneous B-cell lymphoma composed of neoplastic small B cells, plasma cells, and a variable number of reactive T cells.

# ICD-O coding / ICD-11 coding

9699/3 Primary cutaneous marginal zone lymphoma

2A85.2 Extranodal marginal zone B-cell lymphoma, primary site skin

# Related terminology

Acceptable: primary cutaneous marginal zone lymphoproliferative disorder.

Not recommended: primary cutaneous immunocytoma; primary cutaneous plasmacytoma.

# Subtype(s)

Heavy chain class-switched form $\boldsymbol { \mathrm { l } } \boldsymbol { \mathrm { g } } \boldsymbol { \mathrm { G } } +$ ${ | \mathtt { Q A } + }$ or $\mid \mathrm { g } \in +$ ${ \sim } 90\%$ of cases; non-class-switched form (${ \mathsf { I } } { \mathsf { Q } } { \mathsf { M } } +$ ${ \sim } 10\%$ of cases)

# Localization

The trunk and arms are the most common sites [18,19].

# Clinical features

Patients present with multifocal or (less frequently) solitary red or violaceous plaques or nodules.

# Imaging

None

# Spread

None

# Epidemiology

PCMZL accounts for $30{-}40\%$ of all primary cutaneous B-cell lymphomas. PCMZL mostly affects adults in their fifth or sixth decade of life, with a male preponderance [13].

# Etiology

The etiology of PCMZL is unknown in most cases. It may develop from chronic antigenic stimulation by intradermally applied antigens, such as tattoo pigments, vaccines, and tick-borne bacteria [2,10]. An association with Borrelia burgdorferi infection is found in endemic areas in Europe, but not in the USA or Asia [4,8,17,15]. Patients appear to have an increased frequency of various gastrointestinal disorders (reflux, gastric ulcers, positive Helicobacter serology, irritable bowel disease) and of various autoimmune diseases of uncertain significance [9].

# Pathogenesis

Genetic sequencing studies identified deleterious somatic FAS mutations affecting the death domain of the apoptosis regulating FAS (CD95) protein in $> 60\%$ of cases, suggesting that apoptosis defects underlie the pathogenesis of PCMZL [12]. Somatic mutations have also recurrently been identified in SLAMF1, SPEN, and NCOR2 [12].

Macroscopic appearance Not relevant

# Macroscopic appearance

None

# Histopathology

PCMZL is characterized by a dense dermal infiltrate composed of small lymphocytes, plasma cells, and (in most cases) follicles with reactive germinal centres. The plasma cells are typically located at the periphery of the lymphoid infiltrates or in the subepidermal compartment.

The neoplastic B cells are positive for BCL2, and negative for CD5, CD10, BCL6, and cyclin D1. The reactive germinal centres contain BCL6-positive and BCL2-negative B cells and are highlighted by networks of follicular dendritic cells. Plasma cells show monotypic expression of immunoglobulin light chains in most cases. The heavy chain class-switched form is characterized by the expression of IgG, IgA, or IgE, as well as a high number of T cells, and no expression of CXCR3. The non-class-switched form expresses IgM and CXCR3 and presents with large sheets of B cells and only a small number of T cells [20,6]. The class-switched cases more often have peripherally clustered monotypic plasma cells, in contrast to the scattered plasma cells in the non-class-switched cases [3]. Reactive T cells may be very prominent, occasionally obscuring the neoplastic B cells in the class-switched cases [7]. Clusters of plasmacytoid dendritic cells $( \mathsf { C D 1 2 3 + } )$ are typically found in the periphery of the infiltrates [11]. IgG4 is expressed by plasma cells in $1 3 \mathrm { - } 3 5 \%$ of PCMZLs, but this finding is not associated with signs of IgG4-related disease [1,5]. Prominent monocytoid B cells and IgM expression should raise suspicion for a secondary cutaneous extranodal marginal zone lymphoma.

# Immunophenotype

None

# Differential diagnosis

None

# Cytology

Not applicable

# Diagnostic molecular pathology

Heavy and light chain IG genes are clonally rearranged.

# Essential and desirable diagnostic criteria

Essential: presence of CD5-negative, CD10-negative small B cells; demonstration of monotypic plasma cells, monotypic B cells, and/or clonal IG gene rearrangement; no evidence of extracutaneous disease at the time of diagnosis; exclusion of other cutaneous lymphomas.

Desirable: lesions on trunk or arms; reactive lymphoid follicles in lesion.

# Grading / Staging

Cases are staged using the International Society for Cutaneous Lymphomas (ISCL)/ European Organisation for Research and Treatment of Cancer (EORTC) TNM staging system for primary cutaneous lymphomas other than mycosis fungoides and Sézary syndrome [21].

# Prognosis and prediction

The prognosis is favourable, with a 5-year disease-specific survival rate of >98% [16]. Recurrences are common. Extracutaneous spread rarely occurs (4% of all patients) and is more frequently observed in patients with longstanding multifocal disease, in the non-class-switched form and in PCMZL with transformation [6,14]. Some consider the class-switched cases as a clonal chronic cutaneous lymphoproliferative disorder rather than overt lymphoma [6].

# References

prompt: Splenic B-cell lymphoma with prominent nucleoli

System.role_and_constraints: You are a medical guideline writer. Based on authoritative literature, write a chapter of an authoritative medical report. You need to follow the chapter format below.

content:
# Splenic B-cell lymphoma/leukaemia with prominent nucleoli

# Definition

Splenic B-cell lymphoma/leukaemia with prominent nucleoli (SBLPN) is a splenic B-cell neoplasm with some but not all of the cytomorphological and immunophenotypic features of cells of hairy cell leukaemia (HCL); it lacks BRAF mutation and is resistant to conventional HCL therapy. Characteristically, the cells have a single large nucleolus.

# ICD-O coding / ICD-11 coding

9591/3 Splenic B-cell lymphoma/leukaemia with prominent nucleoli

2A82.3 Splenic B-cell lymphoma or leukaemia, unclassifiable

# Related terminology

Acceptable: splenic B-cell lymphoma/leukaemia, unclassifiable. Not recommended: hairy cell leukaemia variant; B-prolymphocytic leukaemia.

# Subtype(s)

None

# Localization

The spleen, bone marrow, and peripheral blood are involved. Peripheral lymphadenopathy is uncommon. Hepatomegaly is observed in less than one third of patients [1].

In the peripheral blood, the proportion of atypical lymphoid cells ranges from $20\%$ to $95\%$ [16,11,2]. These cells are medium-sized to large and have abundant basophilic or pale cytoplasm with variably defined projections. They typically have a large, prominent single nucleolus, but the nucleolus may be smaller and less conspicuous in some cases. The cells are smaller than those of HCL, but occasionally large cells with a bilobed nucleus can be identified. In contrast to HCL, the cells typically do not have circumferential villous processes.

The bone marrow shows normal or increased cellularity, with a variable (often mild) degree of lymphoid infiltration and mild fibrosis. On the trephine biopsy, the most common pattern of infiltration is interstitial and intrasinusoidal. Cases with a mixed nodular and interstitial pattern occur; a diffuse pattern may be seen in the later stages of the disease. The key diagnostic feature is the cytomorphology of the cells: medium-sized atypical lymphoid cells with relatively abundant cytoplasm, a round nucleus, and a generally prominent nucleolus [11,20].

The spleen shows diffuse involvement of the red pulp with atrophic white pulp. There is prominent intrasinusoidal involvement in the red pulp. Blood lakes may be seen occasionally but are not typical as in HCL or splenic diffuse red pulp small B-cell lymphoma (SDRPL) [9]. The neoplastic cells are medium-sized atypical lymphoid cells with abundant cytoplasm, a round nucleus, and a generally prominent nucleolus.

# Clinical features

The main clinical features are splenomegaly, high lymphocytosis, and cytopenia without monocytopenia. Autoimmune phenomena are rare [1].

# Imaging

None

# Spread

None

# Epidemiology

SBLPN is rare and accounts for approximately $0.4\%$ of chronic lymphoid malignancies [13,14,15], with an incidence of approximately 0.03 cases per 100,000 person-years [21,22]. The disease mostly affects elderly patients (median age: 71 years), with a slight male predominance (M:F ratio: 1.6:1), which is much less marked than that of HCL (M:F ratio: 4:1). Because the definition of SBLPN is evolving, the incidence figures are likely to be imprecise.

# Etiology

The etiology is unknown. There is no evidence suggesting that genetic or environmental factors play a role in its pathogenesis [13].

# Pathogenesis

Because SBLPN is a newly introduced entity absorbing cases previously classified as HCL variant and cases of CD5-negative B-prolymphocytic leukaemia, aspects of pathogenesis need to be revised as the definition evolves. Based on limited data from series on HCL variant and B-prolymphocytic leukaemia, cases are probably biased for their IG gene repertoire, with a predominance of IGHV4-34 gene usage, and most cases carry a relatively low load of somatic hypermutation within the clonotypic rearranged IGHV genes [6]. Complex karyotypes involving 14q32 as well as 8q24, 7p deletion, and trisomy 12 have been reported [23,24]. BRAF p.V600E (NP_004324.2) mutation is absent, but MAP2K1 mutations are present probably in 38–42% of cases [4,19,5,7]. Other genetic events include KMT2C mutations, U2AF1 and CCND3 mutations (~21–24%), and 7q deletions. TP53 mutations and/or 17p deletions and MYC alterations seem to be associated with a high risk [15,8]. Molecular evidence supports the biological distinction from HCL and splenic marginal zone lymphoma (SMZL) [8,25,1,26] (see Table 4.19, p.379, in Splenic B-cell lymphomas and leukaemias: Introduction).

# Macroscopic appearance

The spleen is enlarged and shows a diffuse homogeneous appearance without presence of a micronodular aspect.

# Histopathology

None

# Immunophenotype

The neoplastic cells express monotypic surface immunoglobulin (most frequently IgG, either alone or with another isotype), pan-B-cell antigens (CD19, CD20, and CD22), DBA44, CD11c, CD103, and FMC7, but not HCL markers (CD25, ANXA1, TRAP, and CD123) [11,3,2,9].

# Differential diagnosis

SBLPN should be distinguished from other splenic B-cell lymphomas/leukaemias (HCL, SMZL, and SDRPL). SBLPN characteristically differs from other splenic B-cell lymphomas/leukaemias by its cytological atypia and a generally prominent, single nucleolus. SBLPN can be distinguished from HCL by the immunophenotype and the lack of BRAF mutation. Like patients with SBLPN, patients with SMZL and SDRPL may show intrasinusoidal infiltrates in bone marrow core biopsies; however, intrasinusoidal infiltrates are mixed with other patterns, and in SMZL, an intrasinusoidal pattern is less prominent. Furthermore, the lymphoid cells in SMZL and SDRPL lack the atypia and prominent nucleoli seen in SBLPN (see Table 4.18, p.378, in Splenic B-cell lymphomas and leukaemias: Introduction).

# Cytology

See above.

# Diagnostic molecular pathology

No molecular test is pathognomonic of SBLPN, because its genetics remain to be characterized.

# Essential and desirable diagnostic criteria

Essential: circulating medium-sized lymphoid cells with prominent nucleoli or convoluted nuclei—rare cells in the peripheral blood may show poorly defined cytoplasmic projections, but circumferential fine villous (hairy) projections are absent; presence of B-cell antigens (CD19, CD20, CD79a, or PAX5); absence of the characteristic phenotype of HCL, including expression of CD25, ANXA1, cyclin D1, and TRAP.

Desirable: diffuse involvement of the splenic red pulp with atrophic white pulp, but most cases are diagnosed without a spleen specimen; absence of BRAF mutation.

# Grading / Staging

No staging system is routinely used.

# Prognosis and prediction

The clinical course is variable, with half of the patients dying from unrelated causes. The evolution seems to be more aggressive than that of classic HCL. The median survival time is approximately 9 years, with only $15\%$ of patients surviving for longer than 15 years [11,13,12]. There is limited information on therapy, and treatment responses to a variety of agents have been disappointing. In contrast to classic HCL, patients with splenic B-cell lymphoma/leukaemia with hairy cell features do not usually respond to interferon alfa, and only half of them achieve transient partial responses to the purine analogues pentostatin ($Z^{\prime}$-deoxycoformycin) and cladribine ($\boldsymbol{\mathcal{L}}^{\prime}$-chlorodeoxyadenosine) [16,11,15,20,8]. Splenic B-cell lymphoma/leukaemia with hairy cell features may undergo transformation (reported in $6\%$ of cases), which appears to be more frequent in cases harbouring 17p deletion [17].

# References
