Plasmacytoma, NOS
Solitary plasmacytoma of bone (SPB) is a subtype of plasmacytoma.
Extramedullary plasmacytoma (EMP) is a subtype of plasmacytoma.
SPB mainly arises in bones with active haematopoiesis, affecting the axial skeleton more often than the appendicular skeleton.
The spine is most commonly involved in SPB, followed by pelvis, ribs, skull, and long bones.
EMP most commonly arises in the upper respiratory tract (nasal cavity, paranasal sinuses, nasopharynx, larynx).
EMP rarely involves the lymph nodes.
EMP rarely involves the lungs, gastrointestinal tract, genitourinary tract, and skin.
Patients present with localized pain, swelling, fracture, and/or spinal cord compression.
Approximately half of patients have a serum paraprotein; IgG is most common, followed by IgA, and rarely light chain only.
Rare patients with plasmacytoma have a paraneoplastic syndrome, such as POEMS syndrome (polyneuropathy, organomegaly, endocrinopathy, myeloma protein, and skin changes).
Rare patients with plasmacytoma have TEMPI syndrome (telangiectases, elevated erythropoietin and erythrocytosis, monoclonal gammopathy, perinephric fluid collection, and intrapulmonary shunting).
Rare patients with plasmacytoma have AESOP syndrome (adenopathy and extensive skin patch overlying plasmacytoma).
Radiographically, SPB is a solitary lytic bone lesion.
EMP mainly arises in sites of ongoing antigen exposure.
Rare posttransplantation plasmacytomas are related to immune dysregulation.
Cytogenetic abnormalities in EMP overlap with those of plasma cell myeloma/multiple myeloma.
SPB is a soft lesion.
SPB is a gelatinous lesion.
SPB is a haemorrhagic lesion.
Neoplastic plasma cells grow in sheets.
In bone, neoplastic plasma cells replace marrow and trabeculae, sometimes causing fracture.
The cellular morphology of neoplastic plasma cells varies among cases.
Some neoplastic plasma cells are composed of normal-appearing plasma cells.
Some neoplastic plasma cells are composed of enlarged, atypical plasma cells with distinct nucleoli.
A few neoplastic plasma cells are composed of plasmablasts with large nuclei, vesicular chromatin, and prominent central nucleoli.
Rare cases of neoplastic plasma cells have anaplastic morphology.
Rare cases of neoplastic plasma cells have amyloid deposition.
Dutcher bodies may be seen in neoplastic plasma cells.
The immunophenotype is similar to that of plasma cell myeloma/multiple myeloma, except that expression of cyclin D1 and CD56 is rare or absent in EMP.
IgG is most commonly expressed in EMP, followed by IgA.

The incidence rate of EMP in the USA is 0.09 cases per 100,000 person-years.
About 2-5% of all plasma cell neoplasms are plasmacytomas.
EMP is less common than SPB.

Plasmacytomas affect more men than women with an M:F ratio of approximately 1.5-2:1 for SPB.
Plasmacytomas affect more men than women with an M:F ratio of approximately 2-3:1 for EMP.
Black people are at higher risk than White people for SPB.
Hispanic individuals have an intermediate risk for SPB.
Risk for EMP shows less difference among racial groups.
Patients with plasmacytomas are mostly middle-aged and older adults with a median age of 55-65 years.
The incidence of plasmacytomas increases with age.
Young adults are occasionally affected by plasmacytomas.

comprehensiveness.criterion: Complete Workflow from Presentation to Follow-up
comprehensiveness.explanation: The guideline should cover the entire clinical workflow for plasmacytoma, from initial presentation and imaging to pathology, molecular diagnostics, integrated diagnosis, management, and follow-up. This ensures that all aspects of patient care are addressed, providing a comprehensive framework for clinicians to follow.

The guideline must provide a complete definition of plasmacytoma, including boundaries and subtypes, and use accurate ICD-O/ICD-11 codes. This ensures clarity in diagnosis and facilitates consistent communication across healthcare providers.

The guideline should detail the anatomic distribution, dissemination patterns, and epidemiological data, including demographic stratification. This information is crucial for understanding disease prevalence and risk factors, aiding in accurate diagnosis and targeted interventions.

comprehensiveness.criterion: Comprehensive Clinical and Imaging Phenotype Coverage
comprehensiveness.explanation: The guideline should cover clinical presentations and imaging findings across modalities like CT, MRI, and PET. This ensures that all potential diagnostic scenarios are considered, aiding in accurate and timely diagnosis.

The guideline must include detailed descriptions of macroscopic, microscopic, and immunophenotypic features. This is essential for pathologists to accurately identify plasmacytoma and differentiate it from other neoplasms.

The guideline should cover essential molecular alterations, co-alterations, and progression-related events. This ensures that molecular diagnostics are fully integrated into the diagnostic process, providing a comprehensive understanding of the disease.

The guideline should provide structured guidance on differential diagnosis, highlighting similarities and distinguishing tests. This helps clinicians differentiate plasmacytoma from other similar conditions, ensuring accurate diagnosis and treatment.

insight_depth.criterion: Mechanistic Understanding of Pathogenesis
insight_depth.explanation: The guideline should provide a deep mechanistic understanding of plasmacytoma pathogenesis, linking cell-of-origin hypotheses with genetic and epigenetic alterations. This helps in understanding disease progression and potential therapeutic targets.

insight_depth.criterion: Integration of Imaging, Histology, and Molecular Data
insight_depth.explanation: The guideline should demonstrate high-quality integration of imaging, histology, and molecular data into a coherent diagnostic framework. This layered approach aids in accurate diagnosis and personalized treatment planning.

insight_depth.criterion: Transparent Diagnostic Reasoning
insight_depth.explanation: The guideline should outline clear diagnostic reasoning, including decision points, branching logic, and common pitfalls. This transparency helps clinicians navigate complex cases and resolve conflicts between diagnostic modalities.

insight_depth.criterion: Prognostic and Predictive Stratification
insight_depth.explanation: The guideline should provide detailed prognostic and predictive stratification, with biologically plausible rationale and clear risk grouping. This aids in patient counseling and management decisions.

insight_depth.criterion: Evidence Synthesis and Conclusion Drawing
insight_depth.explanation: The guideline should demonstrate mature evidence synthesis, acknowledging heterogeneity and limitations while conveying the strength of evidence. This ensures that conclusions are well-founded and reliable.

insight_depth.criterion: Clinical Impact of Biomarkers and Features
insight_depth.explanation: The guideline should explain how specific biomarkers or histologic features impact diagnosis, grading, management, and trial eligibility. This helps in translating diagnostic findings into clinical practice.

accuracy_standards.criterion: Correct WHO Entity Naming and Grading
accuracy_standards.explanation: The guideline should use correct and current WHO entity naming and grading conventions, aligning with major interim updates. This ensures consistency with international standards and facilitates accurate diagnosis.

accuracy_standards.criterion: Accurate ICD-O/ICD-11 Coding
accuracy_standards.explanation: The guideline must use accurate ICD-O/ICD-11 coding and handle synonyms and deprecated terms clearly. This ensures precise classification and communication across healthcare systems.

accuracy_standards.criterion: Molecular Terminology and Assay Interpretation
accuracy_standards.explanation: The guideline should use accurate molecular terminology and provide correct assay interpretation. This ensures that molecular diagnostics are reliable and informative for clinical decision-making.

accuracy_standards.criterion: Internal Consistency Across Document
accuracy_standards.explanation: The guideline should maintain internal consistency, avoiding contradictory thresholds, criteria, or marker interpretations. This ensures clarity and reliability of the information provided.

accuracy_standards.criterion: Diagnostic Accuracy Safeguards
accuracy_standards.explanation: The guideline should address assay limitations, false positives/negatives, pre-analytic variables, and sampling bias. This ensures that diagnostic processes are robust and reliable.

accuracy_standards.criterion: Consistency with Reporting Standards
accuracy_standards.explanation: The guideline should align with widely used reporting standards and best practices for integrated diagnosis narratives. This ensures that reports are comprehensive and facilitate effective communication.

readability_utility.explanation: The guideline should be logically organized, with arguments and recommendations progressing in a clinically sensible order. Clear transitions should be used to avoid circular reasoning and enhance understanding.
Key claims in the guideline should be supported with concrete quantitative anchors, such as sample sizes and effect sizes. This ensures that recommendations are evidence-based and credible.
The guideline should provide explicit guidance on diagnosis, reporting, testing strategy, management, and follow-up. This ensures that clinicians can easily implement recommendations in practice.
The guideline should use tables, figures, and algorithms to improve decision-making. These tools should be informative and not merely decorative, enhancing the guideline's practical utility.

The guideline should clearly define terms, thresholds, and required reporting elements for integrated diagnosis. This ensures that all users have a common understanding and can apply the guideline consistently.

The guideline should consider resource-stratified pathways, turnaround time awareness, and practical constraints. This ensures that recommendations are feasible and applicable in diverse healthcare settings.

System.role_and_constraints: You are a medical guideline writer. Based on authoritative literature, write a chapter of an authoritative medical report. You need to follow the chapter format below.

content:
# Rosai-Dorfman disease

# Definition

Rosai-Dorfman disease (RDD) is a histiocytosis characterized by nodal or extranodal accumulation of large, S100-positive histiocytes/macrophages that commonly exhibit emperipolesis.

# ICD-O coding / ICD-11 coding

9749/3 Rosai-Dorfman disease

EK92 Histiocytoses of uncertain malignant potential

# Related terminology

Acceptable: Rosai-Dorfman-Destombes disease.  
Not recommended: sinus histiocytosis with massive lymphadenopathy.

# Subtype(s)

None

# Localization

RDD most frequently involves lymph nodes, particularly in the cervical region. Extranodal disease occurs in 43% of cases, most often involving the head and neck, skin, and CNS [5,20,6,11,21] (see Table 3.01).

# Clinical features

The typical presentation is that of painless, slow-growing lymphadenopathy, with accompanying B symptoms in one third of patients, or symptoms related to involved extranodal sites [5, 1]. Involved lymph nodes can be huge and matted together.

# Imaging

None

# Spread

None

# Epidemiology

The global incidence of RDD is unknown; the annual prevalence in the USA is 1:200,000 [1]. RDD affects all ethnic groups but is more common in patients of African descent. Men are 3.2 times as frequently affected as women, except with cutaneous RDD, which is more prevalent in Asian women [6,11]. Nodal RDD usually arises in the second and third decades of life, whereas extranodal RDD arises in the fifth decade of life [5].

# Etiology

The etiology is unknown. Rare inherited disorders predispose to familial RDD (see Table 3.01), including disorders associated with germline mutations in the nucleoside transporter gene SLC29A3 (H syndrome / Faisalabad histiocytosis), and FAS deficiency with heterozygous germline mutations in FAS (TNFRSF6) (autoimmune lymphoproliferative syndrome) [15,14,12]. These mutations are not known to be associated with sporadic RDD.

# Pathogenesis

The pathogenesis is incompletely understood. Approximately 50% of cases carry gain-of-function mutations in MAPK/ERK pathway genes, including KRAS, NRAS, MAP2K1, ARAF, CSF1R, and (rarely) BRAF p.V600E, suggesting that at least a proportion of cases of RDD represent a neoplastic disorder [4,18,13,7,10,3].

# Macroscopic appearance

Lymph nodes are enlarged and matted, and they often form multinodular masses.

# Histopathology

Lymph nodes show marked sinus expansion by distinctive large histiocytes that possess round nuclei and prominent nucleoli, surrounded by abundant pale cytoplasm with poorly defined borders. Emperipolesis—the presence of intact haematopoietic cells (typically lymphocytes, but also plasma cells, neutrophils, and erythrocytes) within the histiocyte cytoplasm—is characteristic of RDD. Cytological atypia, mitoses, and multinucleation are infrequent. The microenvironment contains small B and T cells and numerous polytypic plasma cells within medullary cords. Neutrophils and neutrophilic microabscesses may be present, and eosinophils are inconspicuous. Capsular and stromal fibrosis may be prominent and can extend into perinodal soft tissue. Advanced cases may show nodal effacement.

For extranodal RDD, histiocytic clusters and the lymphoplasmacytic background impart an alternating light and dark pattern at low magnification, resembling the architectural features of nodal RDD despite an absence of sinuses. Inconspicuous emperipolesis, admixed foamy histiocytes, and stromal fibrosis may be present, making the diagnosis challenging. Perivascular plasma cells are a helpful feature in the diagnosis of extranodal RDD.

# Immunophenotype

RDD histiocytes are immunoreactive for S100, and the emperipolesis phenomenon can be remarkably highlighted. They are positive for OCT2 [5,17], frequently positive for phosphorylated ERK and cyclin D1 [7,2], and variably positive for histiocytic markers such as CD68 and CD163. They lack CD1a and CD207 (langerin) [7,21,5].

# Differential diagnosis

The differential diagnosis includes infectious diseases, IgG4-related disease, and other histiocytoses [23,24,25,26] (see Table 3.02). IgG4-positive plasma cells may be numerous, but other features of IgG4-related disease are usually absent [27]. RDD can also coexist with Langerhans cell histiocytosis or lymphoma [8].

# Cytology

FNAC shows large histiocytes with emperipolesis associated with plasma cells and lymphocytes. S100 positivity is confirmatory [19,16,9].

# Diagnostic molecular pathology

For suspected familial RDD, the workup should include SLC29A3 and FAS (TNFRSF6) germline mutation analysis [14,15,12].

# Essential and desirable diagnostic criteria

Essential: large histiocytes with round nuclei, prominent nucleoli, and abundant pale cytoplasm, often with emperipolesis; abundant plasma cells in background; positive immunostaining for S100, which also highlights the emperipolesis.

Desirable: in diagnostically challenging cases, expression of OCT2 and cyclin D1 in the absence of CD1a, CD207 (langerin), and ALK.

# Grading / Staging

Not applicable

# Prognosis and prediction

The disease often resolves spontaneously. Surgical intervention is warranted if the upper airway, spinal cord, or end organs are compromised. Persistent disease or recurrence may require further treatment. In such patients, MAPK/ERK pathway-targeting MEK inhibitors may be helpful [52]. Immune dysregulation and involvement of select extranodal sites (kidney, tracheobronchial tract/lung, and liver) contribute to an adverse prognosis; disease-specific death is reported in $5 - 11\%$ of patients [5].

# References

comprehensiveness.explanation: Assess the guideline's coverage from initial presentation through to management and follow-up. This includes clinical features, pathology, molecular diagnostics, and treatment strategies. Comprehensive coverage ensures that all aspects of Rosai-Dorfman disease are addressed, facilitating accurate diagnosis and effective management.

Evaluate the guideline's clarity in defining Rosai-Dorfman disease, including ICD-O and ICD-11 coding. Accurate definitions and coding are crucial for consistent diagnosis and research. The guideline should clearly delineate entity boundaries and handle NOS/NEC uncertainties.

Examine the guideline's detail on the anatomic distribution of the disease, dissemination patterns, and demographic stratification. Understanding these aspects aids in recognizing disease patterns and tailoring management to specific populations.

Assess the guideline's coverage of macroscopic and microscopic pathology, including immunophenotype. Comprehensive pathology information is essential for accurate diagnosis and differentiation from other histiocytoses.

Evaluate the guideline's inclusion of molecular alterations, co-alterations, and progression-related events. This includes CNV patterns and epigenetic classes. Comprehensive molecular pathology is vital for understanding disease mechanisms and potential therapeutic targets.

Assess the guideline's coverage of differential diagnoses, including structured guidance on similarities and distinguishing tests. This helps clinicians differentiate Rosai-Dorfman disease from other conditions with overlapping features.

Evaluate the guideline's coverage of prognostic factors and treatment evidence, including recurrence and progression contexts. Comprehensive evidence coverage supports informed clinical decision-making and patient management.

insight_depth.criterion: Mechanistic depth linking genetics and phenotype
insight_depth.explanation: Assess the guideline's depth in linking genetic alterations to the clinical phenotype of Rosai-Dorfman disease. Understanding these mechanisms provides insights into disease pathogenesis and potential therapeutic targets.

insight_depth.criterion: Integration across imaging, histology, and molecular diagnostics
insight_depth.explanation: Evaluate the guideline's ability to integrate findings from imaging, histology, and molecular diagnostics into a coherent diagnostic framework. This integration is crucial for accurate diagnosis and management.

insight_depth.criterion: Transparent diagnostic reasoning and decision points
insight_depth.explanation: Assess the guideline's clarity in outlining diagnostic reasoning, including decision points and branching logic. Transparency in reasoning helps clinicians navigate complex cases and avoid common pitfalls.

insight_depth.criterion: Prognostic and predictive stratification depth
insight_depth.explanation: Evaluate the guideline's depth in stratifying patients based on prognostic and predictive factors. Clear stratification aids in risk assessment and personalized treatment planning.

insight_depth.criterion: Evidence synthesis maturity and limitations acknowledgment
insight_depth.explanation: Assess the guideline's maturity in synthesizing evidence, including acknowledgment of limitations and heterogeneity. Mature evidence synthesis supports robust clinical recommendations.

accuracy_standards.criterion: Correct and current WHO entity naming and conventions
accuracy_standards.explanation: Evaluate the guideline's adherence to current WHO naming conventions and grading standards. Accurate terminology ensures consistency with global standards and facilitates communication among clinicians.

accuracy_standards.criterion: Accurate ICD-O/ICD-11 coding and synonym handling
accuracy_standards.explanation: Assess the guideline's accuracy in using ICD-O/ICD-11 codes and handling synonyms or deprecated terms. Correct coding is essential for epidemiological studies and clinical documentation.

accuracy_standards.criterion: Molecular terminology accuracy and assay interpretation
accuracy_standards.explanation: Evaluate the guideline's accuracy in molecular terminology, including variant naming and assay interpretation. Precise terminology is crucial for correct diagnosis and treatment planning.

accuracy_standards.criterion: Internal consistency and absence of contradictions
accuracy_standards.explanation: Assess the guideline for internal consistency, ensuring no contradictory thresholds or criteria. Consistency is vital for reliable clinical application and avoiding diagnostic errors.

accuracy_standards.criterion: Diagnostic accuracy safeguards and assay limitations
accuracy_standards.explanation: Evaluate the guideline's inclusion of safeguards against diagnostic inaccuracies, such as assay limitations and pre-analytic variables. Safeguards help prevent false positives/negatives and ensure reliable results.

readability_utility.explanation: Assess the guideline's logical organization and flow, ensuring recommendations progress in a clinically sensible order. Coherent flow aids in understanding and applying the guideline in practice.
Evaluate the guideline's use of evidence-backed statements, supported by quantitative data where appropriate. Concrete evidence enhances the credibility and applicability of recommendations.

readability_utility.criterion: Actionable guidance for diagnosis and management
readability_utility.explanation: Assess the guideline's provision of explicit, actionable guidance for diagnosis, reporting, testing strategy, management, and follow-up. Actionable guidance facilitates practical application in clinical settings.
Evaluate the guideline's use of tables, figures, and algorithms that enhance decision-making. High-yield presentation aids in quick reference and application of key concepts.

Assess the guideline's feasibility for implementation across various clinical settings, considering resource availability and practical constraints. Feasibility ensures the guideline's utility in real-world practice.

System.role_and_constraints: You are a medical guideline writer. Based on authoritative literature, write a chapter of an authoritative medical report. You need to follow the chapter format below.

content:
# Langerhans cell histiocytosis

# Definition

Langerhans cell histiocytosis (LCH) is a clonal neoplasm of myeloid dendritic cells that have a Langerhans cell phenotype (CD1a and CD207 expression) and lack high-grade cytological features.

# ICD-O coding / ICD-11 coding

9751/1 Langerhans cell histiocytosis  
9751/3 Langerhans cell histiocytosis, disseminated

2B31.2 Langerhans cell histiocytosis

# Related terminology

Not recommended: Langerhans cell granulomatosis; histiocytosis X; eosinophilic granuloma (solitary lesions); Hand-Schuller-Christian disease (multifocal); Letterer-Siwe disease (disseminated or visceral involvement).

# Subtype(s)

None

# Localization

Single-system LCH (SS-LCH): Occurs in one organ or one system and can be unifocal or multifocal. Commonly there is skin, pituitary, or bone involvement [28].

Multisystem LCH (MS-LCH): Occurs in two or more organ systems. Commonly there is bone, skin, liver, and spleen involvement, with additional extraosseous involvement, including lymph node involvement, visceral mass lesions, and cystic pulmonary nodules. MS-LCH with bone marrow, spleen, or liver involvement is considered high-risk [12].

In both SS-LCH and MS-LCH, the most commonly involved bones include the craniofacial bones, femur, spine, pelvis, and/or ribs.

# Clinical features

SS-LCH in older children and adults manifests with painless to painful osteolytic lesions of the bone cortex and/or vertebra plana of the spine. Adults presenting with cutaneous SS-LCH may go on to develop systemic LCH in $20\%$ of cases [8]. Multifocal involvement of a single system (i.e. skin or bone) may occur.

MS-LCH typically arises in neonates and infants, manifesting with multiple bony lesions, soft tissue masses, skin rash (vesicles/bullae in early infancy, dermatitis until late infancy, and nodules). MS-LCH includes fever, cytopenias, hepatosplenomegaly with jaundice, increased direct hyperbilirubinaemia, and hypoalbuminaemia. Active sites of disease are often PET-avid. Endocrine dysfunction and diabetes insipidus may occur with solitary CNS LCH involvement and in MS-LCH disease. Long-term consequences of LCH-associated neurodegeneration may manifest years later, including progressive ataxia, dysarthria, dysmetria, and learning and psychosocial/behavioural problems, with typical bilateral and symmetrical MRI signal intensity alterations, often in the cerebellum, brainstem, and basal ganglia [15,3,30,31].

# Imaging

None

# Spread

None

# Epidemiology

The annual incidence rate in the paediatric population is 5-9 new cases per 1 million population [14,27], and in adults it is 1-2 new cases per 1 million population. SS-LCH disease of the bone usually occurs in patients aged $> ~ 1$ year, whereas MS-LCH disease with high-risk organ involvement often occurs in the youngest patients (aged $< ~ 1$ year), with nearly all cases arising before the age of 5 years. There is a slight male predominance [14]. The disease is more common among individuals of European descent and Hispanic individuals. LCH can also be associated with Erdheim-Chester disease, either concomitantly or preceding it; in such cases, shared molecular alterations have been reported [32].

# Etiology

Unknown

# Pathogenesis

MAPK pathway gene mutations are present in the majority (> 85%) of cases, with near-universal expression of phosphorylated ERK. The BRAF p.V600E mutations are the most prevalent and carry prognostic significance, especially in MS-LCH [3, 16]. MAP2K1 mutations are the second most frequent, and ARAF mutations are rare; ERBB3, NRAS, and KRAS mutations have been reported in adults [3].

The cell of origin of LCH is closer to a bone marrow-derived myeloid dendritic cell than to an epidermal Langerhans cell, and MAPK pathway activation plays a central role in LCH pathogenesis [2,6,9,23,29,3,19,21,10].

A misguided myeloid differentiation model has been proposed, in which activating MAPK mutations at a pluripotent haematopoietic, tissue-restricted, or local precursor level give rise to high-risk multisystem, multifocal low-risk, or unifocal LCH, respectively [6,3]. These mutations, especially BRAF p.V600E, seem to confer an advantage for tissue site accumulation via disrupted cell migration, apoptosis inhibition, and oncogene-induced senescence, rather than proliferation [17,33]. Late permanent tissue consequences appear related to clonal myeloid-derived histiocytes inciting inflammation and fibrosis at the site (i.e. biliary cirrhosis in the liver, neurodegeneration/gliosis in the brain in older children) and/or activating a hyperinflammatory pathway, with macrophage activation (in the youngest patients with MS-LCH) [34,35]. In adults, SS-LCH of the lung is almost universally related to smoking, and MAPK mutations are prevalent [36]; a new murine model suggests a possible link between smoking and MAPK mutation in the pathogenesis of LCH [20]. Rare familial cases have also been reported [37].

Rare cases of LCH occur in association with or follow follicular lymphoma or lymphoblastic leukaemia (B or T lineage). This is considered a transdifferentiation phenomenon and may be associated with more aggressive behaviour [38,39,40,41]. A small subset of LCH cases, without a known prior lymphoma, have shown clonal IGH, IGK, and/or TR gene rearrangements [41].

# Macroscopic appearance

Not clinically relevant

# Histopathology

LCH is characterized by an accumulation of large (10-25 μm), round to oval histiocytes with a complex nuclear contour that often assumes a nuclear groove, and moderately abundant eosinophilic cytoplasm. Nuclear atypia is minimal, and mitotic activity is variable but without atypical forms. The inflammatory milieu contains eosinophils, lymphocytes, and multinucleated histiocytes (both LCH-type and osteoclastic-type, especially in bone), with rarer neutrophils and plasma cells. Central necrosis is occasionally found and is of no prognostic significance. In active lesions, LCH cells predominate, often with eosinophils. In late lesions, diagnostic cells are decreased and fibrosis is increased.

Pathological descriptions of the neurodegenerative manifestations in LCH are limited [13,22]. Reports describe inflammatory infiltrates (either foamy cell collections containing CD1a $^ +$ cells or perivascular infiltrates of mononuclear cells carrying the BRAF p.V600E mutation), and there may be associated neurodegeneration or demyelination [13,22].

In patients with a previous diagnosis of LCH, tissue sampling demonstrating only histiocytes with a monocyte-macrophage immunophenotype should not be considered burnt-out if BRAF p.V600E mutations are detected in tissue cells or blood, even in the absence of phenotypic LCH cells, as documented in the bone marrow [18] and CNS [22].

The diagnostic ultrastructural hallmark is the Birbeck granule, which is a 200-400 nm long and 33 nm wide cytoplasmic structure with a tennis-racket shape and a zipper-like appearance [7].

# Immunophenotype

LCH cells express CD1a (membranous), S100 (nuclear and cytoplasmic), CD207 (langerin; granular cytoplasmic), and CD68 (Golgi dot-like staining). CD207 (langerin) immunohistochemical stain is the surrogate for Birbeck granules. CD45 expression is low. The Ki-67 proliferation index is variable (typically < ~10% by double-staining). Downstream markers of MAPK activation include cyclin D1 and phosphorylated ERK in LCH cells.

# Differential diagnosis

It is important to distinguish clonal histiocytic lesions from accumulations of reactive histiocytes in other conditions. LCH cells should be distinguished from the inflammatory CD1a+ dendritic cells that have branching morphology. The morphology, immunophenotype, and correct pattern/distribution of involvement at each site or organ system are all important considerations. The differential diagnosis is site-dependent and includes other histiocytic neoplasms, reactive lesions (such as dermatopathic lymphadenopathy for lymph node), osteomyelitis and chondroblastoma (for bone), and other reactive and neoplastic disorders containing high numbers of dendritic/Langerhans cells.

# Cytology

Cytological diagnosis of histiocytic neoplasms should be approached with caution, particularly in the lymph node, because the architecture or pattern of involvement is critical for distinguishing a clonal histiocytic disorder from reactive paracortical histiocytic accumulations. In specific settings, the assessment of cytological samples by FNA may represent a useful additional tool when integrated with other appropriate investigations, including immunostaining and molecular investigations, and interpreted in the correct clinical and radiographic setting. The longitudinal nuclear grooves of the LCH cells are best appreciated with Pap staining [44].

# Diagnostic molecular pathology

Properly validated mutation-specific immunohistochemistry (anti-BRAF VE1 antibody clone) may be used in conjunction with sensitive nucleic acid-based assays for detecting BRAF p.V600E [24,4].

# Essential and desirable diagnostic criteria

Essential: large, round to oval histiocytes with grooved to convoluted nuclei and a Langerhans cell phenotype (CD1a+, CD207+) in the correct histological/anatomical context. Desirable: mutation analysis in the MAPK pathway, with sensitive testing for low-frequency allele detection.

# Grading / Staging

Not relevant

# Prognosis and prediction

Diagnostic and staging evaluation should document the extent of systemic disease involvement after tissue biopsy. Radiographic skeletal survey, 99mTc bone scan, and brain MRI with gadolinium are used in diagnostic staging, but FDG PET-CT imaging is increasingly used for the detection of active lesions, especially in follow-up [1,11,27]. SS-LCH and MS-LCH may be distinguished to assist in the choice of treatment [21,10].

The prognosis is largely dependent on the stage at presentation. SS-LCH has a 99% survival rate, although a subset of patients may subsequently develop permanent consequences or MS-LCH, including hepatic failure and hypothalamic pituitary involvement [28]. MS-LCH with risk-organ involvement has a variable course, with only about 60% of patients achieving no evidence of disease after 1 year on standard LCH-II therapy. In MS-LCH without high-risk organ involvement, there is still about a 37% relapse/reactivation rate after 1 year of standard therapy with vinblastine and prednisone [12].

Although there has been no formal recommendation set forth for upfront molecular testing, emerging evidence shows that BRAF p.V600E is highly associated with an increased risk of relapse, development of MS-LCH [5,16], and adverse clinical outcomes. Irreversible clinical consequences such as diabetes insipidus and neurodegenerative complications may develop, especially in the case of involvement of the base of the skull vault and craniofacial bones [16,15]. Therapy targeted at BRAF p.V600E has shown efficacy as a single agent, and its role in treatment is being further explored [45,46,35,47].

claims.claim: Langerhans cell granulomatosis is not recommended terminology.
Histiocytosis X is not recommended terminology.
Eosinophilic granuloma (solitary lesions) is not recommended terminology.
Hand-Schuller-Christian disease (multifocal) is not recommended terminology.
Letterer-Siwe disease (disseminated or visceral involvement) is not recommended terminology.
Langerhans cell histiocytosis (LCH) is a clonal neoplasm of myeloid dendritic cells.
Langerhans cell histiocytosis (LCH) cells have a Langerhans cell phenotype characterized by CD1a and CD207 expression.
Langerhans cell histiocytosis (LCH) cells lack high-grade cytological features.
9751/1 Langerhans cell histiocytosis
9751/3 Langerhans cell histiocytosis, disseminated
2B31.2 Langerhans cell histiocytosis
SS-LCH in older children and adults manifests with painless to painful osteolytic lesions of the bone cortex and/or vertebra plana of the spine.
Adults presenting with cutaneous SS-LCH may go on to develop systemic LCH in 20% of cases.
Multifocal involvement of a single system (i.e. skin or bone) may occur in SS-LCH.
MS-LCH typically arises in neonates and infants, manifesting with multiple bony lesions, soft tissue masses, skin rash (vesicles/bullae in early infancy, dermatitis until late infancy, and nodules).
MS-LCH includes fever, cytopenias, hepatosplenomegaly with jaundice, increased direct hyperbilirubinaemia, and hypoalbuminaemia.
Active sites of MS-LCH disease are often PET-avid.
Endocrine dysfunction and diabetes insipidus may occur with solitary CNS LCH involvement and in MS-LCH disease.
Long-term consequences of LCH-associated neurodegeneration may manifest years later, including progressive ataxia, dysarthria, dysmetria, and learning and psychosocial/behavioural problems.
Typical bilateral and symmetrical MRI signal intensity alterations often occur in the cerebellum, brainstem, and basal ganglia in LCH-associated neurodegeneration.
Single-system LCH (SS-LCH) occurs in one organ or one system and can be unifocal or multifocal.
Commonly there is skin, pituitary, or bone involvement in SS-LCH.
Multisystem LCH (MS-LCH) occurs in two or more organ systems.
Commonly there is bone, skin, liver, and spleen involvement in MS-LCH.
MS-LCH can have additional extraosseous involvement, including lymph node involvement, visceral mass lesions, and cystic pulmonary nodules.
MS-LCH with bone marrow, spleen, or liver involvement is considered high-risk.
In both SS-LCH and MS-LCH, the most commonly involved bones include the craniofacial bones, femur, spine, pelvis, and/or ribs.
The annual incidence rate in the paediatric population is 5-9 new cases per 1 million population.
In adults, the annual incidence rate is 1-2 new cases per 1 million population.
SS-LCH disease of the bone usually occurs in patients aged > ~ 1 year.
MS-LCH disease with high-risk organ involvement often occurs in the youngest patients (aged < ~ 1 year), with nearly all cases arising before the age of 5 years.
There is a slight male predominance in LCH cases.
The disease is more common among individuals of European descent and Hispanic individuals.
LCH can be associated with Erdheim-Chester disease, either concomitantly or preceding it.
In cases where LCH is associated with Erdheim-Chester disease, shared molecular alterations have been reported.
LCH cells express CD1a (membranous).
LCH cells express S100 (nuclear and cytoplasmic).
LCH cells express CD207 (langerin; granular cytoplasmic).
LCH cells express CD68 (Golgi dot-like staining).
CD207 (langerin) immunohistochemical stain is the surrogate for Birbeck granules.
CD45 expression is low in LCH cells.
The Ki-67 proliferation index is variable in LCH cells (typically < 10% by double-staining).
Downstream markers of MAPK activation include cyclin D1 in LCH cells.
Downstream markers of MAPK activation include phosphorylated ERK in LCH cells.
MAPK pathway gene mutations are present in the majority (>85%) of cases, with near-universal expression of phosphorylated ERK.
The BRAF p.V600E mutations are the most prevalent and carry prognostic significance, especially in MS-LCH.
MAP2K1 mutations are the second most frequent in LCH.
ARAF mutations are rare in LCH.
ERBB3, NRAS, and KRAS mutations have been reported in adults with LCH.
The cell of origin of LCH is closer to a bone marrow-derived myeloid dendritic cell than to an epidermal Langerhans cell.
MAPK pathway activation plays a central role in LCH pathogenesis.

A misguided myeloid differentiation model has been proposed, in which activating MAPK mutations at a pluripotent haematopoietic, tissue-restricted, or local precursor level give rise to high-risk multisystem, multifocal low-risk, or unifocal LCH, respectively.
BRAF p.V600E mutations seem to confer an advantage for tissue site accumulation via disrupted cell migration, apoptosis inhibition, and oncogene-induced senescence, rather than proliferation.
Late permanent tissue consequences appear related to clonal myeloid-derived histiocytes inciting inflammation and fibrosis at the site.
In adults, SS-LCH of the lung is almost universally related to smoking, and MAPK mutations are prevalent.
A new murine model suggests a possible link between smoking and MAPK mutation in the pathogenesis of LCH.
Rare familial cases of LCH have been reported.
Rare cases of LCH occur in association with or follow follicular lymphoma or lymphoblastic leukaemia (B or T lineage).
This association is considered a transdifferentiation phenomenon and may be associated with more aggressive behaviour.
A small subset of LCH cases, without a known prior lymphoma, have shown clonal IGH, IGK, and/or TR gene rearrangements.

LCH is characterized by an accumulation of large (10-25 μm), round to oval histiocytes with a complex nuclear contour that often assumes a nuclear groove, and moderately abundant eosinophilic cytoplasm.
Nuclear atypia in LCH is minimal, and mitotic activity is variable but without atypical forms.
The inflammatory milieu in LCH contains eosinophils, lymphocytes, and multinucleated histiocytes (both LCH-type and osteoclastic-type, especially in bone), with rarer neutrophils and plasma cells.
Central necrosis is occasionally found in LCH and is of no prognostic significance.
In active LCH lesions, LCH cells predominate, often with eosinophils.
In late LCH lesions, diagnostic cells are decreased and fibrosis is increased.
Pathological descriptions of the neurodegenerative manifestations in LCH are limited.
Reports describe inflammatory infiltrates in LCH as either foamy cell collections containing CD1a $^+$ cells or perivascular infiltrates of mononuclear cells carrying the BRAF p.V600E mutation.
There may be associated neurodegeneration or demyelination in LCH.

In patients with a previous diagnosis of LCH, tissue sampling demonstrating only histiocytes with a monocyte-macrophage immunophenotype should not be considered burnt-out if BRAF p.V600E mutations are detected in tissue cells or blood.
The diagnostic ultrastructural hallmark of LCH is the Birbeck granule, which is a 200-400 nm long and 33 nm wide cytoplasmic structure with a tennis-racket shape and a zipper-like appearance.
Properly validated mutation-specific immunohistochemistry (anti-BRAF VE1 antibody clone) may be used in conjunction with sensitive nucleic acid-based assays for detecting BRAF p.V600E.
It is important to distinguish clonal histiocytic lesions from accumulations of reactive histiocytes in other conditions.
LCH cells should be distinguished from the inflammatory CD1a+ dendritic cells that have branching morphology.
The morphology, immunophenotype, and correct pattern/distribution of involvement at each site or organ system are all important considerations.

The differential diagnosis is site-dependent and includes other histiocytic neoplasms, reactive lesions such as dermatopathic lymphadenopathy for lymph node, osteomyelitis and chondroblastoma for bone, and other reactive and neoplastic disorders containing high numbers of dendritic/Langerhans cells.
Cytological diagnosis of histiocytic neoplasms should be approached with caution, particularly in the lymph node.
The architecture or pattern of involvement is critical for distinguishing a clonal histiocytic disorder from reactive paracortical histiocytic accumulations.

In specific settings, the assessment of cytological samples by FNA may represent a useful additional tool when integrated with other appropriate investigations, including immunostaining and molecular investigations.
The longitudinal nuclear grooves of the LCH cells are best appreciated with Pap staining.
Essential: large, round to oval histiocytes with grooved to convoluted nuclei and a Langerhans cell phenotype (CD1a+, CD207+) in the correct histological/anatomical context.
Desirable: mutation analysis in the MAPK pathway, with sensitive testing for low-frequency allele detection.
Radiographic skeletal survey, 99mTc bone scan, and brain MRI with gadolinium are used in diagnostic staging.
FDG PET-CT imaging is increasingly used for the detection of active lesions, especially in follow-up.
SS-LCH and MS-LCH may be distinguished to assist in the choice of treatment.
The prognosis is largely dependent on the stage at presentation.
SS-LCH has a 99% survival rate.
A subset of patients with SS-LCH may subsequently develop permanent consequences or MS-LCH, including hepatic failure and hypothalamic pituitary involvement.
MS-LCH with risk-organ involvement has a variable course, with only about 60% of patients achieving no evidence of disease after 1 year on standard LCH-II therapy.
In MS-LCH without high-risk organ involvement, there is still about a 37% relapse/reactivation rate after 1 year of standard therapy with vinblastine and prednisone.
Emerging evidence shows that BRAF p.V600E is highly associated with an increased risk of relapse, development of MS-LCH, and adverse clinical outcomes.

Irreversible clinical consequences such as diabetes insipidus and neurodegenerative complications may develop, especially in the case of involvement of the base of the skull vault and craniofacial bones.
Therapy targeted at BRAF p.V600E has shown efficacy as a single agent.
The role of therapy targeted at BRAF p.V600E in treatment is being further explored.

comprehensiveness.criterion: Complete Workflow Coverage from Presentation to Follow-up
comprehensiveness.explanation: The guideline should cover the entire clinical workflow for Langerhans cell histiocytosis, from initial presentation through imaging, pathology, molecular diagnostics, integrated diagnosis, management, and follow-up. This ensures that all aspects of patient care are addressed, providing a comprehensive resource for clinicians managing this disease.

The guideline must provide a clear definition of Langerhans cell histiocytosis, including ICD-O and ICD-11 coding. This ensures accurate classification and facilitates communication across healthcare systems, aiding in research and epidemiological tracking.

The guideline should detail the common sites of involvement and patterns of dissemination for LCH, including single-system and multisystem disease. This information is crucial for accurate diagnosis and staging, impacting treatment decisions and prognostication.

comprehensiveness.criterion: Comprehensive Clinical and Imaging Phenotype Coverage
comprehensiveness.explanation: The guideline should describe the clinical manifestations and imaging findings associated with LCH across modalities such as CT, MRI, and PET. This aids in the differential diagnosis and helps clinicians recognize the disease in various clinical contexts.

The guideline should explore the etiology and pathogenesis of LCH, including genetic mutations and molecular pathways involved. Understanding these mechanisms is essential for developing targeted therapies and improving patient outcomes.

The guideline should provide detailed descriptions of the macroscopic and microscopic pathology of LCH, including immunophenotypic characteristics. This information is vital for pathologists to make accurate diagnoses and differentiate LCH from other histiocytic disorders.
The guideline should cover the molecular alterations defining LCH, including BRAF and MAPK pathway mutations. This is crucial for diagnosis, prognostication, and guiding targeted therapy decisions.

insight_depth.criterion: Mechanistic Depth Linking Genetics and Phenotype
insight_depth.explanation: The guideline should provide a deep understanding of how genetic mutations, such as those in the MAPK pathway, lead to the clinical and pathological features of LCH. This insight is crucial for developing targeted therapies and understanding disease progression.

insight_depth.criterion: Integration Across Imaging, Histology, and Molecular Data
insight_depth.explanation: The guideline should demonstrate how to integrate findings from imaging, histology, and molecular diagnostics into a coherent diagnostic framework. This integration is essential for accurate diagnosis and personalized treatment planning.

insight_depth.criterion: Transparent Diagnostic Reasoning and Decision Points
insight_depth.explanation: The guideline should outline clear diagnostic reasoning, including decision points and branching logic. This transparency helps clinicians navigate complex cases and resolve conflicts between different diagnostic modalities.

insight_depth.criterion: Prognostic and Predictive Stratification Depth
insight_depth.explanation: The guideline should provide detailed stratification of patients based on prognostic and predictive factors, with a biologically plausible rationale. This stratification informs treatment decisions and helps predict clinical outcomes.

insight_depth.criterion: Evidence Synthesis Maturity and Limitations
insight_depth.explanation: The guideline should synthesize evidence from various sources, acknowledging heterogeneity and limitations. This maturity in evidence synthesis ensures that recommendations are based on the best available data.

insight_depth.criterion: Clinical Impact Reasoning for Biomarkers
insight_depth.explanation: The guideline should explain how specific biomarkers or histologic features impact diagnosis, management, and trial eligibility. Understanding these impacts is crucial for personalized medicine and improving patient outcomes.

accuracy_standards.criterion: Correct and Current WHO Entity Naming and Grading
accuracy_standards.explanation: The guideline should use the correct and current WHO nomenclature and grading conventions for LCH. Adherence to these standards ensures consistency and accuracy in diagnosis and communication across healthcare systems.

accuracy_standards.criterion: Accurate ICD-O/ICD-11 Coding Usage
accuracy_standards.explanation: The guideline should accurately use ICD-O and ICD-11 codes for LCH, clearly handling synonyms and deprecated terms. This accuracy is essential for proper classification, billing, and epidemiological studies.

accuracy_standards.criterion: Molecular Terminology and Assay Interpretation Accuracy
accuracy_standards.explanation: The guideline should use precise molecular terminology and provide correct interpretation of assays, such as BRAF mutation testing. This accuracy is crucial for diagnosis, treatment planning, and research.

accuracy_standards.criterion: Internal Consistency Across Document
accuracy_standards.explanation: The guideline should maintain internal consistency, avoiding contradictory thresholds, criteria, or marker interpretations. Consistency ensures that the guideline is reliable and trustworthy for clinical use.

accuracy_standards.criterion: Diagnostic Accuracy Safeguards
accuracy_standards.explanation: The guideline should include safeguards for diagnostic accuracy, addressing assay limitations, false positives/negatives, and pre-analytic variables. These safeguards are essential for ensuring reliable and valid diagnostic results.

accuracy_standards.criterion: Consistency with Reporting Standards
accuracy_standards.explanation: The guideline should align with widely used reporting standards and best practices for integrated diagnosis narratives. Consistency with these standards ensures clarity and uniformity in reporting and communication.

readability_utility.explanation: The guideline should be organized in a logical sequence, with clear transitions between sections. This coherence ensures that readers can easily follow the guideline and apply its recommendations in clinical practice.

The guideline should support key claims with quantitative data, such as sample sizes and effect sizes. This evidence-based approach enhances the credibility and reliability of the guideline's recommendations.

readability_utility.criterion: Actionable Guidance for Diagnosis and Management
readability_utility.explanation: The guideline should provide explicit, actionable guidance for diagnosis, testing strategy, management, and follow-up. This actionability ensures that clinicians can effectively implement the guideline in patient care.
The guideline should include high-yield tools, such as tables and algorithms, that improve decision-making. These tools should be practical and enhance the guideline's utility in clinical settings.

The guideline should clearly define terms, thresholds, and required reporting elements for integrated diagnosis. Clarity in definitions ensures accurate communication and application of the guideline.

readability_utility.criterion: Feasibility Across Settings with Resource-Stratified Pathways
readability_utility.explanation: The guideline should consider feasibility across different healthcare settings, providing resource-stratified pathways. This consideration ensures that the guideline is applicable and practical in diverse clinical environments.

System.role_and_constraints: You are a medical guideline writer. Based on authoritative literature, write a chapter of an authoritative medical report. You need to follow the chapter format below.

content:
# Mantle cell lymphoma

# Definition

Mantle cell lymphoma (MCL) is a mature B-cell neoplasm derived from the mantle zone of lymphoid follicles and typically composed of small to medium-sized monomorphic cells expressing CD5, SOX11, and cyclin D1. It is associated with CCND-family rearrangements, most commonly CCND1.

# ICD-O coding / ICD-11 coding

9673/3 Mantle cell lymphoma  
9673/3 Cyclin D1-positive mantle cell lymphoma  
9673/3 Cyclin D1-negative mantle cell lymphoma

2A85.5 Mantle cell lymphoma

# Related terminology

Not recommended: centrocytic lymphoma; malignant lymphoma, lymphocytic intermediate differentiation, diffuse.

# Subtype(s)

Molecular subtypes: cyclin D1-positive MCL; cyclin D1-negative MCL.  
Morphological subtypes: see Table 4.28.

Essential: lymphoma cells of B lineage (positive for CD20 and usually CD5); morphology of classic variant (monomorphic and centrocyte-like) or, less often, variant morphology; cyclin D1 positivity and/or detection of CCND1 rearrangement.

Desirable: SOX11 expression positivity.

Essential: lymphoma cells of B lineage (positive for CD20 and usually CD5); morphology of classic variant (monomorphic and centrocyte-like) or, less often, variant morphology; immunophenotype consistent with MCL, including SOX11 expression; absence of cyclin D1 expression and CCND1 rearrangement.

Desirable: CCND2 rearrangement.

# Localization

MCL usually involves lymph nodes, but extranodal involvement is also common, particularly of the Waldeyer ring, gastrointestinal tract, spleen, and bone marrow. Gastrointestinal tract involvement can manifest with numerous polypoid lesions. Other organs that may be involved include the skin, endocrine glands, lungs, and CNS, most often occurring as relapsing disease.

# Clinical features

Most patients present with peripheral lymphadenopathy. Splenomegaly and bone marrow involvement are common. Gastrointestinal tract involvement may be associated with diarrhoea, weight loss, or bleeding. Rarely, patients with an aggressive histological variant of MCL present with a clinical picture resembling acute lymphoblastic leukaemia or an aggressive lymphoma with profound cytopenias, and/or with organ compression. MCL may rarely present as localized (stage I/II) disease involving extranodal sites, mimicking extranodal marginal zone lymphoma [72].

Patients may present with splenic enlargement and peripheral lymphocytosis without nodal disease or cytopenias, designated leukaemic non-nodal MCL (nnMCL). These cases often follow a more indolent clinical course.

MCL that is detected incidentally during surgery for an unrelated disease, particularly involving the urogenital and digestive tract, often corresponds to in situ mantle cell neoplasm.

# Imaging

None

# Spread

None

# Epidemiology

MCL accounts for $3 - 10\%$ of B-cell lymphomas worldwide. The median age of occurrence is 68-69 years [19]. The M:F ratio is about 3-4:1 [73].

# Etiology

The etiology is unknown. Rare familial clustering has been reported [59].

# Pathogenesis

Most MCL cases carry unmutated or minimally/borderline mutated IGHV genes, whereas a minority have a higher rate of somatic hypermutation, which is typically more often observed in leukaemic nnMCL [29,23,42,47]. MCL displays a distinctive IG gene repertoire, with preferential usage of distinct IGHV genes (IGHV3-21, IGHV4-34, and IGHV1-8). In $10\%$ of MCL cases, stereotyped variable heavy chain CDR3 sequences are expressed, implying an antigen-driven selection for a proportion of MCLs [23,2,68,47].

The t(11;14)(q13;q32) translocation is the genetic hallmark and primary event in the pathogenesis of MCL, present in $\geq 95\%$ of cases [34,61]. This translocation juxtaposes CCND1 to IGH on chromosome 14q32. Occasionally, IGK or IGL serve as the CCND1 translocation partner [53]. In most MCLs, the translocation is acquired at the precursor B-cell stage, mediated by recombination-activating enzymes, but t(11;14) occurs in mature B cells via activation-induced cytidine deaminase in a small subset of cases [41]. CCND1 rearrangement results in overexpression of the cell-cycle regulatory protein cyclin D1 [14,15]. More rarely, aberrant expression of truncated transcripts with a prolonged cyclin D1 half-life occur [52]. These transcripts, caused by genomic rearrangements at the CCND1 $_{3^{\prime}}$ untranslated region or by $3^{\prime}$ untranslated region mutations, are associated with higher proliferation indexes and an inferior prognosis [67]. Constitutive overexpression of cyclin D1 activates the cyclin D1-dependent kinase pathway, which overcomes the cell-cycle suppressive effect of RB1 and p27, promotes cell growth and malignant transformation, and ultimately leads to the development of MCL [28,49]. In the occasional cases of MCL that strongly express cyclin D1 but do not show CCND1 rearrangement by FISH, genomic studies have revealed cryptic rearrangements of IGK or IGL enhancers with CCND1 [45,20,46]. In a small subset of MCL negative for cyclin D1 expression and CCND1 rearrangements (cyclin D1-negative MCL), CCND2 and CCND3 rearrangements have been identified as alternative mechanisms of cell-cycle dysregulation [54].

Clonal B cells with CCND1 rearrangement have been detected in the blood of healthy individuals [32], which, together with results from mouse models, suggest that CCND1 rearrangement alone is insufficient to cause MCL [36,22]. Secondary genomic alterations are present in $>90\%$ of cases and play important roles in pathogenesis [35]. These secondary copy-number changes include gains or amplifications of 3q, 7p, 8q (MYC), 15q, and 18q (BCL2), and losses of 1p, 2q, 6q, 8p, 9p (CDKN2A or CDKN2B, in $10{-}36\%$ of cases), 9q, 10p, 11q (ATM, in $11-57\%$), 13q (RB1, $25\mathrm{-}55\%$), 17p (TP53, in $21-45\%$), and 19p [53,76,50]. SNP array studies have detected copy-number neutral loss of heterozygosity in as many as $60\%$ of cases including the 17p region (TP53) [78,79,53]. Tetraploid chromosome clones have been detected especially in the pleomorphic variant of MCL [44].

Next-generation sequencing studies have shown that MCL displays a complex mutation landscape [25]. ATM is most frequently mutated ($41-61\%$ of cases), followed by TP53 (14-$31\%$), CCND1 ($14-34\%$), KMT2D ($12-23\%$), NSD2 ($10-13\%$), SMARCA4 ($8\%$), UBR5 ($7-18\%$), BIRC3 ($6-10\%$), NOTCH1 ($5-14\%$), S1PR1 ($3-15\%$), and CARD11 ($3-15\%$) [51,50]. These alterations involve genes encoding components of key signalling pathways in MCL pathogenesis, including (but not limited to) cell-cycle regulation (CCND1, RB1), DNA damage response (ATM, TP53), cell proliferation and apoptosis (BCL2, MYC), B-cell receptor / NF$\cdot\kappa\mathsf{B}$ signalling (CARD11/BIRC3/MAP3K14), and epigenetic modifiers (KMT2D, NSD2, SMARCA4). MYC translocations and TP53 mutations are often associated with disease progression [24,64].

Interestingly, the neural transcription factor SOX11 also plays an important oncogenic role in MCL. SOX11 belongs to the SOX gene family, whose members encode transcriptional factors characterized by containing a high mobility group DNA-binding domain [76]. SOX11 belongs to subgroup C, with high homology to SOX4 and SOX12, which are essential in organogenesis and have overlapping roles in neural development and neurite growth. Contrary to SOX4, which is crucial for T- and B-lymphopoiesis, SOX11 is not known to have lymphopoietic functions and is not expressed in normal lymphoid tissues, progenitors, or normal B cells. Nonetheless, SOX11 is overexpressed in in situ mantle cell neoplasm - suggesting it plays a role in the early stage of MCL pathogenesis [7,1]. Although the causes and consequences of SOX11 expression in MCL require further investigation, it has been postulated to play an important role in multiple signalling pathways, including B-cell differentiation, and in modulating the lymphoma microenvironment, cell-cycle progression, mobility/migration, and apoptosis [74,75,76,77]

# Macroscopic appearance

This is dependent on the site of involvement. Multiple polypoid lesions have been described in cases with extensive involvement of the gastrointestinal tract (previously designated multiple lymphomatous polyposis).

# Histopathology

MCL shows diffuse, nodular, and (rarely) mantle zone growth patterns, and it has various cytological variants, including blastoid, pleomorphic, small cell, and marginal zone-like, which may also co-occur. MCL with a mantle zone growth pattern needs to be distinguished from in situ mantle cell neoplasm (see Table 4.27, p.445, in In situ mantle cell neoplasm).

Mainstream MCL cytology is characterized by small to medium-sized cells with slightly irregular nuclear contours and scant cytoplasm. Nuclear chromatin is irregularly dispersed, and nucleoli are inconspicuous. The mitotic rate and proliferation index can vary greatly among cases. Plasmacytic differentiation with mature light chain-restricted plasma cells can rarely occur. Pleomorphic and blastoid variants (see Table 4.28, p.446) are usually associated with increased mitotic and proliferation indexes.

The cytology and growth pattern may vary between primary diagnosis and relapse, with a trend towards more highly proliferative variants at relapse. Occasionally, cases with blastoid or pleomorphic cytology at diagnosis relapse with the more regular MCL morphology.

The microenvironment of MCL is composed of a low to moderate number of admixed T cells, hyalinized small blood vessels, and singly scattered epithelioid histiocytes. A variable number of follicular dendritic cells is present, usually associated with a mantle zone or nodular pattern. Highly proliferative MCL may show a starry-sky pattern due to the presence of tingible-body macrophages. Infiltration of glands, mimicking lymphoepithelial lesions, is rarely seen.

In the bone marrow, the pattern is variable depending on the extent of infiltration, ranging from scattered cells to dense nodular aggregates. Paratrabecular and diffuse patterns can also be seen. In the spleen, MCL infiltrates the white pulp, with variable involvement of the red pulp.

# Immunophenotype

MCLs express pan-B-cell markers (CD19, CD20, CD22, CD79a). The intensity of immunoglobulin expression (most commonly IgM/IgD, and lambda light chain expression more frequent than kappa) is moderate to strong as detected by flow cytometry [60]. CD5, FMC7, and CD43 are usually positive [8,21], whereas expression of CD10 or BCL6 is rare but is more commonly seen in aggressive variants [21,6].

CD23 is weakly positive by flow cytometry in about $10\%$ of cases; CD23 also labels a variable number of follicular dendritic cells within the lymphoma microenvironment [55,21]. An absence of CD200 expression by flow cytometry is helpful in distinguishing MCL from chronic lymphocytic leukaemia [31, 8]. Expression of cyclin D1 is detected in $>95\%$ of MCLs, due to CCND1 rearrangements [10,58]. SOX11 is detectable in $>90\%$ of MCLs and helps in identifying CD5-negative or cyclin D1-negative MCL [65,17,39,16,3]. However, SOX11 is not specific for MCL and may be expressed in other lymphoid neoplasms, such as B-lymphoblastic leukaemia/lymphoma, Burkitt lymphoma, and hairy cell leukaemia [16,83,84]. Of note, SOX11 is often negative in leukaemic nnMCL. Expression of the markers usually found in chronic lymphocytic leukaemia is rare, with LEF1 more likely to be seen in blastoid or pleomorphic MCL, and CD200 in leukaemic nnMCL [8,85,86,87,88].

Cyclin D1-negative MCL lack expression of cyclin D1 and CCND1 rearrangements [89,90]. Approximately half of the cases harbour rearrangements of CCND2, and the remaining cases have other genetic alterations leading to the expression of cyclin D2 or cyclin D3 [20,54,37]. In contrast to cyclin D1, cyclin D2 and cyclin D3 are expressed in normal lymphocytes and, therefore, are also observed in other small B-cell lymphomas originating from lymphocytes of corresponding developmental stages [38]. CD5, SOX11, and p27 expression is useful for identifying cyclin D1-negative MCL [3330,87]. Rare cases of MCL lack cyclin D1 immunoreactivity despite the presence of CCND1 rearrangements. In such cases, lack of immunoreactivity is due to mutations in CCND1 or truncated cyclin D1 mRNA [91,92].

# Differential diagnosis

This includes other small to medium-sized B-cell lymphomas that can express cyclin D1, such as plasma cell myeloma, which also can have CCND1 rearrangements, as well as neoplasms that do not carry CCND1 rearrangements, such as chronic lymphocytic leukaemia (weakly in proliferation centres), diffuse large B-cell lymphoma, and hairy cell leukaemia. Pleomorphic and blastoid variants of MCL must be differentiated from diffuse large B-cell lymphoma, B-lymphoblastic leukaemia/lymphoma, and Burkitt lymphoma, among others.

# Cytology

Distinguishing MCL from other types of small cell lymphoma on the basis of cytomorphology alone, without ancillary investigations, is very difficult [40]. Cytological specimens typically show a monomorphic proliferation of lymphoid cells that are slightly larger and have more open chromatin than normal small lymphocytes. Neoplastic cells often have slightly to prominently irregular nuclei, although occasional cases of MCL have tumour cells with round nuclei. Nucleoli are usually (but not always) inconspicuous [40]. The blastoid variant of MCL is characterized by larger nuclei with fine chromatin, whereas the pleomorphic variant shows large cells with irregular nuclei, and sometimes bizarre cells [18].

FNAB may be a useful technique to establish a diagnosis of MCL, with high sensitivity and specificity, when combined with flow cytometric immunophenotypic analysis, particularly when a cell block is available for immunohistochemistry [18,5]. Evaluation using FISH to detect CCND1 rearrangement can also be helpful for diagnosis [5]. FNAB is particularly useful in the setting of relapsed MCL [18,5].

# Diagnostic molecular pathology

In the context of routine diagnostics, in a neoplasm with appropriate morphology and immunophenotype, demonstration of uniform nuclear overexpression of cyclin D1 is sufficient to confirm the diagnosis [10,58,48]. If the morphology or the immunophenotype deviates from the expected features of MCL, demonstration of CCND1 rearrangement or IGH:CCND1 fusion and an extended immunohistochemistry panel is recommended, to exclude lymphomas that can express cyclin D1 (see Differential diagnosis). In the absence of cyclin D1 expression and CCND1 rearrangements, cyclin D1-negative MCL may be confirmed by testing for CCND2 translocations.

TP53 mutation analysis may help to define a high-risk MCL cohort [26,4].

# Essential and desirable diagnostic criteria

None

# Grading / Staging

Staging is performed according to the Lugano criteria [9] and includes clinical examination, routine laboratory tests (including white blood cell count and LDH levels), bone marrow aspiration / blood smear and biopsy, and immunophenotyping. It includes CT of the head and neck, cervical, thoracic, abdominal, and pelvic regions.

FDG PET might be used at diagnosis for prognosis and staging, although it may be insensitive to gastrointestinal tract involvement. Patients with gastrointestinal symptoms may require endoscopy (upper and lower). CNS investigation is indicated in patients with neurological symptoms and could be considered in patients with aggressive variants.

# Prognosis and prediction

MCL used to be considered an incurable disease. However, with current therapeutic approaches, the median overall survival time has increased dramatically, from 3 years to more than 5-10 years, and some patients may eventually be cured. A variety of prognostic markers have been evaluated, some of which are well established and may be applied in daily practice (see $5 0 \times 4 . 0 1$). Of note, these prognostic biomarkers are established for conventional MCL, but not for in situ mantle cell neoplasm or leukaemic nnMCL. Because of the rarity of cyclin D1-negative MCL, it is uncertain whether prognostic markers may also apply to this subtype. MCLs presenting with isolated or predominant extranodal manifestations are reported to have a more favourable outcome than nodal MCL [72].

The MCL International Prognostic Index (MIPI) [93,94] defines risk groups but so far does not govern treatment approaches for individual patients. Clinical trials using biomarkers to identify and stratify high-risk MCL are currently in progress to establish a future default set of markers for clinical application. Widely available and established biomarkers from retrospective studies are cytomorphology, Ki-67, and p53 staining (see Box 4.01). TP53 mutation analysis may help to define a high-risk MCL cohort [95, 4]. Detailed guidelines for Ki-67 and $\mathsf { p } 5 3$ assessment in MCL have been established [30,4,12,13]. The currently accepted Ki-67 cut-off point for prognostic stratification is $3 0 \%$, with Ki-67 expression $> 3 0 \%$ being associated with worse outcome and is included in the MIPI in a combined index (MIPI-c) [12, 96]. High p53 expression has been defined as at least $5 0 \%$ positive lymphoma cells (uniformly strong nuclear staining) and is associated with a poor overall survival (median: 2 years) [4].

At first diagnosis, MCL is positive for surface B-cell markers (CD20, CD19) that might serve as therapeutic targets. Retesting for these therapeutic targets at relapse is recommended in order to identify mutations associated with drug resistance, for example against BTK inhibitors [11,27] and other drugs [71]. However, there is a lack of formal predictive marker analyses guiding treatment.
