claims.claim: 9673/3 Mantle cell lymphoma
9673/3 Cyclin D1-positive mantle cell lymphoma
9673/3 Cyclin D1-negative mantle cell lymphoma
2A85.5 Mantle cell lymphoma
Centrocytic lymphoma is not recommended terminology.
Malignant lymphoma, lymphocytic intermediate differentiation, diffuse is not recommended terminology.
Mantle cell lymphoma (MCL) is a mature B-cell neoplasm derived from the mantle zone of lymphoid follicles.
Mantle cell lymphoma (MCL) is typically composed of small to medium-sized monomorphic cells expressing CD5, SOX11, and cyclin D1.
Mantle cell lymphoma (MCL) is associated with CCND-family rearrangements, most commonly CCND1.
MCL usually involves lymph nodes.
Extranodal involvement is also common in MCL, particularly of the Waldeyer ring, gastrointestinal tract, spleen, and bone marrow.
Gastrointestinal tract involvement in MCL can manifest with numerous polypoid lesions.
Other organs that may be involved in MCL include the skin, endocrine glands, lungs, and CNS.
Involvement of the skin, endocrine glands, lungs, and CNS in MCL most often occurs as relapsing disease.
Molecular subtypes include cyclin D1-positive MCL and cyclin D1-negative MCL.
Essential features include lymphoma cells of B lineage positive for CD20 and usually CD5.
Essential features include morphology of classic variant which is monomorphic and centrocyte-like.
Essential features include variant morphology which is less often observed.
Essential features include cyclin D1 positivity and/or detection of CCND1 rearrangement.
Desirable feature includes SOX11 expression positivity.
Essential features include immunophenotype consistent with MCL, including SOX11 expression.
Essential features include absence of cyclin D1 expression and CCND1 rearrangement.
Desirable feature includes CCND2 rearrangement.
Most patients present with peripheral lymphadenopathy.
Splenomegaly and bone marrow involvement are common.
Gastrointestinal tract involvement may be associated with diarrhoea, weight loss, or bleeding.

Patients with an aggressive histological variant of MCL may present with a clinical picture resembling acute lymphoblastic leukaemia or an aggressive lymphoma with profound cytopenias, and/or with organ compression.
MCL may rarely present as localized (stage I/II) disease involving extranodal sites, mimicking extranodal marginal zone lymphoma.
Patients may present with splenic enlargement and peripheral lymphocytosis without nodal disease or cytopenias, designated leukaemic non-nodal MCL (nnMCL).
These cases of leukaemic non-nodal MCL often follow a more indolent clinical course.
MCL that is detected incidentally during surgery for an unrelated disease, particularly involving the urogenital and digestive tract, often corresponds to in situ mantle cell neoplasm.
MCL accounts for $3 - 10 \%$ of B-cell lymphomas worldwide.
The median age of occurrence is 68-69 years.
The M:F ratio is about 3-4:1.
The etiology is unknown.
Rare familial clustering has been reported.
Multiple polypoid lesions have been described in cases with extensive involvement of the gastrointestinal tract.
The condition was previously designated multiple lymphomatous polyposis.
MCL shows diffuse, nodular, and (rarely) mantle zone growth patterns.
MCL has various cytological variants, including blastoid, pleomorphic, small cell, and marginal zone-like, which may also co-occur.
MCL with a mantle zone growth pattern needs to be distinguished from in situ mantle cell neoplasm.
Mainstream MCL cytology is characterized by small to medium-sized cells with slightly irregular nuclear contours and scant cytoplasm.
Nuclear chromatin in MCL is irregularly dispersed, and nucleoli are inconspicuous.
The mitotic rate and proliferation index can vary greatly among MCL cases.
Plasmacytic differentiation with mature light chain-restricted plasma cells can rarely occur in MCL.
Pleomorphic and blastoid variants of MCL are usually associated with increased mitotic and proliferation indexes.
The cytology and growth pattern of MCL may vary between primary diagnosis and relapse, with a trend towards more highly proliferative variants at relapse.
Occasionally, cases with blastoid or pleomorphic cytology at diagnosis relapse with the more regular MCL morphology.
The microenvironment of MCL is composed of a low to moderate number of admixed T cells, hyalinized small blood vessels, and singly scattered epithelioid histiocytes.
A variable number of follicular dendritic cells is present in MCL, usually associated with a mantle zone or nodular pattern.
Highly proliferative MCL may show a starry-sky pattern due to the presence of tingible-body macrophages.
Infiltration of glands, mimicking lymphoepithelial lesions, is rarely seen in MCL.
In the bone marrow, the pattern of MCL is variable depending on the extent of infiltration, ranging from scattered cells to dense nodular aggregates.
Paratrabecular and diffuse patterns can also be seen in the bone marrow in MCL.
In the spleen, MCL infiltrates the white pulp, with variable involvement of the red pulp.
MCLs express pan-B-cell markers (CD19, CD20, CD22, CD79a).
The intensity of immunoglobulin expression (most commonly IgM/IgD, and lambda light chain expression more frequent than kappa) is moderate to strong as detected by flow cytometry.
CD5, FMC7, and CD43 are usually positive in MCLs.
Expression of CD10 or BCL6 is rare in MCLs but is more commonly seen in aggressive variants.
CD23 is weakly positive by flow cytometry in about 10% of MCL cases.
CD23 labels a variable number of follicular dendritic cells within the lymphoma microenvironment.
An absence of CD200 expression by flow cytometry is helpful in distinguishing MCL from chronic lymphocytic leukaemia.
Expression of cyclin D1 is detected in >95% of MCLs, due to CCND1 rearrangements.
SOX11 is detectable in >90% of MCLs and helps in identifying CD5-negative or cyclin D1-negative MCL.
SOX11 is not specific for MCL and may be expressed in other lymphoid neoplasms, such as B-lymphoblastic leukaemia/lymphoma, Burkitt lymphoma, and hairy cell leukaemia.
SOX11 is often negative in leukaemic nnMCL.
Expression of the markers usually found in chronic lymphocytic leukaemia is rare in MCL, with LEF1 more likely to be seen in blastoid or pleomorphic MCL, and CD200 in leukaemic nnMCL.
Cyclin D1-negative MCL lack expression of cyclin D1 and CCND1 rearrangements.
Approximately half of the cyclin D1-negative MCL cases harbour rearrangements of CCND2.
The remaining cyclin D1-negative MCL cases have other genetic alterations leading to the expression of cyclin D2 or cyclin D3.
Cyclin D2 and cyclin D3 are expressed in normal lymphocytes and are also observed in other small B-cell lymphomas originating from lymphocytes of corresponding developmental stages.
CD5, SOX11, and p27 expression is useful for identifying cyclin D1-negative MCL.
Rare cases of MCL lack cyclin D1 immunoreactivity despite the presence of CCND1 rearrangements.
Lack of cyclin D1 immunoreactivity in some MCL cases is due to mutations in CCND1 or truncated cyclin D1 mRNA.
Most MCL cases carry unmutated or minimally/borderline-mutated IGHV genes.
A minority of MCL cases have a higher rate of somatic hypermutation, which is typically more often observed in leukemic nnMCL.
MCL displays a distinctive IG gene repertoire, with preferential usage of distinct IGHV genes (IGHV3-21, IGHV4-34, and IGHV1-8).
In 10% of MCL cases, stereotyped variable heavy chain CDR3 sequences are expressed, implying an antigen-driven selection for a proportion of MCLs.
The t(11;14)(q13;q32) translocation is the genetic hallmark and primary event in the pathogenesis of MCL, present in ≥95% of cases.
This translocation juxtaposes CCND1 to IGH on chromosome 14q32.
Occasionally, IGK or IGL serve as the CCND1 translocation partner.
In most MCLs, the translocation is acquired at the precursor B-cell stage, mediated by recombination-activating enzymes.
t(11;14) occurs in mature B cells via activation-induced cytidine deaminase in a small subset of cases.
CCND1 rearrangement results in overexpression of the cell-cycle regulatory protein cyclin D1.
More rarely, aberrant expression of truncated transcripts with a prolonged cyclin D1 half-life occur.

These transcripts, caused by genomic rearrangements at the CCND1 3' untranslated region or by 3' untranslated region mutations, are associated with higher proliferation indexes and an inferior prognosis.

Constitutive overexpression of cyclin D1 activates the cyclin D1-dependent kinase pathway, which overcomes the cell-cycle suppressive effect of RB1 and p27, promotes cell growth and malignant transformation, and ultimately leads to the development of MCL.
In the occasional cases of MCL that strongly express cyclin D1 but do not show CCND1 rearrangement by FISH, genomic studies have revealed cryptic rearrangements of IGK or IGL enhancers with CCND1.

In a small subset of MCL negative for cyclin D1 expression and CCND1 rearrangements (cyclin D1-negative MCL), CCND2 and CCND3 rearrangements have been identified as alternative mechanisms of cell-cycle dysregulation.
Clonal B cells with CCND1 rearrangement have been detected in the blood of healthy individuals.
Results from mouse models suggest that CCND1 rearrangement alone is insufficient to cause MCL.
Secondary genomic alterations are present in >90% of cases and play important roles in pathogenesis.

These secondary copy-number changes include gains or amplifications of 3q, 7p, 8q (MYC), 15q, and 18q (BCL2), and losses of 1p, 2q, 6q, 8p, 9p (CDKN2A or CDKN2B, in 10-36% of cases), 9q, 10p, 11q (ATM, in 11-57%), 13q (RB1, 25-55%), 17p (TP53, in 21-45%), and 19p.
SNP array studies have detected copy-number neutral loss of heterozygosity in as many as 60% of cases including the 17p region (TP53).
Tetraploid chromosome clones have been detected especially in the pleomorphic variant of MCL.
Next-generation sequencing studies have shown that MCL displays a complex mutation landscape.

ATM is most frequently mutated (41-61% of cases), followed by TP53 (14-31%), CCND1 (14-34%), KMT2D (12-23%), NSD2 (10-13%), SMARCA4 (8%), UBR5 (7-18%), BIRC3 (6-10%), NOTCH1 (5-14%), S1PR1 (3-15%), and CARD11 (3-15%).

These alterations involve genes encoding components of key signalling pathways in MCL pathogenesis, including (but not limited to) cell-cycle regulation (CCND1, RB1), DNA damage response (ATM, TP53), cell proliferation and apoptosis (BCL2, MYC), B-cell receptor / NFκB signalling (CARD11/BIRC3/MAP3K14), and epigenetic modifiers (KMT2D, NSD2, SMARCA4).
MYC translocations and TP53 mutations are often associated with disease progression.
The neural transcription factor SOX11 also plays an important oncogenic role in MCL.
SOX11 belongs to the SOX gene family, whose members encode transcriptional factors characterized by containing a high mobility group DNA-binding domain.
SOX11 belongs to subgroup C, with high homology to SOX4 and SOX12, which are essential in organogenesis and have overlapping roles in neural development and neurite growth.
Contrary to SOX4, which is crucial for T- and B-lymphopoiesis, SOX11 is not known to have lymphopoietic functions and is not expressed in normal lymphoid tissues, progenitors, or normal B cells.
SOX11 is overexpressed in in situ mantle cell neoplasm - suggesting it plays a role in the early stage of MCL pathogenesis.

Although the causes and consequences of SOX11 expression in MCL require further investigation, it has been postulated to play an important role in multiple signalling pathways, including B-cell differentiation, and in modulating the lymphoma microenvironment, cell-cycle progression, mobility/migration, and apoptosis.
Other small to medium-sized B-cell lymphomas can express cyclin D1.
Plasma cell myeloma can express cyclin D1 and can have CCND1 rearrangements.
Chronic lymphocytic leukaemia can express cyclin D1 weakly in proliferation centres.
Diffuse large B-cell lymphoma does not carry CCND1 rearrangements.
Hairy cell leukaemia does not carry CCND1 rearrangements.
Pleomorphic and blastoid variants of MCL must be differentiated from diffuse large B-cell lymphoma.
Pleomorphic and blastoid variants of MCL must be differentiated from B-lymphoblastic leukaemia/lymphoma.
Pleomorphic and blastoid variants of MCL must be differentiated from Burkitt lymphoma.
Distinguishing MCL from other types of small cell lymphoma on the basis of cytomorphology alone, without ancillary investigations, is very difficult.
Cytological specimens typically show a monomorphic proliferation of lymphoid cells that are slightly larger and have more open chromatin than normal small lymphocytes.
Neoplastic cells often have slightly to prominently irregular nuclei, although occasional cases of MCL have tumour cells with round nuclei.
Nucleoli are usually (but not always) inconspicuous in MCL.
The blastoid variant of MCL is characterized by larger nuclei with fine chromatin.
The pleomorphic variant of MCL shows large cells with irregular nuclei, and sometimes bizarre cells.

FNAB may be a useful technique to establish a diagnosis of MCL, with high sensitivity and specificity, when combined with flow cytometric immunophenotypic analysis, particularly when a cell block is available for immunohistochemistry.
Evaluation using FISH to detect CCND1 rearrangement can also be helpful for diagnosis of MCL.
FNAB is particularly useful in the setting of relapsed MCL.

In the context of routine diagnostics, in a neoplasm with appropriate morphology and immunophenotype, demonstration of uniform nuclear overexpression of cyclin D1 is sufficient to confirm the diagnosis.

If the morphology or the immunophenotype deviates from the expected features of MCL, demonstration of CCND1 rearrangement or IGH:CCND1 fusion and an extended immunohistochemistry panel is recommended, to exclude lymphomas that can express cyclin D1.
In the absence of cyclin D1 expression and CCND1 rearrangements, cyclin D1-negative MCL may be confirmed by testing for CCND2 translocations.
TP53 mutation analysis may help to define a high-risk MCL cohort.
Staging is performed according to the Lugano criteria [9].
Staging includes clinical examination, routine laboratory tests (including white blood cell count and LDH levels), bone marrow aspiration / blood smear and biopsy, and immunophenotyping.
Staging includes CT of the head and neck, cervical, thoracic, abdominal, and pelvic regions.
FDG PET might be used at diagnosis for prognosis and staging.
FDG PET may be insensitive to gastrointestinal tract involvement.
Patients with gastrointestinal symptoms may require endoscopy (upper and lower).
CNS investigation is indicated in patients with neurological symptoms.
CNS investigation could be considered in patients with aggressive variants.
MCL used to be considered an incurable disease.
With current therapeutic approaches, the median overall survival time for MCL has increased from 3 years to more than 5-10 years.
Some patients with MCL may eventually be cured.
Prognostic biomarkers are established for conventional MCL, but not for in situ mantle cell neoplasm or leukaemic nnMCL.
It is uncertain whether prognostic markers apply to cyclin D1-negative MCL due to its rarity.
MCLs presenting with isolated or predominant extranodal manifestations are reported to have a more favourable outcome than nodal MCL.
The MCL International Prognostic Index (MIPI) defines risk groups but does not govern treatment approaches for individual patients.
Clinical trials using biomarkers to identify and stratify high-risk MCL are currently in progress.
Widely available and established biomarkers from retrospective studies for MCL are cytomorphology, Ki-67, and p53 staining.
TP53 mutation analysis may help to define a high-risk MCL cohort.
Detailed guidelines for Ki-67 and p53 assessment in MCL have been established.
The currently accepted Ki-67 cut-off point for prognostic stratification in MCL is 30%, with Ki-67 expression > 30% being associated with worse outcome.
High p53 expression in MCL is defined as at least 50% positive lymphoma cells and is associated with a poor overall survival (median: 2 years).
At first diagnosis, MCL is positive for surface B-cell markers (CD20, CD19) that might serve as therapeutic targets.
Retesting for therapeutic targets at relapse is recommended to identify mutations associated with drug resistance, for example against BTK inhibitors.
There is a lack of formal predictive marker analyses guiding treatment for MCL.

comprehensiveness.explanation: Assess the guideline's coverage from initial presentation through imaging, pathology, molecular diagnostics, integrated diagnosis, management, and follow-up. This ensures that all stages of MCL care are addressed, providing a comprehensive framework for clinicians.

comprehensiveness.criterion: Definitional and coding completeness for MCL
comprehensiveness.explanation: Evaluate the guideline's provision of clear definitions, entity boundaries, and accurate ICD-O/ICD-11 coding. This is crucial for consistent diagnosis and classification of MCL, including cyclin D1-positive and negative subtypes.

comprehensiveness.criterion: Subtype and continuum handling with diagnostic sets
comprehensiveness.explanation: Examine how the guideline addresses MCL subtypes and continuum, including minimum and ideal diagnostic sets. This is important for recognizing the spectrum of MCL presentations and ensuring accurate diagnosis.

comprehensiveness.criterion: Anatomic distribution and dissemination pattern coverage
comprehensiveness.explanation: Assess the guideline's description of MCL's anatomic distribution and dissemination patterns, including extranodal involvement. This helps in understanding the disease's clinical manifestations and potential complications.

comprehensiveness.criterion: Comprehensive macro/micro pathology and immunophenotype details
comprehensiveness.explanation: Evaluate the guideline's coverage of macro/micro pathology, immunophenotype, and cytology. This is essential for accurate diagnosis and differentiation of MCL from other lymphomas.

Assess the guideline's coverage of entity-defining alterations, co-alterations, and progression-related events. This ensures that all relevant molecular features of MCL are considered in diagnosis and prognosis.
Evaluate the guideline's inclusion of prognostic and predictive factors, including recurrence and progression contexts. This is crucial for risk stratification and treatment planning in MCL.

insight_depth.criterion: Mechanistic depth linking genetics and phenotype
insight_depth.explanation: Assess the guideline's explanation of the mechanistic links between genetic alterations and MCL phenotype. This provides a deeper understanding of disease pathogenesis and informs targeted therapies.

insight_depth.criterion: Integration across imaging, histology, IHC, and molecular diagnostics
insight_depth.explanation: Evaluate how well the guideline integrates findings from imaging, histology, immunohistochemistry, and molecular diagnostics into a coherent diagnostic framework. This is crucial for accurate and comprehensive MCL diagnosis.

insight_depth.criterion: Transparent diagnostic reasoning and decision points
insight_depth.explanation: Examine the guideline's clarity in outlining diagnostic reasoning, decision points, and how to resolve conflicts between modalities. This transparency aids clinicians in making informed diagnostic decisions.

insight_depth.criterion: Prognostic and predictive stratification depth
insight_depth.explanation: Assess the guideline's depth in stratifying MCL patients prognostically and predictively, with biologically plausible rationale. This is important for tailoring treatment and predicting outcomes.

insight_depth.criterion: Evidence synthesis maturity and heterogeneity handling
insight_depth.explanation: Evaluate the guideline's maturity in synthesizing evidence, handling heterogeneity, and acknowledging limitations. This ensures that recommendations are based on robust and comprehensive evidence.

insight_depth.criterion: Clinical impact reasoning for biomarkers and features
insight_depth.explanation: Assess how the guideline explains the clinical impact of specific biomarkers or histologic features on diagnosis, management, and trial eligibility. This is crucial for translating diagnostic findings into clinical practice.

accuracy_standards.criterion: Correct and current WHO entity naming and grading
accuracy_standards.explanation: Ensure the guideline uses correct and current WHO entity naming and grading conventions, aligning with major updates. This is essential for maintaining consistency with international standards.

accuracy_standards.criterion: Accurate ICD-O/ICD-11 coding and synonym handling
accuracy_standards.explanation: Evaluate the guideline's accuracy in ICD-O/ICD-11 coding and handling of synonyms or deprecated terms. This ensures precise classification and communication of MCL diagnoses.

accuracy_standards.criterion: Molecular terminology accuracy and assay interpretation
accuracy_standards.explanation: Assess the guideline's accuracy in molecular terminology, including variant naming and assay interpretation. This is crucial for reliable molecular diagnostics and avoiding misinterpretation.

accuracy_standards.criterion: Internal consistency across thresholds and criteria
accuracy_standards.explanation: Ensure the guideline maintains internal consistency, with no contradictory thresholds, criteria, or marker interpretations. This prevents confusion and ensures reliable application of the guideline.

accuracy_standards.criterion: Diagnostic accuracy safeguards and assay limitations
accuracy_standards.explanation: Evaluate the guideline's inclusion of diagnostic accuracy safeguards, including assay limitations and potential false positives/negatives. This is important for ensuring reliable diagnostic outcomes.

accuracy_standards.criterion: Consistency with reporting standards and best practices
accuracy_standards.explanation: Assess the guideline's consistency with widely used reporting standards and best practices for integrated diagnosis narratives. This ensures that diagnostic reports are clear, comprehensive, and actionable.

readability_utility.explanation: Evaluate the guideline's logical coherence and clinical flow, ensuring that arguments and recommendations progress in a sensible order. This aids clinicians in following the guideline effectively.

Assess the guideline's use of evidence-backed statements, supported by quantitative anchors such as sample sizes and effect sizes. This enhances the credibility and reliability of the guideline's recommendations.

readability_utility.criterion: Actionable guidance for diagnosis and management
readability_utility.explanation: Evaluate the guideline's provision of explicit, actionable guidance for diagnosis, reporting, testing strategy, management, and follow-up. This ensures that clinicians can readily apply the guideline in practice.
Assess the guideline's use of high-yield presentation tools, such as tables, figures, and algorithms, that materially improve decision-making. This enhances the guideline's practical utility.

readability_utility.criterion: Clear definitions and reporting elements for diagnosis
readability_utility.explanation: Ensure the guideline provides clear definitions of terms, thresholds, and required reporting elements for integrated diagnosis. This clarity is crucial for consistent and accurate diagnostic reporting.

Evaluate the guideline's feasibility across diverse clinical settings, considering resource-stratified pathways and practical constraints. This ensures that the guideline can be applied in various healthcare environments.

System.role_and_constraints: You are a medical guideline writer. Based on authoritative literature, write a chapter of an authoritative medical report. You need to follow the chapter format below.

content:
# Burkitt lymphoma

# Definition

Burkitt lymphoma (BL) is a mature aggressive B-cell neoplasm composed of monomorphic, medium-sized cells with basophilic cytoplasm, multiple small nucleoli, a germinal-centre B-cell phenotype, a high proliferation index, and an IG:MYC rearrangement.

# ICD-O coding / ICD-11 coding

9687/3 Burkitt lymphoma

2A85.6 Burkitt lymphoma including Burkitt leukaemia

# Related terminology

Not recommended: Burkitt cell leukaemia; atypical Burkitt lymphoma; Burkitt-like lymphoma.

# Subtype(s)

9687/3 Endemic Burkitt lymphoma  
9687/3 Sporadic Burkitt lymphoma  
9687/3 Immunodeficiency-associated Burkitt lymphoma

9687/3 EBV-associated Burkitt lymphoma  
9687/3 EBV-negative Burkitt lymphoma

Traditional subtypes

Endemic BL; sporadic BL; immunodeficiency-associated BL.

EBV-associated BL; EBV-negative BL.

# Localization

Extranodal sites are most commonly involved. However, there is geographical variation in the sites of involvement. In endemic BL, the mandible and maxilla, as well as other facial bones, were traditionally reported as most frequently involved [5,25]. More recent literature typically describes frequent abdominal presentations [15]. Sporadic BL commonly involves the abdomen, soft tissues of the orbit, Waldeyer ring, gingiva, thyroid gland, ovary, testis, and breast [8,43]. BLs arising in immune deficiency/dysregulation settings more commonly have nodal involvement, although extranodal involvement is also frequent [7]. Patients with any BL subtype are at high risk of CNS disease [52,15]. Although population-based studies show a low incidence of CNS involvement [31], clinical studies from Malawi [54] and the USA [49] suggest its frequency ranges from $10 \%$ to $30 \%$ of cases.

# Clinical features

Patients with BL commonly present with a high tumour burden, widely disseminated disease, and rapidly growing tumours due to the short doubling time of BL cells. Site-specific symptoms include altered vision, nasal obstruction, enlarged tonsil(s), dental/jaw pain (often with loss of teeth), abdominal masses (ileocaecal lesions, retroperitoneal nodes), renal lesions, and testicular masses. Breast involvement is uncommon except in pregnant and lactating women, in whom the disease is usually bilateral. Lymphadenopathy mainly occurs in BL arising in immune deficiency/dysregulation settings. Systemic symptoms occur in a subset of patients. Leukaemic presentation /bone marrow infiltration occurs in as many as $20 \%$ of cases, especially in BL arising in immune deficiency/dysregulation settings [58]; however, it is rare in patients with endemic BL, although its occurrence may be underestimated in limited-resource settings.

# Imaging

None

# Spread

None

# Epidemiology

Three subtypes of BL, historically recognized as endemic, non-endemic (sporadic), and immunodeficiency-associated, have traditionally been used to describe the epidemiology of BL [59]. BL is the most common childhood cancer in many countries in equatorial Africa and in Papua New Guinea, where it is considered endemic and is associated with EBV in $> 90\%$ of the cases [55]. Elsewhere, BL is less common in children, where it is considered sporadic and is associated with EBV in about $20\%$ of the cases [29]. Immunodeficiency-associated BL is more common in the setting of HIV infection than in other forms of immunosuppression [29].

However, the designation of endemic or sporadic BL subtypes is problematic, because it is primarily based on variations in geographical incidence and epidemiology, and there is no molecular pathological definition of each traditional subtype [57]. It is probable that sporadic BL also occurs in Africa and may account for the rare EBV-negative cases of BL reported from that continent [57]. Indeed, recent research data suggest that EBV-positive BL and EBV-negative BL form discrete groups with similar molecular features, regardless of epidemiological context and geographical region [60,1,61,17,45,28]. Thus, EBV may be the defining etiological feature of the subtypes of BL [47], and EBV-positive and EBV-negative BL have been shown to differ in their underlying cell biology and pathogenetic mechanisms, justifying a new approach to their designation [11,22,57,28,47].

BL rates vary widely (2- to 50-fold) in different populations worldwide [18,23]. The highest age-standardized rates (ASRs) of BL are observed in sub-Saharan Africa, Malawi, Uganda, and Cameroon, consistent with the high malaria prevalence there, but rates are lower in subtropical regions of Africa or in countries at high altitude. BL rates are much lower in Central and South America, Europe, North America, and Asia, but variability is observed within regions. BL incidence rates appear lowest in Asia, being 20 times lower in China than in Uganda [23].

In general, the M:F ratio of BL is 2-4:1 [62,30,63,23]. The M:F ratio is highest before puberty and decreases with age [30]. The ratio appears to be higher (as high as 10:1) for tumours involving the face or head structures [31]. In the USA, $50\%$ of all BL cases occur in adults aged 20-59 years, $28\%$ occur in adults aged $\ge 60$ years, and $22\%$ occur in the paediatric age group (0-19 years). Adult BLs represent $1-2\%$ of non-Hodgkin lymphomas in adults or elderly patients [33]. The age-specific incidence of BL shows a bimodal pattern, with distinct peaks at about 10 years and about 60 years [62, 30,63], suggesting that there might be biological differences in BL diagnosed at different ages [45].

Several studies have shown an association between the complexity of infection and BL risk [14,24,6]. According to these recent data, children who are exposed to mixed $P.$ falciparum infection have a higher risk for BL than those exposed to infection of a single $P.$ falciparum variant, which could explain why $P.$ falciparum may be associated with BL in some but not all areas. Children can be repeatedly infected with $P.$ falciparum-associated malaria, resulting in chronic stimulation of B cells, suppression of T-cell immunity, and induction of activation-induced cytidine deaminase (AID), an enzyme associated with the formation of the MYC translocation typical of BL [46]. Consistent with this, EBV-positive BL shows AID-mediated somatic hypermutation with features of antigen selection in their rearranged IG genes [65,17], as well as loss of T-cell responses to the EBV EBNA1 protein in patients with BL [64].

# Etiology

Risk factors for endemic BL are Plasmodium falciparum (malaria) and EBV infections (for details, see Pathogenesis). Factors causing BL in low-incidence areas, such as Europe, Asia, and South America, are not well understood. EBV is reported in at most $20\%$ of sporadic BL cases [29], although the incidence of EBV-positive BL increases with age in central Europe [45]. Genetic factors appear to be important. This is supported by a consistent finding of a high M:F ratio in children, particularly prepubescent children [32]. Other clues about genetic contribution come from reports of familial BL clusters, including two recent cases in whom germline variants in TCF4 and CHD8 were reported [16]. Reports of a high BL risk in rare inherited disorders, including inborn errors of immunity (e.g. X-linked lymphoproliferative syndrome 1 [XLP1], MIM number: 308240; X-linked immunodeficiency with magnesium defect, EBV infection, and neoplasia [XMEN], MIM number: 300853) and DNA repair disorders (e.g. ataxia-telangiectasia, MIM number: 208900) also support a genetic predisposition to (EBV-positive) BL.

BL occurring in HIV-positive individuals tends to present in patients with relatively high CD4 counts and is frequently the first manifestation of HIV infection. As a consequence, the widespread introduction of combination antiretroviral therapy (cART) has had less effect on the incidence of BL than on the incidence of other types of lymphoma [44]. The association with EBV in this special setting is variable. Similarly, BL also occurs later in posttransplant cases when the immunosuppressive medication has been reduced and chronic immune stimulation is prolonged.

A challenge in understanding the role of EBV in the etiology of BL is the ubiquity of the virus in the human population. Primary infection can occur at $<~1$ year of age in sub-Saharan Africa, resulting in a loss of viral control and a high burden of infected cells [9,13,42], thus potentially increasing the risk of malignant transformation. EBV encodes several transforming proteins, but only EBNA1 is consistently detected in EBV-positive BL [50]. Abrogation of apoptotic pathways is required in B cells to tolerate constitutive MYC activation, suggesting that such pathogenetic events must occur before MYC translocation. EBV, therefore, may be essential early in pathogenesis by allowing B cells to evade apoptosis [66,67] and impact host cell homeostasis in various ways, by epigenetic modification of host genes and by interfering with cellular microRNA expression [68,41,69]. EBV variants in BL that have deletions of EBNA2 are also linked to the suppression of apoptosis [70]. A hit-and-run mechanism in EBV infection has been proposed [35] and is supported by recent evidence suggesting that EBV plays an initiating role in oncogenesis, but the viral genome is subsequently lost with the acquisition of stable (epi)genetic changes by the neoplastic cells [34].

# Pathogenesis

The primary genetic event in BL is the IG::MYC translocation juxtaposing MYC either to the IGH locus by the t(8;14)(q24;q32) translocation (in $80\%$ of cases) or, less commonly, to the IGL or IGK locus by the t(8;22)(q24;q11) and t(2;8)(p12;q24) translocations, resulting in constitutive MYC expression. Breakpoints of MYC in the IGH:MYC are nearby upstream (5') or within the first exon or intron of MYC in most sporadic and immune deficiency/dysregulation-related BLs, but in endemic BL they can map over hundreds of kilobases upstream from the MYC basal promoter [40,27]. In IGK:MYC and IGL:MYC, the breakpoints in 8q24 are usually downstream (3') of MYC, even up to 2 Mb. Other MYC translocations with non-IG genes have not been definitively demonstrated in BL but may be associated with other high-grade B-cell lymphomas (HGBCLs) and diffuse large B-cell lymphomas [20]. Conventional cytogenetic or molecular methods may miss some IG:MYC translocations [53]. Cryptic insertions of MYC into IGH have been described [71]. Alternative mechanisms that dysregulate MYC have been described in a small subset of cases but should foster consideration of the differential diagnosis [72,17,71,73]. A complex karyotype or multiple imbalances are uncommon at initial diagnosis in BL and should challenge the diagnosis and may require additional testing. The IG::MYC translocation is thought to be mediated by aberrant activation of AID [46]. Mutations in genes controlling cell proliferation, growth, and survival have been identified in BL, but there is no consistent oncogene besides MYC that characterizes all BLs. A characteristic molecular BL (mBL) signature is identified by gene expression profiling studies [20,12]. Slightly different signatures occur in endemic BL and sporadic BL [41], and in adult sporadic BL [10].

Next-generation sequencing has documented a high frequency of mutations in TCF3 (which encodes a transcription factor) and its negative regulator ID3, which activates both the PI3K pathway (promoting cell survival) and cell cycle-related genes such as CCND3 (activating cell proliferation) [74,75,76,1]. Mutations in TCF3 or its repressor $ID3$ that result in tonic activation of B-cell receptor signalling are among the predominant coding mutations found in EBV-negative BL, although they are also frequently seen in EBV-positive cases [17]. Other common pathways affected by mutations include B-cell receptor and PI3K signaling, apoptosis, SWI/SNF signalling, and G protein-coupled receptor signalling [77,17,78]. Of note, TP53 mutations/deletions occur in $25\mathrm{-}50\%$ of cases and are also enriched in EBV-negative BL [17,79,78,80].

In comparison with EBV-negative BL, EBV-positive cases show significantly higher levels of AID (A/CDA) mRNA expression and somatic hypermutation activities, particularly in noncoding sequences close to the transcription start site [17]. Despite this, EBV-positive BL harbours fewer driver mutations, particularly in the apoptosis pathway, than EBV-negative BL [17], further corroborating the pathogenetic role of EBV in lymphomagenesis. Thus, the emerging evidence from in-depth genetic analysis suggests a dual mechanism of BL pathogenesis: mutational versus virally driven [1].

# Macroscopic appearance

There is often a bulky mass comprising contiguous lymph nodes and surrounding tissue. The cut surface has a fish-flesh appearance with focal necrosis and haemorrhage. In some cases, jaw excisions are undertaken, revealing a maxilla or mandible with a large necrotic and destructive mass.

# Histopathology

BL shows a diffuse growth pattern and is composed of monomorphic medium-sized lymphoid cells with basophilic cytoplasm, squared-off cytoplasmic borders, round nuclei with finely clumped and dispersed chromatin, and multiple basophilic and paracentrally located nucleoli. The cells display some degree of cohesion, with abundant mitoses and apoptosis. Many macrophages with phagocytic activity containing apoptotic debris are seen in the background. A sprinkling of tingible-body macrophages in a background of cohesive blue cells results in the classic—albeit not specific—starry-sky pattern. Coagulative necrosis is common. Reactive small lymphocytes are rare.

Some EBV-positive cases may have a florid granulomatous reaction obscuring the tumour. These cases are characterized by a proinflammatory microenvironment, typically present with limited-stage disease, and have an especially good prognosis, sometimes even with spontaneous remission [81,82,83]. Some cases of BL show greater nuclear pleomorphism despite clinical, immunophenotypic, and molecular features characteristic of typical BL. In these cases, the nucleoli may be more prominent and fewer in number. In other cases, particularly in adults with immune deficiency/dysregulation, the tumour cells may exhibit plasmacytoid differentiation with eccentric basophilic cytoplasm and often a single central nucleolus [48]. These morphological features are in line with gene expression profiling studies suggesting that the morphological spectrum of BL is broader than generally thought [20].

# Immunophenotype

BL expresses pan-B-cell antigens (CD19, CD20, CD79a, CD22, and PAX5) and germinal centre-associated antigens (CD10 [particularly strong], BCL6, CD38, HGAL, and MEF2B). The cells are variably positive for the germinal-centre marker GCET1 and consistently negative for LMO2, and they frequently show strong expression of IgM. Aberrant expression of CD43, LEF1, and TCL1A is seen [84,85,86,87,36,88]. Intense nuclear MYC expression in $> 80\%$ cells is noted in almost all cases; very rare cases lack MYC positivity despite the presence of MYC rearrangement due to mutations of the gene or other mechanisms [56,3]. Ki-67 expression is typically $> 95\%$. Cytoplasmic lipid vacuoles can be demonstrated by staining with adipophilin antibody [4]. Neoplastic cells are usually negative for CD5, CD23, and CD138, and are characteristically negative for BCL2, CD44, and TdT. However, weak BCL2 expression can be seen in about $20\%$ of cases and does not exclude the diagnosis. Strong expression of BCL2 and expression of cyclin D1 or TdT is not compatible with a diagnosis of BL; a lack of MYC expression would make the diagnosis of BL not very likely.

Characteristically, BL tumour cells are CD45-low. They express CD19, CD20, CD10, CD38, CD43, CD81, FMC7, and monotypic surface kappa or lambda light chain; expression of CD38 and CD81 is particularly bright. They lack expression of BCL2, CD44, and TdT [94,95,96].

# Differential diagnosis

Cases whose morphology and immunophenotype diverge from what is expected for BL (more frequently seen in the context of immune deficiency/dysregulation settings, particularly HIV) warrant investigations for an alternative diagnosis (see Table 4.4O). On the other hand, cases with some variation in the size and shape of the nuclei can still be diagnosed as BL if they show a combination of an isolated IG::MYC translocation with a typical immunophenotype (CD10+, BCL6+, BCL2-, Ki-67 $> 95\%$) [21].

The differential diagnosis includes B-lymphoblastic leukaemia/lymphoma; HGBCL with MYC and BCL2 rearrangements (predominantly in adults); HGBCL-NOS; HGBCL with 11q aberration, and - on small biopsies - paediatric-type follicular lymphoma (predominantly in paediatric patients).

Lymphoblastic lymphoma is excluded by its morphology (fine chromatin, scant cytoplasm), strong expression of TdT, and presence of additional markers of immaturity (e.g. CD34).

Some cases of HGBCL-NOS have histological features similar to those of BL, and some have MYC rearrangement, heightening the similarity to BL. However, cases of HGBCL-NOS usually lack expression of CD10 and show strong expression of BCL2. Dual rearrangements of MYC and BCL2 with or without BCL6 rearrangement exclude a diagnosis of BL; such cases are diagnosed as HGBCL with MYC and BCL2 rearrangements, and cases with concurrent MYC and BCL6 rearrangements are classified as a genetic subtype of HGBCL-NOS with MYC and BCL6 rearrangements. Evaluation of these translocations, therefore, is recommended in adult patients. Cases that lack rearrangements of MYC should be evaluated for the 11q gain/loss pattern associated with HGBCL with 11q aberration [19].

Embryonal rhabdomyosarcoma can occur in the head and neck and, on cytology preparations, can show vacuolization of cytoplasm; it should be ruled out by the polygonal or spindle shape of some cells, the prominent nucleoli, the lack of deeply basophilic cytoplasm, and the characteristic immunophenotype with expression of desmin and myogenin.

Performing genetic studies requires good infrastructure along with good technical and analytical expertise, which is currently not available in many parts of the world with a high incidence of BL. To address this, a refined algorithm for the diagnosis of BL has been proposed, to be used in resource challenged settings. The diagnosis of BL requires the typical morphological features, characteristic phenotype (expression of CD20, CD10, and CD38; negative or very weak BCL2 expression; lack of CD44), intense MYC expression in $> 80\%$ cells, and a Ki-67 index $0\% \geq 95\%$. In a resource-limited setting, such a constellation of morphology and immunophenotype in concert with a typical clinical context would suffice for a diagnosis of BL [36,37].

# Cytology

In cytological preparations of FNAs, medium-sized lymphoid cells have deeply basophilic cytoplasm that often contains some fine lipid vacuoles. Nuclei are round, and multiple basophilic nucleoli are noted [89,90,91].

# Diagnostic molecular pathology

Demonstration of MYC breakage or IG:MYC translocation, preferably with absence of BCL2 and BCL6 translocation, may support the diagnosis of BL in the appropriate morphological and immunophenotypic context. Documented absence of an IG:MYC fusion argues against the diagnosis of typical BL.

# Essential and desirable diagnostic criteria

Essential: medium-sized, monomorphic lymphoma cells with basophilic cytoplasm and multiple small nucleoli; CD20 and CD10 positivity, absence or (rarely) weak expression of BCL2, Ki-67 index $> 95\%$; usually strong expression of MYC (in $> 80\%$ of cells) and/or demonstration of MYC breakage or IG::MYC translocation.

Desirable: starry-sky pattern, cohesive growth pattern; BCL6 positivity, TdT negativity, CD38 positivity; exclusion of BCL2 and BCL6 rearrangements (mainly required in adult BL).

# Grading / Staging

The predominantly extranodal distribution of BL makes Ann Arbor staging suboptimal. The system proposed by Murphy and revised in 2015 is used routinely for children [92].

# Prognosis and prediction

The prognosis for all patients with BL treated with contemporary immunochemotherapy regimens including rituximab in high-resource settings is excellent. The overall survival rate exceeds 90% in children and 80% in adults [2,51,38] in contemporary multicentre clinical trials. The recently proposed BL-IPI subdivides patients into low-, intermediate-, and high-risk groups, with overall survival rates of 96%, 76%, and 59%, respectively [38]. Also, patients with HIV-associated BL are currently successfully treated with similar immunochemotherapy regimens.

In low/middle-resource settings, outcomes for patients with BL have been less good, in part because of inadequate resources for diagnosis and treatment [15,39] and a lack of awareness in the population and among health care workers, leading to delays in presentation and diagnosis. Results have improved since the introduction of more intensive regimens in sub-Saharan Africa [93] and improvements in the comprehensive cancer care delivery infrastructure, but BL still poses a huge burden on cancer care.

claims.claim: Burkitt lymphoma is coded as 9687/3 in ICD-O.
Burkitt lymphoma, including Burkitt leukaemia, is coded as 2A85.6 in ICD-11.
Burkitt cell leukaemia is not recommended terminology.
Atypical Burkitt lymphoma is not recommended terminology.
Burkitt-like lymphoma is not recommended terminology.
Burkitt lymphoma (BL) is a mature aggressive B-cell neoplasm.
Burkitt lymphoma (BL) is composed of monomorphic, medium-sized cells with basophilic cytoplasm.
Burkitt lymphoma (BL) cells have multiple small nucleoli.
Burkitt lymphoma (BL) has a germinal-centre B-cell phenotype.
Burkitt lymphoma (BL) has a high proliferation index.
Burkitt lymphoma (BL) is characterized by an IG:MYC rearrangement.
Extranodal sites are most commonly involved.
There is geographical variation in the sites of involvement.
In endemic BL, the mandible and maxilla, as well as other facial bones, were traditionally reported as most frequently involved [5,25].
More recent literature typically describes frequent abdominal presentations [15].
Sporadic BL commonly involves the abdomen, soft tissues of the orbit, Waldeyer ring, gingiva, thyroid gland, ovary, testis, and breast [8,43].
BLs arising in immune deficiency/dysregulation settings more commonly have nodal involvement, although extranodal involvement is also frequent [7].
Patients with any BL subtype are at high risk of CNS disease [52,15].
Population-based studies show a low incidence of CNS involvement [31].
Clinical studies from Malawi [54] and the USA [49] suggest CNS involvement frequency ranges from 10% to 30% of cases.
Patients with BL commonly present with a high tumour burden, widely disseminated disease, and rapidly growing tumours due to the short doubling time of BL cells.

Site-specific symptoms of BL include altered vision, nasal obstruction, enlarged tonsil(s), dental/jaw pain (often with loss of teeth), abdominal masses (ileocaecal lesions, retroperitoneal nodes), renal lesions, and testicular masses.
Breast involvement in BL is uncommon except in pregnant and lactating women, in whom the disease is usually bilateral.
Lymphadenopathy mainly occurs in BL arising in immune deficiency/dysregulation settings.
Systemic symptoms occur in a subset of patients with BL.
Leukaemic presentation/bone marrow infiltration occurs in as many as 20% of cases of BL, especially in BL arising in immune deficiency/dysregulation settings [58].
Leukaemic presentation/bone marrow infiltration is rare in patients with endemic BL, although its occurrence may be underestimated in limited-resource settings.
Endemic Burkitt lymphoma is classified under code 9687/3.
Sporadic Burkitt lymphoma is classified under code 9687/3.
Immunodeficiency-associated Burkitt lymphoma is classified under code 9687/3.
EBV-associated Burkitt lymphoma is classified under code 9687/3.
EBV-negative Burkitt lymphoma is classified under code 9687/3.
Endemic Burkitt lymphoma is a traditional subtype of Burkitt lymphoma.
Sporadic Burkitt lymphoma is a traditional subtype of Burkitt lymphoma.
Immunodeficiency-associated Burkitt lymphoma is a traditional subtype of Burkitt lymphoma.
EBV-associated Burkitt lymphoma is a traditional subtype of Burkitt lymphoma.
EBV-negative Burkitt lymphoma is a traditional subtype of Burkitt lymphoma.
Risk factors for endemic Burkitt lymphoma (BL) are Plasmodium falciparum (malaria) and EBV infections.
Factors causing BL in low-incidence areas, such as Europe, Asia, and South America, are not well understood.
EBV is reported in at most 20% of sporadic BL cases.
The incidence of EBV-positive BL increases with age in central Europe.
A high male-to-female ratio in children, particularly prepubescent children, supports the importance of genetic factors in BL.
Familial BL clusters have been reported, including cases with germline variants in TCF4 and CHD8.
High BL risk is reported in rare inherited disorders, including inborn errors of immunity and DNA repair disorders.
BL occurring in HIV-positive individuals tends to present in patients with relatively high CD4 counts and is frequently the first manifestation of HIV infection.
The widespread introduction of combination antiretroviral therapy (cART) has had less effect on the incidence of BL than on the incidence of other types of lymphoma.
BL occurs later in posttransplant cases when the immunosuppressive medication has been reduced and chronic immune stimulation is prolonged.
Primary EBV infection can occur at less than 1 year of age in sub-Saharan Africa, resulting in a loss of viral control and a high burden of infected cells.
EBV encodes several transforming proteins, but only EBNA1 is consistently detected in EBV-positive BL.
Abrogation of apoptotic pathways is required in B cells to tolerate constitutive MYC activation, suggesting that such pathogenetic events must occur before MYC translocation.
EBV may be essential early in pathogenesis by allowing B cells to evade apoptosis.
EBV impacts host cell homeostasis by epigenetic modification of host genes and by interfering with cellular microRNA expression.
EBV variants in BL that have deletions of EBNA2 are linked to the suppression of apoptosis.

A hit-and-run mechanism in EBV infection has been proposed, suggesting that EBV plays an initiating role in oncogenesis, but the viral genome is subsequently lost with the acquisition of stable (epi)genetic changes by the neoplastic cells.

The primary genetic event in BL is the IG::MYC translocation juxtaposing MYC either to the IGH locus by the t(8;14)(q24;q32) translocation (in 80% of cases) or, less commonly, to the IGL or IGK locus by the t(8;22)(q24;q11) and t(2;8)(p12;q24) translocations, resulting in constitutive MYC expression.

Breakpoints of MYC in the IGH:MYC are nearby upstream (5') or within the first exon or intron of MYC in most sporadic and immune deficiency/dysregulation-related BLs, but in endemic BL they can map over hundreds of kilobases upstream from the MYC basal promoter.
In IGK:MYC and IGL:MYC, the breakpoints in 8q24 are usually downstream (3') of MYC, even up to 2 Mb.
Other MYC translocations with non-IG genes have not been definitively demonstrated in BL but may be associated with other high-grade B-cell lymphomas (HGBCLs) and diffuse large B-cell lymphomas.
Conventional cytogenetic or molecular methods may miss some IG:MYC translocations.
Cryptic insertions of MYC into IGH have been described.
Alternative mechanisms that dysregulate MYC have been described in a small subset of cases but should foster consideration of the differential diagnosis.
A complex karyotype or multiple imbalances are uncommon at initial diagnosis in BL and should challenge the diagnosis and may require additional testing.
The IG::MYC translocation is thought to be mediated by aberrant activation of AID.
Mutations in genes controlling cell proliferation, growth, and survival have been identified in BL, but there is no consistent oncogene besides MYC that characterizes all BLs.
A characteristic molecular BL (mBL) signature is identified by gene expression profiling studies.
Slightly different signatures occur in endemic BL and sporadic BL, and in adult sporadic BL.

Next-generation sequencing has documented a high frequency of mutations in TCF3 (which encodes a transcription factor) and its negative regulator ID3, which activates both the Pl3K pathway (promoting cell survival) and cell cycle-related genes such as CCND3 (activating cell proliferation).

Mutations in TCF3 or its repressor ID3 that result in tonic activation of B-cell receptor signalling are among the predominant coding mutations found in EBV-negative BL, although they are also frequently seen in EBV-positive cases.
Other common pathways affected by mutations include B-cell receptor and Pl3K signaling, apoptosis, SWI/SNF signalling, and G protein-coupled receptor signalling.
TP53 mutations/deletions occur in 25-50% of cases and are also enriched in EBV-negative BL.

In comparison with EBV-negative BL, EBV-positive cases show significantly higher levels of AID (A/CDA) mRNA expression and somatic hypermutation activities, particularly in noncoding sequences close to the transcription start site.
Despite this, EBV-positive BL harbours fewer driver mutations, particularly in the apoptosis pathway, than EBV-negative BL.
Emerging evidence from in-depth genetic analysis suggests a dual mechanism of BL pathogenesis: mutational versus virally driven.
Three subtypes of BL, historically recognized as endemic, non-endemic (sporadic), and immunodeficiency-associated, have traditionally been used to describe the epidemiology of BL.
BL is the most common childhood cancer in many countries in equatorial Africa and in Papua New Guinea, where it is considered endemic and is associated with EBV in > 90% of the cases.
Elsewhere, BL is less common in children, where it is considered sporadic and is associated with EBV in about 20% of the cases.
Immunodeficiency-associated BL is more common in the setting of HIV infection than in other forms of immunosuppression.

The designation of endemic or sporadic BL subtypes is problematic because it is primarily based on variations in geographical incidence and epidemiology, and there is no molecular pathological definition of each traditional subtype.
It is probable that sporadic BL also occurs in Africa and may account for the rare EBV-negative cases of BL reported from that continent.
Recent research data suggest that EBV-positive BL and EBV-negative BL form discrete groups with similar molecular features, regardless of epidemiological context and geographical region.
EBV may be the defining etiological feature of the subtypes of BL.
EBV-positive and EBV-negative BL have been shown to differ in their underlying cell biology and pathogenetic mechanisms.
BL rates vary widely (2- to 50-fold) in different populations worldwide.
The highest age-standardized rates (ASRs) of BL are observed in sub-Saharan Africa, Malawi, Uganda, and Cameroon, consistent with the high malaria prevalence there.
BL rates are lower in subtropical regions of Africa or in countries at high altitude.
BL rates are much lower in Central and South America, Europe, North America, and Asia, but variability is observed within regions.
BL incidence rates appear lowest in Asia, being 20 times lower in China than in Uganda.
In general, the M:F ratio of BL is 2-4:1.
The M:F ratio is highest before puberty and decreases with age.
The ratio appears to be higher (as high as 10:1) for tumours involving the face or head structures.
In the USA, 50% of all BL cases occur in adults aged 20-59 years, 28% occur in adults aged ≥60 years, and 22% occur in the paediatric age group (0-19 years).
Adult BLs represent 1-2% of non-Hodgkin lymphomas in adults or elderly patients.
The age-specific incidence of BL shows a bimodal pattern, with distinct peaks at about 10 years and about 60 years.
Several studies have shown an association between the complexity of infection and BL risk.
Children who are exposed to mixed P. falciparum infection have a higher risk for BL than those exposed to infection of a single P. falciparum variant.

Children can be repeatedly infected with P. falciparum-associated malaria, resulting in chronic stimulation of B cells, suppression of T-cell immunity, and induction of activation-induced cytidine deaminase (AID), an enzyme associated with the formation of the MYC translocation typical of BL.
EBV-positive BL shows AID-mediated somatic hypermutation with features of antigen selection in their rearranged IG genes.
There is a loss of T-cell responses to the EBV EBNA1 protein in patients with BL.
There is often a bulky mass comprising contiguous lymph nodes and surrounding tissue.
The cut surface has a fish-flesh appearance with focal necrosis and haemorrhage.
In some cases, jaw excisions are undertaken, revealing a maxilla or mandible with a large necrotic and destructive mass.

BL shows a diffuse growth pattern and is composed of monomorphic medium-sized lymphoid cells with basophilic cytoplasm, squared-off cytoplasmic borders, round nuclei with finely clumped and dispersed chromatin, and multiple basophilic and paracentrally located nucleoli.
The cells display some degree of cohesion, with abundant mitoses and apoptosis.
Many macrophages with phagocytic activity containing apoptotic debris are seen in the background.
A sprinkling of tingible-body macrophages in a background of cohesive blue cells results in the classic-albeit not specific-starry-sky pattern.
Coagulative necrosis is common.
Reactive small lymphocytes are rare.
Some EBV-positive cases may have a florid granulomatous reaction obscuring the tumour.
These cases are characterized by a proinflammatory microenvironment, typically present with limited-stage disease, and have an especially good prognosis, sometimes even with spontaneous remission.
Some cases of BL show greater nuclear pleomorphism despite clinical, immunophenotypic, and molecular features characteristic of typical BL.
In these cases, the nucleoli may be more prominent and fewer in number.

In other cases, particularly in adults with immune deficiency/dysregulation, the tumour cells may exhibit plasmacytoid differentiation with eccentric basophilic cytoplasm and often a single central nucleolus.
These morphological features are in line with gene expression profiling studies suggesting that the morphological spectrum of BL is broader than generally thought.
BL expresses pan-B-cell antigens (CD19, CD20, CD79a, CD22, and PAX5) and germinal centre-associated antigens (CD10 [particularly strong], BCL6, CD38, HGAL, and MEF2B).
The cells are variably positive for the germinal-centre marker GCET1 and consistently negative for LMO2.
The cells frequently show strong expression of IgM.
Aberrant expression of CD43, LEF1, and TCL1A is seen.
Intense nuclear MYC expression in > 80% of cells is noted in almost all cases.
Very rare cases lack MYC positivity despite the presence of MYC rearrangement due to mutations of the gene or other mechanisms.
Ki-67 expression is typically > 95%.
Cytoplasmic lipid vacuoles can be demonstrated by staining with adipophilin antibody.
Neoplastic cells are usually negative for CD5, CD23, and CD138, and are characteristically negative for BCL2, CD44, and TdT.
Weak BCL2 expression can be seen in about 20% of cases and does not exclude the diagnosis.
Strong expression of BCL2 and expression of cyclin D1 or TdT is not compatible with a diagnosis of BL.
A lack of MYC expression would make the diagnosis of BL not very likely.
BL tumour cells are CD45-low.
BL tumour cells express CD19, CD20, CD10, CD38, CD43, CD81, FMC7, and monotypic surface kappa or lambda light chain.
Expression of CD38 and CD81 is particularly bright.
BL tumour cells lack expression of BCL2, CD44, and TdT.
In cytological preparations of FNAs, medium-sized lymphoid cells have deeply basophilic cytoplasm that often contains some fine lipid vacuoles.
In cytological preparations of FNAs, nuclei are round.
In cytological preparations of FNAs, multiple basophilic nucleoli are noted.
Demonstration of MYC breakage or IG:MYC translocation may support the diagnosis of BL in the appropriate morphological and immunophenotypic context.
Absence of BCL2 and BCL6 translocation may support the diagnosis of BL in the appropriate morphological and immunophenotypic context.
Documented absence of an IG:MYC fusion argues against the diagnosis of typical BL.
Cases whose morphology and immunophenotype diverge from what is expected for BL warrant investigations for an alternative diagnosis.

Cases with some variation in the size and shape of the nuclei can still be diagnosed as BL if they show a combination of an isolated IG::MYC translocation with a typical immunophenotype (CD10+, BCL6+, BCL2-, Ki-67 > 95%).

The differential diagnosis includes B-lymphoblastic leukaemia/lymphoma; HGBCL with MYC and BCL2 rearrangements (predominantly in adults); HGBCL-NOS; HGBCL with 11q aberration, and paediatric-type follicular lymphoma (predominantly in paediatric patients).
Lymphoblastic lymphoma is excluded by its morphology (fine chromatin, scant cytoplasm), strong expression of TdT, and presence of additional markers of immaturity (e.g. CD34).
Some cases of HGBCL-NOS have histological features similar to those of BL, and some have MYC rearrangement.
Cases of HGBCL-NOS usually lack expression of CD10 and show strong expression of BCL2.
Dual rearrangements of MYC and BCL2 with or without BCL6 rearrangement exclude a diagnosis of BL.
Cases with concurrent MYC and BCL6 rearrangements are classified as a genetic subtype of HGBCL-NOS with MYC and BCL6 rearrangements.
Evaluation of MYC and BCL2 translocations is recommended in adult patients.
Cases that lack rearrangements of MYC should be evaluated for the 11q gain/loss pattern associated with HGBCL with 11q aberration.
Embryonal rhabdomyosarcoma can occur in the head and neck and, on cytology preparations, can show vacuolization of cytoplasm.

Embryonal rhabdomyosarcoma should be ruled out by the polygonal or spindle shape of some cells, the prominent nucleoli, the lack of deeply basophilic cytoplasm, and the characteristic immunophenotype with expression of desmin and myogenin.
Performing genetic studies requires good infrastructure along with good technical and analytical expertise.
A refined algorithm for the diagnosis of BL has been proposed to be used in resource-challenged settings.

The diagnosis of BL requires the typical morphological features, characteristic phenotype (expression of CD20, CD10, and CD38; negative or very weak BCL2 expression; lack of CD44), intense MYC expression in > 80% cells, and a Ki-67 index ≥ 95%.
In a resource-limited setting, a constellation of morphology and immunophenotype in concert with a typical clinical context would suffice for a diagnosis of BL.
The predominantly extranodal distribution of BL makes Ann Arbor staging suboptimal.
The system proposed by Murphy and revised in 2015 is used routinely for children.
Essential diagnostic criteria include medium-sized, monomorphic lymphoma cells with basophilic cytoplasm and multiple small nucleoli.
Essential diagnostic criteria include CD20 and CD10 positivity.
Essential diagnostic criteria include absence or (rarely) weak expression of BCL2.
Essential diagnostic criteria include Ki-67 index > 95%.
Essential diagnostic criteria usually include strong expression of MYC in > 80% of cells.
Essential diagnostic criteria include demonstration of MYC breakage or IG::MYC translocation.
Desirable diagnostic criteria include a starry-sky pattern.
Desirable diagnostic criteria include a cohesive growth pattern.
Desirable diagnostic criteria include BCL6 positivity.
Desirable diagnostic criteria include TdT negativity.
Desirable diagnostic criteria include CD38 positivity.
Desirable diagnostic criteria include exclusion of BCL2 and BCL6 rearrangements, mainly required in adult BL.
The prognosis for all patients with BL treated with contemporary immunochemotherapy regimens including rituximab in high-resource settings is excellent.
The overall survival rate exceeds 90% in children and 80% in adults in contemporary multicentre clinical trials.
The recently proposed BL-IPI subdivides patients into low-, intermediate-, and high-risk groups, with overall survival rates of 96%, 76%, and 59%, respectively.
Patients with HIV-associated BL are currently successfully treated with similar immunochemotherapy regimens.

In low/middle-resource settings, outcomes for patients with BL have been less good, in part because of inadequate resources for diagnosis and treatment and a lack of awareness in the population and among health care workers, leading to delays in presentation and diagnosis.
Results have improved since the introduction of more intensive regimens in sub-Saharan Africa and improvements in the comprehensive cancer care delivery infrastructure.
BL still poses a huge burden on cancer care.
