claims.claim: 3857/0 Atypical spindle cell/ pleomorphic lipomatous tumour
2E80 & XH4E98 Benign lipomatous neoplasm & Spindle cell lipoma
Acceptable: atypical spindle cell lipoma.
Not recommended: spindle cell liposarcoma.
Not recommended: fibrosarcoma-like lipomatous neoplasm.
Atypical spindle cell / pleomorphic lipomatous tumour is a benign adipocytic neoplasm.
Atypical spindle cell / pleomorphic lipomatous tumour is characterized by ill-defined tumour margins.

Atypical spindle cell / pleomorphic lipomatous tumour contains variable proportions of mild to moderately atypical spindle cells, adipocytes, lipoblasts, pleomorphic cells, multinucleated giant cells, and a myxoid or collagenous extracellular matrix.
Atypical spindle cell / pleomorphic lipomatous tumour has a low tendency for local recurrence if incompletely excised.
Unlike conventional atypical lipomatous tumours, atypical spindle cell / pleomorphic lipomatous tumour has no risk for dedifferentiation.
The tumour manifests as a persistent or enlarging soft tissue mass, nodule, or swelling.
The tumour sometimes manifests with tenderness.
Atypical spindle cell/pleomorphic lipomatous tumours arise in the subcutis slightly more frequently than in deep (subfascial) somatic soft tissues.
Atypical spindle cell/pleomorphic lipomatous tumours only occasionally arise in intracavitary or visceral locations.
The anatomical distribution of atypical spindle cell/pleomorphic lipomatous tumours is wide, predominating in the limbs and limb girdles.
The most common locations for atypical spindle cell/pleomorphic lipomatous tumours are the hand and foot and the thigh.
Other common locations for atypical spindle cell/pleomorphic lipomatous tumours include the shoulder and buttock, forearm, knee, lower leg, and upper arm.
Less common locations for atypical spindle cell/pleomorphic lipomatous tumours are the head and neck, genital area, trunk, and back.
Rare sites of involvement for atypical spindle cell/pleomorphic lipomatous tumours include the larynx, mediastinum, retroperitoneum, trachea, and appendix.
Atypical spindle cell / pleomorphic lipomatous tumour occurs predominantly in middle-aged adults.
There is a peak incidence of atypical spindle cell / pleomorphic lipomatous tumour in the sixth decade of life.
Atypical spindle cell / pleomorphic lipomatous tumour can affect patients of any age.
Cases of atypical spindle cell / pleomorphic lipomatous tumour have been described in patients aged 6-87 years.
The large majority of patients with atypical spindle cell / pleomorphic lipomatous tumour are older than 30 years.
There is a slight male predominance in atypical spindle cell / pleomorphic lipomatous tumour cases.
Deletions or losses of 13q14, including RB1 and its flanking genes RCBTB2, DLEU1, and IM2B, have been identified in a substantial subset of cases.
Monosomy 7 has been reported in some cases.
Atypical spindle cell/pleomorphic lipomatous tumours are unencapsulated.
Atypical spindle cell/pleomorphic lipomatous tumours show a nodular or multinodular growth pattern.
Atypical spindle cell/pleomorphic lipomatous tumours demonstrate ill-defined tumour margins.
The size of atypical spindle cell/pleomorphic lipomatous tumours is variable, ranging from 0.5 to 28 cm.
The median size of atypical spindle cell/pleomorphic lipomatous tumours is 5 to 8.5 cm.
FNA may show cells similar to those seen histologically.
Molecular studies have shown a consistent absence of MDM2 or CDK4 amplification.

Essential diagnostic criteria include variable proportions of atypical spindle cells, adipocytes, univacuolated or bivacuolated to multivacuolated lipoblasts, pleomorphic (multinucleated) cells, and a myxoid to collagenous extracellular matrix.
In a substantial subset of cases, RB1 expression is lost, correlating with RB1 deletion.
Lack of MDM2 or CDK4 amplification is desirable in selected cases.
Atypical spindle cell / pleomorphic lipomatous tumour has a low rate of local recurrence (10-15%) for incompletely removed lesions.
There is no documented risk for metastasis of atypical spindle cell / pleomorphic lipomatous tumour.
Most patients will have an excellent prognosis if the atypical spindle cell / pleomorphic lipomatous tumour is completely excised.

A wide range of microscopic appearances can be observed, even regionally within the same lesion, depending on the relative proportions of atypical spindle cells, adipocytes, lipoblasts, and pleomorphic (multinucleated) cells, as well as the variable amount of collagenous and/or myxoid extracellular matrix.
The adipocytic component has a predominantly mature morphology, with variation in adipocytic size and shape.
Patchy, often mild to moderate adipocytic atypia with chromatin coarsening, nuclear enlargement, and focal binucleation or multinucleation can be observed.
Morphologically, the lipoblasts can vary from small and univacuolated or bivacuolated to larger and multivacuolated (pleomorphic).
Bizarre, hyperchromatic, and sometimes pleomorphic multinucleated cells are often scattered within the spindle cell or adipocytic components.
Mitotic figures are often present but mostly scarce.
Tumour necrosis is absent.
The morphology of these tumours can best be described as a broad spectrum defined by two morphological extremes.
At one extreme, these tumours can be paucicellular, with few, cytologically bland spindle cells with minimal nuclear atypia set in a prominent extracellular matrix.

These spindle cell-poor subtypes of atypical spindle cell/pleomorphic lipomatous tumour, which can have abundant myxoid matrix, tend to occur in the hands and feet and morphologically may resemble myxoid spindle cell lipoma, except for the presence of nuclear atypia/hyperchromasia and the anatomical location.

At the other extreme, at the high-cellularity end of the spectrum, atypical spindle cell/pleomorphic lipomatous tumours may be quite cellular, composed of numerous spindle cells showing diffuse, mild to moderate cytonuclear atypia, with easily identified lipoblasts and less extracellular matrix.
A rare finding is heterologous (metaplastic) differentiation, including the presence of smooth muscle, cartilaginous, and/or osseous elements.
The tumour cells show variable expression of CD34, S100, and desmin.
Weak and/or focal expression of MDM2 or CDK4 can be rarely seen.
The combination of MDM2 and CDK4 expression is not encountered.
Loss of nuclear RB1 expression is observed in about 50-70% of cases.

System.role_and_constraints: You are a medical guideline writer. Based on authoritative literature, write a chapter of an authoritative medical report. You need to follow the chapter format below.

content:
# Undifferentiated pleomorphic sarcoma

# Definition

Undifferentiated pleomorphic sarcoma (UPS) is a pleomorphic malignant neoplasm of bone with no identifiable line of differentiation; this is a diagnosis of exclusion.

# ICD-O coding / ICD-11 coding

8802/3 Pleomorphic sarcoma, undifferentiated

2B54 & XH0947 Unclassified pleomorphic sarcoma, primary site & Malignant fibrous histiocytoma

# Related terminology

Not recommended: malignant fibrous histiocytoma of bone; pleomorphic fibrosarcoma of bone.

Subtype(s) Nor

# Subtype(s)

In higher power, marked cytological pleomorphism characterizes giant tumour cells with large hyperchromatic nuclei and atypical mitoses.

Eighth decades of life, but most patients are aged > 40 years. Only 10–15% of cases occur in patients aged < 20 years.

# Localization

UPS has a predilection for long tubular bones, particularly around the knee [1, 2]. The most frequently involved bone is the femur, followed by the tibia and humerus. Among the bones of the trunk, the pelvis is most commonly affected. UPS rarely occurs in the spine.

# Clinical features

Symptomatic patients typically report pain and occasionally swelling. Pathological fractures are common, particularly in weight-bearing long tubular bones.

# Imaging

The radiological features of UPSs are nonspecific, but the majority of cases demonstrate an aggressive osteolytic neoplasm with ill-defined margins, cortical destruction, and an associated soft tissue mass [3]. Periosteal reaction is an infrequent finding, and mineralized matrix is absent. The tumours are usually centred in the metadiaphyseal region of long bones and occasionally extend into the epiphysis.

# Spread

None

# Epidemiology

UPS of bone is rare, representing $< 2 \%$ of all primary malignant bone tumours. Males are more frequently affected than females. There is a broad age distribution, from the second to

i maid

# Etiology

The etiology of primary UPS of bone is unknown. Secondary UPS, representing approximately $2 8 \%$ of cases, arises in association with a pre-existing bone condition or disease [2, 4, 5]. The majority of secondary UPSs are associated with a bone infarct, Paget disease, or prior irradiation in the field of the affected bone [6, 7]. Rare examples of UPS of bone occur at the site of a metallic orthopaedic prosthesis or hardware [8, 9]. Diaphyseal medullary stenosis, a rare autosomal dominant bone dysplasia, is another less common setting associated with UPS of bone [10, 11]. This disorder is characterized by cortical growth abnormalities, including diffuse diaphyseal medullary stenosis with overlying endosteal cortical thickening, metaphyseal striations, and scattered infarctions [12]. Approximately $3 5 \%$ of patients with this syndrome develop UPS of bone [13, 14, 15]. The disorder is caused by mutations in the gene encoding methylthioadenosine phosphorylase, MTAP [15].

# Pathogenesis

UPS of bone is highly aneuploid and shows complex chromosomal complements with numerous structural aberrations and marker chromosomes [33]. These cytogenetic observations are confirmed by array hybridization studies, which reveal frequent losses of 8p, 9p, 10, 13q, and 18q and gains of 4q, 5p, 6p, 7p, 8q, 12p, 14q, 17q, 19p, 20q, 22q, and $\mathsf X$ [16]. Homozygous deletions of CDKN2A, RB1, TP53, and ING1 are present in a subset of UPSs [16]. Mutations in TP53 $( \sim 3 0 \% )$ and/or chromatin-remodelling genes $( \sim 4 0 \% )$ are most frequent [17]. In general, the copy-number alterations in UPS of bone overlap with those identified in the same tumours originating in soft tissue [17, 18]. UPS of bone exhibits multiple somatic gene fusions, two of which, CLTC-VMP1 and FARP1-STK24, appear to be recurrent and were reported previously in multiple cancers [17, 19]. RNA sequencing expression data indicate that UPS has an expression profile distinct from that of its soft tissue counterpart and is characterized by activation of the FGF23 pathway [17].

# Macroscopic appearance

The gross appearance of UPS is quite variable. The cut surface texture ranges from fibrous to soft. Areas of necrosis and haemorrhage are frequently present. The tumours exhibit a range of colours, including greyish-white, yellow, and brownish-tan. Cortical destruction and a soft tissue mass are commonly present.

# Histopathology

The tumour is diffusely composed of spindle-shaped and epithelioid or polygonal cells with marked pleomorphism arranged in a haphazard, storiform, and fascicular growth pattern. Variable numbers of large, bizarre multinucleated giant cells and numerous typical and atypical mitotic figures are readily identified. The tumour cells are often embedded within a collagen rich stroma, which may be hyalinized. A background population of scattered foamy histiocytes and inflammatory cells can be seen among the neoplastic cells. Importantly, the tumour lacks any evidence of malignant osteoid or cartilage, thus necessitating thorough sampling in order to rule out osteosarcoma and dedifferentiated chondrosarcoma. Trabeculae of reactive woven bone are occasionally present, particularly at the periphery or in the setting of a pathological fracture [20].

# Immunophenotype

Immunohistochemistry is an essential part of the diagnosis because, by definition, UPS lacks an identifiable line of differentiation and is a diagnosis of exclusion. Caution is required when interpreting myogenic markers, because approximately $50\%$ of UPSs of bone show focal positivity with a single myogenic marker [21, 22]. SMA positivity with an additional myogenic marker (desmin or h-caldesmon) supports leiomyosarcoma in an appropriate histological context. Desmin, myogenin, and MYOD1 aid in ruling out rhabdomyosarcoma. SATB2 immunoreactivity can be seen in UPS, a limiting factor when exploring the possibility of osteosarcoma [23]. H3.3p.Gly34Trp (G34W) expression is occasionally seen in bone sarcomas without associated giant cell tumour histology; these are usually sited in the epiphysis in young people, suggesting a relationship with a giant cell tumour. Therefore, there is a move to expand the definition of primary malignant giant cell tumour on the basis of an H3-3A (H3F3A) or H3-3B (H3F3B) p.Gly34 mutation [24, 25, 26, 27]. Focal cytokeratin positivity can be seen in UPS, but abundant expression should raise concern for metastatic sarcomatoid carcinoma. Several melanocytic markers are indicated in the setting of focal S100 expression in order to exclude melanoma.

# Differential diagnosis

The differential diagnosis is broad, including a variety of high grade sarcomas, metastatic carcinoma, and metastatic melanoma. When features are similar to those of high-grade myxofibrosarcoma of soft tissue, some pathologists favour a diagnosis of high-grade myxofibrosarcoma of bone [22].

# Cytology

Not clinically relevant

# Diagnostic molecular pathology

The absence of IDH1 or IDH2 mutations in UPS of bone can be used in the differential diagnosis with dedifferentiated chondrosarcoma [28]. Similarly, the presence of an H3-3A (H3F3A) or H3-3B (H3F3B) p.Gly34 mutation may suggest a relationship with giant cell tumour of bone, and there is a move to expand the definition of primary malignant giant cell tumour of bone on the basis of an H3-3A (H3F3A) or H3-3B (H3F3B) p.Gly34 mutation [24, 25, 26].

# Essential and desirable diagnostic criteria

Essential: bone tumour with compatible imaging; pleomorphic spindle-shaped and epithelioid cells in a storiform or fascicular growth pattern; no malignant cartilage or bone production by the tumour cells; no specific line of differentiation. Desirable: atypical mitotic figures; necrosis.

# Grading / Staging

Staging is according to bone sarcoma protocols (see TNM staging of tumours of bone, p. 339). See also the information on staging in Bone tumours: Introduction (p. 340).

# Prognosis and prediction

Patients with UPS of bone are generally treated with chemotherapy and complete en bloc resection [29, 30, 31]. Patients with localized disease and adequate therapy have a 5-year survival rate of $50\mathrm{-}67\%$ [32, 4, 1]. Pulmonary metastases are common, occurring in approximately $35\mathrm{-}50\%$ of cases. Secondary UPS and metastatic disease are associated with a poorer prognosis. Incomplete expression of myogenic markers is not thought to affect the prognosis [22].

# References

claims.claim: Malignant fibrous histiocytoma of bone is not recommended terminology.
Pleomorphic fibrosarcoma of bone is not recommended terminology.
Undifferentiated pleomorphic sarcoma (UPS) is a pleomorphic malignant neoplasm of bone.
Undifferentiated pleomorphic sarcoma (UPS) has no identifiable line of differentiation.
Undifferentiated pleomorphic sarcoma (UPS) is a diagnosis of exclusion.
The ICD-O code for Pleomorphic sarcoma, undifferentiated is 8802/3.
The ICD-11 code for Unclassified pleomorphic sarcoma, primary site is 2B54.
The ICD-11 code for Malignant fibrous histiocytoma is XH0947.
Marked cytological pleomorphism is characterized by giant tumour cells with large hyperchromatic nuclei and atypical mitoses.
Most patients are aged > 40 years.
Only 10 - 15% of cases occur in patients aged < 20 years.
UPS has a predilection for long tubular bones, particularly around the knee.
The most frequently involved bone in UPS is the femur.
The tibia is the second most frequently involved bone in UPS.
The humerus is the third most frequently involved bone in UPS.
Among the bones of the trunk, the pelvis is most commonly affected by UPS.
UPS rarely occurs in the spine.
Symptomatic patients typically report pain.
Symptomatic patients occasionally report swelling.
Pathological fractures are common, particularly in weight-bearing long tubular bones.
The radiological features of UPSs are nonspecific.
The majority of UPS cases demonstrate an aggressive osteolytic neoplasm with ill-defined margins, cortical destruction, and an associated soft tissue mass.
Periosteal reaction is an infrequent finding in UPS cases.
Mineralized matrix is absent in UPS cases.
UPS tumors are usually centred in the metadiaphyseal region of long bones.
UPS tumors occasionally extend into the epiphysis.
The etiology of primary UPS of bone is unknown.
Secondary UPS represents approximately 28% of cases.
Secondary UPS arises in association with a pre-existing bone condition or disease.
The majority of secondary UPSs are associated with a bone infarct, Paget disease, or prior irradiation in the field of the affected bone.
Rare examples of UPS of bone occur at the site of a metallic orthopaedic prosthesis or hardware.
Diaphyseal medullary stenosis is a rare autosomal dominant bone dysplasia associated with UPS of bone.

Diaphyseal medullary stenosis is characterized by cortical growth abnormalities, including diffuse diaphyseal medullary stenosis with overlying endosteal cortical thickening, metaphyseal striations, and scattered infarctions.
Approximately 35% of patients with diaphyseal medullary stenosis develop UPS of bone.
Diaphyseal medullary stenosis is caused by mutations in the gene encoding methylthioadenosine phosphorylase, MTAP.
UPS of bone is rare, representing $< 2 \%$ of all primary malignant bone tumours.
Males are more frequently affected by UPS of bone than females.
There is a broad age distribution for UPS of bone, from the second decade of life onwards.
UPS of bone is highly aneuploid and shows complex chromosomal complements with numerous structural aberrations and marker chromosomes.
Array hybridization studies reveal frequent losses of 8p, 9p, 10, 13q, and 18q and gains of 4q, 5p, 6p, 7p, 8q, 12p, 14q, 17q, 19p, 20q, 22q, and X in UPS of bone.
Homozygous deletions of CDKN2A, RB1, TP53, and ING1 are present in a subset of UPSs.
Mutations in TP53 (∼30%) and/or chromatin-remodelling genes (∼40%) are most frequent in UPS of bone.
The copy-number alterations in UPS of bone overlap with those identified in the same tumours originating in soft tissue.
UPS of bone exhibits multiple somatic gene fusions, two of which, CLTC-VMP1 and FARP1-STK24, appear to be recurrent and were reported previously in multiple cancers.
RNA sequencing expression data indicate that UPS has an expression profile distinct from that of its soft tissue counterpart and is characterized by activation of the FGF23 pathway.
The gross appearance of UPS is quite variable.
The cut surface texture of UPS ranges from fibrous to soft.
Areas of necrosis and haemorrhage are frequently present in UPS.
The tumours exhibit a range of colours, including greyish-white, yellow, and brownish-tan.
Cortical destruction and a soft tissue mass are commonly present in UPS.
The tumour is diffusely composed of spindle-shaped and epithelioid or polygonal cells with marked pleomorphism arranged in a haphazard, storiform, and fascicular growth pattern.
Variable numbers of large, bizarre multinucleated giant cells and numerous typical and atypical mitotic figures are readily identified.
The tumour cells are often embedded within a collagen-rich stroma, which may be hyalinized.
A background population of scattered foamy histiocytes and inflammatory cells can be seen among the neoplastic cells.
The tumour lacks any evidence of malignant osteoid or cartilage, thus necessitating thorough sampling in order to rule out osteosarcoma and dedifferentiated chondrosarcoma.
Trabeculae of reactive woven bone are occasionally present, particularly at the periphery or in the setting of a pathological fracture.
The differential diagnosis is broad, including a variety of high-grade sarcomas, metastatic carcinoma, and metastatic melanoma.
When features are similar to those of high-grade myxofibrosarcoma of soft tissue, some pathologists favour a diagnosis of high-grade myxofibrosarcoma of bone.
The absence of IDH1 or IDH2 mutations in UPS of bone can be used in the differential diagnosis with dedifferentiated chondrosarcoma.
The presence of an H3-3A (H3F3A) or H3-3B (H3F3B) p.Gly34 mutation may suggest a relationship with giant cell tumour of bone.
There is a move to expand the definition of primary malignant giant cell tumour of bone on the basis of an H3-3A (H3F3A) or H3-3B (H3F3B) p.Gly34 mutation.
Essential: bone tumour with compatible imaging.
Essential: pleomorphic spindle-shaped and epithelioid cells in a storiform or fascicular growth pattern.
Essential: no malignant cartilage or bone production by the tumour cells.
Essential: no specific line of differentiation.
Desirable: atypical mitotic figures.
Desirable: necrosis.
Staging is according to bone sarcoma protocols.
Immunohistochemistry is an essential part of the diagnosis because, by definition, UPS lacks an identifiable line of differentiation and is a diagnosis of exclusion.
Approximately 50% of UPSs of bone show focal positivity with a single myogenic marker.
SMA positivity with an additional myogenic marker (desmin or h-caldesmon) supports leiomyosarcoma in an appropriate histological context.
Desmin, myogenin, and MYOD1 aid in ruling out rhabdomyosarcoma.
SATB2 immunoreactivity can be seen in UPS, a limiting factor when exploring the possibility of osteosarcoma.

H3.3 p.Gly34Trp (G34W) expression is occasionally seen in bone sarcomas without associated giant cell tumour histology; these are usually sited in the epiphysis in young people, suggesting a relationship with a giant cell tumour.
There is a move to expand the definition of primary malignant giant cell tumour on the basis of an H3-3A (H3F3A) or H3-3B (H3F3B) p.Gly34 mutation.
Focal cytokeratin positivity can be seen in UPS, but abundant expression should raise concern for metastatic sarcomatoid carcinoma.
Several melanocytic markers are indicated in the setting of focal S100 expression in order to exclude melanoma.
Patients with UPS of bone are generally treated with chemotherapy and complete en bloc resection.
Patients with localized disease and adequate therapy have a 5-year survival rate of 50-67%.
Pulmonary metastases are common, occurring in approximately 35-50% of cases.
Secondary UPS and metastatic disease are associated with a poorer prognosis.
Incomplete expression of myogenic markers is not thought to affect the prognosis.

comprehensiveness.criterion: Complete Workflow from Presentation to Follow-up
comprehensiveness.explanation: Assess the guideline's coverage of the entire clinical workflow, from initial presentation and imaging to pathology, molecular diagnostics, integrated diagnosis, management, and follow-up. This ensures that all critical steps are addressed, providing a comprehensive understanding of UPS management.

Evaluate the guideline's clarity in defining UPS, including its boundaries, grading, and accurate ICD-O/ICD-11 coding. This is crucial for ensuring consistent diagnosis and classification across clinical settings.

Check the guideline's detail on UPS's anatomic predilection, dissemination patterns, and demographic stratification. This information is vital for understanding the disease's clinical presentation and guiding diagnostic suspicion.
Assess the guideline's coverage of clinical symptoms and imaging findings across modalities like CT, MRI, and PET. This ensures that clinicians have a complete picture of the disease's presentation.

Evaluate the guideline's discussion on the etiology and mechanisms of UPS, including genetic and environmental factors. Understanding these aspects is crucial for risk assessment and potential preventive strategies.
Check the guideline's detail on the macroscopic and microscopic pathology, as well as the immunophenotype of UPS. This is essential for accurate diagnosis and differentiation from other sarcomas.
Evaluate the guideline's discussion on prognostic and predictive factors, including treatment evidence and recurrence context. This information is vital for patient management and counseling.

insight_depth.criterion: Mechanistic Depth Linking Genetics and Phenotype
insight_depth.explanation: Assess the guideline's depth in linking genetic and epigenetic alterations to the clinical and pathological phenotype of UPS. This understanding is crucial for developing targeted therapies and personalized medicine approaches.

insight_depth.criterion: Integration Across Imaging, Histology, and Molecular Data
insight_depth.explanation: Evaluate the guideline's ability to integrate data from imaging, histology, and molecular diagnostics into a coherent diagnostic framework. This integration is essential for accurate diagnosis and treatment planning.

insight_depth.criterion: Transparent Diagnostic Reasoning and Decision Points
insight_depth.explanation: Check the guideline's clarity in outlining diagnostic reasoning, including decision points and common pitfalls. This transparency helps clinicians navigate complex cases and avoid diagnostic errors.

insight_depth.criterion: Prognostic and Predictive Stratification Depth
insight_depth.explanation: Assess the guideline's depth in stratifying patients based on prognostic and predictive factors, with biologically plausible rationale. This stratification is crucial for tailoring treatment and improving outcomes.

insight_depth.criterion: Evidence Synthesis Maturity and Limitations Acknowledgment
insight_depth.explanation: Evaluate the guideline's maturity in synthesizing evidence, acknowledging heterogeneity, and conveying the strength of evidence. This maturity is essential for informed clinical decision-making.

insight_depth.criterion: Clinical Impact Reasoning of Biomarkers
insight_depth.explanation: Check the guideline's reasoning on how specific biomarkers or histologic features impact diagnosis, management, and trial eligibility. This reasoning is crucial for translating research findings into clinical practice.

accuracy_standards.criterion: Correct WHO Entity Naming and Grading Conventions
accuracy_standards.explanation: Assess the guideline's adherence to current WHO naming and grading conventions for UPS. Accurate terminology is essential for consistent communication and classification.

accuracy_standards.criterion: Accurate ICD-O/ICD-11 Coding Usage
accuracy_standards.explanation: Evaluate the guideline's accuracy in using ICD-O/ICD-11 codes and handling synonyms or deprecated terms. Correct coding is crucial for epidemiological studies and healthcare reporting.

accuracy_standards.criterion: Molecular Terminology and Assay Interpretation Accuracy
accuracy_standards.explanation: Check the guideline's accuracy in molecular terminology, including variant naming and assay interpretation. Precise language is essential for clear communication and avoiding diagnostic errors.

accuracy_standards.criterion: Internal Consistency Across Document
accuracy_standards.explanation: Evaluate the guideline for internal consistency, ensuring no contradictory thresholds or criteria. Consistency is crucial for maintaining credibility and reliability.

accuracy_standards.criterion: Diagnostic Accuracy Safeguards
accuracy_standards.explanation: Assess the guideline's inclusion of safeguards against diagnostic inaccuracies, such as assay limitations and sampling bias. These safeguards are essential for ensuring reliable diagnosis.

accuracy_standards.criterion: Consistency with Reporting Standards
accuracy_standards.explanation: Check the guideline's consistency with widely used reporting standards and best practices for integrated diagnosis narratives. Adherence to standards ensures uniformity and quality in reporting.

readability_utility.explanation: Assess the guideline's logical organization and flow, ensuring that arguments and recommendations progress in a clinically sensible order. Clear transitions are crucial for reader comprehension and application.
Evaluate the guideline's use of evidence-backed statements, supported by quantitative data where appropriate. This approach enhances credibility and aids in clinical decision-making.

readability_utility.criterion: Actionable Guidance for Diagnosis and Management
readability_utility.explanation: Check the guideline's provision of explicit, actionable guidance for diagnosis, reporting, testing strategy, management, and follow-up. Clear guidance is essential for practical application in clinical settings.

readability_utility.criterion: High-Yield Presentation with Effective Use of Visuals
readability_utility.explanation: Assess the guideline's use of tables, figures, and algorithms to enhance decision-making. Effective visuals can clarify complex information and improve usability.
Evaluate the guideline's clarity in defining terms, thresholds, and required reporting elements for integrated diagnosis. Clear definitions are crucial for consistent interpretation and reporting.

Check the guideline's feasibility for implementation across various clinical settings, considering resource availability and practical constraints. Feasibility ensures broader applicability and utility.

System.role_and_constraints: You are a medical guideline writer. Based on authoritative literature, write a chapter of an authoritative medical report. You need to follow the chapter format below.

content:
# Giant cell tumour of soft tissue

# Definition

Giant cell tumour of soft tissue is morphologically similar to but genetically unrelated to giant cell tumour of bone.

# ICD-O coding / ICD-11 coding

9251/1 Giant cell tumour of soft parts NOS

2F7C & XH81M1 Neoplasms of uncertain behaviour of connective or other soft tissue & Giant cell tumour of soft parts NOS

# Related terminology

Not recommended: giant cell tumour of low malignant potential.

# Subtype(s)

None

# Localization

Giant cell tumour of soft tissue usually occurs in superficial soft tissues of the upper and lower extremities (70% of tumours). Affected less frequently are the trunk (20%) and head and neck (7%) regions [1, 2, 3, 4, 5]. Occasional cases have been reported in other anatomical locations [6, 7, 8].

# Clinical features

The tumours usually present as painless growing masses [2, 3], with an average duration of 6 months [3]. Peripheral mineralization is common.

# Imaging

None

# Spread

None

# Epidemiology

Giant cell tumour of soft tissue occurs predominantly in the fifth decade of life, but it can affect patients ranging in age from 5 to 89 years. Giant cell tumour of soft tissue shows no apparent difference in incidence with regard to sex or ethnicity [1, 2, 3].

# Etiology

Unknown

# Pathogenesis

Giant cell tumour of soft tissue lacks the mutations of the H3-3A (H3F3A) gene that are present in the vast majority of giant cell tumours of bone, suggesting a different pathogenesis [9,10].

# Macroscopic appearance

Tumours range in size from 0.7 to $10~\mathrm{cm}$ (mean: 3 cm) [1,2,3]. Subcutaneous adipose tissue or dermis is involved in $70\%$ of tumours; $30\%$ are situated deep to superficial fascia. Giant cell tumour of soft tissue is a well-circumscribed, mostly solid, nodular mass with a fleshy, reddish-brown or grey cut surface. Gritty regions of mineralized bone are frequently present at the periphery [2].

# Histopathology

Giant cell tumour of soft tissue displays a multinodular architecture ($85\%$). Cellular nodules are separated by fibrous septa of varying thickness, containing haemosiderin-laden macrophages [2]. The nodules are composed of a mixture of round to oval mononuclear cells and osteoclast-like multinucleated giant cells, with both cell types immersed in a richly vascularized stroma. Mitotic activity is readily seen in giant cell tumour of soft tissue [1,2,3]. Nuclear pleomorphism and bizarre giant cells are absent, and necrosis is rarely found [1,2,3]. Metaplastic bone formation is present in approximately $50\%$ of tumours, most often in the form of a peripheral shell of woven bone. Aneurysmal bone cyst-like changes may be seen. Vascular invasion is identified in about $30\%$ of tumours [1,3]. Additional histological features include stromal haemorrhage ($50\%$) and regressive changes in the form of marked stromal fibrosis and clusters of foamy macrophages ($70\%$). Immunohistochemistry is not helpful.

clear cells. The cellular nodules contain a mixture of round oval mononuclear and multinucleated osteoclast-like giant cells.

l tumour of soft tissue.

# Immunophenotype

None

# Differential diagnosis

None

# Cytology

Not clinically relevant

Diagnostic molecular pathology not clinically relevant

# Diagnostic molecular pathology

None

# Essential and desirable diagnostic criteria

Essential: multinodular superficial soft tissue neoplasm; histiocytoid mononuclear cell population with osteoclastic giant cells; haemosiderin deposition and metaplastic bone formation are commonly observed.

# Grading / Staging

Not clinically relevant

# Prognosis and prediction

Giant cell tumour of soft tissue is associated with a local recurrence rate of $12\%$ with very rare metastasis [1,2,3].

This includes clinical features, imaging, pathology, and molecular diagnostics. Evaluate the guideline's clarity in defining giant cell tumour of soft tissue, including ICD-O/ICD-11 coding. Accurate definitions and coding are crucial for consistent diagnosis, reporting, and epidemiological tracking, ensuring alignment with international standards.

Examine the guideline's detail on anatomical localization, dissemination patterns, and demographic stratification. Understanding where and in whom these tumours occur aids in accurate diagnosis and tailored management strategies.

Assess the guideline's coverage of macroscopic and microscopic pathology, including immunophenotype. Comprehensive pathology descriptions are essential for distinguishing giant cell tumour of soft tissue from other entities and guiding treatment decisions.

Evaluate the guideline's inclusion of molecular pathology, such as entity-defining alterations and CNV patterns. Molecular insights are critical for accurate diagnosis and understanding tumour biology, impacting treatment and prognosis.

Assess the guideline's coverage of differential diagnosis, including structured guidance on similarities and distinguishing tests. This ensures accurate identification of giant cell tumour of soft tissue, avoiding misdiagnosis and inappropriate treatment.

Evaluate the guideline's detail on prognostic factors and treatment evidence, including recurrence and progression context. Comprehensive evidence coverage supports informed clinical decision-making and patient management.

System.role_and_constraints: You are a medical guideline writer. Based on authoritative literature, write a chapter of an authoritative medical report. You need to follow the chapter format below.

content:
# Pulmonary hamartoma

# Definition

Pulmonary hamartomas are benign mesenchymal neoplasms with variable amounts of at least two mesenchymal elements and entrapped respiratory epithelium.

Aubry M-C Boland JM

Chondroma is a benign neoplasm of hyaline cartilage.

# ICD-O coding / ICD-11 coding

8992/0 Pulmonary hamartoma

2F00.Y & XH3UD9 Other specified benign neoplasm of middle ear or respiratory system & Pulmonary hamartoma

9220/0 Chondroma

2F00.Y & XH0NS4 Other specified benign neoplasm of middle ear or respiratory system & Chondroma, NOS

# Related terminology

Not recommended: chondroid hamartoma; mesenchymoma.

None

# Subtype(s)

None

# Localization

Most are peripheral, with approximately 10% located centrally in a bronchus. Hamartomas occur in all lobes [18].

# Clinical features

Peripheral pulmonary hamartomas are usually asymptomatic, solitary, well-circumscribed nodules; multifocality is rare [18]. Endobronchial hamartomas often produce signs and symptoms of obstruction [18, 6].

# Imaging

Popcorn calcification is a helpful radiographic feature but is present in a minority of cases [10]. Presence of adipose tissue on CT is a specific feature [10, 9]. Hamartomas have low FDG avidity (mean maximum standardized uptake value: 1.5) [12].

# Spread

None

# Epidemiology

Hamartomas are the most common benign pulmonary neoplasm, accounting for $8\%$ of radiographically detected coin lesions [17]. Hamartomas have a male predominance, with peak incidence in the sixth decade of life, and they rarely occur in children [18, 6].

# Etiology

Molecular and cytogenetic data suggest a neoplastic origin, with recurrent translocations leading to fusion genes that drive tumorigenesis [14, 16].

# Pathogenesis

Pulmonary hamartomas frequently have the translocation t(3;12) (q27-q28;q14-q15). The resulting HMGA2-LPP fusion gene usually consists of exons 1-3 of HMGA2 and exons 9-11 of $L P P,$ and it is consistently expressed in tumours with this translocation [7, 14, 2, 4]. The same translocation and fusion gene are common in lipomas [14, 16, 8].

# Macroscopic appearance

Hamartomas are firm, round to multilobulated, well-circumscribed nodules that often shell out from the surrounding lung. They are white to bluish and may be gritty. Most are $< 40 ~ \mathsf { mm }$, but they may uncommonly reach a large size $\left( > 90 \mathsf { mm } \right.$ [12, 18, 10]. Cystic change is uncommon. Endobronchial hamartomas are often yellow to grey and sessile.

# Histopathology

Pulmonary hamartomas are usually composed predominantly of hyaline cartilage, which may show myxoid change, intermixed with variable amounts of other mesenchymal components, including fat, bland myxoid spindle cells, smooth muscle, fibrous tissue, and bone [18]. Clefts of entrapped respiratory epithelial cells invaginate between the lobules of cartilage and connective tissue. Endobronchial hamartomas may have prominent adipose tissue (termed lipomatous hamartoma), whereas epithelial inclusions tend to be inconspicuous [18]. In some cases, other mesenchymal elements may dominate, such as smooth muscle (termed adenoleiomyomatous hamartoma) or rarely fibrous tissue. Immunohistochemistry is usually not necessary, because hamartomas would show the expected staining pattern for the represented mesenchymal elements.

# Immunophenotype

None

# Differential diagnosis

Pulmonary hamartomas are differentiated from monomorphic soft tissue tumours by the presence of more than one mesenchymal component. Other soft tissue neoplasms may enter the differential diagnosis on small biopsies where only one mesenchymal component is represented. Pulmonary chondromas typically arise in patients with Carney triad (i.e. gastrointestinal stromal tumours, pulmonary chondromas, paragangliomas); sporadic examples are very uncommon. Chondromas lack entrapped epithelium, frequently show calcification / bone metaplasia, and are bounded by a fibrous pseudocapsule [13]. SDHB immunohistochemistry may be useful; it shows abnormal loss in Carney-associated chondromas but not in hamartomas [5]. Endobronchial lipoma may show overlapping morphological and genetic features with hamartoma [3], but the distinction between hamartoma and lipoma is generally not critical. The differential diagnosis may be particularly challenging if only the myxoid spindle cell component is sampled, which could lead to consideration of myxoid peripheral nerve sheath tumour or even myxoid sarcoma; however, unlike those of sarcomas, the spindle cells of hamartoma are generally very bland, without atypia, and they have very low cellularity.

# Cytology

Cytological preparations commonly include abundant fibromyxoid stroma with cartilage, as well as benign reactive epithelial cells [19, 15]. Mesenchymal elements may be subtle on a Pap stain, and epithelial cells may be abundant, leading to false positive diagnoses of malignancy [11].

# Diagnostic molecular pathology

Not relevant

# Essential and desirable diagnostic criteria

· At least two types of benign mesenchymal tissue, most commonly hyaline cartilage, fat, and/or myxoid spindle cells, with entrapped invaginated bronchial epithelium

# Grading / Staging

Not relevant

# Prognosis and prediction

Pulmonary hamartomas are slow-growing neoplasms with excellent prognosis [18]. Surgical resection is the optimal treatment for endobronchial lesions and parenchymal tumours that are large/symptomatic. Recurrence and malignant transformation are very rare [18, 1].

# References
1. Bile nant change in a benign pulmonary hamartoma. Thorax. 1989 Mar;44(3):232-3. PMID:2705156
2. Beasley MB, Dembitzer FR, Flores RM. Surgical pathology of early stage non-small cell lung carcinoma. Ann Transl Med. 2016 Jun;4(12):238. PMID:27429964
3. Boland JF, Ritchie KJ, Erickson-Joo MR et al. Overall clinicopathological analysis of 12 cases with molecular cytogenetic evidence supporting classification as “lipoma". Am J Surg Pathol. 2013 Nov;37(11):1715-21. PMID:24121172
4. Cellerin L, Marcq M, Sagan C, et al. [Malignant pleural effusion as the presenting site of cancer: comparison with metastatic pleural effusions from known cancers]. Rev Mal Respir. 2008 Nov;25(9):1104-9. French. PMID:19106906
5. Chatzopoulos K, Fritchie bry et al. Loss of succinate dehydrogenase B immunohistochemical expression distinguishes pulmonary chondromas from hamartomas. Histopathology. 2019 Dec;75(6):825-32. PMID:31236950
6. Cosio G et al. Endobronchial hamartoma. Chest. 2002 Jul;122(1):202-5. PMID:12114359
7. Dal Cin P, Kools P, De Jonge et al. Rearrangement of 12q14-15 in pulmonary chondroid hamartoma. Genes Chromosomes Cancer. 1993 Oct;8(2):131-3. PMID:7504517
8. Fletcher JA, Longtine J, Wallace K, et al. Cytogenetic and histologic findings in 17 pulmonary chondroid hamartomas: evidence for a pathogenetic relationship with lipomas and leiomyomas. Genes Chromosomes Cancer. 1995 Mar;12(3):220-3. PMID:7536462
9. Gleeson T, Thiessen R, Hannigan A et al. Pulmonary hamartomas: CT pixel analysis for fat attenuation using radiologic-pathologic correlation. J Med Imaging Radiat Oncol. 2013 Oct;57(5):534-43. PMID:24119266

16. Schoenmakers EF, Wanschura S, Mols R et al. Recurrent etsinegh mobility group protein gene-Cig mesenchymal tumours. Nat Genet. 1995 Aug;10(4):436-44. PMID:7670494 2675 metastases in the axial plane. A combined radiological-pathological study. Radiol. 1977;46(4):248-65. PMD:195302
17. Toomes H, Delphendahl A, Manke HG, et al. The coin lesion of the lung. A review of 955 resected coin lesions. Cancer. 1983 Feb 1;51(3):534-7. PMID:6821831
18. van den Bosch JM, Wagenaar SS, Corrin B, et al. Mesenchymoma of the lung (so-called hamartoma): a review of 154 parenchymal and endobronchial cases. Thorax. 1987 Oct;42(10):790-3. PMID:3321538
19. Hughes JH, Young NA, Wilbur DC, et al. Fine-needle aspiration of pulmonary hamartoma: a common source of false-positive diagnoses in the College of American Pathologists Interlaboratory Comparison Program in Nongynecologic Cytology. Arch Pathol Lab Med. 2005 Jan;129(1):19-22. PMID:15628904

comprehensiveness.criterion: Complete Workflow Coverage from Presentation to Follow-up
comprehensiveness.explanation: Assess if the guideline covers the entire clinical workflow for pulmonary hamartoma, from initial presentation and imaging to pathology, molecular diagnostics, integrated diagnosis, management, and follow-up. This ensures that all clinical stages are addressed, providing a comprehensive guide for clinicians.

Evaluate the guideline's clarity in defining pulmonary hamartoma, including its ICD-O and ICD-11 coding. Accurate coding is crucial for proper classification and billing, and clear definitions help avoid diagnostic ambiguity.

Check if the guideline provides detailed information on the anatomic distribution, epidemiology, and demographic stratification of pulmonary hamartomas. This information is vital for understanding the disease's prevalence and typical patient profiles.

Assess the guideline's coverage of clinical and imaging phenotypes across modalities like CT and PET. Comprehensive imaging descriptions help in accurate diagnosis and differentiation from other pulmonary lesions.

Evaluate the guideline's description of macroscopic and microscopic pathology, including immunophenotype. Detailed pathology descriptions are essential for accurate diagnosis and differentiation from other neoplasms.

Check if the guideline provides a broad differential diagnosis with structured guidance on similarities and distinguishing tests. This helps clinicians differentiate pulmonary hamartomas from other similar lesions.
Assess the guideline's coverage of prognostic factors and treatment evidence, including recurrence and progression. This information is crucial for patient management and counseling.

insight_depth.criterion: Mechanistic Depth Linking Genetics and Phenotype
insight_depth.explanation: Evaluate the guideline's depth in explaining the genetic and phenotypic mechanisms of pulmonary hamartomas, such as the role of HMGA2-LPP fusion genes. Understanding these mechanisms aids in accurate diagnosis and potential therapeutic targeting.

insight_depth.criterion: Integration Across Imaging, Histology, and Molecular Data
insight_depth.explanation: Assess the guideline's ability to integrate data from imaging, histology, and molecular diagnostics into a coherent diagnostic framework. This integration is crucial for accurate and comprehensive diagnosis.

insight_depth.criterion: Transparent Diagnostic Reasoning and Pitfall Resolution
insight_depth.explanation: Check if the guideline provides clear diagnostic reasoning, including decision points, common pitfalls, and strategies to resolve conflicts between modalities. This transparency aids clinicians in making informed diagnostic decisions.

insight_depth.criterion: Prognostic and Predictive Stratification Depth
insight_depth.explanation: Evaluate the guideline's depth in stratifying patients prognostically and predictively, with biologically plausible rationales. Clear stratification helps in tailoring patient management and follow-up strategies.

insight_depth.criterion: Evidence Synthesis Maturity and Limitations Acknowledgment
insight_depth.explanation: Assess the guideline's maturity in synthesizing evidence, handling heterogeneity, and acknowledging limitations. Mature evidence synthesis ensures that recommendations are based on robust data.

accuracy_standards.criterion: Correct and Current WHO Entity Naming and Grading
accuracy_standards.explanation: Ensure the guideline uses correct and current WHO naming and grading conventions for pulmonary hamartomas. Adherence to these standards is crucial for consistency and accuracy in diagnosis.

accuracy_standards.criterion: Accurate ICD-O/ICD-11 Coding Usage
accuracy_standards.explanation: Check the guideline's accuracy in using ICD-O and ICD-11 codes, including handling of synonyms and deprecated terms. Accurate coding is essential for classification and billing purposes.

accuracy_standards.criterion: Molecular Terminology and Assay Interpretation Accuracy
accuracy_standards.explanation: Evaluate the guideline's accuracy in molecular terminology and assay interpretation, such as variant naming and CNV conventions. Accurate molecular descriptions are crucial for diagnosis and research.

accuracy_standards.criterion: Internal Consistency Across Document
accuracy_standards.explanation: Ensure there are no contradictory thresholds, criteria, or marker interpretations within the guideline. Internal consistency is vital for maintaining the guideline's credibility and reliability.

accuracy_standards.criterion: Diagnostic Accuracy Safeguards
accuracy_standards.explanation: Assess the guideline's inclusion of safeguards against diagnostic inaccuracies, such as assay limitations and sampling bias. These safeguards help prevent misdiagnosis and ensure patient safety.

readability_utility.explanation: Evaluate the guideline's logical coherence and clinical flow, ensuring that arguments and recommendations progress in a sensible order. Clear flow aids in understanding and applying the guideline in practice.
Check if key claims in the guideline are supported by quantitative data, such as sample sizes and effect sizes. Evidence-backed statements enhance the guideline's credibility and utility.

readability_utility.criterion: Actionable Guidance for Diagnosis and Management
readability_utility.explanation: Assess the guideline's provision of explicit guidance for diagnosis, reporting, testing strategy, management, and follow-up. Actionable guidance is crucial for practical application in clinical settings.
Evaluate the guideline's use of tables, figures, and algorithms that materially improve decision-making. High-yield visuals enhance understanding and application of the guideline.

Check if the guideline provides resource-stratified pathways and considers practical constraints like turnaround time. Feasibility ensures the guideline's applicability across various healthcare settings.

prompt: Large cell neuroendocrine carcinoma of the lung

System.role_and_constraints: You are a medical guideline writer. Based on authoritative literature, write a chapter of an authoritative medical report. You need to follow the chapter format below.

content:
# Large cell neuroendocrine carcinoma of the lung

# Definition

Large cell neuroendocrine carcinoma (LCNEC) is a high-grade non-small cell carcinoma with neuroendocrine morphology and a mitotic count of $> 10$ mitoses $I 2 \mathsf { m m } ^ { 2 }$, that expresses one or more neuroendocrine immunohistochemical markers. Combined LCNEC is an LCNEC with components of adenocarcinoma, squamous cell carcinoma, or spindle or giant cell carcinoma.

Melanoma of the lung is a malignant melanocytic neoplasm arising in the lung.

# ICD-O coding / ICD-11 coding

8013/3 Large cell neuroendocrine carcinoma
8013/3 Combined large cell neuroendocrine carcinoma

2C25.4 & XHoNL5 Carcinoid or other malignant neuroendocrine neoplasms of bronchus or lung & Large cell neuroendocrine carcinoma

2C25.Y & XH4846 Other specified malignant neoplasms of bronchus or lung & Melanoma  
2C25.Y & XH5QP3 Other specified malignant neoplasms of bronchus or lung & Mucosal lentiginous melanoma  
2C25.Y & XH4QG5 Other specified malignant neoplasms of bronchus or lung & Nodular melanoma

# Related terminology

None

Acceptable: mucosal melanoma; mucosal lentiginous melanoma; pulmonary melanoma; lung melanoma.

ICD-O coding 8720/3 Melanoma

# Subtype(s)

Large cell neuroendocrine carcinoma; combined large cell neuroendocrine carcinoma

Combined LCNECs occur most commonly with adenocarcinoma, but any non-neuroendocrine NSCLC histological type may be present. Combined tumours account for approximately $20{-}25\%$ of resected LCNECs [43]. LCNEC and NSCLC components are clonally related, as revealed by genomic studies, supporting the concept of phenotypic divergence rather than a collision phenomenon [33].

None

# Localization

The majority $(>75\%)$ of LCNECs are located in the lung periphery, but some tumours arise in the central compartment adjacent to or involving central airways [37, 25].

Scolyer RA
de la Fouchardiere A
Travis WD

Primary pulmonary melanoma can arise anywhere in the lower respiratory tract, including in the large airways or periphery of the lung.

# Clinical features

The clinical presentation of LCNEC is usually similar to that of non-small cell lung carcinoma (NSCLC; see Tumours of the lung: Introduction, p.20). Patients with LCNEC are more likely to present with operable disease ($\sim 50\%$) than patients with small cell lung carcinoma (SCLC). Distant metastases are present in $40{-}50\%$ of patients at presentation [51, 22]. Common sites of metastasis include brain, liver, and bone, and metastases may result in constitutional symptoms such as weight loss or fatigue, or symptoms such as pain or neurological deficits attributable to specific metastatic sites [36]. Brain metastases occur in approximately $50\%$ of patients with LCNEC, which is similar to the frequency in SCLC [36]. Although associated paraneoplastic syndromes are a feature of small cell carcinoma, they are rare in LCNEC.

Primary pulmonary melanoma usually occurs either as a unifocal polypoid obstructing lesion within the tracheobronchial tree or as a mass within the lung parenchyma [55]. The presenting symptoms are determined by the site and size of the tumour [26]. Pigment in sputum has been reported [88]. Careful clinical evaluation is necessary to assess the possibility of previous or current extrapulmonary primary melanoma, including cutaneous and extracutaneous melanomas.

# Imaging

LCNEC is generally radiologically similar to NSCLC (see Tumours of the lung: Introduction, p.20). On CT, most tumours are peripherally located, exhibiting expansive growth and irregular margins. Cavitation is uncommon. Hilar or mediastinal lymph nodes are commonly involved, but bulky lymphadenopathy is rare. Airway obstruction resulting in postobstructive pneumonia occurs in approximately $25\%$ of patients [37, 38].

# Spread

None

# Epidemiology

LCNEC accounts for approximately $3 \%$ of resected lung carcinomas [47]. There has been an increase in the incidence of LCNEC in various countries in recent epidemiological studies [10, 57, 8, 22], which could be related to better pathological recognition of this entity. LCNEC tends to occur more commonly in males and in people aged $> 6 5$ years, and $\geq 9 0 \%$ of patients are heavy smokers [36, 43].

Primary melanoma involving the respiratory tract is extremely rare and accounts for $< 0 . 1 \%$ of all lung malignancies [32, 44]. There is no racial predisposition. Primary melanomas of the lung represent $< 0 . 1 \%$ of all melanomas.

# Etiology

The major etiological factor for LCNEC is smoking, as supported by a high exonic mutation rate (8.6 non-synonymous mutations per 1 million bp) and a mutation signature strongly associated with smoking, characterized by G>T and $C { > } A$ transversions [14]. A distinct etiological pathway for LCNEC development in recent years is transformation from pre-existing EGFR-mutated adenocarcinoma after treatment with EGFR (HER1) inhibitors. Although such transformation predominantly occurs in the form of SCLC, instances of LCNEC transformation have been described [23, 34].

The etiology of primary melanomas of the lung is unknown.

# Pathogenesis

Recent studies using comprehensive next-generation sequencing revealed a high frequency of TP53 and RB1 inactivation in LCNEC [41, 33, 42, 11, 14]. Tumours largely comprise two distinct genomic subsets: one with an SCLC-like genomic profile (RB1/TP53 inactivation, MYCL [MYCL1?] amplification) and the other with an NSCLC-like genomic profile with predominant similarity to that of adenocarcinoma, characterized by STK11 (LKB1), KEAP1, KRAS, and other RAS pathway gene alterations [41,11,14]. Notably, by transcriptomic analysis, the SCLC-like and the NSCLC-like genomic subsets of LCNEC are distinct from conventional SCLC and NSCLC, respectively. In particular, the NSCLC-like genomic subset was associated with an ASCL1-high/DLL3-high/Notch-low expression profile (typical of SCLC), whereas the SCLC-like genomic subset was associated with an ASCL1-low/DLL3-low/Notch-high profile [14]. These molecular data indicate that LCNEC harbours a unique combination of genomic and transcriptional programs compared with both SCLC and NSCLC, supporting that it represents a distinct entity. These data also highlight the heterogeneity of LCNEC, potentially reflecting phenotypic convergence of tumour groups derived from distinct cells of origin. Molecular subsets cannot be reliably distinguished by histopathological examination alone, although SCLC-like (RB1-deficient) tumours tend to exhibit higher proliferation rates [41] and central location [62].

Although the cell of origin and pathogenesis of LCNEC are not known, a relationship with both SCLC and NSCLC is suggested in experimental models [31, 11]. In genetically engineered mouse models of small cell carcinoma arising primarily from neuroendocrine precursors, there are frequent subpopulations of LCNEC [13]. The close pathogenetic link of at least a subset of LCNEC with SCLC is in line with the observation that some human SCLCs are combined with LCNEC, and that cytotoxic chemotherapy may induce evolution of SCLC to LCNEC [5]. An alternative pathway of LCNEC development from non-neuroendocrine precursors has also been suggested in mouse models [2, 24], consistent with the observation of combined LCNEC with other histological types of NSCLC, as well as the transformation of lung adenocarcinoma to SCLC/LCNEC after EGFR inhibitor therapy [23, 34]. The potential derivation of LCNEC from different cells of origin may underlie recent genomic data revealing distinct subsets of LCNEC with SCLC-like and NSCLC-like genomic profiles. Conversely, the combination of LCNEC with conventional carcinoids is not an established phenomenon, and, unlike carcinoids, LCNECs do not arise in the setting of diffuse idiopathic pulmonary neuroendocrine cell hyperplasia [28].

Fig. Combined LCNEC with adenocarcinoma. C, D Synaptophysin (C) and napsin A (D) show strikingly dichotomous expression in LCNEC versus adenocarcinoma components, respectively.

Because melanocytes are not normally found in the tracheobronchial tree, it is difficult to explain the pathogenesis of primary melanomas of the lung: They may arise from neuroendocrine precursor cells in lung tissue [38] or from a melanoblast with aberrant migration from the neural crest to the pulmonary anlage [6]. It has been questioned whether the condition actually exists. The difficulty arises because an unrecognized primary cutaneous melanoma can occasionally undergo complete regression and disappear without a trace, while a lung metastasis that has arisen from it continues to grow and may eventually be detected as an apparently isolated focus of melanoma [20]. This hypothesis is supported by data from a recent study of patients with lung-only melanoma using next-generation sequencing, which showed that all cases had a dominant ultraviolet (UV) light mutation signature, suggesting they represented metastases from occult or unrecognized regressed skin primary melanomas rather than primary lung tumours [56].

# Macroscopic appearance

LCNECs are usually peripheral tumours with a size range of 10 to $> 100 \ \mathrm{mm}$ [37, 1, 25]. The cut surface is generally well circumscribed, tan-red, and necrotic.

Primary melanoma of the lung usually occurs as a polyp in a bronchus or as a circumscribed nodular mass within the lung parenchyma [12, 45]. Polyps usually show surface ulceration. The cut surface is often tan and haemorrhagic.

# Histopathology

LCNEC is characterized by an organoid nesting trabecular growth pattern, peripheral palisading, and rosettes—the patterns that can be encountered in other neuroendocrine tumours [49, 39, 47, 16]. The cytological characteristics of LCNEC are those of NSCLC, which include frequent moderate to prominent nucleoli and/or moderate to abundant cytoplasm with distinct cell borders such that intercellular membranes are visible. Cell size of LCNEC is usually more than the size of 3 small resting lymphocytes, exceeding that of SCLC. Chromatin is generally coarsely granular/stippled, but it may be vesicular or have intermediate quality. Some LCNECs have nuclear characteristics that are analogous to SCLC (granular chromatin without prominent nucleoli), but they qualify for the diagnosis of LCNEC by virtue of abundant cytoplasm. Necrosis is present in virtually all LCNECs and is typically extensive, with large confluent areas, but in some cases necrosis may be more limited. Associated stromal desmoplasia and marked stromal inflammation are common. By definition, mitotic counts in LCNEC are $> 10$ mitoses/ $2 \mathsf{mm}^2$, and generally they are substantially above this threshold (median: 70 mitoses $/ 2 \mathsf{mm}^2$), although less commonly LCNECs can have $< 30$ mitoses/2 $\mathsf{mm}^2$ [49, 50].

The tumour is usually formed by sheets or expansive nodules of large pleomorphic epithelioid or, less commonly, spindle malignant melanocytic cells [7]. The bronchial epithelium is unusually ulcerated. Necrosis is often present. The nuclei often have vesicular chromatin and prominent nucleoli [3]. An intramucosal in situ component with pagetoid scatter may be seen in the bronchial or tracheal epithelium adjacent to the invasive component [32]. This feature has also been reported in the vicinity of metastases [27]. However, an intramucosal component is lacking in many reported cases.

# Immunophenotype

Expression of a neuroendocrine marker is required for the diagnosis of LCNEC. Most tumours express two or three of the three standard neuroendocrine markers (synaptophysin, chromogranin A, CD56 (NCAM1)), and expression of at least one marker is typically diffuse [9]. However, if morphological features are convincing for LCNEC, any extent of expression of even a single neuroendocrine marker is accepted to support the diagnosis [59]. Caution should be exercised in interpreting cases labelling for CD56 alone because of its lower specificity [82]. Although additional neuroendocrine markers have been emerging in recent years, especially ASCL1 (hASH1) and INSM1, their role in the diagnosis of LCNEC remains to be clarified. Importantly, NSE should not be used, because of its low specificity [59].

Approximately $50\%$ of LCNECs express TTF1. Napsin A is usually entirely negative, but it is expressed weakly/focally in a minority of LCNECs [40, 4]. The combination of diffuse/strong TTF1 with negative or minimal napsin A is a distinctive feature of LCNEC (or SCLC), given that such a profile is uncommon for lung adenocarcinoma [40]. Labelling for squamous markers (p40/p63, CK5/6, 34βE12) can be seen in scattered cells in LCNEC, but diffuse labelling is not expected unless there is a combined LCNEC with squamous cell carcinoma [46, 41]. Pankeratins usually have strong and diffuse labelling in LCNEC; however, some LCNECs have granular (dot-like) labelling analogous to that of SCLC [91]. The Ki-67 proliferation index in LCNEC is consistently $>30\%$ and usually $>40\%$, with some cases reaching the typical range of SCLC, of $\geq 80\%$ [65,41,4].

The term “large cell carcinoma with neuroendocrine morphology" refers to rare tumours that have the morphology of LCNEC but lack demonstrable neuroendocrine marker expression by immunohistochemistry. Clinical data are limited but suggest aggressive behaviour similar to that of LCNEC [19, 60]. The term “non-small cell lung carcinoma with neuroendocrine differentiation" refers to pulmonary adenocarcinoma; squamous cell carcinoma; and large cell, spindle cell, or giant cell carcinomas that express neuroendocrine marker(s) in the absence of neuroendocrine morphology. As mentioned above, this occurs in $10{-}20\%$ of NSCLCs, most commonly adenocarcinomas. Given the lack of consistent data to support clinical relevance [86,87], staining for neuroendocrine markers in the absence of neuroendocrine morphology is not recommended [48, 59] (see Lung neuroendocrine neoplasms: Introduction, p.127).

# Differential diagnosis

LCNEC is distinguished from SCLC by the presence of prominent nucleoli and/or abundant cytoplasm, and in most cases by larger cell size. Nested architecture or larger cell size alone should not be used as the sole criterion for the diagnosis of LCNEC over SCLC [78,79,80]. Due to the spectrum of nucleocytoplasmic morphology that exists in both tumours, distinguishing LCNEC from SCLC can be challenging in a subset of cases, and there is no immunohistochemical or genetic marker that can reliably distinguish LCNEC from SCLC [39, 47]. The presence of any amount of SCLC in a predominant LCNEC qualifies a tumour for the diagnosis of combined SCLC and LCNEC (see Small cell lung carcinoma, p.139).

LCNEC is distinguished from pulmonary adenocarcinomas with a solid/nested or cribriform pattern and solid/nested large cell carcinoma by the presence of nuclear palisading and rosettes and by neuroendocrine marker expression. Although $10{-}20\%$ of NSCLCs lacking neuroendocrine morphology express neuroendocrine markers, such expression is usually focal and limited to a single marker [9,4,80].

LCNEC is distinguished from atypical carcinoid by a higher mitotic count $(>10$ mitoses $/2 \mathsf{mm}^2$) together with greater nuclear membrane irregularities, moderate to prominent nucleoli, and in most cases extensive necrosis. Tumours with carcinoid morphology that qualify as LCNEC due to mitotic counts exceeding 10 mitoses/2 $\mathsf{mm}^2$—usually only mildly—occur rarely as lung primary tumours, but they are relatively common in the metastatic setting [41,89,90]. Emerging data suggest that such tumours have genomic and clinical characteristics similar to those of carcinoid tumours [41], but more data are needed (see Lung neuroendocrine neoplasms: Introduction, p.127, and Carcinoid/neuroendocrine tumour of the lung, p.133).

Basaloid squamous cell carcinomas can have nests, palisading, and rosette-like structures. They are distinguished from LCNEC by consistent expression of squamous markers (p40/p63, high-molecular-weight cytokeratin 34βE12) even though some neuroendocrine markers (especially CD56) may be occasionally positive [46].

Thoracic SMARCA4-deficient undifferentiated tumours commonly express synaptophysin and may mimic LCNEC clinically and pathologically, especially in small biopsies [83,84,85,80]. They are distinguished from LCNEC by the loss of SMARCA4 (BRG1) expression and by several other distinctive morphological and immunohistochemical features (see Thoracic SMARCA4-deficient undifferentiated tumour, p.111).

# Cytology

The definitive diagnosis of LCNEC is difficult in cytology samples, but the diagnosis may be suggested in cases with cellular cell blocks that allow for the assessment of neuroendocrine morphology (palisading, rosettes) and immunohistochemistry. In smears, LCNECs exhibit intermediate to large cells containing variable nucleoli, frequently in a necrotic background [54, 58, 29, 21, 17, 18]. Key features distinguishing LNEC from other NSCLCs include a prominent single-cell component and frequent crush artefact with nuclear streaming that on low power may resemble SCLC [21]. Naked nuclei are common in air-dried preparations and can also mimic SCLC or haematolymphoid neoplasms. The distinction from SCLC relies on the same criteria as applied to histological specimens, including the presence of nucleoli and/or more abundant cytoplasm and overall larger cell size. Marked nuclear membrane irregularities, including nuclear notching, are more characteristic of LCNEC than SCLC, whereas cell spindling is uncommon.

FNA specimens of melanomas in the lung are usually obtained from metastases. Similar to those of melanomas occurring elsewhere, typical cytological appearances include a dissociated population of large epithelioid or sometimes spindle cells [35]. Occasional giant cells and intranuclear pseudoinclusions are often present. Melanin pigment can be present either in the cytoplasm of tumour cells or within pigmented macrophages.

# Diagnostic molecular pathology

Given that the effect on patient management and the tools of distinguishing LCNEC subsets remain investigational, performing molecular studies or RB1/p53 immunohistochemistry to identify subtypes of LCNEC in routine practice is not currently recommended. The prevalence of targetable NSCLC oncogenic drivers, such as EGFR and ALK alterations, is low in LCNEC, but such alterations do occur, and patients with these alterations may respond to targeted therapies [66,67,68].

A recent study of lung-only melanomas showed frequent mutations known to be associated with cutaneous melanoma, including BRAF, NRAS, NF1, KIT, and KRAS mutations and UV mutation signatures, suggesting that these neoplasms represented cutaneous melanoma metastases to the lung [56]. Previous studies reported that most cases lack BRAF, NRAS, or $K / T$ mutations, although one case harbouring an oncogenic NRAS mutation has been described [15, 53].

# Essential and desirable diagnostic criteria

The definitive diagnosis of LCNEC is possible in small biopsies provided that biopsy size is sufficient to assess neuroendocrine morphology and immunohistochemical markers [65,4]. In scant or disrupted samples of NSCLC where neuroendocrine morphology and/or marker expression is not definitive, the diagnosis of “non-small cell lung carcinoma, possible large cell neuroendocrine carcinoma” is appropriate. Historically, the diagnosis of LCNEC was considered rarely feasible in small biopsies [76]. However, with the recent trend of obtaining larger volumes of tissue in thoracic biopsies for molecular testing, the diagnosis is possible more often [36,9,64,77].

The distinction of LCNEC from SCLC in biopsies can be limited by crush artefact and extensive necrosis. In the absence of well-preserved areas to allow evaluation of cytological features, the diagnosis of “high-grade neuroendocrine carcinoma NOS" is appropriate. However, this term should be used as infrequently as possible. In such difficult cases, correlation with a concurrent cytology specimen may be helpful, because the morphology may be clearer than on the biopsy.

The distinction of LCNEC from other NSCLCs requires neuroendocrine morphology and immunohistochemical marker expression, which can be appreciated in most but not all biopsies of LCNECs [4], depending in part on the biopsy size [9]. A semiquantitative score based on a combination of morphological features, expression of neuroendocrine markers, and Ki-67 index $> 40\%$ allowed the distinction of LCNEC from other NSCLCs on biopsies with high sensitivity and specificity [4]. Labelling for $\geq 2$ neuroendocrine markers is substantially more common in LCNECs $(\geq 80\%)$ than NSCLCs $1\%$ to ${\sim}4\%$ [9]; however, because occasionally such expression does occur in non-neuroendocrine NSCLC, the diagnosis of LCNEC should only be made in the context of appropriate morphology.

Essential:

· Neuroendocrine morphology: organoid nesting, trabeculae, peripheral palisading, rosettes  
· Non-small cell cytology: prominent nucleoli and/or moderate to abundant cytoplasm, larger cell size than SCLC (> 3 lymphocytes), and chromatin may be either granular/stippled or vesicular  
· High proliferation rate: $> 10$ mitoses/2 $\mathsf{mm}^2$ with a median of 70 mitoses/2 $\mathsf{mm}^2$  
· Positive immunohistochemical staining for one or more neuroendocrine markers (other than NSE)

Desirable:

· Necrosis: generally in large confluent zones but may be limited to the centres of tumour nests  
· High Ki-67 index: $> 30\%$, generally $40{-}80\%$  
· Negative p40 immunohistochemistry

Essential:

· Demonstration of malignancy and melanocytic differentiation  
· Relation to mucosa of the lung

# Grading / Staging

Staging should be performed according to the eighth-edition TNM classification.

# Prognosis and prediction

LCNEC is a clinically aggressive disease. Patients with resected tumours are more likely to develop locally recurrent or metastatic disease and have shorter survival than patients with other histological subtypes of NSCLC (40–70% recurrence rate), even with stage I tumours [69,70,71,43,72]. In a nonsurgical metastatic setting, outcomes are generally similar to those of SCLC, with median survival times of approximately 10 months [36,51,22]. Adjuvant chemotherapy is suggested as potentially beneficial even for patients with stage I disease, but this requires clinical validation [69,71,72]. Treatment of stage IV CNEC is controversial, with both CLC and NSCLC chemotherapy regimens commonly used in practice [36]. Several recent studies suggest that selection of systemic therapies may be aided by identification of genomic subsets of LCNEC [41,63,64]; however, further validation is needed.

Activity of immunotherapy in LCNEC is not well established, but robust responses to checkpoint inhibitors have been reported in isolated cases [52, 30]. PDL1 is expressed in about 15% of LCNECs (5% with >50% of cells labelled), with equal distribution in RB1-mutated and wildtype tumours [73,74,75]. Clinical data on the predictive value of PDL1 specifically in LCNEC is lacking.

# References

3. Allen MS Jr, Drash EC. Primary melanoma of the lung. Cancer. 1968 Jan;21(1):154-9. PMID:5634845
