claims.claim: Bronchioloalveolar/bronchoalveolar carcinoma is considered obsolete terminology.
Invasive non-mucinous adenocarcinoma is a non-small cell lung carcinoma (NSCLC).
Invasive non-mucinous adenocarcinoma has morphological or immunohistochemical evidence of glandular differentiation.
8140/3 Invasive non-mucinous adenocarcinoma
8250/3 Lepidic adenocarcinoma
8551/3 Acinar adenocarcinoma
8260/3 Papillary adenocarcinoma
8265/3 Micropapillary adenocarcinoma
8230/3 Solid adenocarcinoma
2C25.0 Adenocarcinoma of bronchus or lung
Adenocarcinomas are more likely to be peripherally located in the lung than other types of NSCLC.
Adenocarcinomas may also occur in a central location in the lung.
Clinical manifestations of lung cancer relate to local growth of the primary lesion, locoregional extension into neighbouring structures or lymph nodes, distant spread, or paraneoplastic syndromes.
Adenocarcinomas are not different from other types of lung cancers, although paraneoplastic symptoms are less common than in squamous cell or small cell carcinoma.
Most patients with lung cancer present with locoregionally advanced or metastatic disease, apart from those identified through screening programmes.

Commonly occurring symptoms of lung cancer are cough, dyspnoea, haemoptysis, or symptoms related to distant spread, such as central neurological symptoms due to brain metastases or peripheral pain due to bone metastases.
Non-mucinous adenocarcinoma often appears initially on CT as a small pure ground-glass nodule, which evolves over several years into a part-solid and eventually completely solid tumour.
In some cases, non-mucinous adenocarcinoma first becomes visible as a completely solid nodule.
The histological correlate for the solid component of non-mucinous adenocarcinoma is typically invasive adenocarcinoma.
The non-solid component of non-mucinous adenocarcinoma corresponds to a lepidic growth pattern.

When adenocarcinoma contains both solid and non-solid components radiologically, the prognosis correlates more closely with the size of the solid component than with the size of the non-solid component.

Focal calcification can be seen in a small proportion of all lung cancer cell types, including adenocarcinoma, due to dystrophic calcification or incorporation of pre-existing postinfectious calcium deposits.
Multiple primary lung cancers, usually adenocarcinomas, are increasingly recognized and must be distinguished from metastatic disease.
FDG PET is important for detecting sites of local and distant metastatic disease.
The standardized uptake value for the tracer in the tumour has prognostic implications.
MRI may be useful in selected cases for assessing direct invasion of the chest wall, e.g., for superior sulcus tumours.
See Tumours of the lung: Introduction(p.20).

Invasion is defined as histological patterns other than a lepidic pattern, such as acinar, papillary, micropapillary, and/or solid, or less commonly invasive mucinous, colloid, fetal, and enteric adenocarcinoma.
Invasion is defined as myofibroblastic stroma associated with invasive tumour cells.
Invasion is defined as vascular or pleural invasion.
Invasion is defined as STAS.
Tumour STAS is defined as tumour cells within airspaces in the lung parenchyma beyond the edge of the main tumour.
STAS in adenocarcinoma is composed of three morphological patterns: micropapillary structures, solid nests of tumour cells filling airspaces, and discohesive single cells.
A number of independent studies have shown STAS to be a predictor of worse clinical outcome in resected lung adenocarcinoma as well as all major histological lung cancer types investigated.
In patients with STAS, limited resection probably contributes to a significantly higher risk of recurrence than lobectomy.
Because STAS is thought to represent a manifestation of tumour spread, it is not included in the total percentage of patterns or in tumour size for staging.
Lepidic adenocarcinoma is characterized by bland pneumocytic cells (type II pneumocytes or club cells) growing along the surface of alveolar walls.
In lepidic-predominant adenocarcinoma, there is an invasive component measuring > 5 mm in greatest dimension.
The invasive component in lepidic-predominant adenocarcinoma is defined as a histological pattern other than a lepidic pattern.
A diagnosis of lepidic adenocarcinoma rather than minimally invasive adenocarcinoma (MIA) should be made if the cancer invades the lymphatic system, blood vessels, or pleura.
A diagnosis of lepidic adenocarcinoma rather than MIA should be made if the cancer exhibits tumor necrosis.
A diagnosis of lepidic adenocarcinoma rather than MIA should be made if the cancer contains an invasive component > 5 mm.
A diagnosis of lepidic adenocarcinoma rather than MIA should be made if the cancer shows spread through airspaces (STAS).
If there are multiple foci of invasion, the invasive size can be estimated by multiplying the total size of the tumor by the total percentage of the non-lepidic or invasive components.
Lepidic growth can occur in metastatic tumors to the lung as well as in invasive mucinous adenocarcinomas (IMAs).
The specific term 'lepidic adenocarcinoma' refers to a non-mucinous adenocarcinoma showing lepidic growth as its predominant component.
Lepidic growth may be composed of neoplastic cells with more-marked nuclear atypia resembling that of the adjacent invasive patterns.

Compared with tumors with lepidic components resembling type II pneumocytes or club cells ('precursor lepidic'), those with marked nuclear atypia ('outgrowth lepidic') show increased Ki-67 proliferation index and p53 immunohistochemistry alterations.
It is not recommended to make a distinction between 'outgrowth lepidic' and 'precursor lepidic' in clinical practice due to lack of sufficient evidence.
ALK fusions are detected in various tumors of the lung, thyroid, esophagus, soft tissue, kidney, colorectum, and bladder.
EML4-ALK fusion is most frequently and almost exclusively found in carcinomas of the lung.
ALK rearrangement in lung cancer is strongly associated with adenocarcinoma histology, particularly with acinar and/or solid growth pattern.
EML4-ALK fusion accounts for >90% of ALK rearrangements in lung adenocarcinomas.
ALK rearrangement is frequent in never-smokers, but it is less associated with female sex.
The median age of patients with ALK-positive lung cancer is about 10 years younger than that of patients with ALK-negative cancer.
ALK-positive lung adenocarcinoma constitutes 4–5% of non-small cell lung carcinomas.
ALK rearrangement is predictive of response to ALK inhibitor treatment, but it is not a prognostic factor.
Lepidic adenocarcinoma may mimic papillary adenocarcinoma, particularly in areas of lepidic pattern in which there has been tangential sectioning of alveolar walls or lung parenchymal collapse.
Papillary adenocarcinoma shows a morphological spectrum based on size of papillary structures and nuclear grade, which corresponds to prognosis.
If papillary structures are noted filling airspaces surrounded by acinar or lepidic patterns, the tumor pattern is classified as papillary adenocarcinoma.
The micropapillary pattern consists of tumor cells growing in delicate, lace-like, narrow stacks of ≥ 3 cells, without fibrovascular cores.
When airspaces surrounded by lepidic, acinar, or papillary patterns contain micropapillary adenocarcinoma, the pattern should be classified as micropapillary.
Micropapillary adenocarcinoma can also show a stromal invasive pattern.
In Europe, smoking accounts for approximately 90% of lung cancers in men and 60% in women.
The strongest relationship between smoking and lung cancer is observed in squamous cell carcinoma and small cell lung carcinoma, followed by lung adenocarcinoma.
Heavy smokers have a 30-fold increased risk of dying of lung cancer by the age of 65 years compared with non-smokers.
Lung adenocarcinoma is associated with non-smokers, especially female non-smokers.

More-modern filtered cigarettes that contain lower tar and nicotine are thought to have contributed to the increased incidence of lung adenocarcinoma compared with other histological types of lung cancer.
Other tobacco smoking products, such as cigars, cigarillos, and pipes, are associated with a greater risk of lung cancer.
No clear association has been identified with vaping products and lung cancer.
Exposure to environmental tobacco smoke is a known risk factor for lung adenocarcinoma in non-smokers.
Indoor air pollution from wood fire, the burning of other solid fuels, and high-temperature cooking is associated with lung cancer among non-smokers.
Occupational exposures to silica, asbestos, heavy metals, polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons, and welding fumes contribute to lung cancer development.
Residential and occupational radiation exposure contributes to lung cancer development.
Air pollution contributes to lung cancer development.
Pulmonary tuberculosis contributes to lung cancer development.
HIV infection contributes to lung cancer development.
A family history of lung cancer contributes to lung cancer development.
Genome-wide association studies have shown genetic alterations at 15q25 (CHRNA5, CHRNA3, and CHRNB4) are associated with increased genetic susceptibility to lung cancer.
Genetic alterations of the TERT gene on 5p15 are associated with increased genetic susceptibility to lung cancer.
Genetic alterations at 6q23-q25 are associated with increased genetic susceptibility to lung cancer.
Germline mutations in TP53 (Li-Fraumeni syndrome) are associated with the development of lung adenocarcinoma.
EGFR p.T790M mutations are associated with the development of lung adenocarcinoma.
Some ERBB2 mutations are associated with the development of lung adenocarcinoma.
More than 80 carcinogens have been identified in cigarette smoke [127].
Carcinogens in cigarette smoke produce DNA adducts that can lead to mutations crucial to the initiation and progression of lung adenocarcinomas [55].
Molecular alterations are thought to accumulate in a stepwise manner in the development of lung adenocarcinomas [72].
Early alterations identified in preneoplastic lesions include KRAS and EGFR mutations, as well as loss of heterozygosity involving multiple tumour suppressor genes [134, 62].
Growth signalling pathways frequently involved in the pathogenesis of lung adenocarcinomas include the EGFR (HER1)/RAS/Pl3K pathway and the p53/RB1/p14/STK11 growth inhibitory pathway [20].
Inactivating mutations of the tumour suppressor gene TP53 occur in approximately 45% of lung adenocarcinomas [75, 41].
Activating mutations in KRAS occur in approximately 20% of lung adenocarcinomas and are strongly associated with smoking [120, 75].
Activating mutations involving EGFR occur more commonly in females, never-smokers, and Asians [87].
A small subset of adenocarcinomas are driven by genetic rearrangements such as ALK fusions [88, 165].
Patients with tumours driven by ALK fusions are younger than those with ALK-negative tumours, with no clear ethnic differences [88, 165].
Other factors involved in the pathogenesis of lung adenocarcinoma include inhibition of apoptosis, telomerase activation, and evasion of host immunity.
EGFR mutations occur in the kinase domain of the receptor tyrosine kinase, leading to constitutive activation of downstream signalling without ligand binding.
The two most common EGFR mutations – the point mutation at codon 858 (p.L858R) and the in-frame deletions in exon 19 – account for >90% of cases.
EGFR mutations are highly suggestive of lung adenocarcinoma.
Genetic alterations of other major lung cancer driver genes, such as KRAS, ALK, ROS1, BRAF, RET, and ERBB2, are mutually exclusive with EGFR mutations.
Rare families with germline EGFR mutations have higher risk of lung adenocarcinoma.
EGFR mutations in lung adenocarcinoma show ethnic differences, with prevalence ranges of 10–15% in people of European descent and 30–40% in Asians.
EGFR mutation is a prognostic factor as well as a factor predictive of response to EGFR tyrosine kinase inhibitor treatment.
EGFR mutations in exon 20, in-frame insertions, and (rarely) p.T790M mutations are associated with primary resistance to first- and second-generation EGFR tyrosine kinase inhibitors.
Acquisition of an additional p.T790M mutation is the most common cause of secondary resistance to EGFR tyrosine kinase inhibitors.
Within the tumour microenvironment, there are various nonneoplastic cells including stromal fibroblasts and endothelial cells, as well as immune cells including T cells, B cells, and macrophages.
Most invasive adenocarcinomas appear as grey-white nodules with central scarring fibrosis associated with anthracotic pigmentation and pleural puckering.
The peripheral lepidic component may result in a poorly defined border, and individual preserved alveolar spaces may be visible.
In fresh unfixed specimens, lepidic tumour components may be difficult to discern.
This pattern consists of polygonal tumour cells arranged in sheets, lacking recognizable lepidic, acinar (including cribriform), papillary, or micropapillary architecture.

To confirm glandular differentiation in solid tumours, immunohistochemical expression of a pneumocyte marker (TTF1 and/or napsin A) with negative p40 or histochemical demonstration of intracellular mucin (e.g. PASD or mucicarmine stain) in ≥ 5 tumour cells in each of two high-power fields (∼0.4 mm²) is required for diagnosis.
TTF1 immunohistochemistry is more sensitive than mucin stains to diagnose solid adenocarcinoma.
Both squamous and large cell carcinomas may show rare tumour cells with intracellular mucin.
The differential diagnosis of pulmonary adenocarcinomas involves distinction from other lung cancer types.
The differential diagnosis of pulmonary adenocarcinomas involves distinction from mesothelioma.
The differential diagnosis of pulmonary adenocarcinomas involves distinction of multiple lung primaries from intrapulmonary metastasis.
The differential diagnosis of pulmonary adenocarcinomas involves distinction from metastases from extrapulmonary sites.

For the distinction from other lung cancers, particularly squamous cell carcinoma or large cell neuroendocrine carcinoma (LCNEC), close attention to morphological features, as well as appropriate immunohistochemical stains, is usually sufficient.

For multiple lung adenocarcinomas, essential factors include comprehensive histological subtyping considering the predominant and secondary/tertiary growth patterns, the presence/absence of lepidic growth, cytological characteristics (e.g. cell size, clear cell change, nuclear atypia, mitotic count), and stromal features (e.g. degree of desmoplasia and inflammation) [52, 96].
Comparative molecular profiling can also be helpful in the differential diagnosis of multiple lung adenocarcinomas [53, 85, 5, 3].
A multidisciplinary approach with clinical, radiological, morphological, and molecular correlation may be needed in difficult cases of differential diagnosis of pulmonary adenocarcinomas [39].

Separation of lung adenocarcinomas from metastases from extrapulmonary sites is based on respective clinical and radiological information, along with comparative morphological criteria, immunophenotyping, and/or molecular testing.
Immunohistochemistry can be a useful ancillary technique to confirm a primary lung adenocarcinoma rather than a pulmonary metastasis.
Immunohistochemistry is required for the diagnosis of solid-predominant lung adenocarcinoma unless histochemical staining confirms the presence of intracytoplasmic mucin.
Immunohistochemistry is used to make a diagnosis of 'non-small cell lung carcinoma, favour adenocarcinoma' in a morphologically undifferentiated tumour in small biopsy or cytology specimens.
There is no immunohistochemical marker with 100% sensitivity or specificity for pulmonary adenocarcinomas.
The pneumocyte markers TTF1 and napsin A are positive in the majority of pulmonary adenocarcinoma cases (∼75-80%).
TTF1 is recommended when a limited panel is used.
The currently available antibody clones for TTF1 have a variable staining performance, with clone 8G7G3/1 being the most specific and SPT24 being more sensitive.
Even focal positivity for TTF1 is sufficient to favour glandular differentiation in the appropriate context.
Double positivity in the same tumour for TTF1 and p40 usually indicates an adenocarcinoma.
Adenosquamous carcinoma should also be considered when there is double positivity for TTF1 and p40.
When evaluating tumours, positive immunoreaction for TTF1 in entrapped pneumocytes must be carefully excluded.
Tumours other than lung adenocarcinomas also express TTF1, such as neuroendocrine tumours, thyroid tumours, and some female genital tract carcinomas.
Napsin A is expressed in tumours other than lung adenocarcinomas, such as renal cell carcinomas.
CK7 is not specific for pulmonary adenocarcinomas.
The diagnosis of adenocarcinoma is readily established based on cytology.
Histological subtyping of adenocarcinoma can be difficult.
Acinar or papillary structures are easier to detect in cell block sections.
Ethanol-fixed conventional smears or liquid-based preparations are superior in terms of nuclear and cytoplasmic details.
There has generally been a good correlation of morphological NSCLC subtyping between histological and cytological specimens.
Several morphological features of cell aggregates or individual cells have been shown to be associated with adenocarcinoma.
3D clusters are more common than true acinar or papillary formations in adenocarcinoma.

Typical features of individual non-mucinous adenocarcinoma cells are columnar cell shape, delicate or vacuolated cytoplasm, nuclear grooves and/or intranuclear cytoplasmic inclusions, and non-hyperchromatic vesicular nuclei with open chromatin and large nucleoli.
Cytopathologists often use typical features to suspect or even diagnose non-mucinous adenocarcinoma.

There is insufficient published evidence to allow for a definitive diagnosis of non-mucinous adenocarcinoma based on single or combined cytomorphological features other than unequivocal acinar or papillary formations.
In the presence of typical cytological features, it is reasonable to comment that a non-mucinous adenocarcinoma is likely.
Cytology specimens, particularly cell blocks, can be very useful for immunohistochemistry and molecular testing.
When specimens like large pleural effusions are obtained, the fluid available beyond what is needed for diagnosis should be processed to prepare cell blocks.

Non-mucinous lung adenocarcinomas usually consist of a complex admixture of architectural patterns (lepidic, acinar, papillary, micropapillary, and solid) and are classified into subtypes according to the predominant architectural pattern using comprehensive histological subtyping.
In resection specimens, each pattern should be estimated in a semiquantitative manner and recorded in 5-10% increments totaling 100%.
Using 5% increments allows for greater flexibility in choosing a predominant pattern when tumors have two patterns of relatively similar percentages.
Even small amounts of 5% of micropapillary or solid patterns have consistently been shown to correlate with poor prognosis.
The acinar pattern is characterized by glands that may be round to oval or have a more jagged outline with central luminal spaces surrounded by tumor cells.
The neoplastic glands invade through myofibroblastic stroma and/or replace the background alveolar architecture of the lung.
In lepidic adenocarcinoma, the background alveolar architecture is intact.
Acinar adenocarcinoma can occasionally be difficult to distinguish from lepidic adenocarcinoma, particularly when there is lung parenchymal collapse.

The presence of alveolar macrophages in residual compressed alveolar spaces, preservation of the underlying alveolar architecture, and absence of myofibroblastic stroma are helpful features in favor of lepidic adenocarcinoma.
Neoplastic glands invading through myofibroblastic stroma indicate acinar adenocarcinoma.
The cribriform pattern is defined by invasive back-to-back fused tumor glands with multiple spaces lacking intervening stroma.
Cribriform arrangements are regarded as a pattern of acinar adenocarcinoma that is associated with poor prognosis.
Clear cell and signet-ring changes are cytological features that can be seen in a variety of patterns, including acinar, papillary, solid, and micropapillary patterns.
Clear cell and signet-ring changes are not regarded to be specific patterns and are not included in comprehensive histological description.
It may be useful to record the percentage of tumor cells that show clear cell or signet-ring cytological features for comparison of histological features if these tumors recur or metastasize.

Reproducibility studies evaluating the major adenocarcinoma patterns for the distinction of adenocarcinoma in situ, MIA versus invasive adenocarcinoma, and measurement of invasion have shown variable results depending on whether the analysis focuses on difficult cases or on cohorts representative of routine clinical practice.
Reproducibility was moderate to substantial and fair to moderate in selected typical and difficult cases, respectively.
After a training session, reproducibility was shown to improve to almost perfect.
When cohorts representative of routine hospital cases were used, the agreements were good for distinction among adenocarcinoma in situ, MIA, and stage IA adenocarcinoma.

In resected adenocarcinomas of all histological patterns, 21% of predominant patterns with different prognostic scores were mismatched, suggesting that the classification scheme captured prognostic differences.
In studies evaluating whether invasion is present or not, for typical cases the reported agreement is moderate to good.
For difficult cases, the agreement regarding whether invasion is present ranges from slight to fair.
Agreement regarding measurement of invasion in typical cases has been reported to be excellent.
For difficult cases, agreement regarding measurement of invasion is reported to be slight or poor.

Malignant epithelial tumour with glandular differentiation by architecture (lepidic, acinar, papillary, micropapillary, cribriform) or a pure solid pattern with immunohistochemical expression of pneumocyte markers associated with adenocarcinoma (e.g., TTF1 or napsin A) or histochemical demonstration of intracytoplasmic mucin (e.g., PASD) in a solid tumour in ≥ 5 tumour cells in each of two high-power fields (∼ 0.4 mm²).
The tumour does not fulfil criteria of other types of adenocarcinoma (e.g., IMA), although minor components of other types may be present (up to 5%).
After comprehensive histological subtyping in 5-10% increments, the tumours are classified according to their predominant pattern.

Record the percentages of each histological pattern in pathology reports to document the predominant histological pattern (subtype) and any components of high-grade patterns to determine the tumour grade.
Immunohistochemical and/or molecular characterization of driver mutations is desirable.

The predominant histological pattern of lung adenocarcinoma is associated with prognosis, with lepidic-predominant tumours having the best prognosis, acinar and papillary-predominant tumours having an intermediate prognosis, and solid-predominant and micropapillary-predominant tumours having the worst prognosis.

A combination of predominant histological pattern plus worst pattern (≥ 20% of a high-grade solid, micropapillary, cribriform, or complex glandular pattern) significantly improved prediction of patient outcome in a training cohort and an independent validation cohort of early-stage resected lung adenocarcinomas.

The eighth edition of the Union for International Cancer Control (UICC)/ American Joint Committee on Cancer (AJCC) TNM staging system for NSCLC is used for staging all NSCLCs, including adenocarcinomas, to provide prognostic information that can assist in management decisions.
The updated TNM staging schema came into effect in January 2017 and was based on an IASLC database of 94,708 patients from 16 countries, with the majority of cases from Japan.
The most significant change in the new staging classification is the use of the solid or invasive tumour size for the cT or pT factor, respectively.
The use of the solid or invasive tumour size for the cT or pT factor only applies to non-mucinous lung adenocarcinomas with a lepidic component.

The IASLC database was unable to address the prognostic value of measuring the invasive tumour component of non-mucinous lung adenocarcinomas rather than total tumour size due to lack of pre-planned collection of these data elements.
The approach of measuring the invasive tumour component of non-mucinous lung adenocarcinomas rather than total tumour size is recommended by the UICC.
The approach of measuring the invasive tumour component of non-mucinous lung adenocarcinomas rather than total tumour size is supported by several retrospective studies.
Several oncogenic driver gene alterations are now known in lung adenocarcinomas, including in EGFR, KRAS, BRAF, ALK, ROS1, RET, NTRK1-3, MET, ERBB2 (HER2), MAP2K1 (MEK1), NRAS, and NRG1.
Targeted therapies are available for patients whose tumours harbour EGFR, ALK, ROS1, BRAF, MET, RET, and NTRK-family alterations.
Clinical trials of targeted agents for KRAS p.G12C mutations are showing significant promise.
Optimal management of patients with lung adenocarcinomas requires comprehensive analysis of a substantial number of oncogenes.
EGFR and ERBB2 mutations and ALK, ROS1, RET, and NTRK-family fusions are enriched in TTF1-positive adenocarcinomas arising in never smokers.
EGFR mutations are uniquely enriched in women and in eastern Asian populations.
KRAS, NRAS, and MAP2K1 (MEK1) mutations are highly enriched in smokers.
BRAF and MET mutations (specifically those leading to MET exon 14 skipping) are observed in both smokers and never-smokers.
About two thirds of lung adenocarcinomas harbour an oncogenic mutation or fusion within one of the aforementioned genes, all of which drive signalling through the RAS/MAPK pathway.
Of the remaining 'oncogene-negative' tumours, a small subset may contain rare fusion events, enriched in never-smokers.
Other 'oncogene-negative' tumours show a combination of tumour suppressor gene alterations and focal amplifications in members of the RAS/MAPK pathway, enriched in smokers.
High tumour mutation burden tends to correlate with smoking status.
Smoking mutation signatures are highly enriched in lung tumours, including adenocarcinomas.
Other mutation processes implicated in development and progression of adenocarcinoma include DNA cytosine deaminase (APOBEC) activity and, rarely, mismatch repair deficiency.

Hypermutation processes such as APOBEC, as well as acquisition of additional oncogenic mutations in genes such as Pik3CA and NF1, contribute to tumour clonal heterogeneity and adenocarcinoma progression.
There are no specific histological-molecular correlations in lung cancer.
Statistical associations have been drawn between KRAS mutations and IMA and tumours with a predominantly solid pattern.
Statistical associations have been drawn between EGFR mutation and lepidic-predominant non-mucinous adenocarcinomas.
Statistical associations have been drawn between ALK and ROS1 rearrangements and cribriform pattern, signet-ring cell features, and psammomatous calcification.
TNM classification and performance status significantly influence the choice of treatment and strongly predict survival.
Never-smoker status and female sex are favourable prognostic factors, independent from the stage of the disease.
Tumour size ≥ 25 mm and solid and micropapillary patterns are predictors of poor prognosis.
The prognosis for stage I lepidic-predominant adenocarcinoma is excellent.
Most of the tumours that recur have some high-risk factor, such as a close margin in limited resection and presence of a micropapillary component, or invasion of blood vessels and/or pleura.
In some studies, the prognostic significance is also preserved in more-advanced stages.
The relatively good prognosis of CT screen-detected lung cancer is driven by the predominance of early-stage adenocarcinomas with favourable histological features.
STAS has been shown to be associated with poor prognosis in multiple studies, particularly in patients who underwent limited resection.
The proposed IASLC grading system has demonstrated prognostic significance but needs further validation.
Lung tumours frequently harbour driver mutations, which are generally mutually exclusive.

EGFR mutation, ALK/ROS1/RET rearrangements, MET exon 14 skipping alterations, and BRAF p.V600E mutation are strongly correlated with the probabilities of response to targeted tyrosine kinase inhibitors.
Immune checkpoint inhibitors such as anti-PD1/PDL1 antibodies improve survival of a subset of advanced lung cancer patients, including patients with adenocarcinomas.
PDL1 expression and tumour mutation burden have been associated with treatment response but are not sufficient to ensure the response.

comprehensiveness.criterion: Complete Workflow from Presentation to Follow-up
comprehensiveness.explanation: Assess the guideline's coverage of the entire clinical workflow, from initial presentation and imaging to pathology, molecular diagnostics, integrated diagnosis, management, and follow-up. This ensures that all critical steps in patient care are addressed, providing a comprehensive framework for clinicians.

Evaluate the guideline's clarity in defining invasive non-mucinous adenocarcinoma, including ICD-O and ICD-11 coding. Accurate definitions and coding are essential for consistent diagnosis and reporting across clinical settings.

Examine the guideline's handling of subtypes and the continuum of disease, including criteria for distinguishing between subtypes and addressing NOS/NEC uncertainties. This is crucial for accurate diagnosis and treatment planning.

Assess the guideline's coverage of the anatomic distribution, dissemination patterns, and epidemiologic factors, including demographic stratification. This information is vital for understanding disease prevalence and risk factors.
Evaluate the guideline's coverage of clinical and imaging phenotypes across modalities such as CT, MRI, and PET. Comprehensive imaging guidance is essential for accurate diagnosis and staging.
Review the guideline's coverage of etiology and mechanisms, including germline risks and molecular pathways. Understanding these factors is crucial for risk assessment and targeted therapies.

Assess the guideline's coverage of diagnostic molecular pathology, including entity-defining alterations and co-alterations. Comprehensive molecular diagnostics are essential for personalized treatment strategies.

insight_depth.criterion: Mechanistic Depth and Cell-of-Origin Hypotheses
insight_depth.explanation: Evaluate the guideline's depth in linking cell-of-origin hypotheses with genetic and phenotypic characteristics. This understanding is crucial for developing targeted therapies and understanding disease progression.

insight_depth.criterion: Integration Across Diagnostic Modalities
insight_depth.explanation: Assess the guideline's ability to integrate imaging, histology, IHC, and molecular data into a coherent diagnostic framework. This integration is essential for accurate diagnosis and treatment planning.

insight_depth.criterion: Transparent Diagnostic Reasoning
insight_depth.explanation: Examine the guideline's clarity in outlining diagnostic decision points, branching logic, and common pitfalls. Transparent reasoning helps clinicians make informed decisions and avoid diagnostic errors.

insight_depth.criterion: Prognostic and Predictive Stratification Depth
insight_depth.explanation: Evaluate the guideline's depth in stratifying patients based on prognostic and predictive factors, with biologically plausible rationales. This stratification is crucial for personalized treatment and management.

insight_depth.criterion: Evidence Synthesis Maturity
insight_depth.explanation: Assess the guideline's ability to synthesize evidence, handle heterogeneity, and convey the strength of evidence. Mature evidence synthesis supports robust clinical recommendations.

insight_depth.criterion: Clinical Impact Reasoning
insight_depth.explanation: Review the guideline's reasoning on how specific biomarkers or histologic features influence diagnosis, management, and trial eligibility. Understanding clinical impact is essential for effective patient care.

accuracy_standards.criterion: Correct WHO Entity Naming and Grading
accuracy_standards.explanation: Ensure the guideline uses correct and current WHO entity naming and grading conventions, aligning with major updates. Accurate terminology is essential for consistent communication and reporting.

accuracy_standards.criterion: Accurate ICD-O/ICD-11 Coding Usage
accuracy_standards.explanation: Evaluate the guideline's use of ICD-O and ICD-11 codes, ensuring accuracy and clarity in handling synonyms and deprecated terms. Proper coding is crucial for data collection and analysis.

accuracy_standards.criterion: Molecular Terminology and Assay Interpretation
accuracy_standards.explanation: Assess the guideline's accuracy in molecular terminology, including variant naming and assay interpretation. Precise molecular language is vital for correct diagnosis and treatment decisions.

accuracy_standards.criterion: Internal Consistency and Non-Contradictory Criteria
accuracy_standards.explanation: Ensure the guideline maintains internal consistency, with no contradictory thresholds or criteria. Consistency is crucial for reliable diagnosis and management.

accuracy_standards.criterion: Diagnostic Accuracy Safeguards
accuracy_standards.explanation: Evaluate the guideline's safeguards against diagnostic inaccuracies, including assay limitations and pre-analytic variables. These safeguards are essential for minimizing diagnostic errors.

accuracy_standards.criterion: Consistency with Reporting Standards
accuracy_standards.explanation: Ensure the guideline aligns with widely used reporting standards and best practices for integrated diagnosis narratives. Consistent reporting supports effective communication and data sharing.

readability_utility.explanation: Assess the guideline's logical coherence and clinical flow, ensuring arguments and recommendations progress in a sensible order. Clear flow aids in understanding and applying the guideline.
Evaluate the guideline's use of evidence-backed statements, with quantitative anchors where appropriate. Evidence-based guidance supports informed clinical decision-making.

readability_utility.criterion: Actionable Guidance for Diagnosis and Management
readability_utility.explanation: Ensure the guideline provides explicit, actionable guidance for diagnosis, reporting, testing strategy, management, and follow-up. Actionable guidance is crucial for practical application.

readability_utility.criterion: High-Yield Presentation with Effective Use of Visuals
readability_utility.explanation: Assess the guideline's use of tables, figures, and algorithms to enhance decision-making. Effective visuals improve comprehension and application of complex information.
Ensure the guideline provides clear definitions of terms, thresholds, and required reporting elements for integrated diagnosis. Clarity in definitions supports accurate communication.

Evaluate the guideline's feasibility across different clinical settings, considering resource-stratified pathways and practical constraints. Feasibility ensures the guideline's applicability in diverse environments.

System.role_and_constraints: You are a medical guideline writer. Based on authoritative literature, write a chapter of an authoritative medical report. You need to follow the chapter format below.

content:
# Meningioma of the lung

# Definition

Pulmonary meningiomas are identical to meningothelial (arachnoidal) cell neoplasms that typically arise from dura mater of the CNS but are without a demonstrable CNS lesion.

# ICD-O coding / ICD-11 coding

9530/0 Meningioma

2F71.3 & XH11P5 Neoplasms of uncertain behaviour of trachea, bronchus, or lung & Meningioma, NOS

# Related terminology

None

# Subtype(s)

None

# Localization

There is no predilection for a specific anatomical site in the lung [9].

# Clinical features

Most cases are incidental, although some patients present with respiratory symptoms including haemoptysis [4]. Images show slow-growing, well-circumscribed nodules on CT, with high metabolic activity on FDG PET [4].

# Imaging

None

# Spread

None

# Epidemiology

There is a slight female predominance. The median age is 56 years (range: 18-108 years) [6, 16, 10].

# Etiology

Hypotheses include origin from pluripotent cells, heterotopic embryonic rests, and meningothelioid nodules [9, 4]. Although isolated meningothelioid nodules [5] lack mutation damage, multiple meningothelioid nodules show increased genetic alterations and may represent transition to neoplasia. Occasional meningiomas occur in the setting of multiple meningothelioid nodules [3].

# Pathogenesis

Unknown

# Macroscopic appearance

Most are solitary, well circumscribed, and firm, with a yellow-tan to grey cut surface. Size is markedly variable (median: $2\ 4\ \mathrm{mm}$; range: $4{-}150\ \mathrm{mm}$) [10, 15].

# Histopathology

Most tumours are well circumscribed and consist of a solid proliferation of tumour cells growing in whorls and lobular nests, most often with transitional or fibrous patterns. Rare examples of anaplastic [16, 7] and chordoid meningiomas are reported [13, 11, 1]. CNS criteria have been used for grading. Cells are positive for vimentin, EMA, PR, SSTR2A [8], and (rarely) S100 [10]. CD56 can be positive. Cytokeratin, other melanoma markers, and neuroendocrine markers are negative. Increased proliferation may be seen in anaplastic meningiomas [16]. Metastatic meningioma requires exclusion, as do spindle cell thymoma, solitary fibrous tumour, and monophasic synovial sarcoma, using appropriate immunohistochemistry [9]. Meningothelioid nodules are distinguished from meningiomas by their interstitial and frequently perivenular pattern of growth and ill-defined borders. In contrast to meningiomas, they typically lack a solid growth pattern and a sharply circumscribed border. Most meningothelioid nodules are $\leq 3\ \mathsf{mm}$ in size, but they can be as large as $5\ \textrm{mm}$ [14, 2].

# Immunophenotype

None

# Differential diagnosis

None

# Cytology

Tumours can rarely be diagnosed by FNA [3]. The presence of intranuclear inclusions and psammoma bodies can create confusion with thyroid carcinoma [10].

# Diagnostic molecular pathology

In the CNS, most meningiomas have allelic loss on chromosome 22 and NF2 mutations [8].

# Essential and desirable diagnostic criteria

Essential:

· A circumscribed solid proliferation of tumour cells growing in whorls and lobular nests, most often with transitional or fibrous patterns
· Absence of CNS disease with histological and immunohistochemical features of CNS meningioma
· Lack of predominant interstitial or perivenular distribution, ill-defined borders
· Size usually $> 4 ~ \mathsf { mm }$

# Grading / Staging

Not clinically relevant

# Prognosis and prediction

Most tumours show indolent growth. Rare aggressive behaviour is associated with atypical or anaplastic features (WHO grades II-III) [12, 16].

comprehensiveness.criterion: Complete workflow from presentation to follow-up
comprehensiveness.explanation: Assess the guideline's coverage of the entire clinical workflow, from initial presentation and imaging to pathology, molecular diagnostics, integrated diagnosis, management, and follow-up. This ensures that all stages of patient care are addressed, providing a comprehensive framework for clinicians managing pulmonary meningiomas.

Evaluate the guideline's clarity in defining pulmonary meningioma, including its boundaries, grading, and coding using ICD-O and ICD-11. Accurate definitions and coding are crucial for consistent diagnosis and reporting across clinical settings.

Examine the guideline's coverage of epidemiological data, including gender predominance and age distribution. Understanding demographic patterns aids in risk assessment and tailoring clinical approaches to specific patient populations.

Assess the guideline's description of clinical presentations and imaging findings across modalities like CT, MRI, and PET. Comprehensive imaging phenotype coverage is essential for accurate diagnosis and differentiation from other lung lesions.

Evaluate the guideline's detail in describing macroscopic and microscopic pathology, including immunophenotype. This ensures that pathologists have the necessary information to distinguish pulmonary meningiomas from other entities.

Review the guideline's coverage of molecular pathology, including entity-defining genetic alterations and progression-related events. Molecular diagnostics are crucial for confirming diagnosis and understanding tumor biology.

Assess the guideline's guidance on differential diagnosis, including similarities and distinguishing tests for pulmonary meningiomas versus other lung tumors. This helps prevent misdiagnosis and ensures appropriate treatment.

insight_depth.criterion: Mechanistic depth linking hypotheses and phenotype
insight_depth.explanation: Evaluate the guideline's exploration of mechanistic links between cell-of-origin hypotheses, genetic/epigenetic factors, and clinical phenotype. This depth of analysis aids in understanding tumor biology and potential therapeutic targets.

insight_depth.criterion: Integration across imaging, histology, IHC, and molecular
insight_depth.explanation: Assess the guideline's ability to integrate findings from imaging, histology, immunohistochemistry, and molecular diagnostics into a coherent diagnostic framework. This integration is crucial for accurate diagnosis and personalized treatment planning.

insight_depth.criterion: Transparent diagnostic reasoning and decision points
insight_depth.explanation: Review the guideline's clarity in outlining diagnostic reasoning, including decision points, branching logic, and common pitfalls. Transparent reasoning helps clinicians navigate ambiguous cases and resolve conflicts between modalities.

insight_depth.criterion: Prognostic and predictive stratification depth
insight_depth.explanation: Evaluate the guideline's depth in stratifying prognostic and predictive factors, with biologically plausible rationale and clear risk grouping. This stratification informs treatment decisions and patient counseling.

insight_depth.criterion: Evidence synthesis maturity and heterogeneity handling
insight_depth.explanation: Assess the guideline's maturity in synthesizing evidence, acknowledging limitations, and conveying strength-of-evidence. Effective synthesis supports robust clinical decision-making and guideline credibility.

insight_depth.criterion: Clinical impact reasoning for biomarkers and histologic features
insight_depth.explanation: Review the guideline's reasoning on how specific biomarkers or histologic features influence diagnosis, grade, management, surveillance, and trial eligibility. This reasoning is essential for translating diagnostic findings into clinical action.

accuracy_standards.criterion: Correct WHO entity naming and grading conventions
accuracy_standards.explanation: Evaluate the guideline's adherence to current WHO entity naming and grading conventions, ensuring alignment with authoritative classification standards. Accurate naming and grading are essential for consistent diagnosis and communication.

accuracy_standards.criterion: Accurate ICD-O/ICD-11 coding usage
accuracy_standards.explanation: Assess the guideline's precision in using ICD-O and ICD-11 codes, including handling of synonyms and deprecated terms. Accurate coding is crucial for standardized reporting and epidemiological tracking.

accuracy_standards.criterion: Molecular terminology accuracy and assay interpretation
accuracy_standards.explanation: Review the guideline's accuracy in molecular terminology, including variant naming and assay interpretation. Correct molecular language ensures reliable diagnostics and informs treatment decisions.

accuracy_standards.criterion: Internal consistency across document
accuracy_standards.explanation: Evaluate the guideline for internal consistency, ensuring no contradictory thresholds, criteria, or marker interpretations. Consistency is vital for maintaining guideline integrity and clinician trust.

accuracy_standards.criterion: Diagnostic accuracy safeguards
accuracy_standards.explanation: Assess the guideline's inclusion of safeguards against diagnostic inaccuracies, such as assay limitations, false positives/negatives, and sampling bias. These safeguards protect against misdiagnosis and inappropriate treatment.

accuracy_standards.criterion: Consistency with reporting standards and best practices
accuracy_standards.explanation: Review the guideline's alignment with widely used reporting standards and best practices for integrated diagnosis narratives. Consistent reporting enhances communication and facilitates multidisciplinary care.

readability_utility.explanation: Evaluate the guideline's logical organization and flow, ensuring arguments and recommendations progress in a clinically sensible order. Clear transitions prevent confusion and support effective clinical decision-making.
Assess the guideline's use of evidence-backed statements, supported by quantitative data such as sample sizes and effect sizes. Concrete data enhances credibility and aids in clinical application.

readability_utility.criterion: Actionability for diagnosis, testing, and management
readability_utility.explanation: Review the guideline's explicit guidance on diagnosis, testing strategy, management, and follow-up. Actionable recommendations are crucial for translating guideline content into clinical practice.

readability_utility.criterion: High-yield presentation with tables and algorithms
readability_utility.explanation: Evaluate the guideline's use of tables, figures, and algorithms that materially improve decision-making, such as diagnostic criteria summaries and decision trees. Effective visual aids enhance comprehension and utility.

readability_utility.criterion: Clear definitions and required reporting elements
readability_utility.explanation: Assess the guideline's clarity in defining terms, thresholds, and required reporting elements for integrated diagnosis. Clear definitions prevent misinterpretation and ensure consistent reporting.

Review the guideline's feasibility across different clinical settings, including resource-stratified pathways and turnaround time awareness. Practical constraints must be considered to ensure guideline applicability.

System.role_and_constraints: You are a medical guideline writer. Based on authoritative literature, write a chapter of an authoritative medical report. You need to follow the chapter format below.

content:
# Sclerosing pneumocytoma

# Definition

Sclerosing pneumocytoma is a tumour of pneumocytic origin composed of a dual population of surface cells resembling type II pneumocytes and round cells. The tumour demonstrates varying amounts of solid, papillary, sclerotic, and haemorrhagic patterns.

# ICD-O coding / ICD-11 coding

8832/0 Sclerosing pneumocytoma

2F0Z& XH7436 Benign neoplasms of respiratory and intrathoracic organs, unspecified & Sclerosing pneumocytoma

# Related terminology

Not recommended: sclerosing haemangioma (obsolete)

# Subtype(s)

None

# Localization

Sclerosing pneumocytoma is typically solitary and peripheral. Rarely, tumours are multiple; occur as an endobronchial mass; or are situated in the hilum, visceral pleura, or mediastinum [10, 16].

# Clinical features

Patients are typically asymptomatic, with the tumour often discovered incidentally. Radiographs show a solitary circumscribed mass, which may rarely be calcified or cystic [24, 25, 28, 33, 31].

# Imaging

None

# Spread

None

# Epidemiology

Sclerosing pneumocytoma occurs in a wide age range (11-80 years), with female predominance. The incidence is higher in eastern Asian populations and rare among individuals of European descent [8, 11, 15, 17, 21].

# Etiology

The tumour is thought to derive from primitive respiratory epithelium [8, 11, 15, 7, 1].

# Pathogenesis

Although sclerosing pneumocytoma is presumably derived from primitive pulmonary epithelium, the pathogenesis is unknown. However, molecular data have demonstrated the same monoclonal pattern in both the round and surface cells, consistent with a true neoplasm [32]. AKT1 internal tandem duplications (ITDs), point mutations, and short indels have been identified in nearly all sclerosing pneumocytomas. These mutations are mutually exclusive and occur in the Pleckstrin homology domain, a critical component in the activation of the AKT1 protein [14, 36]. A BRAF p.V600E mutation has been reported in one case [13].

# Macroscopic appearance

The tumours are well-circumscribed masses that are solid and show a variegated grey-tan to yellow on cut section, with foci of haemorrhage. Cystic degeneration and calcification may be evident.

# Histopathology

The key feature of sclerosing pneumocytoma is the presence of two cell types: cuboidal surface cells and round stromal cells, both of which are considered neoplastic. The surface cells are cuboidal and morphologically similar to type II pneumocytes. The round cells are small, with well-defined borders and central bland nuclei with fine chromatin and scant small nucleoli. The tumours typically have a combination of growth patterns: papillary, sclerotic, solid, and haemorrhagic. In the papillary pattern, complex papillary structures are covered by surface cells overlying a core of round cells as opposed to true fibrovascular cores. The sclerotic area is composed of fibrosis with hyalinized collagen, in which variable inflammatory cells, haemosiderin deposition, cholesterol clefts, calcification, and ossification may be seen. The solid pattern is composed of sheets of round cells, in which there may be tubular or incomplete adenoid structures surrounded by surface cells. In the haemorrhagic pattern, large blood-filled spaces may be present, lined by cuboidal epithelial cells [8, 11, 17]. Fusion of surface cells into multinucleated giant cells, formation of sclerotic papillae due to the replacement of round cells by collagen, delicate papillary structures mimicking true papillae with fibrovascular cores, a spindle or signet-ring morphology of the round cells, and myxoid matrix surrounding round cell cords may be present as diagnostic pitfalls [19, 20].

# Immunophenotype

Both surface and round cells are positive for EMA and TTF1. Pancytokeratin, CAM5.2, CK7, and napsin A stain the surface cells diffusely, but the round cells are usually negative or weakly positive. Neuroendocrine markers are negative. ER and PR staining has also been reported in some cases in the round cells [11, 5, 7, 2, 26, 29, 30].

# Differential diagnosis

The differential diagnosis consists primarily of carcinoid tumours and papillary adenocarcinoma. Rarely, either may coexist with sclerosing pneumocytoma [20, 34, 9]. In general, the multiple growth patterns, presence of bland cytological features, a dual cell population, and the characteristic immunostaining profile discriminate sclerosing pneumocytoma from other entities. However, accurate diagnosis on small biopsy, cytology, or frozen section may be difficult [6, 22, 35, 4].

# Cytology

FNA may show a dual cell population, as well as hyalinized stromal fragments. The round cells are small, round to spindle shaped, and arranged in cohesive papillary clusters or flat sheets. Nuclear pleomorphism, overlap, grooves, and inclusions may be seen. Prominent nucleoli and hyperchromasia may be present [23, 3, 12, 18].

# Diagnostic molecular pathology

Molecular data have demonstrated the same monoclonal pattern in both the round and surface cells, consistent with a true neoplasm [32]. AKT1 alterations are present in nearly all sclerosing pneumocytomas and may be useful for diagnosis in selected cases.

# Essential and desirable diagnostic criteria

Essential:

· Well-circumscribed lesion composed of a dual population of surface and round cells
· A mixture of papillary, sclerotic, solid, and haemorrhagic growth patterns is usually present

Desirable:

· Distinctive immunostaining profile: TTF1 and EMA being positive in surface and round cells and cytokeratin staining being positive in the surface cells but weak or absent in the round cells

# Grading / Staging

Not relevant

# Prognosis and prediction

None

claims.claim: Sclerosing pneumocytoma is a tumour of pneumocytic origin.
Sclerosing pneumocytoma is composed of a dual population of surface cells resembling type II pneumocytes and round cells.
The tumour demonstrates varying amounts of solid, papillary, sclerotic, and haemorrhagic patterns.
8832/0 Sclerosingpneumocytoma
2F0Z& XH7436 Benign neoplasms of respiratory and intrathoracic organs, unspecified & Sclerosing pneumocytoma
Sclerosing haemangioma is not recommended and is considered obsolete.
Patients are typically asymptomatic.
The tumour is often discovered incidentally.
Radiographs show a solitary circumscribed mass.
The mass may rarely be calcified.
The mass may rarely be cystic.
Sclerosing pneumocytoma is typically solitary and peripheral.
Rarely, sclerosing pneumocytoma tumours are multiple.
Sclerosing pneumocytoma can occur as an endobronchial mass.
Sclerosing pneumocytoma can be situated in the hilum.
Sclerosing pneumocytoma can be situated in the visceral pleura.
Sclerosing pneumocytoma can be situated in the mediastinum.
The tumour is thought to derive from primitive respiratory epithelium
Sclerosing pneumocytoma occurs in a wide age range (11-80 years).
Sclerosing pneumocytoma has a female predominance.
The incidence of sclerosing pneumocytoma is higher in eastern Asian populations.
Sclerosing pneumocytoma is rare among individuals of European descent.
Sclerosing pneumocytoma is presumably derived from primitive pulmonary epithelium, but the pathogenesis is unknown.
Molecular data have demonstrated the same monoclonal pattern in both the round and surface cells, consistent with a true neoplasm.
AKT1 internal tandem duplications (ITDs), point mutations, and short indels have been identified in nearly all sclerosing pneumocytomas.
These mutations are mutually exclusive and occur in the Pleckstrin homology domain, a critical component in the activation of the AKT1 protein.
A BRAF p.V600E mutation has been reported in one case.
The tumours are well-circumscribed masses that are solid and show a variegated grey-tan to yellow on cut section.
The tumours show foci of haemorrhage.
Cystic degeneration may be evident in the tumours.
Calcification may be evident in the tumours.
Both surface and round cells are positive for EMA and TTF1.
Pancytokeratin, CAM5.2, CK7, and napsin A stain the surface cells diffusely.
The round cells are usually negative or weakly positive for Pancytokeratin, CAM5.2, CK7, and napsin A.
Neuroendocrine markers are negative.
ER and PR staining has been reported in some cases in the round cells.
The differential diagnosis consists primarily of carcinoid tumours and papillary adenocarcinoma.
Rarely, either carcinoid tumours or papillary adenocarcinoma may coexist with sclerosing pneumocytoma.
The multiple growth patterns, presence of bland cytological features, a dual cell population, and the characteristic immunostaining profile discriminate sclerosing pneumocytoma from other entities.
Accurate diagnosis on small biopsy, cytology, or frozen section may be difficult.
The key feature of sclerosing pneumocytoma is the presence of two cell types: cuboidal surface cells and round stromal cells, both of which are considered neoplastic.
The surface cells are cuboidal and morphologically similar to type II pneumocytes.
The round cells are small, with well-defined borders and central bland nuclei with fine chromatin and scant small nucleoli.
The tumours typically have a combination of growth patterns: papillary, sclerotic, solid, and haemorrhagic.
In the papillary pattern, complex papillary structures are covered by surface cells overlying a core of round cells as opposed to true fibrovascular cores.
The sclerotic area is composed of fibrosis with hyalinized collagen, in which variable inflammatory cells, haemosiderin deposition, cholesterol clefts, calcification, and ossification may be seen.
The solid pattern is composed of sheets of round cells, in which there may be tubular or incomplete adenoid structures surrounded by surface cells.
In the haemorrhagic pattern, large blood-filled spaces may be present, lined by cuboidal epithelial cells.

Fusion of surface cells into multinucleated giant cells, formation of sclerotic papillae due to the replacement of round cells by collagen, delicate papillary structures mimicking true papillae with fibrovascular cores, a spindle or signet-ring morphology of the round cells, and myxoid matrix surrounding round cell cords may be present as diagnostic pitfalls.
FNA may show a dual cell population, as well as hyalinized stromal fragments.
The round cells are small, round to spindle-shaped, and arranged in cohesive papillary clusters or flat sheets.
Nuclear pleomorphism, overlap, grooves, and inclusions may be seen.
Prominent nucleoli and hyperchromasia may be present.
Molecular data have demonstrated the same monoclonal pattern in both the round and surface cells, consistent with a true neoplasm.
AKT1 alterations are present in nearly all sclerosing pneumocytomas.
AKT1 alterations may be useful for diagnosis in selected cases.
Well-circumscribed lesion composed of a dual population of surface and round cells
A mixture of papillary, sclerotic, solid, and haemorrhagic growth patterns is usually present
Distinctive immunostaining profile: TTF1 and EMA being positive in surface and round cells
Cytokeratin staining being positive in the surface cells but weak or absent in the round cells

comprehensiveness.explanation: The guideline should cover the entire workflow from clinical presentation to integrated diagnosis, including pathology and molecular diagnostics. This ensures that all aspects of sclerosing pneumocytoma are addressed, providing a comprehensive understanding of the disease and facilitating accurate diagnosis and management.

The guideline must provide a clear definition of sclerosing pneumocytoma, including ICD-O and ICD-11 coding. This ensures that the entity is correctly identified and classified, which is crucial for diagnosis, research, and epidemiological studies.

The guideline should detail the typical and atypical locations of sclerosing pneumocytoma and provide epidemiological data, including demographic stratification. This information is essential for understanding the disease's prevalence and guiding clinical suspicion.

Comprehensive coverage of macroscopic and microscopic pathology, along with immunophenotype details, is essential for accurate diagnosis. This includes the description of cell types, growth patterns, and immunostaining profiles.

The guideline should cover all known molecular alterations associated with sclerosing pneumocytoma, such as AKT1 mutations. This is crucial for confirming diagnosis and understanding the molecular basis of the disease.

The guideline must provide a comprehensive list of differential diagnoses, with guidance on distinguishing tests. This helps in differentiating sclerosing pneumocytoma from other similar entities, reducing diagnostic errors.
While prognosis is generally favorable, the guideline should discuss any known prognostic or predictive factors, even if limited, to guide clinical management and follow-up strategies.

insight_depth.criterion: Mechanistic Depth Linking Genetics and Phenotype
insight_depth.explanation: The guideline should explore the mechanistic links between genetic alterations, such as AKT1 mutations, and the phenotypic presentation of sclerosing pneumocytoma. This enhances understanding of the disease's pathogenesis.

insight_depth.criterion: Integration Across Imaging, Histology, and Molecular Data
insight_depth.explanation: A deep integration of imaging, histological, and molecular data into a coherent diagnostic framework is essential. This ensures that all available information is used to arrive at an accurate diagnosis.

insight_depth.criterion: Transparent Diagnostic Reasoning
insight_depth.explanation: The guideline should clearly outline the diagnostic reasoning process, including decision points and common pitfalls. This transparency aids clinicians in making informed diagnostic decisions.

insight_depth.criterion: Prognostic Stratification with Biological Rationale
insight_depth.explanation: Even though prognosis is generally good, any stratification should be based on a biologically plausible rationale, providing clear risk grouping for clinical decision-making.

insight_depth.criterion: Evidence Synthesis Maturity
insight_depth.explanation: The guideline should demonstrate mature evidence synthesis, acknowledging limitations and conveying the strength of evidence. This helps in understanding the reliability of the recommendations.

insight_depth.criterion: Clinical Impact Reasoning
insight_depth.explanation: The guideline should discuss how specific biomarkers or histologic features influence clinical decisions, such as management and surveillance strategies, enhancing its practical utility.

accuracy_standards.criterion: Correct WHO Entity Naming and Classification
accuracy_standards.explanation: The guideline must use the correct and current WHO naming conventions and classification for sclerosing pneumocytoma, ensuring alignment with international standards.

accuracy_standards.criterion: Accurate ICD-O/ICD-11 Coding Usage
accuracy_standards.explanation: Accurate use of ICD-O and ICD-11 codes is essential for proper classification and reporting. The guideline should clearly handle synonyms and deprecated terms.

accuracy_standards.criterion: Molecular Terminology and Assay Interpretation Accuracy
accuracy_standards.explanation: The guideline should use precise molecular terminology and provide correct interpretation of assays, such as AKT1 mutations, to ensure diagnostic accuracy.

accuracy_standards.criterion: Internal Consistency Across Document
accuracy_standards.explanation: The guideline should maintain internal consistency, avoiding contradictory thresholds or criteria. This ensures clarity and reliability of the information provided.

accuracy_standards.criterion: Diagnostic Accuracy Safeguards
accuracy_standards.explanation: The guideline should address potential diagnostic pitfalls, such as assay limitations and sampling bias, to safeguard against false positives/negatives.

accuracy_standards.criterion: Consistency with Reporting Standards
accuracy_standards.explanation: The guideline should align with widely used reporting standards and best practices for integrated diagnosis narratives, ensuring clarity and uniformity in reporting.

readability_utility.explanation: The guideline should be organized in a logical sequence, with clear transitions between sections. This ensures that the information is easy to follow and understand.
Key claims in the guideline should be supported by quantitative evidence, such as sample sizes or effect sizes, to enhance credibility and reliability.

readability_utility.criterion: Actionable Guidance for Diagnosis and Management
readability_utility.explanation: The guideline should provide explicit guidance on diagnosis, reporting, testing strategy, and management, making it practical and useful for clinicians.
The use of tables, figures, and algorithms should be employed to summarize diagnostic criteria and decision-making processes, enhancing the guideline's utility.
The guideline should clearly define terms, thresholds, and required reporting elements for integrated diagnosis, ensuring clarity and consistency in communication.
The guideline should consider resource-stratified pathways and practical constraints, ensuring its applicability across various clinical settings.

System.role_and_constraints: You are a medical guideline writer. Based on authoritative literature, write a chapter of an authoritative medical report. You need to follow the chapter format below.

content:
# Cardiac myxoma

# Definition

Cardiac myxoma is a benign neoplasm consisting of stellate, ovoid, or plump spindle cells within a vascular myxoid matrix.

Sheppard MN G

Cardiac fibroma is a benign mesenchymal tumour, composed of bland fibroblasts in a variably collagenized stroma.

# ICD-O coding / ICD-11 coding

8840/0 Myxoma, NOS

2F01 & XH6Q84 Benign neoplasm of intrathoracic organs & Myxoma, NOS

8810/0 Fibroma, NOS

2F01 & XH8E66 Benign neoplasm of intrathoracic organs & Fibroma, NOS

# Related terminology

Not recommended: left atrial myxoma; atrial myxoma.

Not recommended: cardiac fibromatosis; fibrous hamartoma; fibroelastic hamartoma.

# Subtype(s)

None

None

# Localization

Cardiac myxomas are intracavitary endocardial lesions, most often arising in the left atrium (in $80{-}90\%$ of cases) in the region of the fossa ovalis [33, 68]. The tumours have also been reported in all other cardiac chambers, and very rare examples of valvular myxomas have been described [17, 52, 67]. About half of the cardiac myxomas that arise in the setting of Carney complex occur outside of the left atrium, whereas $<20\%$ occur outside of the left atrium in non-syndromic circumstances [33]. Thus, a myxoma occurring in a non-left atrial site should raise suspicion for underlying Carney complex. The tumours can be multicentric, raising further suspicion for Carney complex [60].

Nearly all cardiac fibromas arise in the ventricular septum; for the remaining cases, the most common site is the ventricular free wall (localization in the left ventricle is 5 times as common as in the right ventricle), followed by the right and left atria [59].

# Clinical features

The clinical presentation of cardiac myxoma is diverse and (as is the case with most cardiac tumours) primarily dependent on tumour location, size, shape, mobility, and rate of growth. Patients with cardiac myxomas may be entirely asymptomatic ($10-20\%$), have symptoms of heart failure, or rarely present with sudden death [30].

Cardiac myxomas can produce several cardiac symptoms, including syncope, dyspnoea, chest pain, and palpitations [53]. Most cardiac symptoms are related to tumoural obstruction of blood flow through the heart. Occasionally, atrial myxomas may obstruct the atrioventricular valve orifice, causing a diastolic tumour plop on auscultation. Some authors have noted an association between septal tumour location and heart failure, and between extraseptal location and neurological events [58].

Constitutional symptoms, which can include fever, weight loss, and arthralgias, are frequently associated with large or multiple tumours [16]. Clinical signs such as anaemia, leukocytosis, and elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate may also be observed. Many of the phenomena are believed to be related to cytokine elaboration by the tumours themselves, including of IL-6, I-4, I12, interferon $\gamma$, and TNF [55]. A wide range of paraneoplastic syndromes (e.g. vasculitis/vasculopathy, pancreatitis, demyelinating neuropathy, and epistaxis) have been reported [43, 50, 55].

Fig. Cardiac myxoma gross morphology.

Fig. Cardiac myxoma immunohistochemistry. Note that the background inflammatory cells have retained expression.

Fig. Consequence of intratumoural haemorrhage. Gamna-Gandy bodies within a cardiac myxoma. Intratumoural giant cells within a cardiac myxoma.

Less than $10\%$ of cardiac myxomas arise in the setting of Carney complex, an autosomal dominant disorder characterized by the constellation of myxomas (cardiac or otherwise), endocrinopathy (Cushing syndrome and acromegaly), and spotty skin pigmentation. See Carney complex (p.478) for more detailed information on this syndrome.

Due to the predilection of cardiac fibroma for the ventricular septum, one third of patients present with arrhythmias [5, 22]. Cardiac fibromas may cause obstruction or abnormal valvular function, leading to heart failure. Patients with asymptomatic tumours may present with a heart murmur, or the tumour may be identified incidentally on imaging.

# Imaging

Transthoracic echocardiography is the diagnostic imaging modality of choice [9]. Echocardiographically, a cardiac myxoma usually appears as a mobile endocardial mass of irregular shape, most often arising in the left atrium in the region of the fossa ovalis. Areas of variable echogenicity and calcification may be seen. More advanced echocardiographical modalities, such as contrast and 3D echocardiography, may help provide a more complete characterization of the tumour appearance, size, and location [34, 38, 3].

If a definitive tumour stalk is not visible, cardiac MRI and CT may help to exclude the myocardial infiltration seen in malignant processes [18]. Most frequently, myxomas are T1-isointense and T2-hyperintense, owing to their gelatinous nature and high extracellular water content [13]. Myxomas usually show no or weak contrast enhancement on CT [13]

# Spread

Spread to distant sites through embolization, after which they can infiltrate the arterial wall and produce histologically identical tumours at the embolization site. Myxoma cell infiltration can result in arterial wall weakening and aneurysm formation, even years after resection of the primary tumour [25, 64, 51].

# Epidemiology

Cardiac myxoma is the second most common primary cardiac neoplasm of adults, after papillary fibroelastoma. It occurs in all age groups (having been described prenatally through the age of 97 years), but it manifests most commonly between the fourth and seventh decades of life [47]. Cardiac myxomas occur, on average, twice as commonly in women as in men [66], but this female predominance is less pronounced in patients aged $> 65$ years [8].

More than $90\%$ of cardiac myxomas arise in an isolated fashion. The remaining cases arise in association with Carney complex. Syndromic tumours generally occur in younger patients, with no sex predominance [19].

Most cardiac fibromas ($90\%$) occur in children, and they are the second most common cardiac tumour of childhood after rhabdomyoma. One third arise before the age of 1 year, without sex predilection [62].

# Etiology

Villiform tumours and tumours with abundant myxoid stroma are more likely to be associated with embolization. Tumour degeneration by overexpression of matrix metalloproteinases may hasten embolization [40]. Embolization of associated surface thrombi can also occur [27]. Embolization can result in pulmonary embolism, acute myocardial ischaemia, or stroke [29, 61].

The histogenesis of cardiac myxomas has been controversial since they were first described. Initially, they were thought to be reactive lesions or organizing thrombi; however, their locations within the heart, their genetics, and their overall histology are more characteristic of a neoplastic process. The origin of the myxoma cell (or lepidic cell) has also proved enigmatic.

Myxomas can recur locally, a phenomenon that is usually explained by incomplete resection [33, 46]. They can also

The PTCH1 gene appears to play a role in at least a subset of cardiac fibromas. Mutations may be somatic, resulting in sporadic tumour formation, or within a syndromic context, associated with Gorlin syndrome (naevoid basal cell carcinoma syndrome). Gorlin syndrome is an autosomal dominant disorder caused by germline PTCH1 mutation, characterized by multiple basal cell carcinomas, skeletal anomalies, distinct facies, and intracranial calcifications.

# Pathogenesis

Recent molecular characterization studies have revealed protein expression profiles similar to those seen during endocardial-mesenchymal transformation of the endocardial cushion [28, 39, 15]. Whether these cells ultimately derive from developmental remnants or from redifferentiation of a terminally differentiated cardiac cell is not yet clear.

Although initially thought to play a role only in syndromic tumour formation [32, 35], PRKAR1A, encoding the cAMP dependent protein kinase type Iα regulatory subunit, has also been implicated in a subset of patients without Carney complex [33, 63]. Cytogenetic data on myxoma are limited, but 15 previously analysed myxomas contained clonal numerical or non-clonal structural abnormalities. Cytogenetic analyses of 3 cases of Carney complex revealed similar chromosomal patterns as in non-syndromic cases [14, 20].

Unknown

# Macroscopic appearance

The gross appearance of cardiac myxomas is quite variable. The tumours can measure $< 10 \mathrm{~mm}$ or $> 150~\mathrm{mm}$ [12, 44, 48, 70]. They may be either sessile and broad-based or pedunculated—arising from a stalk. Tumour stalks may occasionally be long, resulting in free mobility of the tumour within the chamber or through a valvular orifice. Two basic gross appearances have been described: solid and viliform. Tumours of the solid type may be globular or elongated, with a smooth, shiny, and sometimes undulant surface. Tumours of the villiform type (as the name would imply) have irregular, often friable, extensions. The cut surface is variegated, usually owing to the myxoid tissue and areas of intratumoural haemorrhage, which is common. Areas of necrosis, cystic change, fibrosis, and calcification can also be seen [2, 42]. Rarely, cardiac myxomas show extensive calcifications with a stone-like appearance—the so-called lithomyxoma [4].

Cardiac fibromas are often large (mean: $50~\mathsf{mm}$), well-circumscribed, solitary tumours with a white whorled appearance. Although rare, multiple tumours have been reported. Calcification is common in this tumour and helps distinguish it from rhabdomyoma, particularly on imaging [56].

# Histopathology

Cardiac myxomas exhibit considerable histological variability, sometimes even in different regions of the same tumour. The one defining characteristic is the presence of the so-called myxoma cell (or lepidic cell), a cytologically bland cell with eosinophilic cytoplasm and an oval or round nucleus. These stellate, ovoid, or plump spindle cells may occur singly or in groups. When in groups, the cells can form cords, nests, or rings. Rings frequently occur around capillaries or small vessels and have been termed perivascular rings.

The cells reside in a myxoid matrix that is rich in mucopolysaccharides, with variable amounts of proteoglycan, collagen, and elastin [24]. The matrix shows strong reactivity with Alcian blue, is resistant to hyaluronidase, and shows patchy positivity with mucicarmine and PAS stains. The background may also contain variable numbers of inflammatory cells and (rarely) multinucleated giant cells [10, 21].

Larger, thick-walled vessels are often present near the stalk or base of the lesion, while smaller vessels are present throughout [39]. Haemorrhage, both recent and remote, is frequently encountered and is probably a product of the vascularity and trauma throughout the cardiac cycle. Remote haemorrhage can manifest as haemosiderin-laden macrophages and/or iron encrustation of intratumoural elastic fibres (Gamna-Gandy bodies). Secondary degenerative changes such as fibrosis, cystic change, necrosis, thrombosis, calcification, and metaplastic bone formation can also be present [4].

Glandular elements are very rare—identified in $< 3\%$ of all myxomas [69]. The glands are predominantly located at the base of the tumour, without local infiltration. Similarly, diffuse large B-cell lymphomas, some associated with EBV, have been described in the background of otherwise classic cardiac myxomas (see Cardiac fibrin-associated diffuse large B-cell lymphoma, p.269) [57, 41].

Foci of extramedullary haematopoiesis may be seen in $7\%$ of myxomas [31]. Thymic rests and cellular thymoma-like elements have been observed [37]. There have also been rare reports of gastric heterotopia, chondroid differentiation, and prominent oncocytic change [6, 11, 45, 65].

# Immunophenotype

Myxoma cells exhibit reactivity with antibodies directed against calretinin in nearly all cases, while variable reactivity has been observed with antibodies directed against NSE, S100, synaptophysin, SMA, and desmin [1]. Endothelial markers (e.g. CD31, CD34, thrombomodulin, and endothelin) are, as expected, positive in endothelium, with variable reactivity reported in myxoma cells [49].

Demonstration of lost PRKAR1A expression within the myxoma cells raises the possibility of underlying Carney complex, which should prompt clinical consideration of the syndrome (possibly including germline genetic testing) [33].

# Differential diagnosis

The primary differential diagnoses of cardiac myxoma include organizing thrombus, so-called calcified amorphous tumour (when calcification is present), papillary fibroelastoma (particularly with viliform myxomas), and other rare entities. When heterologous glands are identified, differentiation from metastatic adenocarcinoma is paramount, and this can usually be achieved with clinical history, imaging, and ancillary studies.

# Cytology

Cytological descriptions are scarce, with < 15 cases reported in the literature [23, 36]. The characteristic features are polyhedral or stellate tumour cells in a myxoid or mucinous background. Interspersed inflammatory cells, haemosiderin-laden macrophages, and Gamna-Gandy bodies may also be seen [26].

# Diagnostic molecular pathology

Not relevant

# Essential and desirable diagnostic criteria

· Myxoma cells within a myxoid stroma  
· Appropriate location

Desirable:

· Calretinin reactivity within the myxoma cells  
· Perivascular rings

# Grading / Staging

Not relevant

# Prognosis and prediction

Non-syndromic and familial myxomas differ not only in their clinical features and etiology, but also in their prognosis. The tumour recurrence rate is relatively low in non-syndromic patients ($< 5\%$) but it approaches $10{-}20\%$ in Carney complex patients. Patients with Carney complex may also develop multiple tumours (in a synchronous or metachronous fashion), and they are more likely to have atypical cardiac locations [7, 54].

# References
1. Acebo E, Val-Bernal JF, Gomez-Roman JJ. Thrombomodulin, calretinin and c-kit (CD117) expression in cardiac myxoma. Histol Histopathol. 2001 Oct;16(4):1031-6. PMID:11642722
