claims.claim: Cardiac myxoma is a benign neoplasm consisting of stellate, ovoid, or plump spindle cells within a vascular myxoid matrix.
Cardiac fibroma is a benign mesenchymal tumour composed of bland fibroblasts in a variably collagenized stroma.
8840/0 Myxoma, NOS
2F01 & XH6Q84 Benign neoplasm of intrathoracic organs & Myxoma, NOS
8810/0 Fibroma, NOS
2F01 & XH8E66 Benign neoplasm of intrathoracic organs & Fibroma, NOS
Left atrial myxoma is not recommended.
Atrial myxoma is not recommended.
Cardiac fibromatosis is not recommended.
Fibrous hamartoma is not recommended.
Fibroelastic hamartoma is not recommended.
Transthoracic echocardiography is the diagnostic imaging modality of choice.
Echocardiographically, a cardiac myxoma usually appears as a mobile endocardial mass of irregular shape, most often arising in the left atrium in the region of the fossa ovalis.
Areas of variable echogenicity and calcification may be seen in cardiac myxomas.
Advanced echocardiographical modalities, such as contrast and 3D echocardiography, may help provide a more complete characterization of the tumour appearance, size, and location.
If a definitive tumour stalk is not visible, cardiac MRI and CT may help to exclude the myocardial infiltration seen in malignant processes.
Most frequently, myxomas are T1-isointense and T2-hyperintense, owing to their gelatinous nature and high extracellular water content.
Myxomas usually show no or weak contrast enhancement on CT.
Cardiac myxomas are intracavitary endocardial lesions, most often arising in the left atrium (in 80-90% of cases) in the region of the fossa ovalis.
Cardiac myxomas have been reported in all other cardiac chambers, and very rare examples of valvular myxomas have been described.
About half of the cardiac myxomas that arise in the setting of Carney complex occur outside of the left atrium.
Less than 20% of cardiac myxomas occur outside of the left atrium in non-syndromic circumstances.
A myxoma occurring in a non-left atrial site should raise suspicion for underlying Carney complex.
The tumours can be multicentric, raising further suspicion for Carney complex.
Nearly all cardiac fibromas arise in the ventricular septum.
For the remaining cases of cardiac fibromas, the most common site is the ventricular free wall.
Localization of cardiac fibromas in the left ventricle is 5 times as common as in the right ventricle.
The most common sites for cardiac fibromas after the ventricular septum and ventricular free wall are the right and left atria.
Myxomas can spread to distant sites through embolization.
After embolization, myxomas can infiltrate the arterial wall and produce histologically identical tumours at the embolization site.
Myxoma cell infiltration can result in arterial wall weakening and aneurysm formation, even years after resection of the primary tumour.
The clinical presentation of cardiac myxoma is diverse and primarily dependent on tumour location, size, shape, mobility, and rate of growth.
Patients with cardiac myxomas may be entirely asymptomatic (10-20%), have symptoms of heart failure, or rarely present with sudden death.
Cardiac myxomas can produce several cardiac symptoms, including syncope, dyspnoea, chest pain, and palpitations.
Most cardiac symptoms are related to tumoural obstruction of blood flow through the heart.
Atrial myxomas may obstruct the atrioventricular valve orifice, causing a diastolic tumour plop on auscultation.
There is an association between septal tumour location and heart failure, and between extraseptal location and neurological events.
Constitutional symptoms, which can include fever, weight loss, and arthralgias, are frequently associated with large or multiple tumours.
Clinical signs such as anaemia, leukocytosis, and elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate may also be observed.
Many of the phenomena are believed to be related to cytokine elaboration by the tumours themselves, including of IL-6, I-4, I12, interferon γ, and TNF.
A wide range of paraneoplastic syndromes (e.g., vasculitis/vasculopathy, pancreatitis, demyelinating neuropathy, and epistaxis) have been reported.

Less than 10% of cardiac myxomas arise in the setting of Carney complex, an autosomal dominant disorder characterized by the constellation of myxomas (cardiac or otherwise), endocrinopathy (Cushing syndrome and acromegaly), and spotty skin pigmentation.
Due to the predilection of cardiac fibroma for the ventricular septum, one third of patients present with arrhythmias.
Cardiac fibromas may cause obstruction or abnormal valvular function, leading to heart failure.
Patients with asymptomatic tumours may present with a heart murmur, or the tumour may be identified incidentally on imaging.
Cardiac myxoma is the second most common primary cardiac neoplasm of adults, after papillary fibroelastoma.
Cardiac myxoma occurs in all age groups, having been described prenatally through the age of 97 years.
Cardiac myxoma manifests most commonly between the fourth and seventh decades of life.
Cardiac myxomas occur, on average, twice as commonly in women as in men.
The female predominance of cardiac myxomas is less pronounced in patients aged > 65 years.
More than 90% of cardiac myxomas arise in an isolated fashion.
The remaining cases of cardiac myxomas arise in association with Carney complex.
Syndromic tumours generally occur in younger patients, with no sex predominance.
Most cardiac fibromas (90%) occur in children.
Cardiac fibromas are the second most common cardiac tumour of childhood after rhabdomyoma.
One third of cardiac fibromas arise before the age of 1 year, without sex predilection.
Recent molecular characterization studies have revealed protein expression profiles similar to those seen during endocardial-mesenchymal transformation of the endocardial cushion.
Whether these cells ultimately derive from developmental remnants or from redifferentiation of a terminally differentiated cardiac cell is not yet clear.
PRKAR1A, encoding the cAMP-dependent protein kinase type Iα regulatory subunit, has been implicated in a subset of patients without Carney complex.
Cytogenetic data on myxoma are limited, but 15 previously analysed myxomas contained clonal numerical or non-clonal structural abnormalities.
Cytogenetic analyses of 3 cases of Carney complex revealed similar chromosomal patterns as in non-syndromic cases.
Villiform tumours and tumours with abundant myxoid stroma are more likely to be associated with embolization.
Tumour degeneration by overexpression of matrix metalloproteinases may hasten embolization.
Embolization of associated surface thrombi can also occur.
Embolization can result in pulmonary embolism, acute myocardial ischaemia, or stroke.
The histogenesis of cardiac myxomas has been controversial since they were first described.
Initially, cardiac myxomas were thought to be reactive lesions or organizing thrombi.
The locations within the heart, genetics, and overall histology of cardiac myxomas are more characteristic of a neoplastic process.
The origin of the myxoma cell (or lepidic cell) has proved enigmatic.
Myxomas can recur locally, a phenomenon that is usually explained by incomplete resection.
The PTCH1 gene appears to play a role in at least a subset of cardiac fibromas.
Mutations in PTCH1 may be somatic, resulting in sporadic tumour formation, or within a syndromic context, associated with Gorlin syndrome.
Gorlin syndrome is an autosomal dominant disorder caused by germline PTCH1 mutation.
Gorlin syndrome is characterized by multiple basal cell carcinomas, skeletal anomalies, distinct facies, and intracranial calcifications.
The gross appearance of cardiac myxomas is quite variable.
Cardiac myxomas can measure < 10 mm or > 150 mm.
Cardiac myxomas may be either sessile and broad-based or pedunculated-arising from a stalk.
Tumour stalks may occasionally be long, resulting in free mobility of the tumour within the chamber or through a valvular orifice.
Two basic gross appearances of cardiac myxomas have been described: solid and villiform.
Tumours of the solid type may be globular or elongated, with a smooth, shiny, and sometimes undulant surface.
Tumours of the villiform type have irregular, often friable, extensions.
The cut surface of cardiac myxomas is variegated, usually owing to the myxoid tissue and areas of intratumoural haemorrhage, which is common.
Areas of necrosis, cystic change, fibrosis, and calcification can also be seen in cardiac myxomas.
Rarely, cardiac myxomas show extensive calcifications with a stone-like appearance, known as lithomyxoma.
Cardiac fibromas are often large (mean: 50 mm), well-circumscribed, solitary tumours with a white whorled appearance.
Although rare, multiple cardiac fibromas have been reported.
Calcification is common in cardiac fibromas and helps distinguish it from rhabdomyoma, particularly on imaging.
Myxoma cells exhibit reactivity with antibodies directed against calretinin in nearly all cases.
Variable reactivity has been observed with antibodies directed against NSE, S100, synaptophysin, SMA, and desmin in myxoma cells.
Endothelial markers (e.g., CD31, CD34, thrombomodulin, and endothelin) are positive in endothelium.
Variable reactivity is reported in myxoma cells with endothelial markers (e.g., CD31, CD34, thrombomodulin, and endothelin).
Demonstration of lost PRKAR1A expression within the myxoma cells raises the possibility of underlying Carney complex.
Lost PRKAR1A expression within the myxoma cells should prompt clinical consideration of Carney complex syndrome, possibly including germline genetic testing.

The primary differential diagnoses of cardiac myxoma include organizing thrombus, so-called calcified amorphous tumour (when calcification is present), papillary fibroelastoma (particularly with viliform myxomas), and other rare entities.
When heterologous glands are identified, differentiation from metastatic adenocarcinoma is paramount.
Differentiation from metastatic adenocarcinoma can usually be achieved with clinical history, imaging, and ancillary studies.
Cytological descriptions are scarce, with fewer than 15 cases reported in the literature.
The characteristic features are polyhedral or stellate tumour cells in a myxoid or mucinous background.
Interspersed inflammatory cells, haemosiderin-laden macrophages, and Gamna-Gandy bodies may also be seen.
Myxoma cells are present within a myxoid stroma.
Myxoma cells are located in an appropriate location.
Calretinin reactivity is present within the myxoma cells.
Perivascular rings are a desirable feature.
Cardiac myxomas exhibit considerable histological variability, sometimes even in different regions of the same tumour.
The defining characteristic of cardiac myxomas is the presence of the so-called myxoma cell (or lepidic cell), a cytologically bland cell with eosinophilic cytoplasm and an oval or round nucleus.
Stellate, ovoid, or plump spindle cells in cardiac myxomas may occur singly or in groups.
When in groups, the cells in cardiac myxomas can form cords, nests, or rings.
Rings in cardiac myxomas frequently occur around capillaries or small vessels and have been termed perivascular rings.
The cells in cardiac myxomas reside in a myxoid matrix that is rich in mucopolysaccharides, with variable amounts of proteoglycan, collagen, and elastin.
The matrix in cardiac myxomas shows strong reactivity with Alcian blue, is resistant to hyaluronidase, and shows patchy positivity with mucicarmine and PAS stains.
The background of cardiac myxomas may contain variable numbers of inflammatory cells and rarely multinucleated giant cells.
Larger, thick-walled vessels are often present near the stalk or base of the lesion in cardiac myxomas, while smaller vessels are present throughout.
Haemorrhage, both recent and remote, is frequently encountered in cardiac myxomas and is probably a product of the vascularity and trauma throughout the cardiac cycle.
Remote haemorrhage in cardiac myxomas can manifest as haemosiderin-laden macrophages and/or iron encrustation of intratumoural elastic fibres (Gamna-Gandy bodies).
Secondary degenerative changes such as fibrosis, cystic change, necrosis, thrombosis, calcification, and metaplastic bone formation can be present in cardiac myxomas.
Glandular elements are very rare in cardiac myxomas, identified in less than 3% of all myxomas.
The glands in cardiac myxomas are predominantly located at the base of the tumour, without local infiltration.
Diffuse large B-cell lymphomas, some associated with EBV, have been described in the background of otherwise classic cardiac myxomas.
Foci of extramedullary haematopoiesis may be seen in 7% of myxomas.
Thymic rests and cellular thymoma-like elements have been observed in cardiac myxomas.
There have been rare reports of gastric heterotopia, chondroid differentiation, and prominent oncocytic change in cardiac myxomas.
Non-syndromic and familial myxomas differ not only in their clinical features and etiology, but also in their prognosis.
The tumour recurrence rate is relatively low in non-syndromic patients (< 5%).
The tumour recurrence rate approaches 10-20% in Carney complex patients.
Patients with Carney complex may develop multiple tumours in a synchronous or metachronous fashion.
Patients with Carney complex are more likely to have atypical cardiac locations.

comprehensiveness.criterion: Complete Workflow Coverage from Presentation to Follow-up
comprehensiveness.explanation: Assess the guideline's coverage of the entire clinical workflow for cardiac myxoma, from initial presentation through imaging, pathology, molecular diagnostics, integrated diagnosis, management, and follow-up. This ensures that all critical steps are addressed, providing a comprehensive framework for clinicians managing this condition.

Evaluate the guideline's clarity in defining cardiac myxoma, including its ICD-O and ICD-11 coding. Accurate definitions and coding are essential for consistent diagnosis and reporting, facilitating communication across healthcare systems.

Check the guideline's detail on the anatomic distribution of cardiac myxomas and their epidemiological characteristics, such as age and sex distribution. This information is crucial for understanding the disease's demographic patterns and potential risk factors.

Assess the guideline's coverage of clinical presentations and imaging findings across modalities like CT, MRI, and echocardiography. Comprehensive phenotype descriptions aid in accurate diagnosis and differentiation from other cardiac conditions.

Evaluate the guideline's discussion on the etiology and mechanisms of cardiac myxoma, including genetic and syndromic associations. Understanding these factors is vital for risk assessment and potential genetic counseling.

Review the guideline's detail on the macroscopic and microscopic pathology of cardiac myxomas, including immunophenotypic characteristics. This information is essential for accurate histopathological diagnosis and differentiation from other cardiac tumors.

Assess the guideline's coverage of differential diagnoses, providing structured guidance on similarities and distinguishing tests. This helps clinicians differentiate cardiac myxoma from other cardiac masses, ensuring accurate diagnosis and treatment.

insight_depth.criterion: Mechanistic Depth Linking Genetics and Phenotype
insight_depth.explanation: Evaluate the guideline's depth in linking genetic and epigenetic factors to the clinical phenotype of cardiac myxoma. Understanding these mechanisms provides insights into disease pathogenesis and potential therapeutic targets.

insight_depth.criterion: Integration Across Imaging, Histology, and Molecular Data
insight_depth.explanation: Assess the guideline's ability to integrate data from imaging, histology, and molecular diagnostics into a coherent diagnostic framework. This integration is crucial for accurate diagnosis and personalized treatment planning.

insight_depth.criterion: Transparent Diagnostic Reasoning and Decision Points
insight_depth.explanation: Review the guideline's clarity in outlining diagnostic reasoning, including decision points and branching logic. This transparency helps clinicians navigate complex cases and resolve conflicts between diagnostic modalities.

insight_depth.criterion: Prognostic and Predictive Stratification Depth
insight_depth.explanation: Evaluate the guideline's depth in stratifying patients based on prognostic and predictive factors, with biologically plausible rationale. This stratification aids in risk assessment and individualized patient management.

insight_depth.criterion: Evidence Synthesis Maturity and Limitations Acknowledgment
insight_depth.explanation: Assess the guideline's maturity in synthesizing evidence, handling heterogeneity, and acknowledging limitations. This ensures that recommendations are based on robust evidence and that uncertainties are transparently communicated.

accuracy_standards.criterion: Correct and Current WHO Entity Naming and Grading
accuracy_standards.explanation: Ensure the guideline uses correct and current WHO naming and grading conventions for cardiac myxoma. Adherence to authoritative standards is crucial for consistency in diagnosis and reporting.

accuracy_standards.criterion: Accurate ICD-O/ICD-11 Coding Usage
accuracy_standards.explanation: Evaluate the guideline's accuracy in using ICD-O and ICD-11 codes, including handling of synonyms and deprecated terms. Precise coding is essential for accurate disease classification and epidemiological studies.

accuracy_standards.criterion: Molecular Terminology and Assay Interpretation Accuracy
accuracy_standards.explanation: Review the guideline's accuracy in molecular terminology and interpretation of assays, such as variant naming and CNV conventions. Correct interpretation is vital for reliable molecular diagnostics and treatment decisions.

accuracy_standards.criterion: Internal Consistency Across Document
accuracy_standards.explanation: Check for internal consistency in thresholds, criteria, and marker interpretations throughout the guideline. Consistency ensures that clinicians receive clear and unambiguous guidance.

accuracy_standards.criterion: Diagnostic Accuracy Safeguards
accuracy_standards.explanation: Assess the guideline's inclusion of safeguards against diagnostic inaccuracies, such as assay limitations and pre-analytic variables. These safeguards help prevent misdiagnosis and ensure reliable patient care.

readability_utility.explanation: Evaluate the guideline's logical organization and flow, ensuring that arguments and recommendations progress in a clinically sensible order. Clear transitions and avoidance of circular reasoning enhance comprehension.
Assess the guideline's use of evidence-backed statements, supported by quantitative data such as sample sizes and effect sizes. This approach strengthens the credibility of recommendations.

Review the guideline's explicit guidance on diagnosis, reporting, testing strategy, management, and follow-up. Actionable recommendations are crucial for practical implementation in clinical settings.

readability_utility.criterion: High-Yield Presentation with Effective Use of Visuals
readability_utility.explanation: Evaluate the guideline's use of tables, figures, and algorithms to enhance decision-making. Effective visuals can clarify complex information and improve clinical utility.

Assess the guideline's feasibility for implementation across different clinical settings, considering resource availability and practical constraints. Resource-stratified pathways ensure applicability in varied healthcare environments.

System.role_and_constraints: You are a medical guideline writer. Based on authoritative literature, write a chapter of an authoritative medical report. You need to follow the chapter format below.

content:
# Small cell lung carcinoma

# Definition

Small cell lung carcinoma (SCLC) is a malignant epithelial tumour composed of small cells with scant cytoplasm, finely granular nuclear chromatin, and absent or inconspicuous nucleoli, with a high mitotic count and frequent necrosis. Most SCLCs express neuroendocrine markers. Combined SCLC has an additional component of non-small cell carcinoma (NSCC), which may include large cell neuroendocrine carcinoma (LCNEC), adenocarcinoma, squamous cell carcinoma (SCC), large cell carcinoma (LCC), spindle cell carcinoma, or giant cell carcinoma.

# ICD-O coding / ICD-11 coding

8041/3 Small cell carcinoma  
8045/3 Combined small cell carcinoma

2C25.1 Small cell carcinoma of bronchus or lung

# Related terminology

Not recommended (obsolete): oat cell carcinoma; undifferentiated SCLC; intermediate cell-type SCLC; mixed small cell / large cell carcinoma.

# Subtype(s)

Small cell carcinoma; combined small cell carcinoma

# Localization

SCLC is usually located centrally in the major airways, frequently also involving the mediastinal lymph nodes, but it may occur peripherally in the lungs in about $5 \%$ of cases [51]. Metastatic spread, most frequently to the liver, bone, brain, ipsilateral and contralateral lung, and adrenal glands, is commonly present at the time of presentation. Malignant pleural and pericardial effusions are also common.

# Clinical features

Patients often present with rapid-onset signs or symptoms due to local intrathoracic tumour growth, extrapulmonary distant spread, paraneoplastic syndromes, or a combination of these features. The symptoms of SCLC are similar to those of other lung cancers (see Tumours of the lung: Introduction, p. 20). Paraneoplastic effects are more frequent in SCLC than in other histological types, and they can be the presenting symptoms. Ectopic hormone secretion can cause hyponatraemia or Cushing syndrome, and immune-mediated paraneoplastic syndromes can cause Lambert-Eaton myasthenic syndrome, peripheral neuropathy, or limbic encephalopathy [53]. Serum tumour markers for diagnosis, monitoring, and evaluation of SCLC response to therapy have had little clinical impact to date.

# Imaging

Patients with SCLC present with the full spectrum of imaging findings of lung cancer (see Tumours of the lung: Introduction, p. 20), but because of rapid growth, the tumour at presentation tends to be larger and at a more advanced stage than non small cell lung carcinoma [29], typically occurring as a large hilar mass and bulky mediastinal lymph nodes. Approximately $1 5 \%$ of neurologically asymptomatic patients will have metastases detected on brain MRI [19].

# Spread

None

# Epidemiology

SCLC accounts for approximately $1 5 \%$ of all lung carcinomas diagnosed worldwide. In the USA, the incidence in both sexes appears to have peaked in the mid-1980s / late 1990s and has been declining [20, 21]. Although the rates in certain countries in Asia and eastern Europe with high smoking rates continued to increase into the 2000s, a stabilization or slight decline has more recently been reported [60, 1].

# Etiology

The majority of SCLCs arise in patients with a history of heavy smoking, although de novo SCLC can rarely occur in never smokers [54]. SCLC can also occur as a resistance mechanism secondary to tyrosine kinase inhibitor therapy for treatment of EGFR-mutated lung adenocarcinomas or other oncogene driven non-small cell lung carcinomas [48, 37].

The smoking-related etiology is supported by the characteristic tobacco carcinogen-associated molecular signature, with a high frequency (28%) of G>T and C>A transversions and an extremely high mutation frequency [33, 17]. Like in SCLC in general, RB1 loss is a common feature in SCLCs arising in the setting of acquired resistance to tyrosine kinase inhibitor therapy for EGFR-mutant adenocarcinoma. The EGFR mutations are typically maintained, suggesting a common cell lineage and a role for RB1 loss in the pathogenesis of SCLC [3, 54, 32].

# Pathogenesis

TP53 and RB1 biallelic loss of function is obligatory in SCLC, as supported by its requirement in mouse models [12, 46, 44, 17]. TP53 mutations affect the functional domain, but RB1 mutations are more complex, with multiple incompletely detected rearrangements. In addition, significantly mutated genes are CRACD (KIAA1211) and COL22A1, RGS7, and FPR1, involved in G protein-coupled receptor signalling. Inactivating mutations of two histone acetylases (CREBBP and EP300) and damaging mutations of FMN2 and NOTCH-family genes are often seen. Significant clustered mutations occur in the ASPM, ALMS1, and PDE4DIP genes (involved in centrosome function) and in XRN1 (involved in RNA regulation). The TP73 homologue of TP53 is affected by clustered somatic rearrangements/breaks creating N-terminally truncated transcript p73 variants, which include p73 delta $_ { \mathrm { e x 2 } }$ delta $_ { \mathrm { e x } 2 / 3 }$ and delta ex10, all of which lack fully competent transactivation domains, as inferred from transcriptome sequencing. CREBBP, EP300, TP73, RBL1, RBL2, and NOTCH-family gene mutations are largely mutually exclusive [17, 33, 44].

The majority of SCLCs have downregulated Notch signalling. TP53/RB1 knock-out mouse models with an activated extramembrane Notch domain (NiCD) are found to have a decreased number of tumours, with a lower proliferation rate, decreased neuroendocrine expression, and increased survival time. These findings confirm Notch signaling as a tumour suppressor in SCLC and a regulator of neuroendocrine differentiation [46, 17].

Reported somatic gene copy-number alterations include amplification of the MYC-family genes MYCL (MYCL1), MYCN, MYC, FGFR1, and IRS2 and genomic losses of 3p genes: FHIT (3p14) and ROBO1 (3p12) [33, 16].

In contrast with carcinoids, SCLC does not occur in the context of multiple endocrine neoplasia type 1 disease, and it has no constitutive or somatic MEN1 mutations [13, 14]. The neuroendocrine nature of SCLC is thought to be driven by ASCL1, with or without NeuroD1 participation [45].

Additionally, SCLC may show greater heterogeneity than previously thought. A recent comprehensive study of animal models, xenografts, cell lines, and human primaries of untreated or treated tumours identified four SCLC classes according to expression of four predominant transcription factors (opening consideration of new concepts of pathogenesis and therapeutic options): ASCL1 is dominant in SCLC type A, NeuroD1 in type D, YAP1 in type Y, and POU2F3 in type P. It was recognized, however, that the vast majority of human untreated SCLCs belong to type A and a minority to type D, enriched in pretreated SCLC and rarely others [45, 5]. Further study is needed to elucidate how this might translate to disease management.

A defined precursor lesion for SCLC has not been identified in humans, and, unlike carcinoids, SCLC is not associated with the precursor lesion of diffuse idiopathic pulmonary neuroendocrine cell hyperplasia. Precursor lesions have been reported in mouse models, typically associated with Tp53/Rb1 double knock-out, in some studies by adding Pten and/or Rbl2 (p130) ablation [12, 46] or by introducing a Myc mutant allele [27]. Genetically engineered mouse models have identified a minimum of two gene ablations (of Tp53 and Rb1) as being required and sufficient for initiation and progression of SCLC [15].

Although a precursor cell has not been definitely characterized in humans, putative cells of origin can be any lung epithelial cell: a basal cell, a neuroendocrine cell, or a totipotent epithelial cell with plasticity towards different phenotypes. This is supported by the reported effect of chemotherapy in human SCLC [7, 47], with reports describing posttherapy morphological changes towards a larger size, combined SCLC, and multiple differentiation in the same tumours and/or individual cells [47, 7], which would also support a common lineage for combined SCLC.

# Macroscopic appearance

SCLC typically occurs as an unresectable large perihilar mass, which may be associated with bronchial compression and nodal involvement. The tumour has a tan, necrotic cut surface and may spread along bronchi in a subepithelial pattern. Approximately $5\%$ of carcinomas occur as a circumscribed peripheral nodule [51].

# Histopathology

Tumour cells have scant cytoplasm, poorly defined cell borders, and finely granular nuclear chromatin. The cells are usually less than the diameter of 3 small resting lymphocytes and are oval to spindle in shape. Nucleoli are absent or inconspicuous. Nuclear moulding is common. The tumour cells are usually densely packed and commonly grow in a sheet-like pattern. Necrosis and apoptotic cells are frequent and often extensive. Crush artefact and encrustation of basophilic nuclear DNA around blood vessels (the Azzopardi effect) may be encountered. Neuroendocrine growth patterns (organoid, trabecular, rosette formation, etc.) are typically absent in biopsies but may be prominent in resected tumours [2]. Occasional larger cells, including pleomorphic tumour giant cells, may be admixed but should not make $4p > 10\%$ of the tumour. Mitotic count is high; by definition, it is $>10$ mitoses/2 $\mathsf{mm}^2$, but the average is 60 mitoses/2 $\mathsf{mm}^2$ and the median is 80 mitoses $12 \mathsf{mm}^2$ [51].

Combined SCLCs are tumours consisting of SCLC with an additional component of NSCC, including LCNEC. Most commonly, the second component is LCC, adenocarcinoma, or SCC, but it rarely may consist of spindle or giant cell carcinoma [30, 52]. A diagnosis of combined SCLC should include mention of each of the NSCC components present. As noted above, SCLC may show occasional larger cells. Therefore, large cells should make up $\geq 10\%$ of the tumour for a tumour to be classified as combined SCLC/LCNEC or combined SCLC and LCC. The percentage requirement is not applicable to other non-small cell elements, such as adenocarcinoma and SCC. The presence of an adenocarcinoma component in a combined SCLC or a new diagnosis of SCLC in a never-smoker should prompt molecular testing and consideration of a driver mutation such as an EGFR mutation or ALK rearrangement. This occurs most often as a manifestation of resistance to targeted therapy in known EGFR-mutated or ALK-rearranged lung adenocarcinomas, but it can be found de novo in untreated patients. Tumours with mixtures of SCLC and equal or greater amounts of spindle cell and/or giant cell carcinoma or heterologous sarcomatous components should be classified as combined SCLC with mention of the various histologies identified.

# Immunophenotype

Classically, SCLC is considered a light-microscopic diagnosis, and immunostains are not required to confirm a diagnosis of SCLC. However, immunostains are commonly used and are of particular utility in excluding or confirming an alternate diagnosis.

SCLC typically stains for cytokeratins such as AE1/AE3 and CAM5.2, often with a rim-and-dot-type pattern, but it is negative for high-molecular-weight cytokeratins [50]. CK7 is positive in $< 50\%$ of cases and CK20 is usually negative [10]. Most SCLCs will stain for neuroendocrine markers such as chromogranin, synaptophysin, and CD56 (NCAM), the last of which is most sensitive but less specific [43, 36]. Staining for chromogranin may be weak and focal [57]. SCLC may be negative for all three of these markers in approximately $5-10\%$ of cases. Studies have shown variable results in regard to whether INSM1 is more or less specific than a combination of chromogranin, synaptophysin, and CD56, but INSM1 has been shown to be a consistently reliable marker, particularly in the setting of SCLC [23, 28]. ASCL1 (hASH1) is emerging as an additional marker of neuroendocrine differentiation, but its value in SCLC needs further evaluation [26, 58]. TTF1 expression has been reported in $90-95\%$ of SCLCs, depending on the clone used, but it is not specific for pulmonary origin in the setting of a small cell carcinoma [22, 25]. Napsin A is negative in SCLC [40]. p63 and p40 are generally negative in SCLC. Focal staining has been reported in some series, but diffuse nuclear staining is not observed [41]. The vast majority of SCLCs show loss of RB1 protein and p53 overexpression or null expression [6, 31]. Ki-67, although not part of the diagnostic criteria for SCLC or neuroendocrine tumours (NETs) in general, may be useful in the setting of crushed biopsies to avoid misdiagnosing carcinoid tumours, which may also show crush artefact. The reported Ki-67 index for SCLC is $65-100\%$ and for typical carcinoids is $<5\%$, although proliferation rates as high as $30\%$ have been reported in atypical carcinoid (see Lung neuroendocrine neoplasms: Introduction, p.127) [34, 35]. In virtually all cases, a very high proliferation rate would exclude a carcinoid tumour, whereas a very low rate would exclude SCLC [42, 34]. In cytology specimens, it has been reported that CytoLyt fixation reduces Ki-67 when the MiB1 clone is used, but not with the Ki-67 30-9 antibody [8]. Orthopedia homeobox protein (OTP) has been shown to preferentially stain carcinoids as opposed to high-grade tumours [18, 55].

# Differential diagnosis

The differential diagnosis of SCLC includes other neuroendocrine carcinomas (NECs), particularly LCNEC, as well as basaloid SCC, small round cell sarcomas, and lymphoma.

SCLC should be differentiated from other pulmonary NETs (i.e. carcinoids and LCNEC). Immunostains are generally not useful in this regard, because all of these tumours will be positive for cytokeratins and neuroendocrine markers. The differentiation from carcinoid tumours is based primarily on morphology and mitotic count (see Table 1.14, p. 127), although, as noted above, Ki-67 may aid in discriminating SCLC from carcinoid tumours in crushed biopsies. The discrimination of SCLC from LCNEC is based primarily on light-microscopic features, with LCNEC usually having more abundant cytoplasm, polygonal cell shape, distinct cell borders, and vesicular nuclear chromatin (often with nucleoli). Owing to the spectrum of cell sizes that may occur in both tumours, discriminating SCLC from LCNEC may be particularly challenging, and there is currently no immunohistochemical stain or genetic marker to discriminate between the two [38, 51]. Because the diagnosis depends primarily on morphological features, good-quality H&E sections elucidating these features are critical. Merkel cell carcinoma, a NEC of the skin, is typically positive for CK20, NFP, and Merkel cell polyomavirus but negative for TTF1, which can help distinguish it from SCLC [9].

Basaloid squamous carcinoma is characterized by nests of basaloid cells, often with peripheral palisading around tumour nests and a high mitotic count. Although most tumours show cytological features of an NSCC, some have small tumour cell size along with palisading and focal rosette-like structures morphologically identical to SCLC. Basaloid squamous carcinoma is characterized by strong diffuse staining for p40 or p63 (which are only rarely focally positive in SCLC [41]) and is positive for keratin 34βE12, which is negative in SCLC [50, 49]. Of note, CD56 may rarely be diffusely and strongly positive in basaloid SCC. In TTF1-negative tumours that appear to be SCLC, a p40 stain should be performed to exclude basaloid SCC.

SMARCA4-deficient thoracic undifferentiated tumours may have small-sized tumour cells. These tumours frequently express synaptophysin and may have focal TTF1 expression (see Thoracic SMARCA4-deficient undifferentiated tumour, p. 111) [39]. Diagnosis is facilitated by immunohistochemistry demonstrating loss of SMARCA4 protein (also known as BRG1).

Small round cell sarcomas, both in the Ewing sarcoma family (e.g. Ewing sarcoma) and recently described morphologically similar tumours lacking EWSR1 gene rearrangement (e.g. CICDUX4-rearranged and BCOR-CCNB3-rearranged tumours [4, 11, 24]), may enter the differential diagnosis because they may occasionally show positive staining for neuroendocrine markers, particularly CD56. Patient age, tumour morphology, and smoking history, as well as lack of cytokeratin immunoreactivity, should raise the index of suspicion for an alternate diagnosis from SCLC. Appropriate FISH studies should confirm the diagnosis in most cases.

SCLC may occasionally have greater than expected loss of cell cohesion, raising the possibility of lymphoma. The absence of keratin staining and the presence of lymphoid markers readily discriminate lymphoma from SCLC.

Rare SCLCs are keratin-negative. Tumours suspected to be SCLC that are keratin-negative should be carefully evaluated with markers to exclude lymphoma, sarcoma, and melanoma. The diagnosis in such cases would also require classic morphology in addition to immunohistochemical markers showing characteristic features such as neuroendocrine markers, RB1 loss, and aberrant p53 expression.

# Cytology

Cytology preparations serve as an important diagnostic tool in the diagnosis of SCLC, and in many cases they highlight nuclear features that may be difficult to evaluate on crushed tissue biopsies.

Smears show loosely cohesive round, oval, or occasionally spindle cells with minimal cytoplasm, present singly or in small clusters. Abundant apoptotic or pyknotic dark nuclei are commonly seen, although mitotic figures are poorly preserved in smears. Necrotic background is usually evident. Chromatin streaking is a prominent feature. In alcohol-fixed preparations, chromatin has a distinctive finely or coarsely granular quality, lacking prominent nucleoli. Nuclear moulding can be seen. In air-dried Diff-Quik (Giemsa)-stained preparations, single-cell pattern with round cells containing minimal or stripped cytoplasm closely mimics haematolymphoid malignancies.

# Diagnostic molecular pathology

There is no established role for molecular testing in the diagnosis of SCLC. Whole-genome sequencing of SCLC [17, 33, 44] demonstrates biallelic alteration of both TP53 and RB1.

# Essential and desirable diagnostic criteria

Essential - SCLC:

· Tumour composed of small cells (usually less than the size of 3 resting lymphocytes) with scant cytoplasm, oval to spindle shape, and high mitotic count $> 10$ mitoses/2 $\mathsf{mm}^2$ but usually higher, $\mathord{\sim} 60$ mitoses/2 $mm^2$, often with necrosis  
· Tumour cells have finely granular nuclear chromatin  
· Nucleoli are absent or inconspicuous

Essential - combined SCLC:

· Features of SCLC but with a component of a non-small cell lung carcinoma (LCC, LCNEC, adenocarcinoma, SCC, or less commonly spindle and/or giant cell carcinoma)  
· In the case of SCLC combined with LCNEC or LCC, but not the other histological types, the second component should make up $\geq 10\%$ of the tumour

Desirable:

· Positive immunohistochemistry for low-molecular-weight cytokeratin  
· Frequent expression of neuroendocrine markers ($> 90\%$ of cases)  
· Lack of diffuse p40 expression, unless in areas of SCC in a combined SCLC

# Grading / Staging

None

# Prognosis and prediction

None

claims.claim: The term 'oat cell carcinoma' is not recommended and considered obsolete.
The term 'undifferentiated SCLC' is not recommended and considered obsolete.
The term 'intermediate cell-type SCLC' is not recommended and considered obsolete.
The term 'mixed small cell / large cell carcinoma' is not recommended and considered obsolete.
Small cell lung carcinoma (SCLC) is a malignant epithelial tumour composed of small cells with scant cytoplasm, finely granular nuclear chromatin, and absent or inconspicuous nucleoli.
SCLC has a high mitotic count and frequent necrosis.
Most SCLCs express neuroendocrine markers.
Combined SCLC has an additional component of non-small cell carcinoma (NSCC).

The non-small cell carcinoma (NSCC) component in combined SCLC may include large cell neuroendocrine carcinoma (LCNEC), adenocarcinoma, squamous cell carcinoma (SCC), large cell carcinoma (LCC), spindle cell carcinoma, or giant cell carcinoma.
8041/3 Small cell carcinoma
8045/3 Combined small cell carcinoma
2C25.1 Small cell carcinoma of bronchus or lung
SCLC is usually located centrally in the major airways.
SCLC frequently involves the mediastinal lymph nodes.
SCLC may occur peripherally in the lungs in about 5% of cases.
Metastatic spread of SCLC is most frequently to the liver, bone, brain, ipsilateral and contralateral lung, and adrenal glands.
Malignant pleural and pericardial effusions are common in SCLC.
Small cell carcinoma is a subtype.
Combined small cell carcinoma is a subtype.
Patients often present with rapid-onset signs or symptoms due to local intrathoracic tumour growth, extrapulmonary distant spread, paraneoplastic syndromes, or a combination of these features.
The symptoms of SCLC are similar to those of other lung cancers.
Paraneoplastic effects are more frequent in SCLC than in other histological types.
Paraneoplastic effects can be the presenting symptoms in SCLC.
Ectopic hormone secretion can cause hyponatraemia or Cushing syndrome.
Immune-mediated paraneoplastic syndromes can cause Lambert-Eaton myasthenic syndrome, peripheral neuropathy, or limbic encephalopathy.
Serum tumour markers for diagnosis, monitoring, and evaluation of SCLC response to therapy have had little clinical impact to date.
Patients with SCLC present with the full spectrum of imaging findings of lung cancer.
Because of rapid growth, the tumour at presentation in SCLC tends to be larger and at a more advanced stage than nonsmall cell lung carcinoma [29].
SCLC typically occurs as a large hilar mass and bulky mediastinal lymph nodes.
Approximately 15% of neurologically asymptomatic patients with SCLC will have metastases detected on brain MRI [19, 59].
SCLC accounts for approximately 15% of all lung carcinomas diagnosed worldwide.
In the USA, the incidence of SCLC in both sexes appears to have peaked in the mid-1980s / late 1990s and has been declining.
In certain countries in Asia and eastern Europe with high smoking rates, the rates of SCLC continued to increase into the 2000s.
A stabilization or slight decline in SCLC rates has more recently been reported in certain countries in Asia and eastern Europe.
The majority of SCLCs arise in patients with a history of heavy smoking.
De novo SCLC can rarely occur in never-smokers.
SCLC can occur as a resistance mechanism secondary to tyrosine kinase inhibitor therapy for treatment of EGFR-mutated lung adenocarcinomas or other oncogene-driven non-small cell lung carcinomas.

The smoking-related etiology of SCLC is supported by the characteristic tobacco carcinogen-associated molecular signature, with a high frequency (28%) of G>T and C>A transversions and an extremely high mutation frequency.
RB1 loss is a common feature in SCLCs arising in the setting of acquired resistance to tyrosine kinase inhibitor therapy for EGFR-mutant adenocarcinoma.

The EGFR mutations are typically maintained in SCLCs arising in the setting of acquired resistance to tyrosine kinase inhibitor therapy, suggesting a common cell lineage and a role for RB1 loss in the pathogenesis of SCLC.
SCLC typically occurs as an unresectable large perihilar mass.
SCLC may be associated with bronchial compression and nodal involvement.
The tumour has a tan, necrotic cut surface.
The tumour may spread along bronchi in a subepithelial pattern.
Approximately 5% of carcinomas occur as a circumscribed peripheral nodule.
TP53 and RB1 biallelic loss of function is obligatory in SCLC.
TP53 mutations affect the functional domain.
RB1 mutations are more complex, with multiple incompletely detected rearrangements.
Significantly mutated genes in SCLC include CRACD (KIAA1211), COL22A1, RGS7, and FPR1, involved in G protein-coupled receptor signalling.
Inactivating mutations of two histone acetylases (CREBBP and EP300) and damaging mutations of FMN2 and NOTCH-family genes are often seen in SCLC.
Significant clustered mutations occur in the ASPM, ALMS1, and PDE4DIP genes (involved in centrosome function) and in XRN1 (involved in RNA regulation).
The TP73 homologue of TP53 is affected by clustered somatic rearrangements/breaks creating N-terminally truncated transcript p73 variants.
CREBBP, EP300, TP73, RBL1, RBL2, and NOTCH-family gene mutations are largely mutually exclusive in SCLC.
The majority of SCLCs have downregulated Notch signalling.

TP53/RB1 knock-out mouse models with an activated extramembrane Notch domain (NiCD) are found to have a decreased number of tumours, with a lower proliferation rate, decreased neuroendocrine expression, and increased survival time.
Notch signaling is confirmed as a tumour suppressor in SCLC and a regulator of neuroendocrine differentiation.
Reported somatic gene copy-number alterations in SCLC include amplification of the MYC-family genes MYCL (MYCL1), MYCN, MYC, FGFR1, and IRS2.
Genomic losses of 3p genes in SCLC include FHIT (3p14) and ROBO1 (3p12).
SCLC does not occur in the context of multiple endocrine neoplasia type 1 disease and has no constitutive or somatic MEN1 mutations.
The neuroendocrine nature of SCLC is thought to be driven by ASCL1, with or without NeuroD1 participation.
SCLC may show greater heterogeneity than previously thought.
A recent comprehensive study identified four SCLC classes according to expression of four predominant transcription factors: ASCL1 in type A, NeuroD1 in type D, YAP1 in type Y, and POU2F3 in type P.
The vast majority of human untreated SCLCs belong to type A and a minority to type D, enriched in pretreated SCLC and rarely others.
A defined precursor lesion for SCLC has not been identified in humans.
SCLC is not associated with the precursor lesion of diffuse idiopathic pulmonary neuroendocrine cell hyperplasia.
Precursor lesions have been reported in mouse models, typically associated with Tp53/Rb1 double knock-out.
Genetically engineered mouse models have identified a minimum of two gene ablations (of Tp53 and Rb1) as being required and sufficient for initiation and progression of SCLC.

Although a precursor cell has not been definitely characterized in humans, putative cells of origin can be any lung epithelial cell: a basal cell, a neuroendocrine cell, or a totipotent epithelial cell with plasticity towards different phenotypes.
Chemotherapy in human SCLC has been reported to cause posttherapy morphological changes towards a larger size, combined SCLC, and multiple differentiation in the same tumours and/or individual cells.
Posttherapy morphological changes in SCLC support a common lineage for combined SCLC.
SCLC is considered a light-microscopic diagnosis, and immunostains are not required to confirm a diagnosis of SCLC.
Immunostains are commonly used and are of particular utility in excluding or confirming an alternate diagnosis.
SCLC typically stains for cytokeratins such as AE1/AE3 and CAM5.2, often with a rim-and-dot-type pattern.
SCLC is negative for high-molecular-weight cytokeratins.
CK7 is positive in < 50% of SCLC cases.
CK20 is usually negative in SCLC.
Most SCLCs will stain for neuroendocrine markers such as chromogranin, synaptophysin, and CD56 (NCAM).
CD56 (NCAM) is the most sensitive but less specific neuroendocrine marker for SCLC.
Staining for chromogranin may be weak and focal in SCLC.
SCLC may be negative for all three neuroendocrine markers in approximately 5-10% of cases.
INSM1 has been shown to be a consistently reliable marker, particularly in the setting of SCLC.
ASCL1 (hASH1) is emerging as an additional marker of neuroendocrine differentiation, but its value in SCLC needs further evaluation.
TTF1 expression has been reported in 90-95% of SCLCs, depending on the clone used.
TTF1 is not specific for pulmonary origin in the setting of a small cell carcinoma.
Napsin A is negative in SCLC.
p63 and p40 are generally negative in SCLC.
Focal staining for p63 and p40 has been reported in some series, but diffuse nuclear staining is not observed in SCLC.
The vast majority of SCLCs show loss of RB1 protein and p53 overexpression or null expression.
Ki-67 is not part of the diagnostic criteria for SCLC or neuroendocrine tumours (NETs) in general.
Ki-67 may be useful in the setting of crushed biopsies to avoid misdiagnosing carcinoid tumours.
The reported Ki-67 index for SCLC is 65-100%.
The reported Ki-67 index for typical carcinoids is < 5%.
Proliferation rates as high as 30% have been reported in atypical carcinoid.
In virtually all cases, a very high proliferation rate would exclude a carcinoid tumour, whereas a very low rate would exclude SCLC.
In cytology specimens, it has been reported that CytoLyt fixation reduces Ki-67 when the MiB1 clone is used, but not with the Ki-67 30-9 antibody.
Orthopedia homeobox protein (OTP) has been shown to preferentially stain carcinoids as opposed to high-grade tumours.
Tumour cells have scant cytoplasm, poorly defined cell borders, and finely granular nuclear chromatin.
The cells are usually less than the diameter of 3 small resting lymphocytes and are oval to spindle in shape.
Nucleoli are absent or inconspicuous.
Nuclear moulding is common.
The tumour cells are usually densely packed and commonly grow in a sheet-like pattern.
Necrosis and apoptotic cells are frequent and often extensive.
Crush artefact and encrustation of basophilic nuclear DNA around blood vessels (the Azzopardi effect) may be encountered.
Neuroendocrine growth patterns (organoid, trabecular, rosette formation, etc.) are typically absent in biopsies but may be prominent in resected tumours.
Occasional larger cells, including pleomorphic tumour giant cells, may be admixed but should not make up > 10% of the tumour.
Mitotic count is high; by definition, it is > 10 mitoses/2 mm², but the average is 60 mitoses/2 mm² and the median is 80 mitoses/2 mm².
Combined SCLCs are tumours consisting of SCLC with an additional component of NSCC, including LCNEC.
Most commonly, the second component is LCC, adenocarcinoma, or SCC, but it rarely may consist of spindle or giant cell carcinoma.
A diagnosis of combined SCLC should include mention of each of the NSCC components present.
Large cells should make up ≥ 10% of the tumour for a tumour to be classified as combined SCLC/LCNEC or combined SCLC and LCC.
The percentage requirement is not applicable to other non-small cell elements, such as adenocarcinoma and SCC.

The presence of an adenocarcinoma component in a combined SCLC or a new diagnosis of SCLC in a never-smoker should prompt molecular testing and consideration of a driver mutation such as an EGFR mutation or ALK rearrangement.
This occurs most often as a manifestation of resistance to targeted therapy in known EGFR-mutated or ALK-rearranged lung adenocarcinomas, but it can be found de novo in untreated patients.

Tumours with mixtures of SCLC and equal or greater amounts of spindle cell and/or giant cell carcinoma or heterologous sarcomatous components should be classified as combined SCLC with mention of the various histologies identified.
There is no established role for molecular testing in the diagnosis of SCLC.
Whole-genome sequencing of SCLC demonstrates biallelic alteration of both TP53 and RB1.
Cytology preparations serve as an important diagnostic tool in the diagnosis of SCLC.
Cytology preparations highlight nuclear features that may be difficult to evaluate on crushed tissue biopsies.
Smears show loosely cohesive round, oval, or occasionally spindle cells with minimal cytoplasm, present singly or in small clusters.
Abundant apoptotic or pyknotic dark nuclei are commonly seen in smears.
Mitotic figures are poorly preserved in smears.
Necrotic background is usually evident in smears.
Chromatin streaking is a prominent feature in smears.
In alcohol-fixed preparations, chromatin has a distinctive finely or coarsely granular quality, lacking prominent nucleoli.
Nuclear moulding can be seen in smears.
In air-dried Diff-Quik (Giemsa)-stained preparations, single-cell pattern with round cells containing minimal or stripped cytoplasm closely mimics haematolymphoid malignancies.
The differential diagnosis of SCLC includes other neuroendocrine carcinomas (NECs), particularly LCNEC, as well as basaloid SCC, small round cell sarcomas, and lymphoma.
SCLC should be differentiated from other pulmonary NETs (i.e., carcinoids and LCNEC).
Immunostains are generally not useful in differentiating SCLC from other pulmonary NETs because all of these tumours will be positive for cytokeratins and neuroendocrine markers.
The differentiation from carcinoid tumours is based primarily on morphology and mitotic count.
Ki-67 may aid in discriminating SCLC from carcinoid tumours in crushed biopsies.

The discrimination of SCLC from LCNEC is based primarily on light-microscopic features, with LCNEC usually having more abundant cytoplasm, polygonal cell shape, distinct cell borders, and vesicular nuclear chromatin (often with nucleoli).
There is currently no immunohistochemical stain or genetic marker to discriminate between SCLC and LCNEC.
Good-quality H&E sections elucidating morphological features are critical for the diagnosis of SCLC.
Merkel cell carcinoma, a NEC of the skin, is typically positive for CK20, NFP, and Merkel cell polyomavirus but negative for TTF1, which can help distinguish it from SCLC.
Basaloid squamous carcinoma is characterized by nests of basaloid cells, often with peripheral palisading around tumour nests and a high mitotic count.
Basaloid squamous carcinoma is characterized by strong diffuse staining for p40 or p63, which are only rarely focally positive in SCLC.
Basaloid squamous carcinoma is positive for keratin 34βE12, which is negative in SCLC.
CD56 may rarely be diffusely and strongly positive in basaloid SCC.
In TTF1-negative tumours that appear to be SCLC, a p40 stain should be performed to exclude basaloid SCC.
SMARCA4-deficient thoracic undifferentiated tumours may have small-sized tumour cells.
SMARCA4-deficient thoracic undifferentiated tumours frequently express synaptophysin and may have focal TTF1 expression.
Diagnosis of SMARCA4-deficient thoracic undifferentiated tumours is facilitated by immunohistochemistry demonstrating loss of SMARCA4 protein (also known as BRG1).

Small round cell sarcomas, both in the Ewing sarcoma family and recently described morphologically similar tumours lacking EWSR1 gene rearrangement, may enter the differential diagnosis because they may occasionally show positive staining for neuroendocrine markers, particularly CD56.
Patient age, tumour morphology, and smoking history, as well as lack of cytokeratin immunoreactivity, should raise the index of suspicion for an alternate diagnosis from SCLC.
Appropriate FISH studies should confirm the diagnosis of small round cell sarcomas in most cases.
SCLC may occasionally have greater than expected loss of cell cohesion, raising the possibility of lymphoma.
The absence of keratin staining and the presence of lymphoid markers readily discriminate lymphoma from SCLC.
Rare SCLCs are keratin-negative.
Tumours suspected to be SCLC that are keratin-negative should be carefully evaluated with markers to exclude lymphoma, sarcoma, and melanoma.

The diagnosis of keratin-negative tumours suspected to be SCLC would require classic morphology in addition to immunohistochemical markers showing characteristic features such as neuroendocrine markers, RB1 loss, and aberrant p53 expression.

Tumour composed of small cells usually less than the size of 3 resting lymphocytes with scant cytoplasm, oval to spindle shape, and high mitotic count > 10 mitoses/2 mm^2 but usually higher, ~60 mitoses/2 mm^2, often with necrosis.
Tumour cells have finely granular nuclear chromatin.
Nucleoli are absent or inconspicuous in tumour cells.
Essential-combined SCLC features SCLC but with a component of a non-small cell lung carcinoma (LCC, LCNEC, adenocarcinoma, SCC, or less commonly spindle and/or giant cell carcinoma).
In the case of SCLC combined with LCNEC or LCC, but not the other histological types, the second component should make up ≥ 10% of the tumour.
Positive immunohistochemistry for low-molecular-weight cytokeratin is desirable.
Frequent expression of neuroendocrine markers (> 90% of cases) is desirable.
Lack of diffuse p40 expression is desirable unless in areas of SCC in a combined SCLC.

comprehensiveness.criterion: Complete Workflow from Presentation to Follow-up
comprehensiveness.explanation: Assess the guideline's coverage of the entire clinical workflow for SCLC, from initial presentation and imaging to pathology, molecular diagnostics, integrated diagnosis, management, and follow-up. This ensures that all critical steps in patient care are addressed, providing a comprehensive framework for clinicians.

Evaluate the guideline's clarity in defining SCLC, including its boundaries and subtypes, and the accuracy of ICD-O/ICD-11 coding. This is crucial for consistent diagnosis and reporting, facilitating communication across healthcare systems.

Examine the guideline's description of SCLC's typical anatomic locations and patterns of dissemination. Understanding these patterns is vital for accurate staging and treatment planning, impacting prognosis and therapeutic decisions.

Assess the guideline's coverage of SCLC etiology, including smoking-related risks, molecular mechanisms, and resistance pathways. This knowledge is essential for prevention strategies and understanding disease progression.

Evaluate the guideline's detail in describing the macroscopic and microscopic pathology of SCLC, including immunophenotypic characteristics. This information is critical for accurate histopathological diagnosis and differentiation from other lung cancers.

Review the guideline's inclusion of key molecular alterations, co-alterations, and progression-related events in SCLC. Comprehensive molecular profiling is essential for personalized treatment approaches and understanding tumor biology.

Assess the guideline's coverage of differential diagnosis, including similarities and distinguishing tests for SCLC versus other neuroendocrine and small cell tumors. Accurate differential diagnosis is crucial to avoid misdiagnosis and ensure appropriate treatment.

insight_depth.criterion: Mechanistic Depth Linking Genetics and Phenotype
insight_depth.explanation: Evaluate the guideline's depth in linking genetic and epigenetic alterations to the phenotypic presentation of SCLC. Understanding these mechanisms is crucial for developing targeted therapies and improving patient outcomes.

insight_depth.criterion: Integration Across Imaging, Histology, and Molecular Data
insight_depth.explanation: Assess the guideline's ability to integrate data from imaging, histology, and molecular diagnostics into a coherent diagnostic framework. This integration is essential for accurate diagnosis and personalized treatment planning.

insight_depth.criterion: Transparent Diagnostic Reasoning and Pitfall Resolution
insight_depth.explanation: Review the guideline's clarity in outlining diagnostic decision points, potential pitfalls, and strategies for resolving conflicts between diagnostic modalities. This transparency aids clinicians in making informed decisions.

insight_depth.criterion: Prognostic and Predictive Stratification Depth
insight_depth.explanation: Evaluate the guideline's depth in stratifying patients based on prognostic and predictive factors, with a biologically plausible rationale. This stratification is crucial for tailoring treatment and improving patient outcomes.

insight_depth.criterion: Evidence Synthesis Maturity
insight_depth.explanation: Assess the guideline's maturity in synthesizing evidence, acknowledging limitations, and conveying the strength of evidence. This maturity ensures that recommendations are based on robust data and are clinically relevant.

insight_depth.criterion: Clinical Impact Reasoning
insight_depth.explanation: Review the guideline's reasoning on how specific biomarkers or histologic features impact diagnosis, management, and trial eligibility. Understanding these impacts is crucial for optimizing patient care and advancing research.

accuracy_standards.criterion: Correct WHO Entity Naming and Grading Conventions
accuracy_standards.explanation: Ensure the guideline uses current WHO naming and grading conventions for SCLC, aligning with major updates. Accurate terminology is essential for consistent communication and reporting.

accuracy_standards.criterion: Accurate ICD-O/ICD-11 Coding Usage
accuracy_standards.explanation: Evaluate the guideline's use of ICD-O/ICD-11 codes, ensuring accuracy and clarity in handling synonyms and deprecated terms. Proper coding is crucial for epidemiological studies and healthcare management.

accuracy_standards.criterion: Molecular Terminology and Assay Interpretation Accuracy
accuracy_standards.explanation: Assess the guideline's accuracy in molecular terminology, including variant naming and assay interpretation. Precise language is vital for correct diagnosis and treatment planning.

accuracy_standards.criterion: Internal Consistency Across Document
accuracy_standards.explanation: Review the guideline for internal consistency, ensuring no contradictory thresholds or criteria. Consistency is crucial for reliable diagnosis and treatment recommendations.

accuracy_standards.criterion: Diagnostic Accuracy Safeguards
accuracy_standards.explanation: Evaluate the guideline's inclusion of safeguards against diagnostic inaccuracies, such as assay limitations and therapy effects. These safeguards are essential for maintaining diagnostic reliability.

accuracy_standards.criterion: Consistency with Reporting Standards
accuracy_standards.explanation: Ensure the guideline aligns with widely used reporting standards and best practices for integrated diagnosis narratives. Consistent reporting is crucial for effective communication and data sharing.

readability_utility.explanation: Assess the guideline's logical organization and flow, ensuring arguments and recommendations progress in a clinically sensible order. Clear transitions enhance understanding and usability.
Evaluate the guideline's use of evidence-backed statements, with quantitative anchors where appropriate. This approach strengthens the credibility and reliability of recommendations.
Review the guideline's explicit guidance for diagnosis, reporting, testing strategy, management, and follow-up. Clear action steps are crucial for practical implementation in clinical settings.
Assess the guideline's use of tables, figures, and algorithms that materially improve decision-making. These tools should enhance understanding and not serve as mere decoration.
Ensure the guideline provides clear definitions of terms, thresholds, and required reporting elements for integrated diagnosis. Clarity in definitions is essential for consistent application.

Evaluate the guideline's feasibility across different healthcare settings, considering resource availability and practical constraints. Resource-stratified pathways ensure applicability in diverse environments.

System.role_and_constraints: You are a medical guideline writer. Based on authoritative literature, write a chapter of an authoritative medical report. You need to follow the chapter format below.

content:
# Mucoepidermoid carcinoma of the lung

# Definition

Mucoepidermoid carcinoma (MEC) is a malignant salivary gland-type tumour that is composed of mucin-secreting cells, squamoid cells, and intermediate-type cells.

Hyalinizing clear cell carcinoma is a low-grade malignant epithelial tumour with cords, trabeculae, and nests of clear and eosinophilic cells infiltrating within a background of myxohyaline and cellular fibrous stroma.

# ICD-O coding / ICD-11 coding

8430/3 Mucoepidermoid carcinoma

2C25.Y & XH1J36 Other specified malignant neoplasms of bronchus or lung & Mucoepidermoid carcinoma

8310/3 Hyalinizing clear cell carcinoma

2C25.Y Other specified malignant neoplasms of bronchus or lung & XH6L02 Clear cell adenocarcinoma

Related terminology None

Subtype(s) None

# Related terminology

Not recommended: mucoepidermoid tumour.

# Subtype(s)

None

# Localization

MECs are usually endobronchial and are more common in central airways.

Hyalinizing clear cell carcinomas are endobronchial in central airways.

# Clinical features

Presentation is usually secondary to obstruction or irritation of the airways, with symptoms of cough, haemoptysis, and recurrent infection. Symptoms are sometimes mistaken for asthma. Some patients are asymptomatic. On CT, the tumours are rounded or lobulated masses, with features of associated obstruction commonly seen [3, 18, 13, 9, 10].

The central location predominantly causes symptoms of obstruction, cough, and dyspnoea. Haemoptysis is rare [4].

# Imaging

None

# Spread

None

# Epidemiology

MECs account for $< 1 \%$ of lung carcinomas. There is a female predominance in some series and a wide age range [18, 13, 9, 10].

Hyalinizing clear cell carcinomas are rare lung cancers, with only 11 reported cases. The age range is 30-66 years, with a slight female predominance [8, 7, 5, 15].

Husain AN Farver CF Nicholson AG

# Etiology

The etiology is unknown. There is no association with smoking.

Unknown

Pathogenesis Unknown

# Pathogenesis

The t(11;19)(q21;p13) involves the CRTC1 and MAML2 genes, located on chromosomes 19p13 and 11q21, respectively. CRTC1-MAML2 fuses exon 1 of CRTC1 with exons 2-5 of MAML2[17].

# Macroscopic appearance

Low-grade tumours are well demarcated and sometimes polypoid within the airways, with an average size of 30 mm. High-grade tumours are more infiltrative. The cut surface varies from soft to firm and white/grey to yellow, depending on the extent of fibrosis and mucin-filled cells. Tumours may sometimes be cystic.

Hyalinizing clear cell carcinomas are tan-white, relatively circumscribed, unencapsulated tumours. Invasion into bronchial cartilage can occur [15]. Sizes range from 9 to $3 5 ~ \mathsf { m m }$ [5, 16].

# Histopathology

Low-grade tumours comprise varying numbers of mucin secreting, squamoid, and intermediate cells. Cystic areas are typically lined by mucin-secreting cells. Solid areas comprise intermediate cells and/or non-keratinizing squamoid cells. Clear cell and oncocytic changes are often focally seen and may rarely predominate. Mitoses are rare. Background stroma may show calcification, ossification, a granulomatous reaction to extravasated mucin, and sometimes a florid inflammatory host response. High-grade tumours are rare and are mainly composed of atypical squamoid and intermediate cells, with frequent mitosis and necrosis, accompanied by variable numbers of mucin-secreting cells. All cell types are positive for cytokeratins and negative for TTF1, napsin A, SMA, and S100 [11, 6]. Squamoid cells are positive for p40, p63, and CK5/6.

The important differential diagnosis is metastasis from a head and neck salivary gland tumour. Clinical correlation is necessary because MECs arising in other anatomical sites show MAML2 gene rearrangement and a similar morphology. MAML2 gene rearrangement is seen almost exclusively in MEC and can exclude adenosquamous carcinoma in some cases [1]. Criteria more typical of high-grade MEC than adenosquamous carcinoma include (1) proximal exophytic endobronchial location; (2) transitional areas from low grade to high grade; (3) no overlying squamous cell carcinoma in situ; (4) lack of individual cell keratinization or squamous pearl formation; and (5) absence of tubular, acinar, and papillary growth pattern [18, 13]. The lack of intermediate and squamoid cells discriminates mucous gland adenoma from low-grade MEC.

# Immunophenotype

None

# Differential diagnosis

None

# Cytology

All three cell types (squamoid, glandular, and intermediate) may be intermingled [14]. Glandular cells may be present singly or in acinar formations. Intermediate cells are round to elongated, with dense homogeneous cytoplasm. Squamoid cells are larger, with centrally located round nuclei.

# Diagnostic molecular pathology

Testing for CRTC1-MAML2 gene fusion by FISH can help to establish the diagnosis of MEC [1, 11].

# Essential and desirable diagnostic criteria

Essential-low-grade:

· Cytologically bland mucin-secreting, squamoid, and intermediate cells

Essential-high-grade:

· Atypical mucin-secreting, squamoid, and intermediate cells. Transition from low-grade to high-grade areas, absence of keratinization, central location, and absence of carcinoma in situ

Desirable:
· Identification of CRTC1-MAML2 gene fusion.

# Grading / Staging

Staging is according to the Union for International Cancer Control (UICC) TNM classification.

# Prognosis and prediction

Low-grade MECs have a good prognosis. High-grade tumours have a prognosis similar to that of other non-small cell carcinomas. Incomplete resection and nodal metastases are poor prognostic factors [18, 13, 9]. CRTC1-MAML2 fusion positive cases are associated with better survival [2].

# References
1. Achcar RdeO, Nikiforova MN, Dacic S, et al. Mammalian mastermind like 2 11q21 gene rearrangement in bronchopulmonary mucoepidermoid carcinoma. Hum Pathol. 2009 Jun;40(6):854-60. PMID:19269006
2. Behboudi A, Enlund F, Winnes M et al. Molecular classification of mucoepidermoid carcinomas - prognostic significance of the MECT1-MAML2 fusion oncogene. Genes Chromosomes Cancer. 2006 May;45(5):470-81. PMID:16444749
3. Cheng DL, Hu YX, Hu PQ, et al. Clin of primary pulmonary mucoepidermoid carcinoma. Clin Radiol. 2017 Jul;72(7):610.e1-7. PMID:28292512
4. Doxtader EE, Shah AA, Zhang Y tl Primary salivary gland-type tumors of the tracheobronchial tree diagnosed by transbronchial fine needle aspiration: clinical and cytomorphologic features with histopathologic correlation. Diagn Cytopathol. 2019 Nov;47(11):1168-76 PMID:31343850
5. Falk N mary pulmonary salivary gland-type tumors: a review and update. Adv Anat Pathol. 2016 Jan;23(1):13-23. PMID:26645458
6. Huo Z, Wu H, Li J et al. Primary pulmonary mucoepidermoid carcinoma: histopathological and molecular genetic studies of 26 cases. PLoS One. 2015 Nov 17;10(11):e0143169. PMID:26575266
7. rdde tracheal hyalinizing clear cell carcinoma. Lung Cancer. 2018 Nov;125:102. PMID:30429005
8. Jeffus SK, Gardner JM, Steliga MA, et al. Hyalinizing clear cell carcinoma of the lung: case report and review of the literature. Am J Clin Pathol. 2017 Jul 1;148(1):73-80. PMID:28927164
9. Mlina JR, Aubry MC, Lewis JE et l Primary salivary gland-type lung cancer pec trum of cin cale setation ogic and prognostic factors. Cancer. 2007 Nov 15:110(10):2253-9. PMID:17918258
10. Qin BD, Jiao XD, Liu K, et al. Clinical pathological and treatment factors associated with the survival of patients with primary pulmonary salivary gland-type tumors. Lung Cancer 2018 Dec;126:174-81. PMID:30527184
11. Roden AC, Garcia JJ, Wehrs RN, et al. Histopathologic, immunophenotypic and cytogenetic features of pulmonary mucoepidermoid carcinoma. Mod Pathol. 2014 Nov;27(11):1479-88. PMID:24743219
13. Salem A, Bell D, Sepesi B, et al. Clinicopathologic and genetic features of primary bronchopulmonary mucoepidermoid carcinoma: the MD Anderson Cancer Center experience and comprehensive review of the literature. Virchows Arch. 2017 Jun;470(6):619-26. PMID:28343305
14. Segletes LA, Steffe CH, Geisinger KR. Cytology of primary pulmonary mucoepidermoid and adenoid cystic carcinoma. A report of four cases. Acta Cytol. 1999 Nov Dec;43(6):1091-7. PMID:10578983
15. Shah AA, Mehrad M, Kelting SM, et al. An uncommon primary lung tumour: hyalinizing clear cell carcinoma salivary gland type. Histopathology. 2015 Aug;67(2):274-6. PMID:25545688
16. Takamatsu ato Yu t Hyalinizing clear cell carcinoma of the bronchial glands: presentation of three cases and pathological comparisons with salivary gland counterparts and bronchial mucoepidermoid carcinomas. Mod Pathol. 2018 Jun;31(6):923-3. PMID:29434341
17. Tonon G, Modi S, Wu L, et al. t(11;19) (q21:p13) translocation in mucoepidermoid carcinoma creates a novel fusion product that disrupts a Notch signaling pathway. Nat Genet. 2003 Feb:33(2):208-13. PMID:12539049
18. Yousem SA, Hochholzer L. Mucoepidermoid tumors of the lung. Cancer. 1987 Sep 15:60(6):1346-52. PMID:3040215

Squamoid cells are larger, with centrally located round nuclei.
Staging is according to the Union for International Cancer Control (UICC) TNM classification.
Low-grade tumours comprise varying numbers of mucin-secreting, squamoid, and intermediate cells.
Cystic areas are typically lined by mucin-secreting cells.
Solid areas comprise intermediate cells and/or non-keratinizing squamoid cells.
Clear cell and oncocytic changes are often focally seen and may rarely predominate.
Mitoses are rare in low-grade tumours.
Background stroma may show calcification, ossification, a granulomatous reaction to extravasated mucin, and sometimes a florid inflammatory host response.
High-grade tumours are rare and are mainly composed of atypical squamoid and intermediate cells, with frequent mitosis and necrosis, accompanied by variable numbers of mucin-secreting cells.
All cell types are positive for cytokeratins and negative for TTF1, napsin A, SMA, and S100.
Squamoid cells are positive for p40, p63, and CK5/6.
The important differential diagnosis is metastasis from a head and neck salivary gland tumour.
Clinical correlation is necessary because MECs arising in other anatomical sites show MAML2 gene rearrangement and a similar morphology.
MAML2 gene rearrangement is seen almost exclusively in MEC and can exclude adenosquamous carcinoma in some cases.
Criteria more typical of high-grade MEC than adenosquamous carcinoma include proximal exophytic endobronchial location.
Criteria more typical of high-grade MEC than adenosquamous carcinoma include transitional areas from low grade to high grade.
Criteria more typical of high-grade MEC than adenosquamous carcinoma include no overlying squamous cell carcinoma in situ.
Criteria more typical of high-grade MEC than adenosquamous carcinoma include lack of individual cell keratinization or squamous pearl formation.
Criteria more typical of high-grade MEC than adenosquamous carcinoma include absence of tubular, acinar, and papillary growth pattern.
The lack of intermediate and squamoid cells discriminates mucous gland adenoma from low-grade MEC.
Cytologically bland mucin-secreting, squamoid, and intermediate cells are essential diagnostic criteria for low-grade.
Atypical mucin-secreting, squamoid, and intermediate cells are essential diagnostic criteria for high-grade.
Transition from low-grade to high-grade areas is an essential diagnostic criterion for high-grade.
Absence of keratinization is an essential diagnostic criterion for high-grade.
Central location is an essential diagnostic criterion for high-grade.
Absence of carcinoma in situ is an essential diagnostic criterion for high-grade.
Identification of CRTC1-MAML2 gene fusion is a desirable diagnostic criterion.
Low-grade MECs have a good prognosis.
High-grade tumours have a prognosis similar to that of other non-small cell carcinomas.
Incomplete resection and nodal metastases are poor prognostic factors.
CRTC1-MAML2 fusion positive cases are associated with better survival.
