prompt: Squamous cell carcinoma of the lung

System.role_and_constraints: You are a medical guideline writer. Based on authoritative literature, write a chapter of an authoritative medical report. You need to follow the chapter format below.

content:
# Squamous cell carcinoma of the lung

# Definition

Squamous cell carcinoma (SCC) is a malignant epithelial tumour characterized by the presence of keratinization, intercellular bridges, or immunohistochemical markers of squamous cell differentiation.

Chou TY Chang YL Wong MP

Lymphoepithelial carcinoma is a poorly differentiated squamous cell carcinoma (SCC) admixed with variable amounts of lymphoplasmacytic infiltrate, frequently associated with EBV.

# ICD-O coding / ICD-11 coding

8070/3 Squamous cell carcinoma, NOS

2C25.2 Squamous cell carcinoma of bronchus or lung

8082/3 Lymphoepithelial carcinoma

2C25.2 & XH1E40 Squamous cell carcinoma of bronchus or lung & Lymphoepithelial carcinoma

# Related terminology

Not recommended: epidermoid carcinoma.

Not recommended: lymphoepithelioma-like carcinoma; lymphoepithelioma; lymphoepithelial-like carcinoma.

Subtype(s)

# Subtype(s)

SCC, keratinizing; SCC, non-keratinizing; basaloid SCC

# Localization

Pulmonary SCCs usually arise from a main or lobar bronchus and historically at least two thirds are located in the central compartment of the lung. Although precise definitions of “central” and “peripheral" are lacking so far [14], about one third of cases are peripherally located and their incidence seems to be increasing [37]. Peripheral SCC is particularly evident in patients with underlying interstitial lung disease [23].

These tumours are often peripherally located. Intrabronchial components are noted in a minority of cases [16].

# Clinical features

The signs and symptoms of SCC are similar to those of other non-small cell lung carcinomas (see Tumours of the lung: Introduction, p. 20) but are generally related to the tumour location. SCC has a tendency to be locally aggressive, with progressive involvement of peribronchial structures through direct invasion, but distant metastases occur similarly to other non-small cell lung carcinomas. Symptoms are related to endobronchial obstruction, leading to haemoptysis, cough, and recurrent pneumonitis. With central tumours, the proximity to the carina is a crucial factor for planning surgical treatment [53]. Peripherally, SCC may show cavitation with superimposed fungal and bacterial infections [38].

As many as one third of cases are identified by an incidental radiographic finding [39]. Cough with or without blood-tinged sputum is the most common presenting symptom [16]. Others include chest pain, body-weight loss, and haemoptysis [16, 39].

# Imaging

Tumours usually form a discrete coin lesion on imaging. Thin-walled cavitary lesions [29, 63] and pleural effusions are uncommon [16].

# Spread

None

# Epidemiology

See Tumours of the lung: Introduction (p.20).

Like all lung cancers, but to a higher degree than adenocarcinoma, SCC is strongly associated with smoking, and worldwide trends in SCC incidence closely mirror changes in smoking patterns [46]. SCC is the second most prevalent type, accounting for approximately $20\%$ of lung cancer [4]. SCC is the most common histological type of lung cancer arising in patients with idiopathic pulmonary fibrosis and interstitial lung disease [55, 61, 62].

These tumours are rare ($0.92\%$ of non-small cell lung carcinomas), predominantly affecting younger, Asian, non-smokers, with a median age of 51 years (range: 9-74 years) [60, 17, 18, 40, 3]. Most studies [13, 17, 18, 21], but also [39, 27], show a female predominance.

# Etiology

See Tumours of the lung: Introduction (p.20).

More than $90 \%$ of patients with SCC are current or former heavy smokers, and a male predilection is reported [28]. Carcinogenic risk factors include exposure to radon gas, metals (arsenic, cadmium, chromium), radiation, air pollution, and infections [22]. Other risk factors for SCC include age, family history, and exposure to secondhand smoke.

Smoking has a relatively predictable mutagenic signature (signature 4), preferentially acting on guanine base pairs and creating C>A substitutions [1]. Rarely, some SCCs develop in never-smokers or light smokers, and these tumours may harbour driver mutations found more commonly in adenocarcinoma, such as EGFR and ALK mutations [64,65,66,67]. A putative role of HPV in SCC pathogenesis is controversial [54]. Several studies with rigorously controlled HPV detection protocols and exclusion of metastases from cervical or oropharyngeal cancers have questioned the association, at least in populations of European descent [7, 19, 56, 12].

Association with EBV is found in more than $90\%$ of cases occurring in Asian patients, but at a much lower frequency in patients of European descent. The clinicopathological manifestations, as well as patients’ EBV serological profile, the presence of EBV-encoded small RNA 1 (EBER1), expression of LMP1 and BCL2 in the tumour cells, and intratumoural infiltration of $\mathrm{CD8+}$ T lymphocytes suggest a role of EBV in the genesis of the EBV-associated subtype of lymphoepithelial carcinoma [16, 39, 29]. There is a parallel correlation between the EBV serology titre and both tumour burden and stage [16].

# Pathogenesis

SCC develops via multistep transformation, which is associated with progressive accumulation of genetic and epigenetic aberrations and with increasing morphological dysplasia (see Squamous dysplasia and carcinoma in situ of the lung, p.85). Squamous cell carcinogenesis is linked closely to genomic perturbations, genetic mutations, and/or altered expression of key molecules involved in various stages of squamous cell lineage commitment and/or terminal differentiation [22, 34]. Comprehensive genomic characterization of SCC revealed complex genomic alterations, with a mean of 360 exonic mutations, 165 genomic rearrangements, and 323 segments of copy number alteration per tumour [11]. Two pathways appear to be preferentially involved by genetic alterations. The first involves the oxidative stress pathway and includes mutations in NFE2L2, KEAP1, or CUL3. The second is implicated in squamous differentiation and includes overexpression and amplification of SOX2 and TP63; loss-of-function mutations in NOTCH1, NOTCH2, and ASCL4; and focal deletions in FOXP1.

Frequent molecular alterations found in pulmonary SCC include gain/amplification of chromosomes 3q (SOX2, TP63) [5, 52], 7p (EGFR), and 8p (FGFR1), as well as frequent deletion of chromosome 9p (CDKN2A) [30, 20]. Commonly mutated genes include P53, CDKN2A, PEN, PK3CA, K1, KMT2 (MLL2), HLA-A, NFE2L2, NOTCH1, and RB1 [11, 35]. Gene expression profiling has identified four subtypes: primitive, classic, secretory, and basal [33]. However, there is no correlation between the basal gene expression subtype and the basaloid histological subtype [11].

Basaloid SCC shares most of the mutation and copy-number alteration landscape of classic SCCs. However, transcriptomic analyses compared with non-basaloid squamous carcinoma revealed differential upregulation of genes related to cell cycle, embryonic development, mRNA splicing, chromatin modification pathways, and downregulation of the squamous differentiation, in line with the aggressiveness and poor differentiation of this tumour [9].

TP53, KRAS, and EGFR mutations as well as ALK and ROS1 translocations are typically absent, suggesting a molecular tumorigenesis distinct from that of conventional non-small cell lung carcinomas [16, 39, 18, 13, 2].

# Macroscopic appearance

Pulmonary SCC has a firm white, light-brown, or grey cut surface, occasionally with carbon pigment deposits in the centre and star-like retractions on the periphery. Focal haemorrhage may be present. In larger tumours, central cavitation due to necrosis may occur. Central tumours are often endobronchial with exophytic papillary growth. Obstruction of bronchi is common, often with adjacent atelectasis and obstructive pneumonia, which might be superimposed by fungal and/or bacterial infections.

The tumours appear to be solitary, round to ovoid, and circumscribed, ranging in size from 10 to $1 1 0 \mathsf { m m }$. The cut surfaces are pink-white and fleshy [16, 60].

# Histopathology

Keratinizing, non-keratinizing, and basaloid SCC subtypes are recognized, with the same histomorphological features as SCCs at other anatomical regions.

Keratinizing SCC is characterized by morphological features of keratinization (presence of keratinization, pearl formation, and/or intercellular bridges). The extent of keratinization varies with the degree of differentiation: it is prominent in better-differentiated tumours where there is typically widespread keratinization, although it may be present only focally or be less apparent in those that are more poorly differentiated.

Non-keratinizing SCC is a solid non-small cell carcinoma that lacks keratinization. In these tumours, the squamous cell differentiation requires confirmation by immunohistochemical markers (see below). The presence of intracellular mucin in a few cells does not exclude tumours from this category. The issue of small-sample diagnosis is addressed in Small diagnostic samples (p. 29). Some non-keratinizing SCCs may morphologically resemble urothelial transitional cell carcinoma.

Basaloid SCC is characterized by small to intermediate-sized cells with lobular architecture and peripheral palisading that lack squamous morphology, but it shows immunohistochemical expression of squamous markers. Cytoplasm is scant but well defined. Mitotic count is high (15-50 mitoses/2 $\mathsf { m m } ^ { 2 }$) and the Ki-67 index is very high $( 5 0 - 8 0 \% )$. Keratin pearl formation may be seen. Rosettes can be seen. Stroma may show a hyaline or mucoid appearance. Carcinoma in situ is frequent. SCCs (whether keratinizing or non-keratinizing) with a basaloid component of $\geq 5 0 \%$ are classified as basaloid SCC.

All histological subtypes of SCC can show a variety of unusual patterns, including spindle cell morphology and clear cell cytological changes, as well as papillary, pseudovascular, or alveolar-filling patterns.

There is currently no established grading system for SCC of the lung.

Most tumours show a syncytial pattern of growth and a marked lymphoplasmacytic infiltration within and between the tumour islands. In a minority of cases, lymphoplasmacytic infiltration is not so prominent, resulting in a morphology resembling that of non-keratinizing SCC [60, 57]. Tumour cells show a moderate amount of eosinophilic cytoplasm, large vesicular nuclei, and prominent eosinophilic nucleoli. Focal keratinization, spindle cell growth, and intratumoural amyloid deposition can occur [60, 31, 15, 16, 26]. The mitotic count is variable [26]. The tumour mostly has pushing borders at the interface with the adjacent lung parenchyma and grows in the form of irregularly shaped islands or diffuse sheets with infrequent necrosis [16, 39, 31]. Spread through alveolar spaces (spread through airspaces) of the tumour cells can be observed [60]. The stroma may show a non-necrotizing granulomatous reaction [60, 25].

# Immunophenotype

Diffuse positive staining with a squamous cell marker and negative staining for TTF1 confirm the diagnosis of SCC, although some markedly keratinized tumours may show lack of staining with squamous cell markers. In biopsies, the p40-positive staining rate of tumour cells should be $> 50\%$ [59]. p40 is considered the most specific marker for SCCs, whereas high molecular-weight cytokeratins (CK5/6, 34βE12), desmocollin, desmoglein, and p63 are less specific markers of squamous cell differentiation [50, 47, 59]. Neuroendocrine markers can occasionally be positive (especially CD56), but if p40 is diffusely positive, the tumour is classified as SCC.

# Differential diagnosis

The differential diagnosis of SCC varies depending on the histological subtype.

The main differential diagnoses are between poorly differentiated or basaloid SCC versus poorly differentiated non-small cell lung carcinoma NOS, particularly on small biopsy specimens with limited tumour tissue (see Small diagnostic samples, p.29), or large cell carcinoma with a null phenotype in resection specimens. In these cases, phenotyping by a limited panel of immunomarkers, including the most specific and sensitive primary antibodies indicating squamous cell (p40) and adenocarcinoma (TTF1) histology, is required [50, 47, 59]. Mucin stains may be misleading, because SCC cells may be focally positive. Tumour entrapment of bronchial cells or peribronchial salivary glands may erroneously lead to a diagnosis of adenocarcinoma or adenosquamous carcinoma. It is well known that a subset of adenocarcinomas can show (although rarely) a pseudosquamous appearance, and some SCC has a pseudoadenocarcinomatous morphology [45].

Mucoepidermoid carcinoma may be challenging in small biopsy or cytology. However, it should show a mucin-producing glandular component and/or a MAML2 translocation [8].

Basaloid SCC must be differentiated from large cell neuroendocrine carcinoma (LCNEC), small cell carcinoma (small cell lung carcinoma), high-grade adenoid cystic carcinoma, NUT carcinoma, and poorly differentiated squamous cell or adenocarcinoma. Palisading and rosette-like structures, as well as some degree of neuroendocrine marker expression, can be seen in basaloid SCCs mimicking LCNEC. Some basaloid SCCs have very small tumour cells, morphologically resembling or identical to small cell lung carcinoma. However, diffuse p40 expression favours basaloid SCC and excludes LCNEC or small cell lung carcinoma. High-grade adenoid cystic carcinomas can express SOX10, and if better-differentiated areas are present, these may show dual staining of CAM5.2, which is strong in ductal/luminal cells but weak in myoepithelial cells, with positive p63 and S100 in myoepithelial cells. Adenoid cystic carcinomas also can have MYB-NFIB or MYBL1-NFIB fusions by FISH (see Adenoid cystic carcinoma of the lung, p.117). NUT carcinomas express positive staining with the NUT antibody or NUT gene fusion by molecular analysis (see NUT carcinoma of the thorax, p.364) [33, 6] and are more likely to be encountered in young and/or never-smoker patients.

Lymphoepithelial carcinoma has a squamous phenotype but is usually clearly recognized by the dense intratumoural inflammatory cells, positive in situ hybridization for EBV, and frequent lack of strong smoking history in most cases (see Lymphoepithelial carcinoma of the lung, p.94).

SMARCA4-deficient tumours (see Thoracic SMARCA4-deficient undifferentiated tumour, p.111) are undifferentiated neoplasms, mostly representing smoking-related poorly differentiated carcinomas characterized by SMARCA4 (BRG1) deficiency that can express focal p40 staining in some cases [44, 48].

Metastatic tumours (see Metastasis to the lung, p.452) with squamous and/or squamoid differentiation are particularly difficult to differentiate from primary pulmonary SCC. Clinical and radiological correlation is most important in addressing this question. Urothelial carcinomas more often stain positively with GATA3, uroplakin-3, and CK20 [24]. Immunohistochemical stains against CD5 and KIT (CD117) are helpful to demonstrate a thymic origin [36]. Distinguishing primary lung SCC from a metastasis in patients with a history of SCC elsewhere (e.g. head and neck region, oesophagus, uterine cervix) can also be challenging. The presence of associated precursor lesions helps to confirm lung primaries. Molecular comparison using multigenic panel and HPV testing/genotyping of lung tumour and extrathoracic SCC can be helpful in these cases.

Florid squamous cell metaplasia - due to infarction, diffuse alveolar damage, postinflammatory processes (infections and radiation), or benign lesions (granular cell tumour) - should always be considered by integrating morphology with clinical and imaging findings. Squamous papilloma/papillomatosis should be excluded in the presence of endobronchial lesions with or without condylomatous changes.

# Cytology

The cytological features of SCC vary depending on the degree of squamous differentiation and the sampling method [32, 51]. The classic pattern of SCC shows singly scattered or clusters of atypical cells in the background of necrotic debris: a granular, amorphous precipitate with nuclear debris and red blood cells called tumour diathesis. In a well-differentiated SCC, the malignant cells are usually non-cohesive and show a variety of shapes (polygonal, rounded, spindle, tadpole), with abundant smooth, dense cytoplasm filled with keratin. The cytoplasm stains green, yellow, or orange with the Pap stain and robin's egg blue with Romanowsky stains. Nuclei are usually small, hyperchromatic, and smudgy, and nucleoli are often inconspicuous.

In moderately and poorly differentiated SCC, keratinization is less apparent, cytoplasm is scant, and tumour cells appear in cohesive aggregates with elongated or spindle nuclei.

Nuclei are hyperchromatic and they have a prominent nucleolus and a highly irregular pattern of chromatin distribution. The basaloid subtype of SCC shows prominent palisading of nuclei around the perimeter of cell groups.

In exfoliative samples, surface tumour cells predominate and occur as individually dispersed cells with prominent cytoplasmic keratinization and dark pyknotic nuclei. In contrast, in brushings, cells from deeper layers are sampled, showing a much greater proportion of cohesive aggregates.

Centrally located SCC may exfoliate tumour cells that can be identified in sputum cytology.

# Diagnostic molecular pathology

EGFR mutation or ALK rearrangement can occur in lung SCC. This possibility should be considered in young never-smoking patients, in particular those with a history of lung adenocarcinoma with one of these genetic alterations [42, 43, 58, 49].

# Essential and desirable diagnostic criteria

Essential:

· Definitive morphological features such as intercellular bridges and/or keratinization, or a poorly differentiated tumour with immunohistochemical evidence of squamous cell differentiation (positive p40 and negative TTF1). Keratinizing SCC can be diagnosed without the use of immunostains [59].
· The basaloid component should be $\ge 50\%$ for the diagnosis of basaloid SCC.

Desirable:

· Exclusion of possible metastasis from SCC at extrapulmonary sites or other primary neoplasms with squamous cell differentiation (e.g. NUT carcinoma, mucoepidermoid carcinoma, SMARCA4-deficient tumour, or thymic SCC).

# Grading / Staging

SCCs are staged according to the eighth edition of the Union for International Cancer Control (UICC) / American Joint Committee on Cancer (AJCC) TNM classification (see TNM staging of carcinomas of the lung, p.11 [10]). Superficial spreading tumours with an invasive component limited to the bronchial wall are classified as T1a.

# Prognosis and prediction

The prognosis of a SCC patient depends mainly on the patient's performance score and the clinical/tumour stage at time of diagnosis. Although clinicopathological characteristics may differ, the prognoses of central and peripheral SCCs seem to be comparable [41]. There are no validated clinical factors or biomarkers that are predictive of tumour response to local or systemic therapies. Histological subtyping does not correlate with prognosis.

# References
1. Alexandrov LB, Nik-Zainal S, Wedge DC, et al. Signatures of mutational processes in human cancer. Nature. 2013 Aug 22;500(7463):415-21. PMID:23945592
2. Allan BJ, Thorson CM, Davis JS, et al. An analysis of 73 cases of pediatric malignant tumors of the thymus. J Surg Res. 2013 Sep;184(1):397-403. PMID:23570972

A molecular analysis of sixty-six cases, including soft tissue, bone, and visceral lesions showing common involvement of the EWSR1 gene. Genes Chromosomes Cancer. 2010 Dec;49(12):1114-24. claims.claim: Not recommended: epidermoid carcinoma.
Not recommended: lymphoepithelioma-like carcinoma.
Not recommended: lymphoepithelioma.
Not recommended: lymphoepithelial-like carcinoma.
Squamous cell carcinoma (SCC) is a malignant epithelial tumour characterized by the presence of keratinization, intercellular bridges, or immunohistochemical markers of squamous cell differentiation.
Lymphoepithelial carcinoma is a poorly differentiated squamous cell carcinoma (SCC) admixed with variable amounts of lymphoplasmacytic infiltrate.
Lymphoepithelial carcinoma is frequently associated with EBV.
8070/3 Squamous cell carcinoma, NOS
2C25.2 Squamous cell carcinoma of bronchus or lung
8082/3 Lymphoepithelial carcinoma
2C25.2 & XH1E40 Squamous cell carcinoma of bronchus or lung & Lymphoepithelial carcinoma
SCC, keratinizing
SCC, non-keratinizing
Basaloid SCC
Tumours usually form a discrete coin lesion on imaging.
Thin-walled cavitary lesions are uncommon.
Pleural effusions are uncommon.
Pulmonary SCCs usually arise from a main or lobar bronchus.
Historically, at least two thirds of pulmonary SCCs are located in the central compartment of the lung.
Precise definitions of 'central' and 'peripheral' are lacking so far.
About one third of pulmonary SCC cases are peripherally located.
The incidence of peripherally located pulmonary SCC cases seems to be increasing.
Peripheral SCC is particularly evident in patients with underlying interstitial lung disease.
These tumours are often peripherally located.
Intrabronchial components are noted in a minority of cases.
The signs and symptoms of SCC are similar to those of other non-small cell lung carcinomas but are generally related to the tumour location.
SCC has a tendency to be locally aggressive, with progressive involvement of peribronchial structures through direct invasion.
Distant metastases in SCC occur similarly to other non-small cell lung carcinomas.
Symptoms of SCC are related to endobronchial obstruction, leading to haemoptysis, cough, and recurrent pneumonitis.
With central tumours, the proximity to the carina is a crucial factor for planning surgical treatment.
Peripherally, SCC may show cavitation with superimposed fungal and bacterial infections.
As many as one third of SCC cases are identified by an incidental radiographic finding.
Cough with or without blood-tinged sputum is the most common presenting symptom of SCC.
Other symptoms of SCC include chest pain, body-weight loss, and haemoptysis.
SCC is strongly associated with smoking, and worldwide trends in SCC incidence closely mirror changes in smoking patterns.
SCC is the second most prevalent type, accounting for approximately 20% of lung cancer.
SCC is the most common histological type of lung cancer arising in patients with idiopathic pulmonary fibrosis and interstitial lung disease.
These tumours are rare, accounting for 0.92% of non-small cell lung carcinomas.
These tumours predominantly affect younger, Asian, non-smokers, with a median age of 51 years (range: 9-74 years).
Most studies show a female predominance.
More than 90% of patients with SCC are current or former heavy smokers, and a male predilection is reported.
Carcinogenic risk factors for SCC include exposure to radon gas, metals (arsenic, cadmium, chromium), radiation, air pollution, and infections.
Other risk factors for SCC include age, family history, and exposure to secondhand smoke.
Smoking has a relatively predictable mutagenic signature (signature 4), preferentially acting on guanine base pairs and creating C>A substitutions.
Rarely, some SCCs develop in never-smokers or light smokers, and these tumours may harbour driver mutations found more commonly in adenocarcinoma, such as EGFR and ALK mutations.
A putative role of HPV in SCC pathogenesis is controversial.

Several studies with rigorously controlled HPV detection protocols and exclusion of metastases from cervical or oropharyngeal cancers have questioned the association of HPV with SCC, at least in populations of European descent.
Association with EBV is found in more than 90% of SCC cases occurring in Asian patients, but at a much lower frequency in patients of European descent.

The clinicopathological manifestations, as well as patients’ EBV serological profile, the presence of EBV-encoded small RNA 1 (EBER1), expression of LMP1 and BCL2 in the tumour cells, and intratumoural infiltration of CD8+ T lymphocytes suggest a role of EBV in the genesis of the EBV-associated subtype of lymphoepithelial carcinoma.
There is a parallel correlation between the EBV serology titre and both tumour burden and stage.
SCC develops via multistep transformation, which is associated with progressive accumulation of genetic and epigenetic aberrations and with increasing morphological dysplasia.

Squamous cell carcinogenesis is linked closely to genomic perturbations, genetic mutations, and/or altered expression of key molecules involved in various stages of squamous cell lineage commitment and/or terminal differentiation.

Comprehensive genomic characterization of SCC revealed complex genomic alterations, with a mean of 360 exonic mutations, 165 genomic rearrangements, and 323 segments of copy number alteration per tumour.
Two pathways appear to be preferentially involved by genetic alterations in SCC: the oxidative stress pathway and the squamous differentiation pathway.
The oxidative stress pathway includes mutations in NFE2L2, KEAP1, or CUL3.
The squamous differentiation pathway includes overexpression and amplification of SOX2 and TP63; loss-of-function mutations in NOTCH1, NOTCH2, and ASCL4; and focal deletions in FOXP1.
Frequent molecular alterations found in pulmonary SCC include gain/amplification of chromosomes 3q (SOX2, TP63), 7p (EGFR), and 8p (FGFR1), as well as frequent deletion of chromosome 9p (CDKN2A).
Commonly mutated genes in SCC include P53, CDKN2A, PEN, PK3CA, K1, KMT2 (MLL2), HLA-A, NFE2L2, NOTCH1, and RB1.
Gene expression profiling has identified four subtypes of SCC: primitive, classic, secretory, and basal.
There is no correlation between the basal gene expression subtype and the basaloid histological subtype.
Basaloid SCC shares most of the mutation and copy-number alteration landscape of classic SCCs.

Transcriptomic analyses of basaloid SCC compared with non-basaloid squamous carcinoma revealed differential upregulation of genes related to cell cycle, embryonic development, mRNA splicing, chromatin modification pathways, and downregulation of squamous differentiation.

TP53, KRAS, and EGFR mutations as well as ALK and ROS1 translocations are typically absent in SCC, suggesting a molecular tumorigenesis distinct from that of conventional non-small cell lung carcinomas.
Pulmonary SCC has a firm white, light-brown, or grey cut surface, occasionally with carbon pigment deposits in the centre and star-like retractions on the periphery.
Focal haemorrhage may be present in pulmonary SCC.
In larger tumours, central cavitation due to necrosis may occur in pulmonary SCC.
Central tumours in pulmonary SCC are often endobronchial with exophytic papillary growth.
Obstruction of bronchi is common in pulmonary SCC, often with adjacent atelectasis and obstructive pneumonia.
Obstructive pneumonia in pulmonary SCC might be superimposed by fungal and/or bacterial infections.
The tumours in pulmonary SCC appear to be solitary, round to ovoid, and circumscribed, ranging in size from 10 to 110 mm.
The cut surfaces of pulmonary SCC tumours are pink-white and fleshy.
Diffuse positive staining with a squamous cell marker and negative staining for TTF1 confirm the diagnosis of SCC.
Some markedly keratinized tumours may show lack of staining with squamous cell markers.
In biopsies, the p40-positive staining rate of tumour cells should be > 50%.
p40 is considered the most specific marker for SCCs.
High-molecular-weight cytokeratins (CK5/6, 34βE12), desmocollin, desmoglein, and p63 are less specific markers of squamous cell differentiation.
Neuroendocrine markers can occasionally be positive (especially CD56), but if p40 is diffusely positive, the tumour is classified as SCC.
Keratinizing, non-keratinizing, and basaloid SCC subtypes are recognized, with the same histomorphological features as SCCs at other anatomical regions.
Keratinizing SCC is characterized by morphological features of keratinization, including the presence of keratinization, pearl formation, and/or intercellular bridges.
The extent of keratinization varies with the degree of differentiation in keratinizing SCC.
Non-keratinizing SCC is a solid non-small cell carcinoma that lacks keratinization.
In non-keratinizing SCC, squamous cell differentiation requires confirmation by immunohistochemical markers.
Some non-keratinizing SCCs may morphologically resemble urothelial transitional cell carcinoma.
Basaloid SCC is characterized by small to intermediate-sized cells with lobular architecture and peripheral palisading that lack squamous morphology.
Basaloid SCC shows immunohistochemical expression of squamous markers.
In basaloid SCC, the mitotic count is high (15-50 mitoses/2 mm²) and the Ki-67 index is very high (50-80%).
SCCs with a basaloid component of ≥ 50% are classified as basaloid SCC.

All histological subtypes of SCC can show a variety of unusual patterns, including spindle cell morphology and clear cell cytological changes, as well as papillary, pseudovascular, or alveolar-filling patterns.
There is currently no established grading system for SCC of the lung.
Most tumours show a syncytial pattern of growth and a marked lymphoplasmacytic infiltration within and between the tumour islands.
In a minority of cases, lymphoplasmacytic infiltration is not so prominent, resulting in a morphology resembling that of non-keratinizing SCC.
Tumour cells show a moderate amount of eosinophilic cytoplasm, large vesicular nuclei, and prominent eosinophilic nucleoli.
Focal keratinization, spindle cell growth, and intratumoural amyloid deposition can occur.
The mitotic count is variable.
The tumour mostly has pushing borders at the interface with the adjacent lung parenchyma and grows in the form of irregularly shaped islands or diffuse sheets with infrequent necrosis.
Spread through alveolar spaces (spread through airspaces) of the tumour cells can be observed.
The stroma may show a non-necrotizing granulomatous reaction.
The cytological features of SCC vary depending on the degree of squamous differentiation and the sampling method.
The classic pattern of SCC shows singly scattered or clusters of atypical cells in the background of necrotic debris.

In a well-differentiated SCC, the malignant cells are usually non-cohesive and show a variety of shapes (polygonal, rounded, spindle, tadpole), with abundant smooth, dense cytoplasm filled with keratin.
The cytoplasm in well-differentiated SCC stains green, yellow, or orange with the Pap stain and robin's egg blue with Romanowsky stains.
Nuclei in well-differentiated SCC are usually small, hyperchromatic, and smudgy, and nucleoli are often inconspicuous.
In moderately and poorly differentiated SCC, keratinization is less apparent, cytoplasm is scant, and tumour cells appear in cohesive aggregates with elongated or spindle nuclei.
Nuclei in moderately and poorly differentiated SCC are hyperchromatic and have a prominent nucleolus and a highly irregular pattern of chromatin distribution.
The basaloid subtype of SCC shows prominent palisading of nuclei around the perimeter of cell groups.
In exfoliative samples, surface tumour cells predominate and occur as individually dispersed cells with prominent cytoplasmic keratinization and dark pyknotic nuclei.
In brushings, cells from deeper layers are sampled, showing a much greater proportion of cohesive aggregates.
Centrally located SCC may exfoliate tumour cells that can be identified in sputum cytology.
EGFR mutation or ALK rearrangement can occur in lung SCC.

The possibility of EGFR mutation or ALK rearrangement should be considered in young never-smoking patients, in particular those with a history of lung adenocarcinoma with one of these genetic alterations.
The differential diagnosis of SCC varies depending on the histological subtype.

The main differential diagnoses are between poorly differentiated or basaloid SCC versus poorly differentiated non-small cell lung carcinoma NOS, particularly on small biopsy specimens with limited tumour tissue.

Phenotyping by a limited panel of immunomarkers, including the most specific and sensitive primary antibodies indicating squamous cell (p40) and adenocarcinoma (TTF1) histology, is required in certain cases.
Mucin stains may be misleading because SCC cells may be focally positive.
Tumour entrapment of bronchial cells or peribronchial salivary glands may erroneously lead to a diagnosis of adenocarcinoma or adenosquamous carcinoma.
A subset of adenocarcinomas can show a pseudosquamous appearance, and some SCC has a pseudoadenocarcinomatous morphology.
Mucoepidermoid carcinoma should show a mucin-producing glandular component and/or a MAML2 translocation.

Basaloid SCC must be differentiated from large cell neuroendocrine carcinoma (LCNEC), small cell carcinoma, high-grade adenoid cystic carcinoma, NUT carcinoma, and poorly differentiated squamous cell or adenocarcinoma.
Palisading and rosette-like structures, as well as some degree of neuroendocrine marker expression, can be seen in basaloid SCCs mimicking LCNEC.
Diffuse p40 expression favours basaloid SCC and excludes LCNEC or small cell lung carcinoma.

High-grade adenoid cystic carcinomas can express SOX10, and if better-differentiated areas are present, these may show dual staining of CAM5.2, which is strong in ductal/luminal cells but weak in myoepithelial cells, with positive p63 and S100 in myoepithelial cells.
Adenoid cystic carcinomas can have MYB-NFIB or MYBL1-NFIB fusions by FISH.
NUT carcinomas express positive staining with the NUT antibody or NUT gene fusion by molecular analysis and are more likely to be encountered in young and/or never-smoker patients.

Lymphoepithelial carcinoma has a squamous phenotype but is usually clearly recognized by the dense intratumoural inflammatory cells, positive in situ hybridization for EBV, and frequent lack of strong smoking history in most cases.

SMARCA4-deficient tumours are undifferentiated neoplasms, mostly representing smoking-related poorly differentiated carcinomas characterized by SMARCA4 (BRG1) deficiency that can express focal p40 staining in some cases.
Metastatic tumours with squamous and/or squamoid differentiation are particularly difficult to differentiate from primary pulmonary SCC.
Urothelial carcinomas more often stain positively with GATA3, uroplakin-3, and CK20.
Immunohistochemical stains against CD5 and KIT (CD117) are helpful to demonstrate a thymic origin.
Distinguishing primary lung SCC from a metastasis in patients with a history of SCC elsewhere can be challenging.
The presence of associated precursor lesions helps to confirm lung primaries.
Molecular comparison using multigenic panel and HPV testing/genotyping of lung tumour and extrathoracic SCC can be helpful in distinguishing primary lung SCC from metastasis.
Florid squamous cell metaplasia should always be considered by integrating morphology with clinical and imaging findings.
Squamous papilloma/papillomatosis should be excluded in the presence of endobronchial lesions with or without condylomatous changes.

Definitive morphological features such as intercellular bridges and/or keratinization, or a poorly differentiated tumour with immunohistochemical evidence of squamous cell differentiation (positive p40 and negative TTF1) are essential for diagnosis.
Keratinizing SCC can be diagnosed without the use of immunostains.
The basaloid component should be ≥ 50% for the diagnosis of basaloid SCC.

Exclusion of possible metastasis from SCC at extrapulmonary sites or other primary neoplasms with squamous cell differentiation (e.g., NUT carcinoma, mucoepidermoid carcinoma, SMARCA4-deficient tumour, or thymic SCC) is desirable.
SCCs are staged according to the eighth edition of the Union for International Cancer Control (UICC) / American Joint Committee on Cancer (AJCC) TNM classification.
Superficial spreading tumours with an invasive component limited to the bronchial wall are classified as T1a.
The prognosis of a SCC patient depends mainly on the patient's performance score and the clinical/tumour stage at time of diagnosis.
The prognoses of central and peripheral SCCs seem to be comparable.
There are no validated clinical factors or biomarkers that are predictive of tumour response to local or systemic therapies.
Histological subtyping does not correlate with prognosis.

comprehensiveness.criterion: Complete workflow from presentation to follow-up
comprehensiveness.explanation: Assess if the guideline covers the entire clinical workflow for SCC of the lung, from initial presentation and imaging to pathology, molecular diagnostics, integrated diagnosis, management, and follow-up. This ensures comprehensive care and aids in identifying gaps in the guideline that could affect patient outcomes.

Evaluate the guideline's coverage of baseline definitions, entity boundaries, grading, and coding using ICD-O/ICD-11. Accurate definitions and coding are crucial for consistent diagnosis and treatment planning, ensuring alignment with international standards.

Examine how the guideline addresses subtypes of SCC and handles NOS/NEC-style uncertainties, including minimum and ideal diagnostic sets. This is important for accurate classification and treatment decisions, especially in ambiguous cases.

Check the guideline's coverage of SCC's anatomic distribution, dissemination patterns, and epidemiological data, including demographic stratification. This information is vital for understanding disease prevalence and guiding targeted interventions.

Assess the guideline's completeness in describing macro/micro pathology, immunophenotype, and cytology. These elements are essential for accurate diagnosis and differentiation from other lung cancers, impacting treatment strategies.

Evaluate the guideline's coverage of molecular pathology, including entity-defining alterations, co-alterations, progression-related events, and CNV patterns. Comprehensive molecular insights are crucial for personalized treatment approaches.

Review the guideline's coverage of prognostic and predictive factors, including treatment evidence and recurrence/progression context. This information is key to tailoring patient management and improving outcomes.

insight_depth.criterion: Mechanistic depth linking genetics and phenotype
insight_depth.explanation: Assess the guideline's depth in linking genetic and epigenetic mechanisms to SCC phenotype. Understanding these links is crucial for developing targeted therapies and improving diagnostic accuracy.

insight_depth.criterion: Integration across imaging, histology, IHC, and molecular
insight_depth.explanation: Evaluate the guideline's ability to integrate findings from imaging, histology, IHC, and molecular diagnostics into coherent diagnostic reasoning. This integration is essential for accurate diagnosis and effective treatment planning.

insight_depth.criterion: Transparent diagnostic reasoning and decision points
insight_depth.explanation: Review the guideline's clarity in presenting diagnostic reasoning, including decision points, branching logic, and common pitfalls. Transparency in reasoning helps clinicians navigate complex cases and make informed decisions.

insight_depth.criterion: Prognostic stratification with biologically plausible rationale
insight_depth.explanation: Assess the guideline's depth in prognostic stratification, ensuring it provides a biologically plausible rationale for risk grouping. Accurate stratification is vital for predicting patient outcomes and tailoring treatment.

insight_depth.criterion: Evidence synthesis maturity and heterogeneity handling
insight_depth.explanation: Evaluate the guideline's maturity in synthesizing evidence, handling heterogeneity, acknowledging limitations, and conveying strength-of-evidence. Robust evidence synthesis supports reliable clinical recommendations.

insight_depth.criterion: Clinical impact reasoning for biomarkers and histologic features
insight_depth.explanation: Review the guideline's reasoning on how specific biomarkers or histologic features impact diagnosis, grade, management, surveillance, and trial eligibility. Clear impact reasoning aids in personalized patient care.

insight_depth.criterion: Resolution of conflicts between diagnostic modalities
insight_depth.explanation: Assess the guideline's approach to resolving conflicts between different diagnostic modalities. Effective resolution strategies are crucial for ensuring diagnostic accuracy and consistency in patient management.

accuracy_standards.criterion: Correct and current WHO entity naming and grading
accuracy_standards.explanation: Evaluate the guideline's adherence to correct and current WHO entity naming and grading conventions. Accurate naming and grading are essential for consistency in diagnosis and treatment across different healthcare settings.

accuracy_standards.criterion: Accurate ICD-O/ICD-11 coding usage
accuracy_standards.explanation: Assess the guideline's accuracy in using ICD-O/ICD-11 coding and handling synonyms/deprecated terms. Precise coding ensures proper classification and facilitates communication among healthcare providers.

accuracy_standards.criterion: Molecular terminology accuracy and assay interpretation
accuracy_standards.explanation: Review the guideline's accuracy in molecular terminology, including variant/fusion naming and assay interpretation. Correct terminology and interpretation are crucial for reliable molecular diagnostics and treatment decisions.

accuracy_standards.criterion: Internal consistency across thresholds and criteria
accuracy_standards.explanation: Evaluate the guideline's internal consistency, ensuring no contradictory thresholds, criteria, or marker interpretations. Consistency is vital for maintaining diagnostic accuracy and avoiding confusion in clinical practice.

accuracy_standards.criterion: Diagnostic accuracy safeguards and assay limitations
accuracy_standards.explanation: Assess the guideline's inclusion of diagnostic accuracy safeguards, addressing assay limitations, false positives/negatives, and pre-analytic variables. Safeguards are essential for ensuring reliable diagnostic outcomes.

accuracy_standards.criterion: Consistency with reporting standards and best practices
accuracy_standards.explanation: Review the guideline's consistency with widely used reporting standards and best practices for integrated diagnosis narratives. Adherence to standards ensures clarity and reliability in diagnostic reporting.

accuracy_standards.criterion: Terminology precision and factual correctness
accuracy_standards.explanation: Evaluate the guideline's precision in terminology and factual correctness. Accurate terminology and facts are crucial for effective communication and decision-making in clinical practice.

readability_utility.explanation: Assess the guideline's logical coherence and clinical flow, ensuring arguments and recommendations progress in a sensible order. Clear flow aids in understanding and applying the guideline in real-world settings.

Evaluate the guideline's use of evidence-backed statements, supported by quantitative anchors like sample sizes and effect sizes. Concrete evidence enhances the credibility and applicability of recommendations.

readability_utility.criterion: Explicit guidance for diagnosis and management
readability_utility.explanation: Review the guideline's explicit guidance for diagnosis, reporting, testing strategy, management, and follow-up. Clear guidance ensures practical usability and aids clinicians in making informed decisions.
Assess the guideline's use of tables, figures, and algorithms that materially improve decision-making. Effective presentation tools enhance understanding and facilitate clinical application.

readability_utility.criterion: Clear definitions and required reporting elements
readability_utility.explanation: Evaluate the guideline's clarity in defining terms, thresholds, and required reporting elements for integrated diagnosis. Clear definitions ensure consistency and accuracy in diagnostic reporting.

Review the guideline's feasibility across different healthcare settings, considering resource-stratified pathways and practical constraints. Feasibility ensures the guideline's applicability in varied clinical environments.

Assess the guideline's actionability and practical usability, ensuring it provides clear 'what to do' guidance for multidisciplinary care. Actionable guidelines support effective clinical decision-making and patient management.

System.role_and_constraints: You are a medical guideline writer. Based on authoritative literature, write a chapter of an authoritative medical report. You need to follow the chapter format below.

content:
# Type A thymoma (including atypical subtype)

# Definition

Type A thymoma is a thymic epithelial neoplasm with variable growth patterns, composed of usually bland spindle/oval tumour cells with few or no admixed immature lymphocytes.

Type AB thymoma is a thymic epithelial neoplasm composed of variable proportions of a lymphocyte-poor spindle cell (type A) component and a lymphocyte-rich (type B-like) component with a significant population of immature T cells.

# ICD-O coding / ICD-11 coding

8580/3 Thymoma, NOS
8581/3 Thymoma, type A

2C27.2 & XH6WN9 Malignant thymoma & Thymoma, type A

8582/3 Thymoma, type AB

2C27.2 & XHoJH0 Malignant thymoma & Thymoma, type AB

Related terminology Not recommended: mixed thymoma.

Subtype(s) None

# Related terminology

Not recommended: spindle cell thymoma.

# Subtype(s)

Atypical type A thymoma

Rare type A thymomas can display some degree of atypia, such as hypercellularity, increased mitotic counts, and particularly focal necrosis. According to several published case series [38, 3177006?, 34, 5, 1], these atypical features (perhaps with the exception of tumour necrosis) [51, 20] are not associated with higher numbers of chromosomal alterations [5] or a more aggressive behaviour than conventional type A thymomas, and metastases can occur in the absence of such "atypical" histological features. Single atypical type A thymomas with GTF2 mutations are on record [19].

# Localization

Type A thymoma is localized in the anterior (prevascular) mediastinum; ectopic occurrence is rare.

Type AB thymoma is localized in the anterior (prevascular) mediastinum.

# Clinical features

About $17 - 26\%$ of patients with type A thymomas present with myasthenia gravis [13, 24, 6, 12, 53]. Others present with symptoms related to the mass lesion or are incidentally discovered to have a mediastinal tumour on imaging examination. Association with pure red cell aplasia may occur, but is not restricted to type A thymoma [27].

Like other thymomas, type AB thymomas are frequently associated with a variety of paraneoplastic autoimmune disorders [25, 33], among which myasthenia gravis is by far the most frequent. Approximately $18 - 25\%$ of type AB thymomas are associated with myasthenia gravis [53]. Paraneoplastic pure red cell aplasia has also been reported [27]. Other patients present with symptoms related to the mass lesion, or are incidentally discovered to have a mediastinal tumour on imaging examination. The imaging features of type AB thymoma have not been studied individually, but they seem to overlap with those of the other thymoma subtypes [45].

# Imaging

None

# Spread

None

# Epidemiology

Type A thymoma is one of the rarer thymoma subtypes. In a review of more than 2400 thymomas reported in multiple international studies, type A thymomas account for 11.5% (range: 3.1-26.2%) of all thymomas [6, 7, 8]. Their ages range from 8 to 88 years (average: 64 years) [13, 30], being higher than those of patients with other thymomas (average: 50 years) [8, 29]. A slight female preponderance has been reported in most studies [13, 24].

Type AB thymoma is one of the most common thymoma subtypes and accounts for about 25% (range: 15-43%) of cases in most studies [13, 32, 21, 17, 31, 11, 53]. The mean age at disease onset is 57 years (range: 11-89 years), with a slight female predominance [13, 24, 6, 30].

# Etiology

The etiology of type A thymomas is unknown. A minor role of genetic risk factors appears possible given the rare occurrence of thymomas (including type A thymoma) with familial background [46, 3, 50].

Unknown

# Pathogenesis

An origin of type A thymomas from a putative thymic epithelial cell precursor with minimal potential for corticomedullary differentiation has been proposed [49]. In an unsupervised cluster analysis of five data platforms, type A thymomas constituted a distinct subtype related (but not identical) to type AB thymomas [43]. Most type A thymomas harbour few genetic alterations [23, 18, 43], often involving chromosome 6q25.2 [23]. Metastatic cases have a tendency to show slightly higher genetic instability than localized tumours [5]. Virtually $100 \%$ of type A thymomas, including metastatic cases, show mutations of GTF2l (p.L424H, which is considered a founder mutation) [41, 43]. GTF2l is thought to be a positive regulator of cell morphogenesis, receptor tyrosine kinase signalling, retinoic acid receptors, neuronal processes, and WNT and sonic hedgehog signaling, and a negative regulator of apoptosis, cell cycle, DNA damage response, hormone receptor signalling, breast hormone signaling, RAS/MAPK, receptor tyrosine kinase, and TSC/mTOR signaling pathways [43]. HRAS mutations are common $( 8 0 \% )$ in type A and rare in other types of thymoma [43]. Overexpression of a large microRNA cluster on chromosome 19q13.42 [44], which is thought to enhance PI3K/AKT/mTOR signalling, is another characteristic feature of type A thymomas.

A putative thymic epithelial cell precursor with the potential for bilineage, corticomedullary differentiation and restricted terminal medullary maturation has been proposed as the cell of origin [49]. An unsupervised cluster analysis of five data platforms suggested that type AB thymomas are related (but not identical) to type A thymomas [43]. The most frequent genetic alterations in type AB thymoma overlap with those observed in type A thymoma but are more frequent and more complex. These shared alterations include losses of genetic material on chromosomes 2, 4, 6q25.2-q25.3, and 13 [1629, 981]. Moreover, type A and AB thymomas share frequent mutation of GTF2l (p.L424H) [41, 43], as well as overexpression of a microRNA cluster on chromosome 19q13.42 [44], which is thought to enhance PI3K/AKT/mTOR signaling. Nuclei showing dispersed chromatin and inconspicuous nucleoli [28, 35]. The large, vesicular nuclei with distinct nucleoli that are characteristic of the neoplastic cells in type B2 thymoma are only rarely seen [28]. Medullary islands are rare and Hassall corpuscles are generally absent.

# Macroscopic appearance

Type A thymoma is generally well circumscribed or encapsulated. The cut surface is homogeneous and light tan to white, with vague lobulation and occasionally focal cystic change. Average tumour size is 72 mm [24, 40].

Type AB thymomas are usually encapsulated. The cut surface is firm and shows multiple tan-coloured nodules of various sizes separated by white fibrous bands. The average tumour size is 70-80 mm.

# Histopathology

The tumours are typically surrounded by a complete or incomplete fibrous capsule and may display coarse lobulation with thick fibrous bands. Microcystic change can occur but is often more pronounced in subcapsular areas. The tumours commonly show a fascicular, storiform, or haemangiopericytoma-like growth pattern [40]. Other characteristic growth patterns include rosettes with or without a central lumen, glandular or glomeruloid structures, haemangioma-like papillary projections in cystic spaces, and meningioma-like whorls [1561,2024,2249,1357]. Multiple patterns can occur in the same tumour. Perivascular spaces are less commonly seen than in other types of thymoma [7]. Hassall corpuscles are absent. The tumour cells are spindly and/or oval-shaped, with bland nuclei, finely dispersed “powdery" chromatin, and inconspicuous nucleoli. In some cases, subpopulations of tumour cells are polygonal, with uniform round nuclei exhibiting a similar chromatin pattern. Mitotic activity is low, with counts usually $< 4$ mitoses/2 $\mathsf { mm } ^ { 2 }$. There should be no or only very few ("easily countable") immature lymphocytes throughout. Tumours with any lymphocyte dense area (“not countable") or $> 10\%$ areas with moderate ("countable") infiltrate of TdT-positive T cells should be classified as type AB thymomas.

Type AB thymomas are usually well demarcated and often encapsulated. They show a lobulated growth pattern and are composed of a highly variable mixture of a lymphocyte-poor type A component and a type B-like lymphocyte-rich component. These components can either form discrete, separate lobules or be intricately intermingled [35]. All the diverse histological features of type A thymoma can be seen in the type A component. In addition, the type A component may comprise spindle cell fascicles that course around and among lymphocyte-rich nodules (type B-like component) like cellular fibrous septa. The type B-like areas are different from type B1, B2, or B3 thymoma. The tumour cells are small and oval, plump spindly, or polygonal in shape, with round to oval pale-staining

# Immunophenotype

The tumour cells are strongly positive for AE1-defined acidic keratins and p63/p40 [10, 16] and negative for AE3-defined basic keratins. They are always negative for CK20 [28]. In general, keratin expression is stronger in the cystic and glandular structures. EMA is variably and only focally expressed. FOXN1 and CD205 are positive [54, 37], while CD5 and KIT (CD117) are negative [22, 36]. A useful and frequent feature is aberrant expression of CD20 in the neoplastic epithelial cells, although the staining is often focal and may be missed in small biopsies. In very rare cases, single desmin-positive myoid cells may be seen. TdT-positive immature T cells may be completely absent or account for only a minority of CD3-positive T cells. CD20-positive B cells are usually absent.

Immunostaining for pancytokeratin, CK19, and p63/p40 [16] highlights the dense and compact epithelial component, especially in the lymphocyte-rich areas. The elongated spindle cells are often strongly positive for vimentin and EMA, while weakly to moderately positive for cytokeratins (except the glandular structures, which are strongly positive). Tumour cells with aberrant expression of CD20 can be seen in both type A and type B-like areas. CD20-positive B cells and desmin-positive myoid cells are usually absent. The associated lymphocytes are mainly TdT-positive immature CD3-positive T cells. There is no epithelial expression of CD5. The Ki-67 proliferation index is usually low in the neoplastic epithelium, but interpretation can be difficult due to a high number of admixed proliferating immature T cells.

# Differential diagnosis

The diagnosis of type A thymoma can be problematic in needle biopsies. As a spindle cell neoplasm in the mediastinum, its most important differential diagnoses are solitary fibrous tumour (cytokeratin-, STAT $^{3+}$, $\mathsf{CD34+}$) and synovial sarcoma (cytokeratin $+/-$ SS18-SSX $\cdot +$).

Type A thymoma differs from AB thymoma in that it lacks the lymphocyte-rich component. Any thymoma that looks like a type A thymoma but with any lymphocyte-dense area (TdT-positive T cells “impossible to count") or with $>10\%$ area showing moderate infiltrate of TdT-positive T cells ("difficult to count") should be classified as type AB thymoma.

Micronodular thymoma with lymphoid hyperplasia shows segregation of the lymphocyte-poor epithelial nodules from a lymphocyte-rich stroma lacking epithelium. It also features large numbers of B cells.

# Cytology

Reliable classification of thymomas is generally not possible in cytological specimens [2, 9, 52]. Smears of type A thymoma may contain only epithelial cells and thus can mimic other spindle cell lesions such as carcinoid, low-grade sarcoma, mesothelioma, and stromal cells in lymphoma [52, 55]. The epithelial cells do not show vesicular nuclei and have inconspicuous nucleoli. Crush artefacts in clusters were reported to be less abundant than in type AB and type B thymomas [47].

The few published descriptions state that the epithelial cells lack vesicular nuclei, show inconspicuous nucleoli and more prominent crush artefacts in cell clusters than type A thymoma and thymic carcinoma, and usually contain few lymphocytes [47].

# Diagnostic molecular pathology

GTF2 mutations are highly characteristic, although not specific, for type A thymomas and may be helpful in problematic cases.

Molecular tests are not required for the diagnosis. In exceptional cases, when the clinical or anatomical situation is unusual (e.g. ectopic thymoma), the demonstration of a GTF2/p.L424H mutation would strongly support the diagnosis.

# Essential and desirable diagnostic criteria

Essential:

· Athymic epithelial tumour with bland spindle and oval, and rarely polygonal, epithelial cells with a fascicular, storiform, or haemangiopericytomatous growth pattern
· Most cases lack areas of necrosis and have a low mitotic count and a low Ki-67 index
· Atypical type A thymomas may have higher mitotic count and focal necrosis

Desirable:

· Strong expression of epithelial markers (e.g. p63/p40)
· Paucity or absence of immature TdT-positive T cells throughout the tumour

Essential:

· A thymic tumour with a lobulated growth pattern
· Admixed spindle cell-predominant lymphocyte-poor component (type A) and lymphocyte-rich component (type B) Bland spindle, oval, and focally polygonal thymic epithelial cells and focal or diffuse abundance of immature T cells
· In type A tumours with focal lymphocytic stroma in which lymphocytes are difficult to count, ≥10% area showing infiltrate of TdT-positive T cells should be classified as type AB thymoma

Desirable:
· TdT immunostaining to assess TdT-positive cell density for differential diagnosis with type A thymoma

# Grading / Staging

Staging should follow the Union for International Cancer Control (UICC) TNM system [4, 15]. However, because most published series and clinical stratification schemes have been based on the Masaoka-Koga system [14], many experts still use and provide the Masaoka-Koga stage as additional information in their reports.

Most type A thymomas were diagnosed as limited disease, i.e. TNM stage I (corresponding to Masaoka stages I and II), in the International Thymic Malignancy Interest Group (ITMIG) cohort [53] and an independent meta-analysis (see Table 5.01, p.320).

Staging should follow the Union for International Cancer Control (UICC) TNM system [4, 15]. However, because most published series and clinical stratification schemes have been based on the Masaoka-Koga system [14], many experts still use and provide the Masaoka-Koga stage as additional information in their reports.

In the International Thymic Malignancy Interest Group (ITMIG) cohort [53] and an independent meta-analysis (see Table 5.01, p.320), >90% of type AB thymomas are in TNM stage I (corresponding to Masaoka-Koga stages I and I).

# Prognosis and prediction

The overall survival rate of patients with type A thymoma is close to $100 \%$ at 5 years and 10 years [39, 48], even though approximately $20 \%$ of these patients have Masaoka stage II or III tumours. Atypical cases tend to be at a higher stage at presentation [20].

In the ITMIG cohort, the overall survival rates of patients with R0-resected type A thymoma at 5 years and 10 years were $90 \%$ and $80 \%$, respectively. The risk of recurrence is low if the tumour can be completely surgically removed [29, 39, 48]. Cases with local recurrences or distant metastases have been documented [26, 40, 24]. The local recurrence rate after complete resection is in the range of $5 - 10 \%$ after 5 and 10 years [53]. The association with myasthenia gravis has no significant effect on prognosis [7, 39, 42].

Tumours of the thymus

The overall survival rate is $80 - 100 \%$ at 5 years and 10 years [39, 8, 48]. Most type AB thymomas can be cured by radical surgery [29, 8, 48]. Recurrence and metastasis are rare [26, 40] but do occur [24], justifying long-term clinical monitoring. The presence or absence of paraneoplastic myasthenia gravis has no major effect on prognosis [7, 39, 42]. Limited data are available regarding platinum-based chemotherapy or radiosensitivity given the limited number of patients with advanced disease requiring adjuvant treatment.

claims.claim: Spindle cell thymoma is not recommended.
Type A thymoma is a thymic epithelial neoplasm with variable growth patterns.
Type A thymoma is composed of usually bland spindle/oval tumour cells with few or no admixed immature lymphocytes.
Type AB thymoma is a thymic epithelial neoplasm composed of variable proportions of a lymphocyte-poor spindle cell (type A) component and a lymphocyte-rich (type B-like) component.
Type AB thymoma has a significant population of immature T cells.
The ICD-O code for Thymoma, NOS is 8580/3.
The ICD-O code for Thymoma, type A is 8581/3.
The ICD-11 code for Malignant thymoma & Thymoma, type A is 2C27.2 & XH6WN9.
The ICD-O code for Thymoma, type AB is 8582/3.
The ICD-11 code for Malignant thymoma & Thymoma, type AB is 2C27.2 & XHoJH0.
The term 'mixed thymoma' is not recommended.
There are no subtypes for Thymoma, type A or type AB.
Type A thymoma is localized in the anterior (prevascular) mediastinum.
Ectopic occurrence of Type A thymoma is rare.
Type AB thymoma is localized in the anterior (prevascular) mediastinum.
Rare type A thymomas can display some degree of atypia, such as hypercellularity, increased mitotic counts, and particularly focal necrosis.
These atypical features, perhaps with the exception of tumour necrosis, are not associated with higher numbers of chromosomal alterations.
These atypical features are not associated with a more aggressive behaviour than conventional type A thymomas.
Metastases can occur in the absence of such 'atypical' histological features.
Single atypical type A thymomas with GTF2/mutations are on record.
The etiology of type A thymomas is unknown.
A minor role of genetic risk factors appears possible given the rare occurrence of thymomas (including type A thymoma) with familial background.
About 17-26% of patients with type A thymomas present with myasthenia gravis.
Patients with type A thymomas may present with symptoms related to the mass lesion or are incidentally discovered to have a mediastinal tumour on imaging examination.
Association with pure red cell aplasia may occur in patients with type A thymoma, but is not restricted to type A thymoma.
Type AB thymomas are frequently associated with a variety of paraneoplastic autoimmune disorders.
Myasthenia gravis is by far the most frequent paraneoplastic autoimmune disorder associated with type AB thymomas.
Approximately 18-25% of type AB thymomas are associated with myasthenia gravis.
Paraneoplastic pure red cell aplasia has been reported in patients with type AB thymomas.
Patients with type AB thymomas may present with symptoms related to the mass lesion or are incidentally discovered to have a mediastinal tumour on imaging examination.
The imaging features of type AB thymoma have not been studied individually, but they seem to overlap with those of the other thymoma subtypes.
Type A thymoma is generally well circumscribed or encapsulated.
The cut surface of Type A thymoma is homogeneous and light tan to white, with vague lobulation and occasionally focal cystic change.
Average tumour size of Type A thymoma is 72 mm.
Type AB thymomas are usually encapsulated.
The cut surface of Type AB thymomas is firm and shows multiple tan-coloured nodules of various sizes separated by white fibrous bands.
The average tumour size of Type AB thymomas is 70-80 mm.
Type A thymoma is one of the rarer thymoma subtypes.
In a review of over 2400 thymomas reported in multiple international studies, type A thymomas account for 11.5% (range: 3.1-26.2%) of all thymomas.
The ages of patients with type A thymoma range from 8 to 88 years (average: 64 years).
The average age of patients with type A thymoma is higher than those of patients with other thymomas (average: 50 years).
A slight female preponderance has been reported in most studies of type A thymoma.
Type AB thymoma is one of the most common thymoma subtypes and accounts for about 25% (range: 15-43%) of cases in most studies.
The mean age at disease onset for type AB thymoma is 57 years (range: 11-89 years).
There is a slight female predominance in type AB thymoma.
An origin of type A thymomas from a putative thymic epithelial cell precursor with minimal potential for corticomedullary differentiation has been proposed.
In an unsupervised cluster analysis of five data platforms, type A thymomas constituted a distinct subtype related (but not identical) to type AB thymomas.
Most type A thymomas harbour few genetic alterations, often involving chromosome 6q25.2.
Metastatic cases of type A thymomas have a tendency to show slightly higher genetic instability than localized tumours.
Virtually 100% of type A thymomas, including metastatic cases, show mutations of GTF2l (p.L424H, which is considered a founder mutation).
GTF2l is thought to be a positive regulator of cell morphogenesis, receptor tyrosine kinase signalling, retinoic acid receptors, neuronal processes, and WNT and sonic hedgehog signaling.

GTF2l is thought to be a negative regulator of apoptosis, cell cycle, DNA damage response, hormone receptor signalling, breast hormone signaling, RAS/MAPK, receptor tyrosine kinase, and TSC/mTOR signaling pathways.
HRAS mutations are common (80%) in type A thymomas and rare in other types of thymoma.
Overexpression of a large microRNA cluster on chromosome 19q13.42, which is thought to enhance PI3K/AKT/mTOR signalling, is another characteristic feature of type A thymomas.

A putative thymic epithelial cell precursor with the potential for bilineage, corticomedullary differentiation and restricted terminal medullary maturation has been proposed as the cell of origin for type AB thymomas.
An unsupervised cluster analysis of five data platforms suggested that type AB thymomas are related (but not identical) to type A thymomas.
The most frequent genetic alterations in type AB thymoma overlap with those observed in type A thymoma but are more frequent and more complex.
Shared alterations between type A and AB thymomas include losses of genetic material on chromosomes 2, 4, 6q25.2-q25.3, and 13.
Type A and AB thymomas share frequent mutation of GTF2l (p.L424H).
Type A and AB thymomas share overexpression of a microRNA cluster on chromosome 19q13.42, which is thought to enhance PI3K/AKT/mTOR signaling.
Nuclei showing dispersed chromatin and inconspicuous nucleoli are observed in type AB thymomas.
The large, vesicular nuclei with distinct nucleoli that are characteristic of the neoplastic cells in type B2 thymoma are only rarely seen in type AB thymomas.
Medullary islands are rare and Hassall corpuscles are generally absent in type AB thymomas.
The tumours are typically surrounded by a complete or incomplete fibrous capsule and may display coarse lobulation with thick fibrous bands.
Microcystic change can occur but is often more pronounced in subcapsular areas.
The tumours commonly show a fascicular, storiform, or haemangiopericytomalike growth pattern.

Other characteristic growth patterns include rosettes with or without a central lumen, glandular or glomeruloid structures, haemangioma-like papillary projections in cystic spaces, and meningioma-like whorls.
Multiple patterns can occur in the same tumour.
Perivascular spaces are less commonly seen than in other types of thymoma.
Hassall corpuscles are absent.
The tumour cells are spindly and/or oval-shaped, with bland nuclei, finely dispersed 'powdery' chromatin, and inconspicuous nucleoli.
In some cases, subpopulations of tumour cells are polygonal, with uniform round nuclei exhibiting a similar chromatin pattern.
Mitotic activity is low, with counts usually < 4 mitoses/2 mm^2.
There should be no or only very few ('easily countable') immature lymphocytes throughout.
Tumours with any lymphocyte-dense area ('not countable') or > 10% areas with moderate ('countable') infiltrate of TdT-positive T cells should be classified as type AB thymomas.
Type AB thymomas are usually well demarcated and often encapsulated.
Type AB thymomas show a lobulated growth pattern and are composed of a highly variable mixture of a lymphocyte-poor type A component and a type B-like lymphocyte-rich component.
These components can either form discrete, separate lobules or be intricately intermingled.
All the diverse histological features of type A thymoma can be seen in the type A component.
The type A component may comprise spindle cell fascicles that course around and among lymphocyte-rich nodules (type B-like component) like cellular fibrous septa.
The type B-like areas are different from type B1, B2, or B3 thymoma.
The tumour cells are small and oval, plump spindly, or polygonal in shape, with round to oval pale-staining nuclei.
The tumour cells are strongly positive for AE1-defined acidic keratins and p63/p40.
The tumour cells are negative for AE3-defined basic keratins.
The tumour cells are always negative for CK20.
Keratin expression is stronger in the cystic and glandular structures.
EMA is variably and only focally expressed.
FOXN1 and CD205 are positive.
CD5 and KIT (CD117) are negative.
Aberrant expression of CD20 in the neoplastic epithelial cells is a useful and frequent feature.
In very rare cases, single desmin-positive myoid cells may be seen.
TdT-positive immature T cells may be completely absent or account for only a minority of CD3-positive T cells.
CD20-positive B cells are usually absent.
Immunostaining for pancytokeratin, CK19, and p63/p40 highlights the dense and compact epithelial component, especially in the lymphocyte-rich areas.
The elongated spindle cells are often strongly positive for vimentin and EMA.
The elongated spindle cells are weakly to moderately positive for cytokeratins.
Tumour cells with aberrant expression of CD20 can be seen in both type A and type B-like areas.
CD20-positive B cells and desmin-positive myoid cells are usually absent.
The associated lymphocytes are mainly TdT-positive immature CD3-positive T cells.
There is no epithelial expression of CD5.
The Ki-67 proliferation index is usually low in the neoplastic epithelium.
Interpretation of the Ki-67 proliferation index can be difficult due to a high number of admixed proliferating immature T cells.
Reliable classification of thymomas is generally not possible in cytological specimens.
Smears of type A thymoma may contain only epithelial cells and thus can mimic other spindle cell lesions such as carcinoid, low-grade sarcoma, mesothelioma, and stromal cells in lymphoma.
The epithelial cells in type A thymoma do not show vesicular nuclei and have inconspicuous nucleoli.
Crush artefacts in clusters were reported to be less abundant in type A thymomas than in type AB and type B thymomas.
The epithelial cells in thymomas usually contain few lymphocytes.
The diagnosis of type A thymoma can be problematic in needle biopsies.
Type A thymoma is a spindle cell neoplasm in the mediastinum.
The most important differential diagnoses for type A thymoma are solitary fibrous tumour (cytokeratin-, STAT $^{3+}$, $	ext{CD34+}$) and synovial sarcoma (cytokeratin $+/-$, SS18-SSX $	ext{+}$).
Type A thymoma differs from AB thymoma in that it lacks the lymphocyte-rich component.

Any thymoma that looks like a type A thymoma but with any lymphocyte-dense area (TdT-positive T cells “impossible to count”) or with $>10\%$ area showing moderate infiltrate of TdT-positive T cells (“difficult to count”) should be classified as type AB thymoma.
Micronodular thymoma with lymphoid hyperplasia shows segregation of the lymphocyte-poor epithelial nodules from a lymphocyte-rich stroma lacking epithelium.
Micronodular thymoma with lymphoid hyperplasia features large numbers of B cells.
GTF2 mutations are highly characteristic, although not specific, for type A thymomas.
Molecular tests are not required for the diagnosis of type A thymomas.
In exceptional cases, when the clinical or anatomical situation is unusual (e.g. ectopic thymoma), the demonstration of a GTF2 p.L424H mutation would strongly support the diagnosis.
Staging should follow the Union for International Cancer Control (UICC) TNM system.
Most published series and clinical stratification schemes have been based on the Masaoka-Koga system.
Many experts still use and provide the Masaoka-Koga stage as additional information in their reports.
Most type A thymomas were diagnosed as limited disease, i.e., TNM stage I (corresponding to Masaoka stages I and II), in the International Thymic Malignancy Interest Group (ITMIG) cohort.
In the International Thymic Malignancy Interest Group (ITMIG) cohort and an independent meta-analysis, >90% of type AB thymomas are in TNM stage I (corresponding to Masaoka-Koga stages I and II).
Athymic epithelial tumour with bland spindle and oval, and rarely polygonal, epithelial cells with a fascicular, storiform, or haemangiopericytomatous growth pattern.
Most cases lack areas of necrosis and have a low mitotic count and a low Ki-67 index.
Atypical type A thymomas may have higher mitotic count and focal necrosis.
Strong expression of epithelial markers (e.g., p63/p40) is desirable.
Paucity or absence of immature TdT-positive T cells throughout the tumour is desirable.
A thymic tumour with a lobulated growth pattern is essential.
Admixed spindle cell-predominant lymphocyte-poor component (type A) and lymphocyte-rich component (type B) is essential.
Bland spindle, oval, and focally polygonal thymic epithelial cells and focal or diffuse abundance of immature T cells are essential.
In type A tumours with focal lymphocytic stroma in which lymphocytes are difficult to count, ≥ 10% area showing infiltrate of TdT-positive T cells should be classified as type AB thymoma.
TdT immunostaining to assess TdT-positive cell density is desirable for differential diagnosis with type A thymoma.
The overall survival rate of patients with type A thymoma is close to $100 \%$ at 5 years and 10 years.
Approximately $20 \%$ of patients with type A thymoma have Masaoka stage II or III tumours.
Atypical cases of type A thymoma tend to be at a higher stage at presentation.
In the ITMIG cohort, the overall survival rates of patients with R0-resected type A thymoma at 5 years and 10 years were $90 \%$ and $80 \%$, respectively.
The risk of recurrence is low if the tumour can be completely surgically removed.
Cases with local recurrences or distant metastases have been documented.
The local recurrence rate after complete resection is in the range of $5 - 10 \%$ after 5 and 10 years.
The association with myasthenia gravis has no significant effect on prognosis.
The overall survival rate for tumours of the thymus is $80 - 100 \%$ at 5 years and 10 years.
Most type AB thymomas can be cured by radical surgery.
Recurrence and metastasis are rare but do occur, justifying long-term clinical monitoring.
The presence or absence of paraneoplastic myasthenia gravis has no major effect on prognosis.
Limited data are available regarding platinum-based chemotherapy or radiosensitivity given the limited number of patients with advanced disease requiring adjuvant treatment.
